headline: Bermudian Springs Wrestlers Stop Indiana University of Pennsylvania
summary: The Bermudian Springs wrestling team defeated Indiana University of Pennsylvania in a successful match, extending their winning streak.
cleaned_text:
**Bermudian Springs Wrestlers Stop Indiana University of Pennsylvania**

Bermudian Springs wrestlers continued their successful streak Friday night, securing an 11-point victory over Indiana University of Pennsylvania.

In the opening match, Binky Collisi (159 lbs), who was the heaviest wrestler to register a gain, was featured. Michelle McNeill Northferrari scored a victory in 2:11, and Thim Dummer closed the meet by scoring a point in 1:18. The Bermudian Springs wrestlers won the match, with declared wins coming from Jo Diglisy, Carl Petit (18 lbs), Daniel Lehner (18 lbs), Andy Cappelli (177 lbs), and Sharp (18 lbs).

Tom Fink (18 lbs) added two other wins, contributing to a 19-point total victory for the team. Coach Roger Sanders praised the team's performance.

headline: BSC wrestlers stage comeback
summary: Bloomsburg State wrestlers staged a significant comeback victory over West Chester State, while the Berwick Bulldogs prepare for upcoming regional matches and manage scheduling changes.
cleaned_text:
**BSC Wrestlers Stage Comeback**

**Berwick Wrestling Updates**
It has been a long time since the Berwick Bulldogs have achieved back-to-back wrestling wins. However, Coach Jeff Lorson remains optimistic that the team has a chance this week.

Berwick is scheduled to face Shamokin on Thursday night at Berwick High School. The team will then travel to Central Columbia for a match on Saturday night.

Going into a clash with Lourdes Regional tonight (at Central Columbia), the team carries a 5-4 record. “Certainly—these are teams we should be able to compete with,” Lorson commented. “It's going to be interesting to see what will happen.”

Lorson was not pleased with his team’s effort against Milton this past Saturday night, resulting in a 52-12 loss.

**Bloomsburg State Comeback Victory**
The Bloomsburg State team did fall behind in the lightweight bouts Tuesday night, but they quickly staged a massive comeback. The Huskies roared back for a 35-9 victory over West Chester State at Nelson Fieldhouse in Bloomsburg. Ironically, all of West Chester’s points came from a decision at 118 lbs and a pin at 126 lbs.

Notable results from the bout included:
*   126 lbs: Joe Doughery (WE) pinned Bob Kore (WC).
*   134 lbs: Tom Fink (B) defeated Cari (decision).
*   150 lbs: Bucky McCollum pinned Brion McCarthy.
*   158 lbs: Don Lechner defeated Pate Naylor (3-1).
*   167 lbs: Ohvie Pol (decision).
*   177 lbs: Andy Cappelli (decision).
*   190 lbs: Mel Shorp won by forfeit.
*   HWT: Tino Dimarco won by forfeit.

**Team Updates and Scheduling**
In other news, Steve Graybeal, a sophomore 127-pounder, underwent knee surgery at Geisinger Medical Center, and Randy Peters, 132 lbs., sustained a shoulder separation during the Mt. Carmel match last week.

However, Lorson reported that two grapplers who were treated at Berwick Hospital after the Milton bout—Dave Farver and Randy Young—are okay. Young injured his wrist, and Farver had a contusion of the back.

On an administrative note, Mike Lylo, Berwick’s athletic director, announced that the February 2 match with Crestwood will be conducted at Crestwood, despite Berwick’s schedule listing it as a “home” match. Additionally, a bout between Columbia-Montour Tech and Halifax is scheduled for Thursday night at the Tech school.

title: The Evening Sun
headline: Bermudian Springs Eagles Aim to Build Foundation for Championship Run
summary: Coach McCollum and the Bermudian Springs Eagles wrestling team are preparing for the season with the goal of building a strong foundation and challenging for the League championship.
cleaned_text:
**Bermudian Springs Eagles Aim to Build Foundation for Championship Run**

The Bermudian Springs Eagles wrestling team is looking forward to the upcoming season, with coach McCollum expressing high hopes for the squad.

“We want to lay a foundation and build a program,” McCollum stated. “This year, I’m not focused on the won-loss record; we want to build a foundation.”

The team boasts several talented wrestlers. Junior Kevin Messinger is expected to lead the team, starting at 103 pounds. Sophomore Leonard Lobaugh is slated for 112 pounds, while sophomore returning letterman Don Williams will compete at 119 pounds. McCollum claims that another sophomore, [Name unclear, but associated with 125 lbs], is “probably the best sophomore on the team” and will compete at 125 pounds.

Other key starters include aggressive letterman Bill Aiello, who will start at 145 pounds. Sophomore Doug Osten returns as a letterman at 152 pounds. Junior Ron Highlands, who performed well last year, gets the call at 171 pounds. Sophomore Doug Campbell, who is out for the first time, is the choice at 189 pounds.

McCollum also noted that while the team has depth, they currently lack heavyweights.

Looking ahead, McCollum expects the Bermudian Springs Eagles to again assert themselves and contend for the League championship. He identified Northern and Susquenita as the primary challengers.

**Wrestling Season Wrap-Up: Local Teams Show Strong Performance**

The conclusion of the wrestling season saw several local high schools compete fiercely, with records and rankings providing a snapshot of the competitive landscape.

**Palmyra** finished the season with a 4-5 record. Their returning lettermen included Carl Boitz (103), Gary Reesor (119), Dave Frantz (132), and Mike Boyer (167).

**Mechanicsburg**, coached by Steve Sauve, finished 7th with a 3-6 record. Their returning lettermen were Kevin Shaughness (105), Steve Dietrich (112), Dave Weaver (119), Jeff Shipman (126), Todd Flagg (132), Wayne Curtis (138), Fred Pisano (145), Jim Naegele (155), Don Dye (167), Steve Sudak (185), and John Parry (heavyweight). The team showed potential for improvement over previous years.

**Susquehanna Township**, coached by Bob Jacoby, finished 8th with a 2-7 record. The returning lettermen included Brian Barbour (138), John Martin (145), Mark McLaren (155), Dwayne Brewer (167), Jeff Ackerman (185), and Jack Geisel (heavyweight).

**East Pennboro**, coached by Jeb Billet, finished with a 1-8 record. Mark Wolfgang (132) was listed among the returning lettermen.

**Chambersburg**, coached by Jim Olson, finished 6th with a 2-5 record. Their returning lettermen included Don Strait (98), Mike Plum (112), Tony Purvis (126), Jeff Berger (145), Steve Adams (155), and Chris Bender (185).

**New Oxford**, coached by Don Adams, finished 7th with a 7-6 record.

**Scotland**, coached by Ken Katusin, had a record of 38-37.

**Shippensburg**, coached by Lou Butler, achieved a dominant 13-0 record, finishing 1st. Their returning lettermen included Rob McElheny (126), Rick Munkittrick (119), Wayne Hershey (145), Dave Corcoran (138), Roy Myers (167), and Dennis Bell (185).

**Susquequita**, coached by Steve Peters, finished 4th with a 10-3 record. The returning lettermen included Ray Barrick (105), Mark Hammack (112), Harold Lukens (119), Doug Dudley (138), Bob Bornman (145), Jim Dersham (155), and Don Griffin (heavyweight).

**Lafayette** finished with a strong showing, with details of their performance suggesting a competitive season.

summary: New Oxford won a South Central League wrestling meet, securing the victory despite being missing two starters.
cleaned_text:
**The Gettysburg Times**
**Fri, Dec 14, 1979**

**New Oxford Wins South Central League Meet**

New Oxford defeated their opponent in a South Central League meet, winning with a final score of 27 to 39-14. The team, referred to as the Bolts, secured the victory after winning five of the last six weight classes.

The match was tough, as the Bolts were without two of their starters due to illness. Myers defeated Melvin Riser in the 194-pound class to secure the victory for the Bolts.

“It was a real barn-burner, but the kids hung in there and did the job,” said Coach Tarn Weikel. “We beat them in the upper weights where their strength was, but the kids made some mistakes that we are going to have to improve.”

During the match, Andy Allessie tied the score at 27 for Littlestown in the 185-pound class with a pin of Wayne Beaver, before Myers won in the heavyweight class. Gordan Lombard also recorded a pin for the Bolts, while Joe Boyd, Bill Asper, and Ashley Melhorn pinned opponents for New Oxford.

“It was a tough way to lose a match, but Littlestown had some excellent lead, and Allessie, in particular, was good,” said a New Oxford representative.

Despite some talented veterans like Marty and Mike White, and Eric Shaffer, the key to the team event will rest on the performance of the younger athletes.

**TOURNAMENT CHAMPS**

**Bermudian Springs Wins Team Title at Harrisburg High Holiday Tournament**

Bermudian Springs came away with the team title and captured four individual championships at the Harrisburg High Holiday Tournament. The victorious wrestlers, by decision, included Joe Lenker (left) at 105 and Shawn McClain at 132, as well as wrestlers at 115 and Doug Tosten at 167. The Eagles of Cac earned 46 points, 10 more than runner-up Seliphia.

Following were Steelton Highspire with 34 points, West Perry with 32, Susquehanna Twp. with 29, Halifax with 25, Harrisburg with 27, and East Pennsboro with 17. Two other wrestlers reached the finals but were defeated by decisions: Kevin Messinger at 112 and Leonard Lobaugh at 126. (Evening Sun Photos)

headline: Bermudian Springs Captures Title at Harrisburg Tournament
summary: The article reports on a wrestling tournament victory by Bermudian Springs, details a loss for Gettysburg to King's College, and previews the major college football bowl games of 1979.
cleaned_text:
**Bermudian Springs Captures Title at Harrisburg Tournament**

Bermudian Springs won the Harrisburg Tournament on Saturday at the Camp Curtin Gymnasium in Harrisburg, recording four victories in the finals. The Eagles scored 46 points, ten points more than their closest opponent, Simca Gratz of Philadelphia. Steel-High finished third with 34 points.

Bermudian Springs’ Bill Aiello, who won the 155-pound class, was named the tournament’s outstanding wrestler. Aiello also recorded the fastest pin, achieved in the opening round in a time of 31 seconds. The three other tournament champions were Joe Lenker, Shawn McClain, and Doug Tosten. Joe Lobaugh placed second in the 126-pound class.

“I was kind of surprised. I didn't expect to win, and especially by 10 points,” Aiello stated.

Coach McCollum commented on the team's performance: “We have some outstanding individuals, but in individual meets, we don’t have that good of team balance. I was very pleased with the performances of McClain, Aiello, and Tosten. And it was a shock for Lobaugh to finish second in the tournament.”

The remaining team finishes were: fourth, West Perry (3); fifth, Susquehanna (29); sixth, Halifax (28); seventh, Harrisburg (27); and eighth, East Pennsboro (17).

headline: Five County Wrestlers Compete in PIAA Championships
summary: Adams County's five wrestlers are preparing for the PIAA Championships, with matchups scheduled for the Class AA and Class AAA preliminary rounds.
cleaned_text:
**Five County Wrestlers Compete in PIAA Championships**

**The Gettysburg Times**
Wed, Mar 12, 1980

Adams County's five top scholastic wrestlers are set to compete at the PIAA Championships at the Hershey Park Arena. The preliminary rounds are scheduled for Thursday, beginning with the Class AA preliminary round at 9 a.m., followed by the Class AAA preliminary round at 2 p.m., and concluding with the Class AA quarterfinals at 7 p.m.

Gettysburg High School (Class AAA) has the most entries from any county school, with two wrestlers competing. In the opening round of the 138-pound class, Tim Singleton (25-21) will face Jeff Foreman (22-3) of Philipsburg-Osceola. Meanwhile, teammate Perry Smith (25-2-1) is scheduled to face John Connelly (24-4) of Allentown Central Catholic in the Class AA.

The other competitors include Bermudian Springs' Bill Aiello (23-0-1), who will face John Easter (130-3) of Fort LeBoeuf in the preliminary round of the 155-pound class. Also competing in the 155-pound class is Stan Mentzer (19-4) of Biglerville, who will face Wade Ruby (12-3) of Mifflinburg in the first round. The fifth wrestler from the county is Andy Allessie (24-3-1) of Littlestown, who will face Mark Rich (18-2) of Albert Gallatin.

Gettysburg coach Steve Spatz stated that the wrestlers were in good health and ready to compete. Regarding the matchups, Spatz noted that Bill Aiello faced a tough opponent in Easter, who had placed fourth in the state last year in the 149-pound class.

Mentzer is hoping that a pulled back muscle does not hamper his efforts, while Allessie hopes to achieve a significant victory for Littlestown.

**Revised and Corrected Text:**

**Boxing Match Report (Unattributed)**
The champion, unbeaten since 1976, looked to be headed for defeat in the ninth round when O'Grady opened a cut over Watt’s right eye. The referee called for a doctor before deciding if the Scot could continue. Instead of continuing to work on Watt’s face, O'Grady went looking for one decisive punch, and Watt got back to jabbing out piston-like right hands. An apparent clash of heads in the tenth round opened a cut over the bridge of O’Grady’s nose, but the brave champion produced two superb rounds to finish off O’Grady’s challenge. With blood seeping into O’Grady’s eyes, it was clear he could not continue. Watt was well ahead on points when the referee stepped in, but O’Grady’s stern punching frequently had him backpedaling. O'Grady, who received a threatening letter before the fight and was booed when he entered the ring, was given a standing ovation for his bravery by the 2,500 crowd. Watt said afterwards: ‘The injury to O’Grady’s nose was caused by a head clash — but it was accidental.’

**New Mat Job at Bermudian Springs**
Doug Niebel, former Penns Valley High coach, has taken on new duties as assistant coach and senior high counselor at Bermudian Springs. Niebel lettered in football at State College High and later wrestled at Clarion State College. Springs High, a District 3 school, is located in York Springs. Niebel resigned as head coach after the 1979-80 season, and Rich Kepler, a former State College and Penn State wrestler, replaced him. Niebel previously taught and coached nine years at Penns Valley, a Class AA school. Bermudian Springs is also a Class AA school.

**West Chester Wins in College Football**
WEST CHESTER, Pa. (AP) — Quarterback Ron Gaynor passed for one touchdown and ran for another to lead West Chester to a 26-13 win over East Stroudsburg Friday night in college football.

headline: Blue Mountain League Teams Prepare for Wrestling Season
summary: Several high schools in the Blue Mountain League, including Gettysburg, Biglerville, and Littlestown, are preparing for the wrestling season despite challenges like graduation losses and building depth.
cleaned_text:
**The Blue Mountain League Prepares for Season of Wrestling**

As the open of the season approaches, several Blue Mountain League high schools are preparing their wrestling teams. Adams County's other two league entries, Biglerville and Littlestown, have some experienced wrestlers back, while Gettysburg and Bermudian Springs are facing unique challenges.

Gettysburg High School is dealing with the loss of numerous lettermen. According to coach Spatz, the graduating class includes standouts such as Single-ton, Perry Smith, Alan Waybright, Rob Saylor, Randy Rudisill, John Hess, Steve Grube, and Paul Tulley. "The loss of so many lettermen isn’t the only problem," Spatz noted. "Aside from the lack of returning lettermen, we also have kids from the championship football team coming out two weeks late." Despite these setbacks, McCollum, the coach, remains optimistic. "It is nice to have the lettermen back," McCollum said. "And we did get a couple of kids out who are fine to help us. We look a little more balanced up top. We're combining youth with experience. We have a lot of young kids mixed in with the seniors."

In Biglerville, the Canners are expected to have depth, particularly in the middle and upper weights, as they aim to improve upon last year's 7-5 record. Coach Mike Thompson stated, "We feel we're going to be pretty decent. We need to get the boys in the right places and not have injuries. I feel we can be a contender." The team has a mix of talent, including 10 seniors and five juniors, alongside 14 sophomores and one freshman. While the lighter weights may pose a challenge, the team has potential starters like Craig Clapsaddle (junior) at 105 pounds, and sophomore Eric Shawl as a reserve.

Littlestown coach Tom Weikel is focused on developing his young team. He stated, "As a team, I think we are going to take our best shot early in the season. We have to get our freshmen and sophomores through the transition from junior high to varsity." Weikel is building his team around two nucleus wrestlers who reached Round II of the district tournament last spring. Leading the charge for the Bolts is Andy Allessie, a senior who won a sectional championship last season. Allessie plans to switch between 185 pounds and heavyweight, complemented by senior Toby Boyd.

headline: High School Sports Roundup: Wrestling and Basketball Results
summary: The article reports on various high school sports results from December 1980, detailing wrestling upsets and scores from multiple basketball tournaments.
cleaned_text:
**Gettysburg Times**
**Saturday, December 27, 1980**

**High School Sports Roundup: Wrestling and Basketball Results**

**Wrestling Highlights**
The wrestling action featured several key matchups. In the tournament finals, the defending champion at the tournament, Bermudian Springs, was challenged by several opponents.

In one notable bout, the fourth-seeded Rick Hickoff upset top-seed Keith Henderson of Harrisburg at 119 pounds. Another standout performance was by Bill Aiello, 155 pounds, who was highlighted at 119 pounds.

**Basketball Results**
The weekend saw multiple tournaments.

**Boys Basketball:**
*   **Gettysburg at Cedar Cliff Tourney:** The game was played at 7:00 PM.
*   **Delone at Littlestown Tourney:** The game was played at 7:06 PM.
*   **South Western at Fairfield Tourney:** The game was played at 7:00 PM.

In the Girls Basketball section, the article mentions the Cedar Cliff at Delone tournament, where the team was undefeated, going 13-0.

**Other Sports Notes:**
The article also mentions a Men's Basketball game where Steve Carter of East Pennsboro was defeated by the team from Gettysburg.

title: MAINTAINING BALANCE — As (Bermudian Springs High
headline: Bermudian Springs' Bill Aiello Targets State Championship Title
summary: Defending District Three champion Bill Aiello of Bermudian Springs is aiming for the State title, supported by his teammates and coach McCollum.
cleaned_text:
MAINTAINING BALANCE

As Bermudian Springs High teammate Shawn McClain goes for a single-leg takedown, the Eagles’ Bill Aiello, the defending PIAA District Three champion, strives to maintain his balance. By working together in practice sessions, McClain and Aiello have helped each other achieve considerable success during the 1980-81 season.

YORK SPRINGS — There is a well-kept secret down the road at Bermudian Springs, but with the PIAA District Three Tournament getting under way at the end of February, Bill Aiello may well step away from obscurity and, with a little bit of luck, make a big way. Aiello is the defending District Three champion, and this time around the outspoken senior has his sights set on the State title.

“Last year my goal was to just get into the State Tournament, but this year I want to set the championship,” said Aiello.

Aiello, son of Bill and Yolanda Aiello of York Springs, is the youngest of five brothers. Mike, Jave, Jim, and Bob all wrestled for the Eagles before Bill got there.

Aiello has posted a 15-0 record in wins by pi and Bermudian Springs. McCollum still sees his 155-pounder as a strong contender.

“I think Bill has an excellent chance to win as the District champion, although we will most definitely be a target for the other wrestlers. Once he gets to states, it's a whole different story,” said McCollum.

McCollum believes Aiello will have to be doubly ready once the tournaments start.

“The discipline needed to be a good wrestler is totally up to the person. You can make of yourself what you will,” Aiello stated.

The coach, Doug Tosten, noted that the younger kids look up to Bill not only because of his accomplishments but because he sets a fine example through hard work in practice.

Aiello prefers tournament matches because of the individual nature of the competition. He explained, “We are able to relax and focus on the mats, and I think it is the camaraderie that makes it worthwhile.”

title: “Dave h
headline: McClain Hopes for State Tournament Success
summary: Undefeated wrestler Shawn McClain discusses his goal of reaching the state tournament, reflecting on his recovery from serious back injuries.
cleaned_text:
Shawn McClain, one of two undefeated Eagles, is set to compete in the upcoming tournament. McClain, along with teammate Bill Aiello, is hoping his experience will help him reach his goal of competing at the state tournament.

“I've always wanted to go to states,” McClain said. “But you more or less have to take them one at a time. Each tournament is different, so anything can happen. You just have to be ready for every match.”

McCollum, the coach, noted that the tournament format could change, adding that he has been to the district tournament before and knows what it is all about.

McClain detailed his journey, explaining that he first strained his back and later tore some cartilage. He stated that it took him until the sectionals to recover and make up for the injuries, but he did it in a big way.

The article also mentions other athletes, including Tim Stoey of Bermudian Springs, who won the sectional championship earlier in the year. Additionally, Clan, who plans to attend Williamsport Area College, is one of three captains for the Eagles this season, alongside Aiello and another teammate.

title: Six warriors Reach Semifinals
headline: Getsburg Spring Storm Wrestlers Reach Semifinals at National Tournament
summary: The Getsburg Spring Storm wrestling team achieved a successful season, reaching the semifinals at the AAA national tournament hosted by the University of Minnesota.
cleaned_text:
**Six Warriors Reach Semifinals**

By MARK PURIDY
Time Sports Writer

Getsburg's Spring Storm produced the best showing of the season at the AAA national tournament hosted by the University of Minnesota. The team advanced via airwaves, with five fellow Bermudian Springs wrestlers signaling their success. The team also saw the Iowa Spring Storm and Iowa State University Hammer move forward.

Delon also moved to Hammer's. Lebanon to Hammer's.

Getsburg's Tim Butler, backed by a team of six, will now raise high school with two programs eligible for pro in a season at Churchill in Chaska.

headline: Upsets Prevail at Chambersburg Tournament
summary: Upsets characterized the Chambersburg Tournament, where Bermudian Springs wrestlers advanced to Round II of Districts, while other wrestlers achieved notable victories and consolation honors.
cleaned_text:
The Gettysburg Times
Mon, Mar 02, 1981 -Page 10

Upsets prevailed at the Chambersburg Tournament, as only six of the top seeds won their matches. Bermudian Springs had a strong showing in the tournament Saturday, and all five of their wrestlers advanced to Round II of Districts, including sectional champs Aiello and McClain.

As expected, Aiello had little trouble in the tournament, defeating Susquenita’s Dave Parrish 8-2 in the semifinals before beating Bailey in the finals. McClain won an 11-0 decision over Camp Hill's Tim Wolfe in the afternoon to reach the finals.

“The best guy in the tournament is McClain,” said Lenker.

Later, Doug Tosten won a 1-0 overtime decision over Northern's Mike Walsh in the afternoon, then lost to Bingham’s Ron Duncan 2-1 in the evening.

The other Delone wrestler to win consolation honors was John Daigie, who defeated Northeastern’s Ben Smith in the Capen area. Daigie was semi-fast enough to defeat Mark Groft of Hanover on a pin.

“I feel excellent about it,” Bottorf said of sending four boys to Round II. “We had one first and a second and two thirds. I feel really super about it.”

Littlestown had five boys left in action Saturday, and three of them made it through for the chance to compete in Round II. The biggest disappointment for the Bolts was the second-place finish by Allessie, who had beaten Ed Chandier of South Western in the semifinals 15-10, before losing to [opponent name missing].

Littlestown coach Tom Weikel said, “He looked terrible. I don't know what his problem was. He didn’t wrestle well at all. He didn’t make any attempts. He made no countore for takedowns.”

title: 22 Countians Set For Round II Of D
headline: Countians Set for Round II of District Tournament
summary: Over 212 Adam County students are set to compete in the first round of a district tournament in Geysburg, featuring teams from Geysburg, Pendleton State College, Binghamton, and Northern Lebanon.
cleaned_text:
**Countians Set for Round II of District Tournament**

A total of 212 Adam County high school students will compete in the first round of the tournament. Geysburg is the only AAA team to advance to the second round, and Pendleton State College is the only AAA team to advance to the third round. All participating schools will travel to Geysburg to compete. The coaches, including Geysburg coach Steve Spat, will add their experience to the tournament.

In other news, the Binghamton team will defend against Northern Lebanon in a center game.

title: “Dave
headline: Bermudian Springs Targets Championship at Capital City Holiday Wrestling Tournament
summary: The Bermudian Springs wrestling team, coached by McCollum, is preparing for the Capital City Holiday Wrestling Tournament, aiming for a season turning point with experienced champions and promising younger athletes.
cleaned_text:
Gettysburg Coach Steve Spatz stated that while the numbers game might hurt his team, he felt they could still perform well and hoped for some champions.

In addition to the Bermudian Springs entries and host Chambersburg, other teams participating in the tournament include Conemaugh Township, Everett, and Penn Manor. The Capital City Holiday Wrestling Tournament will feature both varsity and junior varsity competition.

McCollum, the coach for Bermudian Springs, expressed confidence, stating, "I think we have a good shot. It all depends on the younger kids. I'm hoping it will be a turning point of our season."

McCollum plans to bring three champions and one runner-up from last year's tournament. Last year's champions included Darryl Silsley at 103, Joe Lenker at 119, and Hickoff at 125. Drew Highlands was the runner-up at 145.

The junior varsity tournament begins at noon on Tuesday, with finals scheduled for Wednesday. Varsity finals will start at 2:00 PM, and junior varsity finals will start at 3:30 PM.

title: The Gettysburg Times (nae)
headline: Bermudian Springs Eagles Find Strength in Underclassmen
summary: Despite league disruptions and relying on younger talent, the Bermudian Springs Eagles are maintaining a competitive season, while other local teams continue to build experience.
cleaned_text:
The Gettysburg Times
Thu, Jan 21, 1982

**Eagles Maintain Spirit Despite Challenges**

On most nights, the Warriors practice with only 17 or 18 boys, and as Spatz points out, "some of them are banged up too." Despite the challenges, Spatz stated, "We haven't given up and we don't plan on it. We have a couple of college prospects in this year’s senior group, and things look bright for next year. We have some good sophomores and juniors."

However, thinking of next year brings another disappointment—the breakup of the league for Spatz. "That irritates me," Spatz said. "I think it’s a major mistake as far as competitiveness goes. It will hurt me and Waynesboro the most."

The Bermudian Springs Eagles, who lost many outstanding wrestlers from last year’s team and have had a lot of new faces in the lineup, are still performing well, holding a 3-4 record in the league and 4-5 overall, in addition to winning the Harrisburg Christmas Tourney Plus.

Bermudian Springs coach McCollum expressed satisfaction with the team's effort. "I feel as though we've been competitive. We were probably going to be taking our lumps, but after the first two matches—close losses to Heynesboro and Gettysburg—I thought maybe we could do better."

**Underclassmen Help Eagles Shine**

McCollum pointed out that the underclassmen have been crucial to the Eagles' success. While only three seniors—Joe Lenker, Jon Miller, and Tim Ruth—are regularly in the lineup, they have needed help from the younger wrestlers, and they have received it. Brian Starner, a junior, is 8-3 at 98 pounds, and sophomore Daryl Silsley is 10-1 at 105 and 112 pounds. Rick Hickoff, a junior, is undefeated at 126 pounds in JV matches, while juniors Charles Rutley (145 lbs) and Drew Highlands (155 lbs) are 9-3.

**Other Teams Look Ahead**

Swope expects his team to continue improving on last season. He realizes that improvement will come soon. "I think we can do it," Swope said.

At Littlestown, Tom Weikel spoke for his inexperienced Bolts. "We have a lot of freshmen; they are not winning, but they are coming around," Weikel said. The Bolts are currently going through a learning curve. Littlestown has not won at 155 pounds or 145 pounds. Only two Littlestown wrestlers have records thus far: one at 98 pounds with an 8-2 record, and senior Dave Sentz.

The other county teams all have several of their representatives advancing. Next in line is Littlestown, which advances six wrestlers in Class AAA, Section I, and is one half joint second in team in Class AA, Section I. Littlestown will advance four, and New Oxford three. Both teams are competing in Delone’s Section: Class AA, Section V.

In Bermudian Springs’ bracket, Delone is seeded one at 113 pounds. Koontz pinned his opponent in 42 seconds. Number two seed Jim Smith also advanced with two pins in 2:41. Also advancing for the Squires were Jim Niederer, Sonny Sneeringer, Brian Kelli, Jeff John Daigle, and Clyde Tipton. New Oxford's two top wrestlers scored two points while Susquehanna had 49, Hanover 47, and Littlestown 35 to round out the top five. New Oxford is eighth with 22.12 points.

“It's looking really interesting,” said Delone coach Ken Bot. “I'm very pleased. The guys have done a good job. But they need tomorrow to go 0—isn't good enough!”

Gettysburg, in Class AAA, advances six wrestlers, led by Sheldon Smith. Smith pinned his opponent at 145 pounds in 3:03. Other Warrior wrestlers advancing are John Eberhart (112), Jim Strausbaugh (119), Ed Cunningham (126), Bill McFadden (167), and Dave Shealer (185). Smith was the top seed at 145, while Eberhart and Cunningham were number two. Smith is now 24-2 on the year.

Also advancing was Rick Hickoff (at 126) with a pin in 5.30. Also advancing was Drew Highlands (155) with a 14-2 decision. Highlands will go against the number two seed, and Hickoff against the number three seed (Kevin Kemper from Northern) in two key matches today.

“We were a little disappointed that Tim Ruth only led Eagle wrestlers not to advance. Tim had pinned him earlier. Hickoff and Highlands will have key matches. The others [will advance],” said Coach Bot.

Biglerville, with only a number three seed to boast of, could advance two.

title: <> Ut "Daven
headline: Local Athletes Receive Varsity Letters and Awards
summary: The article reports on various local high school athletes receiving varsity letters and certificates across multiple sports, including a mention of the wrestling program at Bermudian Springs.
cleaned_text:
Local Athletes Receive Varsity Letters and Awards

The Gettysburg Times reported on local high school athletes who received varsity letters and certificates for their achievements in various sports.

In boys basketball, recipients included Rob Bullock, Larry Stoner, Mike Duncan, Todd Hannan, Dave Horf, Joe Weaver, Eric Ensor, Wayde Ibaugh, and Jim Miller.

For girls basketball, the varsity letters were awarded to:
Seniors: Judy Behney, Suzi Stevens, Candy Cook (manager), and Tammy Griest (manager).
Juniors: Jodi Brough, Sharon Krout, and Tammy Zepp.
Sophomores: Lisa Eisenhard and Chris Sebright.
Freshmen: Barb Heid, Wendy Reese, Teresa Sauble, Tracy Shank, Amy Spangler, Connie Stambaugh, and N Wolf.

Other award recipients included Tracey Miller, Chris Rohrbaugh, and Jill Yeagy.

Additionally, other students received certificates, including Janice Staines (manager) and Fara Whitacre (manager) in the junior class, and Brian Bossom, Colin Campbell, Dave Cook, and Todd Eichelberger in the sophomore class. Freshmen recipients included Paul Chupp, Greg Larson, Tim McCreary, and Meredith Sutherland.

The article also noted the presence of wrestling coach McCollum and the athletic program at Bermudian Springs.

headline: Regional and Local Wrestling Action Continues
summary: The article reports on various regional wrestling tournaments, including matchups in the West, East, and Midwest, alongside local high school wrestling successes at the Districts.
cleaned_text:
The Gettysburg Times
Mon, Mar 08, 1982 -Page 9

**Regional Wrestling Action Continues**

The wrestling season saw action across multiple regions. In the West Regional at Pullman, Idaho, the Louisiana winner and Minnesota's 22-5 team met the winner between North Carolina State and Te Atanoga. In the East Regional at Uniondale on March 14, Alabama (23-4) faced the St. John’s-Penn winner, and Villeora (22-7) competed against the St. John’s-Northeastern winner. The Midwest Regional at Dallas had DePaul (26-1) competing against the Boston Francisco.

**Local High School Wrestling**

At the Districts, Smith was one of three Gettysburg wrestlers. The other two didn't fare as well. Bill Gob poh was pinned past before the buzzer, and Dave Shealer lost.

Bermudian Springs' Silsley took an easy 12-2 decision from Mike Kochen of Halifax before holding on for a 74-win over Kurt Ryan of Hanover. The Eagles had taken five wrestlers to the Districts.

Another local story highlighted the freshman John Johnston of Big Valley, who won an upset victory against Jim of Manheim Central. "It's kind of a surprise," said Big Valley coach Mike Bucher. "He beat a winner. It's a thrill for him and for all of us. He's the only freshman in his weight class. He's been doing a good job."

The Canners’ other entry, Tracy McLaughlin, competed at 132 pounds. "He wrestled well, but the other kid was pretty good," noted one observer, referencing John Cherrie of Brandywine.

title: “Dave
headline: Bermudian Springs Eagles Rely on Quality Over Numbers
summary: Despite having only 20 wrestlers, the Bermudian Springs Eagles are confident in their team's quality and experience to compete for Blue Mountain League honors.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Sat, Nov 27, 1982

**Bermudian Springs Eagles Ready to Soar**

Bermudian Springs (NGS) believes that while the team may lack in numbers, it makes up for it with quality.

“We have 20 wrestlers,” said McCollum. “The low number of people does not indicate a weak team. We have five district qualifiers back, plus a handful of lettermen and several boys who have past varsity experience but had to sit out last year.”

The Eagles return shine lettermen from last season's 10-8 team, including District Three Class A semifinalist Daryl Sisley, a Junior. Other qualifiers include seniors Brian Starner, Doug McCauslin, and Chris Culbert. Sophomore Kevin, along with freshmen Brent Keech and Trevor Byers, also bring varsity experience and are expected to help build a solid lineup.

“We should have good balance and strength if everyone can stay healthy,” added McCollum. “The lettermen, two wrestlers with varsity experience, and some sophomore ninth graders should provide a tough lineup to start.”

Keech, Starner, and Byers are expected to lead the Eagles out of the blocks at 98 through 112, with Miller and Sisley following up at 112 and 119.

The roster also features Don Laughman, who will probably back up Rutley at 155, and junior Carl Divelbiss, who will offer support for Highlands at 167. Another wrestler, listed as the starter at 185, will be paired with Culbert and eighth-grader Charles Rohrbaugh, who anchors the heavyweight position.

McCollum said he hopes to pick up a few more wrestlers as the season gets underway in order to field a full varsity squad.

Aside from the Eagles, the coach sees Biglerville, Get-gear, and Waynesboro as top contenders for Blue Mountain League honors.

“We did it in football and we would like to do it in wrestling,” said McCollum, who previously served as an assistant coach for Bermudian’s BML champion football team.

title: ov we wy!
headline: Gettysburg Warriors and Local Teams Start Strong in Season Openers
summary: The Gettysburg Warriors won their basketball opener, while local wrestling teams Bermudian Springs and Delone recorded key victories and performances in their respective season starts.
cleaned_text:
The Gettysburg Warriors defeated the Scotland Cadettes 62-46 in their Friday BML opener. Coach Mike Nett stated that the team "got the rust out of our gears" in the second half, securing the victory through a strong fourth-quarter surge.

In wrestling news, Bermudian Springs, aiming for the Blue Mountain League title, started its season with a decisive victory, pinning Littlestown 46-18. The Eagles' success was highlighted by ninth-grader Brent Keech, who pinned Chris Reighner in 32 seconds. Veteran Brian Starner won by forfeit, and Trevor Byers secured another pin.

Meanwhile, the Delone team suffered an opening season loss to Central York (36-20). Despite the loss, Delone showed flashes of potential. Sonny Sneeringer pinned Don Simmons in the 112 weight class, and Brian Kelley followed with a win by decision against Rob Saeger at 119 pounds. The program, guided by coach McCollum, continues to build on its raw talent.

title: “Dave h
headline: Mid-Penn Conference Wrestling Contenders Face Uncertainty
summary: Several Pennsylvania high school wrestling teams, including Bermudian Springs, Gettysburg, and James Buchanan, are preparing for the 1983 season amid uncertainty regarding the Mid-Penn Conference membership.
cleaned_text:
The Patriot-News Feature
Sunday, January 2, 1983

The uncertainty regarding the Mid-Penn Conference membership remains high. It is unlikely that Biglerville, Northern, or Susquenita will join the conference, as they have taken their leave. Greencastle has also dropped out. The potential members include:Biglerville, Gettysburg, James Buchanan, Littlestown, New Oxford, Scotland, and Waynesboro.

At the outset, Bermudian Springs could accede to the throne. The coaching staff, led by McCollum, has a host of veterans. Following a winning performance in the Littlestown Tournament, coupled with a tie against Gettysburg and a strong showing against Northern, Bermudian Springs is a major contender. Waynesboro also possesses outstanding personnel, including Kurt Bowman, who was third in the District 3 AAA competition last year. However, the Indians are expected to be young and error-prone this year.

At the Littlestown Tournament, Tracy McLaughlin had a strong year, finishing with a 15-6-1 mark at 132 pounds. Bob Taylor, who wrestled at 98 pounds a year ago, and Bill Weller, who wrestled at 132 pounds, are also noted. Darryl Davis (132), Thad Ecke (167), and Gary Roby (185) are also mentioned.

**Gettysburg:**
Bryant McKinley has taken over the coaching reins and is hopeful of quickly molding a winner. Before the holiday break, the Warriors were 2-2-1 in duals, with a 1-0-1 league mark. At that point, Harvey Bollinger, the 155-pounder, was 4-0-1, but he came up empty in the Chambersburg tournament, where Bill McFadden won the 185-pound title. Dave Shealer returns at 167 pounds after a 15-11 sophomore season.

**James Buchanan:**
The Rockets did not perform as usual last year, winning only three matches for coach Ed Snyder. The strength of the team is highlighted by Bryce Marshall at 126 pounds and Brian Brake, who pinned his way to the 155-pound championship in the Chambersburg Tournament.

Local wrestling action continues to heat up as teams prepare for the crucial matches of the season. The coaches emphasized that the early contests are vital for determining the outcome of the season. Mike Bucher, who mentors the team, noted that the importance of these first few matches cannot be overstated.

The rivalry between Bergen and Bermudian Springs remains a major focus. The success of the team will depend on key victories, such as those anticipated in the 132-pound weight class. The coaching staff, including McCollum, is focused on preparing the athletes for these high-stakes bouts.

title: “Dave h
headline: Koontz Dominates Tournament, Eyes State Title
summary: Koontz secured two early pins and is set to compete for the state title, while other team members, including Silsley and Ruiley, suffered losses during the tournament.
cleaned_text:
The Gettysburg Times
Fri, Mar 18, 1983

Koontz dominated his opening bouts at the tournament, first pinning Ken Visavati of Elk Lake in 2:42, and then returning with a pin of Tom Lannen of Tyrone (2:27).

“Tony is looking tough,” said Koontz’ coach, Dave Hoover. “He handled both opponents easily. We were a little concerned about Lannen; we watched his first match, and he was quick and fast. He wrestled in the tough central Pennsylvania area against a whole way.”

Hoover added, “We hope for more success tomorrow or Friday. Koontz’ opponent today will be Sam Quick of Mahoney Area. If he wins, he will meet the winner of the Matt Johnson (York Catholic) vs. Mike Hudy (Huntingdon) match for the state title. A loss will put him in the consolation bracket.”

Meanwhile, Bermudian Springs’ Daryl Silsley went out after an 84-point loss to Brian Pritt of Minersville. Silsley suffered a collarbone injury in the first 30 seconds of the match and could not recover, forcing the loss. Rutley also lost by an 84-point count, the margin of defeat coming when Rutley was on the receiving end of a Fireman’s Carry in the first period, resulting in four points for his opponent.

The coaches reflected on the day’s events. “We felt Daryl had an excellent chance to continue,” said one coach, while another noted, “We weren’t as confident of Charlie [Ruiley], though. We knew he’d have his hands full. But we’re happy to have done as well as we have this year.” The article concluded by mentioning the continued efforts of McCollum and the team's overall record.

title: “Dave
headline: Blue Mountain League Teams Eye Wrestling Glory
summary: Multiple high school teams are predicting a strong wrestling season, with Waynesboro and James Buchanan highlighted as top contenders in the Blue Mountain League.
cleaned_text:
The Gettysburg Times
Tue, Dec 13, 1983

**Blue Mountain League Wrestling Predictions**

With the personnel the Indians have returning, it's no wonder why they are considered the team to beat in the Blue Mountain League for the 1983-84 season. Mike Bucher of Biglerville says, "I think that they will be the team to beat."

"With Bowman and most of the others back, don't think that anyone can touch them," says Mitch Mentzer of New Oxford.

Most coaches around the league think that this year will be the year for Waynesboro to win the BML wrestling title. Weber stated, "We are looking to win 75 percent of our matches. We expect to be powerful this year."

**James Buchanan Wrestling**

At James Buchanan, Snyder is delighted that he has no idea who will be starting at each weight class. "This is the most wrestlers that has ever come out for the squad. I have three times as many kids out this year than last year. It will help competition on the team greatly," said Snyder. Last year, the Knights were 3-10 and going nowhere, but Snyder believes the team is ready for a major improvement.

**Greencastle and Antrim Sports**

At Greencastle, basketball is the major sport. Mathews, who is a 1979 graduate of Greencastle Antrim and a junior at Shippensburg University, noted that while the school will gain recognition once they become a competitor, they are currently just going to have fun. Last year, Mathews worked with the junior high program and traveled with the remaining seven wrestlers to tournaments throughout the area.

The revamped Antrim squad is as green as the hills in which they live. The young team has three freshmen on the starting squad, four sophomores, and just three seniors. Despite the age of the team, Mathews is quite optimistic.

**Scotland Wrestling**

Scotland is hoping to improve upon last year's fact that they only lost to McCollum. The team includes seven returning wrestlers, including Junior Walt Kelley at 13 and Kelley at 12.

title: 'TTYSBURG TIMES, TUESDAY, DEC
headline: Bermudian Springs Wrestling Team Rebuilds for New Season
summary: Bermudian Springs wrestling coach McCollum stated that the team is in a rebuilding phase but remains optimistic due to the strong foundation and interest in the program.
cleaned_text:
**Bermudian Springs Wrestling Team Rebuilds for New Season**

YORK SPRING — "We are going to have to keep everything in perspective this year," said McCollum, the wrestling coach for Bermudian Springs. The Eagles are currently in a rebuilding phase.

"We lost a lot of talent," McCollum stated. "As of now, we are caught in between—we have some experienced wrestlers and some inexperienced wrestlers."

While the team faced challenges, the coach remains optimistic about the future. He noted that the program has a strong foundation, mentioning that 39 wrestlers are currently part of the unit high program. He added that the team has good cooperation and interest that should help them prepare for the next year.

The wrestling competition requires strict adherence to weight standards. All winners were given their final weights following the matches. title: “Dave
headline: Koontz Wins Second State Wrestling Title
summary: Tony Koontz won his second PIAA State AA heavyweight wrestling championship title, defeating Bob Fleck, while Matt Gerhard secured his fourth straight state title.
cleaned_text:
**Koontz Claims Second State Title in Wrestling Championship**

By WAYNE LOWMAN
Evening Sun Sports Writer

HERSHEY — Delone Catholic High School wrestler Tony Koontz secured his second PIAA State AA heavyweight wrestling championship title, defeating Bob Fleck in the final match with a score of 12-8.

Koontz’s victory completed a perfect 32-0 season and extended his winning streak back to the 1982-83 season. During the final bout, Koontz opened a 12-3 lead early in the third period and worked out of several attempted combinations by Fleck, ultimately gaining the decision after surviving a reversal and two stalling calls.

The tournament featured several notable performances. Arie Silsley, who had pinned Chris Bilbie in the District Three tournament, lost a 7-6 decision to Marc Bartolomucci of Bethlehem Center in the semi-final round. Silsley was then defeated by Bilbie of Susquehannock, 4-0, in the consolation round.

Meanwhile, Matt Gerhard of Catasauqua claimed his fourth straight State title, defeating Bartolomucci in the championship round.

Bermudian Springs coach Dave Collum reflected on the team's effort, stating, “We were one from the finals, but at least we went out on a winning note.”

title: “Dave
headline: Bermudian Springs Wrestlers Shine in State Competition
summary: Several local wrestlers, including Trevor Byers and Jared Daum, are highlighted for their strong performances and preparation for the PIAA state tournament.
cleaned_text:
**Bermudian Springs Wrestlers Shine in State Competition**

The kid is definitely a tournament wrestler,” said Woods, referring to a standout athlete. “He's very familiar with the routine of state tournaments. But he has his goals set, and he knows what he wants to do.”

Of course, DeAngelo’s goal is a State title. The Thundering Herd senior, who will drop to 105 this week after all season at 112, has shown excellent variety, resulting in a 12-0 record. “I like him a lot,” says Cedar Cliff head coach Bob Craig, who adds, laughing, “We tried to recruit him and offer him a scholarship to Cedar Cliff.”

BART CAMPBELL, Newport: Campbell made it to the PIAA tournament the hard way last year. Faced with a tough Round II draw against Susquehanna Twp.’s Larry Stazewski, Campbell had to wrestle through the bracket.

TREVOR BYERS, Bermudian Springs: As far as Byers is concerned, he smiles about it. A bag of concentration on the mat, Byers gives the impression of being mean. Last year, he was mean enough to finish second to Daum at 112 in the District 3-AA tournament and fourth in the PIAA state tournament.

“Trevor's just an outstanding kid,” said his head coach.

MIDDLEWEIGHTS

JARED DAUM, Susquehanna: There are two ways of handling the pressure of having to defend a State title. There is the wrong way, and there is Daum’s way. “I'll tell you a little story that pretty much tells you how Jared is dealing with the pressure,” said Ahern. “For years, people tried to beat Jared on his feet. Now he's beating them there.”

JIM YINGER, Big Spring: Enter the Pulverizer. Make no mistake, Yinger, 12-0, has his share of slick moves and a good variety of moves, but he does them in such dominating, powerful fashion that matches rarely go six minutes. “He's one of those super kids you run into every once in a great while,” says Big Spring head coach John Dunsmore. “He doesn’t go to very many summer tournaments, but in the season, he is 100 percent.”

DOUG McCORMICK, Cedar Cliff: A transfer from Bloomsburg a while back, McCormick has fit nicely into the Colt program, which this year is enjoying statewide attention. In 14 bouts, he has a loss, as the team continues to compete.

headline: State Championships See Byers Advance in Semifinals
summary: Byers advanced in the semifinals of the PIAA State Class AA Championships after defeating Rich Reichwein, while Daum lost to Larry Rehrig.
cleaned_text:
The PIAA State Class AA Championships opened at Hersheypark Arena yesterday with happy cheers for a spunky quartet from Pequea Valley. The day ended with seven survivors from District 3 heading into this morning’s semifinals.

Among the lucky seven was Byers, who is on a determined track toward the 119-pound title. Not among the group was Daum, who won here last year, but met his match yesterday in the person of Larry Rehrig of Northern Lehigh.

While Daum was derailed to the consolation bracket, Susqueni—The Flyers managed to take 29 shots at Peeters, who made several excellent saves. But Froese was even stingier. “He was very sound,” Keené said. “He didn’t have much to do, but he was in control of the whole thing.”

Joining the group in today’s semis are Peter Horst of Schuylkill Valley at 126, Pat Klinedinst of Hanover at 145, and Brian Diem of Pequea Valley at heavyweight.

Byers, fourth at 112 here last year, came into yesterday's action with a chip on his shoulder. He had been bounced by Pequea Valley's Bill Berkheimer in last weekend’s district tourney. The setback left him in a foul mood.

“His practices Monday, Tuesday, and Wednesday of this week were his best,” said the coach. “I think that low [mood] put him in the right frame of mind. There were no smiles all week in practice, and [he was] ing or drinking between sessions in order to hold weight, he tangled with Rich Reichwein of North Schuylkill.”

An early takedown and two tilt points got Byers rolling. A middle-period reversal made it 6-0 after two. With Reichwein tiring, the Eagle matman then rolled out to a 16-0 bulge before the technical fall was whistled.

“He’s very determined,” says McCollum. “Some of the dual-meet season was boring for him, because everything was pointing toward this.”

Byers was hoping for a rematch with Berkheimer, but that notion fell by the wayside when the Pequea grappler was nipped 6-0.

The Daum-Rehrig match eagerly awaited observers, who felt the tension of the very tough weight class. “We at Bermudian Springs are being for the championship,” said the Susqueni coach.

The Daum-Rehrig match was reversed in the second for all the points he would need. Daum selected down in the second period. Rehrig backed him three near-fall points to settle the issue. A late escape by Daum gave him the final 5-1.

headline: Byers Advances After Technical Fall Victory
summary: After a tough district tournament, Byers achieved a technical fall victory over Rich Reichwein, advancing in the tournament despite failing to secure a rematch with Bill Berkheimer.
cleaned_text:
The Patriot-News
Fri, Mar 14, 1986

Byers, fourth at 112 here last year, came into yesterday's action with a chip on his shoulder. He had been bounced by Pequea Valley’s Bill Berkheimer in last weekend's district tournament. The setback left him in a foul mood.

“There were no smiles all week in practice, and there haven't been any here,” Bryan Murray said. “The key was [getting ready for this].”

Byers started yesterday by pinning Northeast Regional champ Tim Ward of Canton early in the third period. In the quarterfinals, after not eating or drinking between sessions in order to hold weight, he tangled with Rich Reichwein of North Schuylkill. An early takedown and two tilt points gave Byers the lead. Washington’s Rod Lang got Byers rolling. A middle-period reversal made it 6-0 after two. With Reichwein tiring, the Eagle matman then rolled out to a 16-0 bulge before the technical fall was whistled.

“He’s very determined,” says McCollum. “Some of the dual-meet season was boring for him, because everything was pointing toward this.”

Byers was hoping for a rematch with Berkheimer, but that notion fell by the wayside when the Pequea grappler was nipped 6-5 in quarterfinals by Rob Ripple of Bedford.

“The chance to meet Berkheimer again was a big part of the motivation today,” said McCollum. Byers will now face Ripple.

The Daum-Rehrig match was eagerly awaited by most observers, who felt the two were the class of the pound weight.

The PIAA State Class AA Championships opened at Hersheypark Arena yesterday with happy cheers for a spunky quartet from Pequea Valley. The day ended with seven survivors from District 3 heading into this morning’s semifinals.

Among the lucky seven was Byers, who is on a determined track toward the 119-pound title. Not among the group was Daum, who won here last year, but met his match yesterday in the person of Larry Rehrig of Northern Lehigh.

Byers, fourth at 112 here last year, came into yesterday’s action with a chip on his shoulder. He had been bounced by Pequea Valley's Bill Berkheimer in last weekend's district tournament. The setback left him in a foul mood.

“His practices Monday, Tuesday, and Wednesday were his best,” said a coach. “I think that [he is] in the right frame of mind. There were no smiles all week in practice, and [he was focused] on drinking or training between sessions in order to hold weight.”

Byers tangled with Rich Reichwein of North Schuylkill. An early takedown and two tilt points got Byers rolling. A middle-period reversal made it 6-0 after two. With Reichwein tiring, the Eagle matman then rolled out to a 16-0 bulge before the technical fall was whistled.

“He's very determined,” said McCollum. “Some of the dual-meet season was boring for him, because everything was pointing toward this.”

The Daum-Rehrig match was eagerly awaited by observers, who felt the tension of the very tough 135-pound weight class. The Northern Lehigh wrestler was versed in the second period for all the points he would need. Daum selected down in the second period. headline: Wrestlers Battle for State AA and AAA Medals
summary: State wrestling tournaments are underway, with top wrestlers competing in Class AA and Class AAA events, while several athletes aim to secure state titles.
cleaned_text:
Wrestlers Battle for State AA and AAA Medals

In Class AA action, two area wrestlers reached the semifinals with victories, placing them among the top six in their weight class. Meanwhile, six grapplers saw their season end after losing in the preliminaries.

Class AAA competition saw Spring Grove’s Neal Metzger (112) remain unbeaten as the class completed its preliminary matches, while the other two local representatives fell by the wayside. Class AA continues today with semifinals scheduled for the morning, and the championships are set for 8:30. Class AAA holds its quarterfinals this evening beginning at 6:30, with the final two rounds scheduled for Saturday.

Hanover’s Pat Klinedinst (29-1-2), District III champion at 145 pounds, knocked off Kane's Roger Blankenship (28-3) with a score of 14-8. Blankenship had reached the state tournament as a consolation finalist in the Northwest Region. Klinedinst secured at least sixth place with his quarterfinal win by technical fall against Tim Robb (17-16-1) from Northern Lehigh.

In a separate match, the Nighthawk 145-pounder fell behind by a 40-point margin.

“He's very relaxed,” said Conover. “He’s under less pressure to win than when he was competing in the district tournament. Everybody expected him to win a district title, but he doesn’t have that pressure to be a state champ.”

Conover continued, “Technically, he’s the best wrestler I’ve ever worked with. The moves he displayed in scoring 35 points are evidence of that. He has an awful lot of skills, and if he puts together two good matches and keeps his head on straight, Pat has a shot at being state champ.”

Bermudian Springs’ Trevor Byers (119) is seeking to redeem himself at the state tournament with title ambitions. Byers (30-1) is aiming to overcome what McCollum described as a mental lapse in a 4-3 semifinal loss to Pequa Valley's Bill Berkheimer.

Byers has wrestled two impressive matches toward his goal, pinning Canton’s Tim Ward (32-3) at 4:21 in his preliminary bout before beating North Schuylkill’s Rich Reichwein (26-7-1) by technical fall, 16-0, at 5:18.

McCollum added that Byers “is very hungry to win this tournament.”

Metzger, who is unbeaten at 26-0, is the sole surviving local talent in AAA competition. He pinned Northeast consolation winner Terry Daubert (27-5) from Northampton at 3:50. The Rocket junior said his original goal was to take the district title, and that there would be less pressure at the state meet. He added that his objective in the tournament would be to learn from the experience of meeting what he called some of the best talent he has ever faced in his weight class.

Six outstanding area AA grapplers showed how tough the state competition is, as they lost preliminary matches when each weight class cut down the number of participants from 16 to eight.

Hanover junior Mike Miller, a heavyweight with a 20-6-1 mark, came out aggressive and was able to maintain control of Chestnut Ridge opponent Rick College, holding a 3-2 lead with about 1:30 left in his preliminary bout. College (28-3) was able to recover with an escape followed by a takedown, and he went on to take a 7-3 decision.

In another match, Sneeringer (105), with a record of 21-7 and a district medal, put up a terrific fight against South Williams, where Scott Roberts won with a 9-2 decision. Mifflinburg’s Pfirman (30-5) knocked off with a 9-2 decision. Flannery earned a silver medal for his performance at the districts.

summary: The article reports on several standout performances at the state wrestling meet, highlighting Duane Blankenship's draw, Trevor Byers' victory, and the overall competitive nature of the tournament.
cleaned_text:
**Bae Meet the CHARGES**

Small wonder that when they passed out the Outstanding Wrestler awards at the end of the meet, Keysaw was the only one in line for the plaque.

“Really, it wasn't his height that bothered me too much,” said Banks, who lost just his second bout in the last two years. “He wrestled a really great match. I didn’t wrestle my best, and he did.”

There were other eye-openers. At 138 pounds, Class AA's outstanding wrestler from the PIAA tournament, Duane Blankenship, battled fatigue and settled for an 8-8 draw with Matt Caro of General McLane, the Class AAA runner-up. And at 167 pounds, Lenny Cory of Troy, the Class AA runner-up but a decided underdog to Class AAA champion Mike McHenry, battled the Meadville star all the way in a 7-4 loss.

Trevor Byers was able to avoid a swarm of upsets with a sharp performance against Harrington. Byers used an unorthodox move from the overhook situation, pulling the far arm, stepping over, and forcing Harrington to his back for a pair of five-pointers.

“Actually, we have kids who use unorthodox moves,” said a coach. “It’s not something we teach, but we use if the situation’s right.”

McCollum, the coach from Bermudian Springs, noted that Byers came into the bout with just early workouts since states, concentrating on baseball as a pitcher and catcher for the Eagles. “It's tough to be in top condition for something like this when you play other sports like Trevor does,” said McCollum. “What it’s amounted to is he’s had to work after baseball practice for these.”

Byers is still one bout shy of conclusion: He will participate in the Pittsburgh Press Invitational class April 5 against the United States All-Star team.

Meanwhile, Biglerville's Tim Cook, the only senior in Class AA states, had two nice offensive moves against Class AAA champ Todd Williams of Clearfield, but again had trouble from the bottom, Williams scoring with cradles for an 11-4 win.

“He wasn't totally dominated by that kid,” said Biglerville coach Mike Bucher.

**Bermudian Springs Coaches Plot Strategy; Local Teams Compete in Playoffs**

**Wrestling Prep**
Bermudian Springs coaches Dave Dries and Dave McCollum plot strategy during Monday’s first session. For stories on the pre-season camps, refer to Page B-5.

**Sports Updates**
**Softball Playoffs:** St. Bartholomew's advanced to the Hanover Church Softball League playoffs by defeating Black Rock 15-4 on Sunday. Roger Krumrine and Frank Fornoff led the way for St. Bart’s, each going 3-for-4, with Krumrine having a double. Barry Berwager and Dan Caler each smacked home runs. For Black Rock, Charlie Wetzel and Pat Boldosser were each 2-for-3. Boldosser had a triple and Wetzel a double. In the first round of the playoffs, St. Paul's (Dubs) broke a 1-1 tie with four runs in the third inning, en route to a win over St. Bart’s. Barry Baumgardner and Craig Rohrbaugh were each 3-for-3, while Steve Sager, Craig Hoover, Greg Davis, Weidler, and Barry Berwager contributed hits.

**Football News:**
Kansas City announced Monday that it had reached an agreement with Irv Eatman, who helped lead the Philadelphia and Baltimore Stars to the last two USFL championships. While the contract involves players with guaranteed USFL contracts, Eatman’s release must first be obtained from Baltimore. Stars General Manager Carl Peterson stated, “It will go…”

**Obituary:**
Former Wimbledon tennis champion Chuck McKinle died at the age of 45. McKinle won the men’s singles title at Wimbledon in 1963 and was a U.S. Open doubles champion in 1963 and 1964.

headline: Eagles Set Sights on League Championship
summary: The Bermudian Springs Eagles secured multiple victories on Wednesday, positioning themselves strongly for a championship showdown next Thursday.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Thu, Feb 05, 1987

**Eagles Set Sights on League Championship**

Should both teams survive matches over the weekend—Biglerville hosts Waynesboro on Friday, while the Eagles welcome Greencastle on Saturday—the two unbeaten teams are scheduled to collide next Thursday for what should ultimately determine the league championship.

The Eagles remained in contention for league honors Wednesday with several clutch performances. Seniors Kirk Keech and Jim Wolf delivered tight wins, while other wrestlers contributed to the team's success.

Keech, who had lost to his opponent in a tournament earlier this season, got his revenge with a third-period reversal that led to a 4-2 decision over David Claggett. Wolf also broke open an even match in the final period with a takedown, which he followed up with a two-point nearfall for an 8-5 decision over Travis Buffington.

Earlier in the day, Darren Harbold went all out, taking a gut-wrenching 7-5 decision over John Hawkins at 155 pounds. A reversal with two seconds left in that match added three more team points for the Eagles. Ric Sowers also experienced a tight contest at 112 pounds, recording an 11-7 decision over Perry Mentzer thanks to a takedown and a nearfall late in the contest.

Tim Sowers and Jeff Showers executed impressive wins, piling up points in their matches at 119 and 126 pounds, respectively, for the Eagles. Sowers used a combination of a reversal to hit a three-point nearfall to score big late in the second period.

Waynesboro coach Dave Webber commented on the competition, noting, “They worked to our strength in moves which attacked the legs. They were able to make it pay off by being more physical and aggressive to finish the moves off.”

The Bermudian Springs coach, McCollum, added, “We're pushing our all. We always want to help potential and maintain our program. We consider it a matter what our team does.”

title: “Dave
headline: Bermudian Springs Defeats Biglerville in Wrestling Match
summary: Bermudian Springs defeated Biglerville in a wrestling match with an 8-2 decision, overcoming an early deficit and capitalizing on a forfeit.
cleaned_text:
“The Eagles of Bermudian Springs” opened up a 2-0 advantage in the first six matches of the meet before Biglerville came storming back.

“Our lightweights are tough and have been asked all year to do it,” said the Bermudian Springs head coach.

The match saw Bermudian Springs win with an 8-2 decision over Drew Bucher at 98 points. Keech held a 3-2 lead after two periods but scored a reversal and three back points with a double-bar arm to claim the first match.

Then, in a tactical move at 105 pounds, the Canners forfeited to Rick Trimmer. Biglerville’s regular 105-pounder, Lorne Peters, was out with an injury, and in his place was first-year sophomore Jeff Myers.

“We thought the forfeit would kill their momentum. And it was a heck of an idea at that,” stated Biglerville’s coach.

title: orts Writer
headline: Canners Wrestling Team Strong at Sectional Tournament
summary: The Canners wrestling team had a successful showing at the Sectional Tournament, with several athletes recording pins and advancing to the next rounds.
cleaned_text:
The Canners Wrestling Team Strong at Sectional Tournament

Despite an injury-slowed night, the Canners continued to build on the strength that powered them to lead in the team score at the Sectional Tournament. The team faced a tough challenge, but their athletes delivered strong performances.

Lonnie Mentzer pinned Lamar Williams from Scotland. Greg Sprenkle pinned Daren Motter at 145 pounds. Daron Sobota pinned Scott Hawk of Hanover at 167 pounds. Ron Garrison pinned Mark Ruppert of Bermudian Springs at 185 pounds. The heavyweight bout saw Mike Hammond of Greencastle fall.

Other Canners advances included Brad Tate at 119 pounds, Gardner at 126 pounds, Durbin at 132 pounds, and Jeff Mentzer at 155 pounds.

In other notable matches, Rick Trimmer pinned Da Walker of Scotland. Eric Sowers flattened Andv. Adams of Camp Hill at 3:13. Tim Sowers defeated Nick Mosley of Scotland.

The article also highlighted the success of other teams. Delone Catholic had a strong showing, with Dan Hoke pinning his way into the quarterfinals. He was joined by teammates Tony Bream (105 lbs), Brian Sneeringer (112 lbs), and John Curran, who also recorded a pin in advancing.

Coach McCollum noted the importance of the tournament, stating, “That match could be crucial for us later. We're put into a situation where we have to [catch] up, and that places more importance on the [tournament].”

headline: Local Wrestlers Head to State Berths
summary: Local wrestlers, including Wildasin and Klinedinst, are advancing to the state semifinals and are highly anticipated to compete against the best in the state.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Thursday, March 5, 1987

**Wrestling Stars Head to State Berths**

The area has a pair of outstanding wrestlers in Mike (with a record of 26-1), a freshman from Hanover, and Klinedinst (with a record of 28-0), a 185-pounder. Each of these athletes' past achievements mean little now as they take the mat against the state's best.

Klinedinst, who pinned each of his first two district opponents, is set to face Kutztown’s Daysen, who is the Section I champion at 17-10. Meanwhile, Miller will take on the Section IV champ, Bill Naugle (17-6), from Juniata. The heavyweight bracket also features another unbeaten wrestler in Mark DiMarco (26-0-1) from Daniel Boone.

With seven wrestlers remaining in the competition, Biglerville is still in the running, along with Northern Lebanon. The Eagles also boast a strong roster.

The 112-pound junior takes on Paul Klick (23-3-1) in the semifinal round. The Eagles’ other district contender is Tim Sowers, who is in the 119-pound bracket along with Bermudian Springs’ Tate. A district championship bout between the locals is feasible if Sowers beats Tate.

Wildasin is the area’s hot-shot prospect as he enters the semifinal round as the only local unbeaten with a 28-0 mark at 112 pounds. The freshman has high ambitions of earning a gold at states, and after recording a pair of technical falls in last week's district competition, his goal may not be too lofty despite encountering possible difficulties in having only one year of experience. Wildasin, who has proven himself one of the best wrestlers in the state, including the wrestler named the tournament’s best at Lititz, is scheduled to compete in the semifinal at 145 pounds against Mark Cumberland.

South West semifinalists are hopeful for a good shot at the state level.

title: The Evening Sun
headline: Sprenkle Wins District III AA Championship Title
summary: Greg Sprenkle won the District III AA championship title in the 145-pound class, while other wrestlers competed in various weight classes throughout the tournament.
cleaned_text:
Biglerville’s Greg Sprenkle (top) maintained control in the District III AA championship bout Saturday. Sprenkle won the match and the title against Schuylkill Valley’s Eric Dietrich with a score of 12-4 in the 145-pound class.

Hanover coach Terry Conover described the wrestling performance in the 132-pound class as "not the most glorious win, but certainly gutsy." James Corbin, who wrestled at 98 pounds, was the only wrestler to score in regulation and 4-4 in overtime, helping to finish out the tournament Saturday.

In the consolation final, Byers (21-5-2) faced state favorite Peter Horst Brubaker. "There are easy wins anymore," noted Miller. "I have to keep losing by technical points."

Coach Conover praised Byers, stating that he "came out aggressively since credit because he had me in good shape and he wasn’t overwhelmed by the Schuylkill Valley wrestlers."

Another match featured Dan Hannum (94.0) from Oley Valley wrestling at 112 pounds, which was a set-up for a big overtime battle. The Nighthawks built up a lead over Lebanon at 126 pounds.

headline: Local Wrestlers Vie for State Titles
summary: Several local wrestlers, including Klinedinst, Wildasin, and Bermudian Springs' Greg Byers, are highly regarded contenders heading into the state wrestling tournament.
cleaned_text:
Klinedinst has become the area’s most dominant wrestler this season, setting school records for pins (27), wins (30), and quickest pin (:11). Klinedinst says he doesn’t dwell on his past accomplishments, stating he is “taking each match one at a time.”

The Bolt opens with Panther Valley’s Chuck Bochicchio (23-9). Coach Frace noted that the key to his wrestler’s success will depend on how he reacts to the cautious and defensive style of opponents at the state tournament. “He’s shown what he can do when people come after him, now we're trying to prepare him,” Frace said.

Another contender is Rich Mentzer, who has state experience after finishing second in the districts last year. Miller, periodically has his wrestlers go three full matches “back-to-back-to-back.”

South Western coach Mark Bowersox noted that Joey has “that extra special desire to make the most of his talents.” He added, “I’m convinced that if his season had ended from a loss to this point, Joey would be back on the mat again the next day to make himself better for next season.” With his win last week at districts, Wildasin is in contention for the state title as he opens with Dan Krutules (27-5).

Schuylkill has Peter Horst (28-0) and Keith Daugherty (27-3), who was second in the district tournament.

Bermudian Springs’ Greg Byers is considered a potential contender at 132 pounds after an impressive performance that came up short in the district championship.

Metzger (28-2-1) is a strong contender at 112 pounds. Metzger was sixth in the district tournament last week and, as in the past, “He will be determined to prove what he’s really at in the tournament.” Metzger opens against the state’s top seed from Beth Catholic. Metzger was sixth at the state a year ago at 112 pounds.

Ben Grim (26-3), who finished second in the districts the last years, will meet Shawn O’Brien (25-7) from Council Rock in the first round.

headline: Canners Head to State Competition After District III Success
summary: The Canners are heading to state competition in Hershey, following a successful District III tournament where Mike Bucher was named Coach of the Year.
cleaned_text:
The Gettysburg Times
Thu, Mar 1, 1987

Considering there are 41 schools in District III Class AA and Biglerville, it is the third smallest school—just ahead of Camp Hill and Sco[ol Name].

Only state competition lies ahead for the Canners, which start today at Hershey Arena. On those mats, we will see the true strength of the Blue Mountain League, Section I, and District competition.

At the District III tournament last weekend, it was refreshing to see a community rally around Biglerville and support the hard work the athletes have put into the season. At this time of year, the athletes need to see such support to keep their main objective in mind: a state gold medal. Keep up the good work, and maybe other communities will follow. Also, congratulations to Mike Bucher on winning the District III AA Coach of the Year honor—a great tribute that could only increase the popularity of wrestling.

[A local team] with coach Dave [Name] finished second in the BML to New Oxford. The season was particularly rewarding, bringing together a group from one cohesive unit. There is extreme competition for the league crown next year from this team. Watch out, New Oxford!

title: < + > 1 0f 6 matches
headline: Local Wrestlers Compete in State Matches
summary: Local wrestlers competed in various matches, with reports detailing upsets, close bouts, and the struggles of athletes in the state tournament.
cleaned_text:
**The Gettysburg Times**
**Fri, Mar 13, 1987 - Page 15**

**Wrestling Action Continues**

Tony Twiss has control of Greg Byers, who prevailed in the bout.

Byers’ second round of consolation didn’t go much better as he was pinned in 4:44 by Brett Keeney of Northeast Bradford and ousted out of state wrestling.

“In the quarters, he (Byers) tried a lateral drop and got caught. It worked for him early so he thought he would use it again. When you get away with it once, it feels good,” Byers commented. “This was one of the best matches of the year for Greg.”

Meanwhile, at Bermudian Springs, the wrestling coach, McCollum, noted that a wrestler ran out of gas after the first period. “The short break between the matches was to his disadvantage. It was a strain losing in the quarters in such a close match.”

In other matches, Biglerville’s Sobota scored an upset over the Northwest Region champion David Sallack of Johnsonburg at 167 pounds in his preliminary bout, winning 3-2. However, he was not as fortunate in the quarterfinals, losing to Palisade’s Tom Socker, 13-3.

Another match featured the Biglerville wrestler Canner, who cut the margin to 3-1 with an escape at the 39-second mark of the period. Mentzer edged closer with another escape just three seconds into the last stanza to make the score 3-2. Fielding sealed the triumph with a takedown with 29 ticks on the clock.

“Lonnie just got off to a slow start. He wasn’t quite sure what to do,” commented Bucher after his preliminary match.

Mentzer’s second bout was much more physical. Morgan scored a takedown and a two-point nearfall in the first period to lead 4-0, but an escape and a takedown by Mentzer narrowed the total to 4-3. The third stanza spelled doom for the Biglerville grappler as Morgan reversed him with 1:30 to go and put him on his back.
