Although wrestling has been over for two months, therulesmakers
haven't forgotten about the sport.

‘The national governing body for high school sports (the National
Federation of State High School Athletic Associations) has decided
to make drastic changes in wrestling for the 1988-89 season.

First, the current 12 classes will be expanded to 13 and the 98-
> pound class will be eliminated.

Next season will be the last for the current weight class alignment =
Of 98, 105, 112, 119, 126, 132, 138, 145, 155, 167, 185 and heavyweight (up
= to 275 pounds).

The new classes, which are the first changes nationally since
1970-71, will be 103, 112, 119, 125, 130, 135, 140, 145, 152, 160, 171, 189 and
275 pounds. Es

Also to be eliminated is the growth rule that allows a wrestler to
add four pounds during the course of a season without having to =
move up a weight class. ES

With the addition of a weight class, the lineup will give eight ;
weights between 125 and 175 compared to the six under the old
alignment.

Since few underclassmen weigh less than 100 pounds, coaches had
to rely on the freshmen to fill that slot. Now, the new system will
relieve the burden put on coaches in seeking a $8-pounder. Even =
those that are 160 pounds or less, find it difficult to maintain the 3
weight during the four-month season.

Inmy opinion, the 13 weight classes will benefit the sport because
a majority of wrestlers are crowded around 135 pounds and the new
setup will give the wrestlers a chance to spread out in those middle =
weights. The classes are spaced out every five pounds. This isa %

fect solution for those wrestlers who are good enough to be in the

jineup but are unable to eliminate a teammate from the weight
class. This will give that particular grappler an opportunity of get-
ting in the lineup. .

The only drawback I see is that the small schools, which have
trouble filling the 12 positions coming up with another grappler for
the added weight class. ;

The growth rule will give a wrestler a better shot at going up
against someone at their truer weight. There won't be so much
jumping around in the weight classes.

How do some of the area wrestling coaches feel about the
changes?

Biglerville head coach Mike Bucher: “I like starting at 103. 1 think
it will help most programs getting that extra kid in the lineup.” He
said about the rule being dropped. “It's better for the kids if
coaches don't make them cut down in the beginning.”

According to the Biglerville mentor, “I think it will hurt the smal-
ler programs. who have a hard time getting 12 into the lineup.” On
the elimination of 98 pounds. Bucher stated, “Generally speaking it
is hard to find $8-pounders. Strategy-wise, I don't know how it will 2
affect it but it is one more body in there to win or lose.”

Bermudian wrestling coach Dave McCollum: “I think the changes
are for the better for those schools that are turning out the numbers.
‘There are a lot of boys from 130-160 pounds and that will give another
boy an opportunity to wrestle. 1 look at it (the changes) as being
positive. I'm a little disappointed that they took 98 pounds away only
because I can find athletes to wrestle that weight.”

Getting rid of the growth rule. according to McCollum, “because
they are trying to condition the high school kids to get used tocollege 7
wrestling.” In college. as the season goes on. the weight of the
wrestler goes down instead of up. “There was a lot of pressure from
doctors and trainers in eliminating the fact that kids cutting weight.
1 dosee the wrestier waiting until February until they start dropping.
weight instead of doing it so early in the season.” commented
McCollum.

Get head coach Marty Rock: “I see it (the change) as
something beneficial. 1t adds another variable to the sport. Itshould §
have happened along time ago. I don’t the think the weight allo-
wance served any purpose. Eliminating that isn't hurting anything.
It puts people at their truer weight — it makes them a more dedi
cated wrestler.”

Whether the changes were a good idea or not, doesn't matter. The
decision is done and now we just have to sit back for a year and see
how it works. It should make the 1988-89 wrestling season a very
interesting one.

ucks trip Celtics

By HOWARD ULMAN
AP Sports Writer

BOSTON (AP) — Terry Cummings
scored 14 fourth-quarter points, in-
cluding the go-ahead jumper with 51
seconds left, as the Milwaukee Bucks
ended Boston's 33-game home winning
streak and stayed alive in the NBA
playoffs with a 129-124 victory over the
Celtics Wednesday night.

The Bucks’ first victory in Boston in
11 games cut the Celtics’ lead to 3-2 in
the best-of-seven Eastern Conference
semifinal and set up a sixth game Fri-
day night in Milwaukee.

The loss was just the second for the
Celtics in their last 81 games at Boston
Garden and stopped their home win-
ning streak in the playoffs at 14, equal
to the NBA record set by the Min-
neapolis Lakers from 1949 to 1951.

Until Wednesday night’s victory,
Milwaukee hadn’t won in Boston since
April 12, 1985.

Milwaukee was led by Sidney Mon-
crief, with a career playoff high 33
points, Cummings with 23 and Jack
Sikma with 21.

For Boston, Robert Parish had 30
points, Kevin McHale 27 and Larry
Bird 26.

The Celtics took their last lead at 124-
123 with 1:11 left on two free throws by
Dennis Johnson.

Cummings, who had just two field
goals in the first three quarters, hit a
15-foot bank shot from the left side to
put Milwaukee ahead to stay.

After Parish was called for traveling
with 37 seconds to go, McHale fouled
Sikma, who was about to dunk the ball.
Sikma made both free throws for a 127-
124 lead with 20 seconds remaining.

With 14 seconds to play, Bird missed
a 3-point field goal attempt and, with
‘six seconds left, Johnson missed an 18-
footer. Cummings got the rebound,
was fouled by Danny Ainge, and clin-
ched the victory by making both shots
with five seconds left.

Trailing by five, Milwaukee went on
an 18-5 run sparked by Cummings’ 10
points, to take a 119-111 lead with 5:32
left in the game. The Bucks capped the
rally with a 10-0 spurt in which Boston
went scoreless for three minutes.

Then it was the Celtics’ turn to come
back.

They went on an 11-2 spurt, climaxed
by Johnson’s 15-foot jumper from the
left that put Boston ahead 122-121 with
2:31 remaining. John Lucas’ two free
throws gave Milwaukee a 123-122 lead
14 seconds later.

Boston took its first lead, 62-61, with
2:30 left in the second quarter on John-
son’s jumper from the right corner.

It came in the midst of a 30-15 run
that turned Milwaukee’s biggest lead
of the game, 47-36, into a 66-62 Boston
advantage with 55 seconds left in the
first half. McHale had 11 points and
Bird nine in the rally.

ATLANTA (AP) — Vinnie Johnson scored 15 of his 17

S. Grove netters defend title

By TIM EYSTER
Times Sports Writer

One title defended, one title to go.

Spring Grove’s Kevin Scott and Car-
tie Bentzel won their second District 3
mixed doubles title in a row Wednes-
day, defeating third-seeded Scott Cor-
reil and Alycia Katrinak of Governor
Mifflin 6-0, 7-5 in the semifinals and
fourth-seeded Kevin Bacha and Cindy
Merrill of Dallastown 6-2, 6-2 in an all-
York County final.

The two Spring Grove juniors will
now look ahead to becoming two-time
state champions during the PLAA state
tournament May 22-23 at Bloomsburg
University. With their District 3 vic-
tory they may receive the top seed at
the state tournament like they did the
District 3 Tournament.

The two looked sharper and sharper
as the district tournament went along.
They hadn’t gotten a chance to prac-
tice together over the weekend be-
cause of a car accident Bentzel and her
mother were involved in last Friday
while returning from the Middle States
spring singles championships in Phi-
ladelphia. :

Bentzel suffered a badly bruised
right knee in the accident but was
given the OK by a doctor to play in the
tournament.

Spring Grove head boys tennis coach
Carole Reitmeyer said Scott and Bent-
zel were thus depending on skills and
the fact that Reitmeyer and Spring
Grove head girls tennis coach Gary
Gault teach the same system of dou-
bles to get by Monday, when the duo
beat Muhlenburg’s Tony Bruno and
Stephanie Davis 6-2, 6-0, Wyornissing’s

Greg Cooper and Amy Niehaus 6-4, 64
and Manheim Township's Scott and
Cyndi Hess 6-2, 6-2.

Bentzel said her knee felt well
enough after Monday’s play to prac-
tice Tuesday, and that, Reitmeyer
said, is when Scott and Bentzel shored
up their teamwork.

And teamwork was certainly evi-
dent Tuesday, as the two played very
aggressively. Often they were on the
offensive attack.

“You have to stay aggressive, but at
the same time, while being aggres-
sive, you have to hit smart shots,”
Scott said. “There’s a time you get too
aggressive and make unforced errors.

“We played a lot better (than Mon-
day) and didn’t make a lot of unforced
errors.”

A big part of Scott and Bentzel’s
attack was Scott's serving, as Scott
never had his serve broken the whole
day. The lefthander’s “American
Twist” serve, as he called it, caused a
lot of havoe for Scott and Bentzel’s
opposition, especially the female
opposition.

“Kevin has to me improved so dra-
matically from last year,” Gault said.
“Throughout the entire tournament,
Kevin has been rock steady . . . and I
think Carrie has benefited from it.”

Scott thought it was the improved
serving of Bentzel that helped the two
play better Wednesday than Monday,
mentioning that Bentzel's serving took
pressure off of him. Bentzel did not
agree, prefering to give Scott credit
for his serving.

“I’m ready to go to states now. I feel
good coming off that last match,” said

Bentzel. who is also a two-time York
County girls singles champion and
one-time District 3 girls singles titlist.

Of her right knee, Bentzel said, “It
feels really good. I didn’t think about it
all day today. When I was on the court.
I didn’t think about it at all.”

Both players agreed they had a
tougher time winning the district title
this year than last year, mentioning
there was pressure from being defend-
ing champions and better competition.

With the exception of a long first
game that went to deuce four times.
Scott and Bentzel didn't have too many
problems with Correll and Katrinak in
the first set of their semifinal match.

It got a little tougher in the second
set, however, as the boys from both
teams held serve in the first, second.
fifth, sixth, ninth and 10th games while
the girls had their serves broken in the
third, fourth, seventh and eighth
games for a 5-5 tie.

Katrinak’s serve was then broken
for the fifth time in the match (she nev-
er did hold serve) to give Spring Grove
a6-5 lead. Two double faults by Bentzel
helped put Scott and she behind 15-40 in
the 12th game, but she rose to the occa-
sion by coming back with three
straight aces and eventually holding
serve for the match.

A big key for Scott and Bentzel in
their match against Bacha and Merrill
was that Bacha, last year’s York Coun-
ty boys singles champion, had his
serve broken the first three times Le
served. When he finally did hold serve
in the sixth game of the second set.
Scott and Bentzel were already ahead
51.

New Oxford outperforms Gettysburg

By MIKE SANDERS
Times Sports Editor

Gettysburg was beaten in all phases
of the game by New Oxford on Thurs-
day — pitching, hitting and fielding,
and also came up short on the
scoreboard, 5-0.

The Colonials got a three-hit shutout
from pitcher Jim Staub; banged out 10
hits of their own, including a 3-for-4
performance out of Danny Lieb; didn’t
commit an error and turned a double
play.

“You saw two flat teams out there
today,” commented Gettysburg head
coach Terry Fox. “The difference was
that New Oxford came to life and we
didn’t; they brought their bats, we
didn’t. They really banged our two
best pitchers, plus they made some
magnificent catches in the field.”

Staub and Gettysburg starting pitch-
er Chad Buckley had things under con-
trol through the first three innings:
both were working on two-hit shutouts.
The Colonials, however, got toBuckley

for an unearned run in the fourth, and
as it turned out, it would be the only run
they would need.

With one out in the top of the fourth,
Treber Rebert singled, advanced to
second when Jamey Welty worked
Buckley for a base on balls, went to
third on a fielder’s choice and crossed
the plate on an error.

The Colonials padded their lead in
the fifth. Eric Flickinger got the inning
started with a base hit, went to second
on a wild pitch, advanced to third ona
fielder’s choice and scored on a single
by Lieb.

Fox brought Jon Defoe into pitch
with runners on second and third and
only one out in the fifth, and Defoe pre-
vented the Colonials from adding to
their lead when he struck out Staub
and Rebert to end the inning.

Defoe, however, would run into trou-
ble in the seventh inning. After hitting
two batters, with one of them scoring
on a steal and an error, he gave up a
run-scoring double to Rebert-and an

RBI-single to Welty.

Jude Schroyer was brought in to re-
lieve Defoe, and after he was tagged
by Joe Shermeyer for a_ single,
Schroyer was able to get the final out.
But the three-run inning put the Colo-
nials up 5-0 with the Warriors down to
one more at bat.

Gettysburg went down in 1-2-3 order
in the seventh. without getting a ball
out of the infield. Staub got Schroyerto

yp-out to second. Chad Deckert on a
strikeout and Randy Fox on a com-
ebacker to the mound.

Only once in the game did the War-
riors advance a runner as far as
second base. In the fifth, Staub gaveup
back-to-back free tickets to Fox and
Rich Harvey, but then got the final out
by getting Charlie Sterner to hit a pop
fly to Lieb at short. .

The only inning the Warriors got
their lead-off man on base was the
third. Fox beat out an infield hit, but
the Colonials turned a double play on
Harvey's comebacker to the mound.

with Staub throwing to Lieb to get Fox
at second and Lieb making the accu-
rate throw to beat Harvey at first.

“That's our first shutout in a long
time.” New Oxford head coach Lee
Rentzel said after the game. “That
might have been our best thrown game
all season; it was definitely Jim's best
outing of the year.

“It was definitely our best defensive
game of the year. We're a lot better
defensive ballclub now than we were
early in the season. It’s always good to
see that kind cf improvement as the
season goes along.”

Both teams are 5-4 in the Blue Moun-
tain League and conclude their sea-
sons today: the Colonials are at home
against Greencastle. while the War-
riors travel to Scotland.

title: The Evening Sun – Thu Dec 10, 1987 – Page 25
headline: Bermudian Springs Aims for Blue Mountain League Title
summary: Bermudian Springs wrestling team, led by experienced co-captains and coach McCollum, is aiming for a Blue Mountain League title despite recent setbacks.
cleaned_text:
**Bermudian Springs Wrestling Team Aims for Blue Mountain League Title**

YORK SPRINGS — The 1987-88 season for Bermudian Springs wrestling was characterized by a blend of youth and experience, providing a strong foundation for a potential Blue Mountain League title.

Although Bermudian Springs lost two key wins, the team remains optimistic that the final round will be a special season. The team has posted an overall record of 13-4 and missed a shot at the BILT title, where they tied 22-4. To improve, the Eagles must learn to build a solid top-tier backup, including Jee Shoores and Greg Byers.

The team co-captains each have three years of district experience, and McCollum sees their team as successful this winter. McCollum reports that he is as healthy as ever, having been with the team since he joined. Despite some injuries that kept him out of the district level, McCollum believes that the match will be bigger than ever to make this final go-round a special season.

Bermudian Springs has provided the team with captain Middletown, accustoming McCollum to off-season make-up. While the team is currently ranked 14th, McCollum believes they could compete later in the season through post-season as he powers up. McCollum will be key in working out with the team.

title: vercome attitude
headline: Bermudian Springs Eagles Aim for BML Crown
summary: The Bermudian Springs Eagles are aiming to win the Blue Mountain League championship, with coach Pannebak-er and McCollum noting the team's strong roster of returning lettermen and promising improvement.
cleaned_text:
The Gettysburg Times
By TIM EYSTER
Sports Writer

**Bermudian Springs Eagles Aim for BML Crown**

Last season, all that kept Bermudian Springs from winning a Blue Mountain League championship was the three points it lost to Biglerville by in a 27-24 Canner victory, which helped give the Canners the league title instead. The Eagles’ only other dual-meet loss that season came at the hands of Milton Hershey in another close match, so the Eagles finished second in the BML.

Bermudian Springs coach, Pannebak-er, noted that the team is in good shape. “The kids seem to be picking things up quickly, so I have every reason to believe that we should see a lot of improvement during the year,” he said. “I have only one kid out at 98 pounds, but in every other weight class we go two or three deep. We will have done a lot of practices before we even get to the opening quad.”

Pannebak-er’s team opens with a quad-rangular meet on Saturday involving Eastern, Cedar Crest, and Littlestown. He also includes Waynesboro among his prospects.

Like most other coaches, Pannebak-er sees Biglerville and Bermudian Springs as the top teams again this season in the BMT.

Meanwhile, McCollum’s team, the Bermudian Springs coach, has another squad that could pull off a similar and perhaps even better season by winning a BML crown. Seven returning lettermen are back, five of whom performed well in post-season action.

“We had an outstanding season last year. It's going to be a challenge for us to equal that season because the BML is, I think, relatively even,” McCollum said.

The Eagles have multiple starters in the lighter weight classes, and four other non-letter-winners will likely start in the heavier weight classes. Among the returning lettermen are senior captain Greg Byers, who is a two-time Section I-Class AA champion, a District II-Class AA runner-up, and a PIAA state Class AA quarterfinalist. Last year, Byers competed at 132 pounds, losing to Ferndale’s Charlie Alt 8-4 in the final four seconds of a quarterfinal match at state when he tried to throw Alt down and got caught on his back.

Another captain is senior Je’ Jowers, a two-time Section I-Class AA runner-up and three-time District III-Class AA quarterfinalist, who will start at 132 pounds this year for the Eagles.

title: The Gettysburg Times
headline: Local Teams Compete in Dual Meets and Wrestling Matches
summary: The article reports on multiple high school wrestling dual meets and individual matches held on December 14, 1987, detailing various weight class results and team scores.
cleaned_text:
**Wrestling Action Continues in Dual Meets**

The Gettysburg Times, Monday, December 14, 1987

Wrestling action was reported across several local dual meets. In individual matches, Shawn Forsythe, Jeff Showers, Erie Sowers, and Greg Byers were noted for their performances, with several pins recorded.

In the weight classes, a number of matches were reported:
*   105 lbs: Baker (SH) defeated Szczesniuk, 4:32.
*   112 lbs: Bucher (B) defeated Kling, 3:57.
*   119 lbs: Stoner (B) achieved a technical fall over McClain.
*   126 lbs: Schneider (SH) defeated Brandt, 6-5.
*   132 lbs: Tate (B) defeated Coffman, 1:49.
*   145 lbs: Gardner (B) defeated Zinobile, 11-1.

In other wrestling news, McCollum, the coach from Bermudian Springs, was involved in matches, including one where he was caught in a reverse over at Fe nibalony. At 119 lbs, Adams defeated Asper (B).

The article also covered several dual meet results:
*   New Oxford won more matches in one day than it did all of last season by capturing all three of its matches during the quadrangular.
*   The Colonials defeated Cedar Crest at 41-26, and the host Eastern defeated local Blue Mountain League at Littlestown 35-31.
*   Gettysburg College suffered a 29-19 defeat to Messiah College in Grantham.
*   Another encounter saw the Falcons defeat the opponent 19-9 in the last six bouts.
*   At the heavyweight level, Stan Brodka secured a four-point decision by Wayne Etterau at 150 lbs, giving Gettysburg a strong finish.

headline: Tournament Offers Valuable Preparation for Dual Season
summary: Coaches from local high schools praised the recent tournament for providing invaluable experience and identifying areas for improvement ahead of the dual season.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Mon, Dec 21, 1987

**Tournament Offers Valuable Preparation for Dual Season**

Coaches echoed sentiments about the quality of the recent tournament, making the experience invaluable for the upcoming dual season. The field was strong, with defending champion Spring Grove among the top contenders. South Western was the runner-up in that sport, while the Blue League sent its top two from last season in Biglerville and Bermudian Springs.

The tournament provided a chance for wrestlers to evaluate their talent against top competition. South Western coach Mark Bowersox noted that the main objective of the tournament is to prepare wrestlers for the dual season. "We had some strong performances here which give us a good idea who we can count on for big points in the close dual matches," Bowersox said. "The talent quality also should make even easy tougher as we get ready for the league bouts. Our guys are making a lot of progress."

Other coaches, including those from Biglerville and Bermudian Springs, echoed Bowersox’s remarks, noting that the event works as a "wake-up call" with the regular season and key matches rapidly approaching.

At the individual level, South Western had a candidate for the tournament’s most dominant wrestler in Joey Wildasin, who swept his way to the 119-pound weight class with falls in each round. His teammate, Steve Elicker, showed signs of being among the elite at 155 pounds with an exciting 9-7 win over Biglerville’s Lonnie Mentzer, the defending district champion in that class. South Western also picked up valuable points at 105 pounds, where the Mustangs finished second.

Coach Bucher highlighted the value of the tournament, stating that it gave him an idea of where his lineup needs work, while several strong showings pointed out spots where his wrestlers could improve. He noted that the upset of Mentzer in the finals at 155 pounds might be a blessing, as it will make Mentzer "unbearable in a competitive sense at practice where he’ll come out working even harder while making him mad and even hungrier to get back to winning."

Bermudian Springs coach McCollum noted that he had a few wrestlers in similar situations, suggesting that they will probably react likewise in having a few guys who were just short of being champions.

The article also detailed several individual results, including a third-place finish for Dave aise at 132 pounds for Biglerville, and a consolation win for John Scott at 112 pounds.

headline: Eagles Win Key Matches Against Milton Hershey
summary: The Bermudian Springs Eagles secured key victories in multiple weight classes, overcoming early setbacks to defeat rivals like Milton Hershey.
cleaned_text:
The Patriot-News
Sun, Jan 10, 1988

The Eagles, superb in six of the first seven weight classes, don’t mind mixing in a little extra oomph with their routine and what they added physically helped roll up extra bout and team points that had Milton Hershey reeling early and often.

The fourth-ranked Eagles were at their meanest yesterday, winning all but one of the first nine bouts to sock the Spartans. The team was motivated, and our kids didn’t get really wired in the locker room. We just came out and wrestled.

Eric Wick, the 153-pounder, squealed "terrible" wins and wasn't seen again until Detter’s fall at 167. Rick Trimmer at 112 and Shannon Forsythe at 119 posted six-point wins. Trimmer won by a major decision when Dan Reynolds defaulted with 1:17 remaining. At 126, Eric Sowers won a crucial 5-3 decision over Wayne Armstrong, building a 5-1 first-period lead, then holding on while Armstrong battled back during the last four minutes.

“I wasn't expecting the falls at 98 and 119,” admitted Warner, “and when Wayne wasn't able to get the win at 126, we were in trouble.”

McCollum made a fine move, getting Jeff Showers down to 132 (he'd been bumped from the lineup when unbeaten Greg Byers dropped from 145 to 138), and Showers handled Steve Urban for an 11-1 major.

Leading 25-6, the Eagles were in great shape with Byers coming out, but Milton Hershey's Erving Flagg took Byers, top-ranked at 13X, to the limit with a scrappy 37-point fall, 10-6.

In other matches, Bermudian Springs pushed to the limit, coming out of a 20-2 lead to snap a 20-2 defeat. Susquehanna Cougars needed a fall to defeat Central in 4-Canners user str. The Cougars won the weights, defeating Indians 31-29, and getting a nice Steve Hoffman over the Breech at 167. Lions got a key win at Jake Mitchell, who won by fall over Ritchie Enc. Middletown used a 105 from Matt Kerst topple Troy Lenker.

The Evening Sun – Monday, January 11, 1988

Colt Wrestlers Cruise Past Competition

The wrestling action was intense Saturday. John Scott worked hard for his team, achieving a quick six points. The teams faced tough competition, with the wrestling action featuring weight classes including 118 and 155.

In other athletic news, the Eagle coach noted that his team had to win two out of three in Saturday’s contest, securing three six-point decisions.

Spring Grove pressed through the IJF championship, earning an easy decision against Charles McDuffey and a major decision against Michael Taylor.

South Western won all but two matches over Central in the high jump finals.

Bryers stayed even with Stump, one of the teams associated with Eisenhower, where McDuffey was competing.

**Susquenita Decks Revere in Wrestling Match**

By Rod Frisco
The Patriot-News
Sun, Jan 24, 1988

At the wrestling match between Susquenita and Revere, the Susquenita team proved dominant. Coach McCollum was not at all certain that his team would stay dominant after Susquenita came to town yesterday, but he was unprepared for a pie-in-the-face routine.

“Susquenita had one thing on their mind; they came to wrestle and to win,” said McCollum. “And they did.”

The Blackhawks blinded the Eagles, stripping them of their strength—a quick start—by building up a 21-0 lead in virtually no time.

The match saw several key moments. Daum's win followed Ahern’s checkmate at the start of the meet. Earlier, Bermudian Springs 105-pounder Brian McCauslin was out with a broken arm. McCollum attempted to sneak freshman 98-pounder John Scott into the 105-pound class, hoping Ahern would send out Steve Hevel before Scott went on the mat. However, Ahern was ready, waiting for McCollum to put a wrestler out first, which McCollum had to do as the home team coach. Instead, McCollum forfeited, and Ahern put out Dan to collect the forfeit, then sent out 10 against Scott.

title: The Patriot-News
headline: Local Coaches and Athletes Compete for Wrestling Honors
summary: The article reviews the state of high school wrestling, discussing Coach of Year contenders, recent team performances, and injury updates, while also noting changes in state championship weight predictions.
cleaned_text:
The Patriot-News, Harrisburg, Pa, Saturday, February 13, 1988

**Coach of Year Award**

The question of who may be named "Coach of Year" is a difficult one, as the competition is fierce. All three teams—Allentown's Bill Bence, Lancaster McCaskey's Harvey Sitler, and the third team—figure to be the primary combatants for a team title that is too close to call. All three are having great seasons, making it difficult to predict the individual winner.

In AA, Garden Spot's Gene Hale will be a favorite. His team has produced a 13-2 record, breezing through the Lebanon-Lancaster II crowd like a runaway subway train with no brakes. The only losses were by two points to Hempfield and a point to Warwick. Even though the Spartans expected a strong season, they have been simply dominating.

However, the article highlights the consistency of other programs. Dave Mecotum of Bermudian Springs (11-1) deserves special mention, especially McCollum, whose team has more holes (exposed badly by Susquehanna in the Eagles’ only loss) than both Garden Spot and Biglerville, yet has performed with amazing consistency otherwise.

**Injury Update**

York Catholic's Bulldog senior, sixth at 105 last year and the point man for one of District 6's most-improved programs, suffered what amounted to a stroke in a dual meet two weeks ago against Tussey Mountain. Musselman was clamped in a headlock by Tussey’s Shawn Husick, and had his carotid artery pinched off, rendering Musselman unconscious for several minutes.

**State Championship Predictions**

Central Pennsylvania wrestling fans were buzzing about the possibility of state champions Dave Hart of Juniata and Troy Sunderland meeting at 167 pounds in today’s dual at Mount Union. However, MU head coach Dan Brown said the matchup would not happen. "We'd like it, but Troy's been weighing in 156, 157, 158, and 167 for the team," said Brown. "But he’s going to be at 145 for tournaments. We told him a while ago to start dropping and getting comfortable with the weight."

title: “Dave
headline: Biglerville Clinches Second Straight Blue Mountain League Title
summary: Biglerville secured its second consecutive Blue Mountain League title after a dominant victory, largely attributed to a key win at 98 pounds.
cleaned_text:
GETTYSBURG — Usually, it's heavyweight that fans look to as the glory. But on Thursday at Gettysburg's Biglerville, the team clinched its second straight Blue Mountain League title. The coaches pointed to the 98-pound match as the biggest factor in allowing Biglerville a runaway win.

Biglerville’s Tom Solberg was the hero on Thursday, recovering from a 10-2 deficit to score over John Scott at 4:58. Bermudian Springs coach McCollum hoped to come out of that match, but instead, after a forfeit to Biglerville’s Drew Bucher at 105, the Canners looked good with a 12-0 score.

The win clinches the BML title for Biglerville (15-0 overall, 8-0 BML), which has one league match left against Greencastle. Even if the Canners lose that one, they take the title on head-to-head wins over either Delone Catholic (7-1 BML) or Bermudian Springs (13-2 and 7-1), with those two teams battling for second at Delone on Saturday.

“That win at 98 was a big job to, but for the other guys plus for us,” noted Tommy Solberg.

Later, at 126, Brad Tate got another key win for the Canners when he put a 17-4 beating on Jeff Showers in a match that many thought would be a toss-up. When Bermudian Springs’ Eric Sowers followed with a pin at 1:24 over Randy Durbin at 132, the Eagles, who never led, were still a point short at 17-16.

Todd Gardner cushioned Biglerville’s lead to 23-16 when he pinned Troy Byers, who wrestled despite a broken nose two days before the match, at 2:25.

The Eagles had hoped to make up some ground with Greg Byers, who is 21-0 on the season, at 145, but Donnie Orner stayed off his back and ate up just three team points, losing 14-7. Biglerville then went into the power part of its lineup with a 23-19 lead.

The Canners’ Lonnie Mentzer, despite a sore back, worked to a 10-7 decision over Eric Wickline at 155. Darren Sobota, at 167, sealed the title for his team with a pin at 2:33 over Steve Silsley. With two matches left, Biglerville had the match won on mat math, ahead 32-19.

Scott Asper added six more points for Biglerville with his 3:17 pin over Ron Decker at 167. Brian Group had a 9-4 lead when he ran out of injury time and was forced into a disqualification against Jake Bankert.

“Truthfully,” noted Biglerville’s Sobota, “we came into this match with a lot of our guys hurt. But this was a total team win with guys ignoring pain to help the team. It came down to who wanted it, happy to see us pull together to make it.”

cleaned_text:
**Wrestling and Swimming Coverage (February 27, 1988)**

**Wrestling Coverage**

At the wrestling tournament, the Bermudian Springs head coach, McCollum, stated, “We wrestled real well. We couldn’t have gotten a better night, really.” While McCollum was pleased that eight of his wrestlers made the districts, he was very upset about the circumstances surrounding one of the most exciting bouts of the night. The match pitted Littlestown’s Brad Dayett against Jake Bankert, and Dayett ended up pinning Bankert in overtime at 2:26.

In other news, the six Squires who made the districts are freshman Spike Kuhn (105 pounds), Tony Bream (112 pounds), Chad Giraffa (126 pounds), Joe Neiderer (145 pounds), Craig Reese (155 pounds), and Aaron Small (167 pounds). Reese won by pin, while Bream and Neiderer won by decision.

Another wrestling report detailed local action: Byers (132 pounds), Erie Sowers (138 pounds), and Eric Wickline (145 pounds) competed. For the Bermudian Springs pins, Showers won by superior decision (13-0) over Camp Hill’s Dan Accurti, Wickline won by major decision (14-3) over Greencastle’s Chet Carbaugh, and Silsley came back from a 5-2 deficit after one period to beat Oiling Springs’ Matt Kempf, 12-7. Ten out of the 12 grapplers were able to win at least one match Friday.

**Swimming Coverage**

In separate news, the Gettysburg men’s swimming team held onto place after Art Kelly won the 200-yard backstroke and helped Gettysburg win the 400-yard medley relay, leading the team to first place on the second day of competition in the Middle Atlantic Conference Championships. Similarly, Shelden won the 200-yard butterfly stroke and helped lead the Gettysburg women’s team to first place.

The Evening Sun
Mon, Feb 29, 1988

**Hanover Wrestlers Shine at Section I Tournament**

The Section I Tournament was a success for the Hanover wrestling team, which showed its strength throughout the season. The Nighthawk heavyweight won a championship, defeating Littlestown’s Brad Dayett in the final two seconds of the match.

Hanover, which also took its lumps through a tough YCL slate, finished the sectional tournament strong, earning respect again. James Corbin helped Hanover to its strong finish with a championship at 105 pounds.

“We don’t do too much for a team through the count,” Hanover coach Bucher reported. “That’s been the case with these guys all year.”

The championship success wasn't the only story. At 132 pounds, Jeff Showers and Greg Byers represented the Eagles proudly, taking championships. Byers (25-0) appears to be on a mission to place higher than he did last year at states. He won at 132 over Biglerville’s Livingston, 11-1. Showers, who was the No. 2 seed, pinned Hanover’s Beckner at 3:13 for the title.

“I’m proud of our seniors earning this season,” Bucher said. “His leadership has helped keep the team together. It’s nice to see Showers do well because he’s gotten through some tough times earlier this season.”

Other notable performances included:
*   Craig Reese was a Delone wrestler who earned second place at 155 pounds.
*   Spike Kuhn (at 105), Chad Giraffa (126), and Joe Neiderer (145) each took thirds for the S4 ires.
*   Aaron Small also qualified for districts with a fourth at 167 pounds.
*   Littlestown's Mike Tackett gave the Bolts another medal with a third at 185 pounds.

In other matches, Joe Delone, at 3:58, defeated Steel-High’s George Orner, the third-highest touted 145-pound wrestler. Darren Sobota, who is nursing a shoulder, got a bonus point and a pin to his pin, adding to his team’s success.

McCollum, the Bermudian Springs wrestling coach, was pleased with the high finishes, though he pointed out slow starts in each of the matches.

At the recent sectionals and finals, local wrestlers had a strong showing. While there was little chance the team was going to lose the title at that point, the wrestlers didn’t wrestle like they usually do.

The championship bracket finals concluded Saturday, culminating in a dramatic battle between Oley Valley and Biglerville. By the time evening arrived, Oley Valley held a slight lead, 76% to 75%, over Biglerville.

“We were behind when we came in here this evening. We knew what we had to do. Our kids just came through for us,” said Bucher. “They just really buckled down. We've done the same thing all year: we've just wrestled for the team. This is probably the best wrestling team that ever wrestled for Biglerville.”

Earlier in the day, Sobota (Biglerville) had secured a critical win against Mark Groff (Conrad Weiser) in a championship-bracket semifinal bout. Sobota then defeated Kevin Tucker (Scotland) before getting a decisive 5-2 win over Bob Detter (Milton Hershey). This victory gave Sobota, who maintains a 27-2 record, his second straight third-place finish at the district level.

The evening's climax came down to the final Class AA bout. Oley Valley heavyweight Jason Conrad pinned Martin Buzas of Milton Hershey to earn Oley Valley six points and a one-point victory over Biglerville. However, Buzas managed to shut Conrad out 7-0.

In other news, Greg Byers of Bermudian Springs showcased remarkable determination, overcoming a hyperextended right arm injury to compete. He earned an 8-5 semifinal victory over David Groth (Daniel Boone).

**AA State Wrestling Preview: Local Stars Head to PIAA Tournament**

Eight local Class AA wrestlers are set to compete in the PIAA State Championship, with Bermudian Springs sending three state qualifiers, followed by Biglerville with four, and Hanover with one. The state tournament begins Thursday at 9 a.m. at Hersheypark Arena, promising a high-quality atmosphere for fans from Thursday morning until late Saturday night.

Biglerville’s Lon Mentzer, Darren Sobota, and Scott Asper are highly touted medal hopefuls. Mentzer, Sobota, and Byers are all returning to the tournament after gaining valuable state-level experience last year. Other contenders include Donnie Orner and Scott Asper from Biglerville, Jeff Showers and Eric Wickline from Bermudian Springs, and James Corbin, Hanover’s sole representative.

Byers, the only AA wrestler from this area to reach this stage with a perfect record, has been on a mission since the season opened in December. According to his coach, McCollum, the senior has always brought his best wrestling skills up a level in March. Byers will meet Jim Say from Cranberry, a senior with a record of 31-3.

Mentzer and Byers have the most experience in state competition. Last year, both seniors advanced to the quarterfinals before being eliminated in consolation matches. Byers, in particular, has been at his best in the postseason.

Mentzer, who lost a match to Steve Elicker of South Western—who went on to win a Class AAA district title—back in December, viewed that loss as an early-season "wake-up call." Coach Mike Bucher was correct; Mentzer rolled to a 30-1 record while fighting off late-season shoulder problems to win a district title last weekend. In earning that title, Mentzer hit back-to-back pins through the final two rounds, indicating a return to top physical form. Mentzer’s first-round opponent is Pete Varischetti, a senior from Brockway with a record of 28-3.

Orner, according to Bucher, is the perfect example of a wrestler peaking at the right time. The 145-pound sophomore worked to a 21-5-1 record this winter, surprising higher-seeded wrestlers to win sectional titles. His first-round opponent is Holby, a junior from Ferndale.

The weekend’s district tournament action includes West Hilltop’s Mark Antonik (28-1) opening the first-round action at 185 pounds. Teammate Sobota (27-2), a junior, meets Hughesville’s Si eese (33-4) at 167 pounds. Reese is noted as the first in the northeast section. Hanover’s Corbin, who made it to states as a sophomore, meets Northern Lehigh’s Weizel in the first-round action. Bermudian Springs’ Showers (21-?) starts against Clayton Griffin, while Wickline takes on Hickory’s Drew Per (30-3).

title: The Gettysburg Times

The Gettysburg Times
March 18, 1988

The local sports scene was active, featuring reports from multiple athletic events. In football, Delone faced Warrior Run, with the latter ultimately defeating the Defenders 72-63. Warrior Run, which had won the District IV-Class AA championship, improved its record to 25-4 and advanced to the state quarter finals. Delone finished its season with a 23-7 record.

The action continued into other sports, including a wrestling match where Byers earned a 1-1 (1-0) decision over Larry Solomon of Pahsaides in the consolation bracket.

Speaking about the wrestling competition, Bermudian Springs head coach BeMcCollum noted, “Greg’s elbow was bothering him a little bit, and things like that seem even bigger when you're at this level of competition.” He added, “I was rather disappointed that he didn’t wrestle as well offensively as he’s capable. When you give up the escape to try for the takedown, you've got to go all out on offense, and I don’t think Greg did that.”

headline: Mentzer Reflects on Three-Match Wrestling Tournament
summary: Despite losing, Mentzer was pleased with his performance after wrestling his opponent three times in one year, while McCollum noted the difficulty of the competition.
cleaned_text:
The Gettysburg Times (AOP)
March 19, 1988

...The third time around, however, Mentzer got a takedown just 11 seconds into the bout, and Byers’ first-period takedown was suddenly over. Flagg got a first-period takedown. Mentzer soon received a penalty point and pulled Byers finally got on the board with a reversal. Flagg got another reversal, and Clark got a reversal from the bottom position. The first period ended.

In the second period, Flagg came up with a takedown with just three seconds left. Neither of the wrestlers scored points in the third period.

“The bottom line is this: You need to ‘wrestle a kid three times in one year, and if you beat him three times, you’re going to be good,” Mentzer said. “I got caught off a reversal before the action—his own reversal. Flagg got another reversal, and Clark got a reversal from the bottom position.”

Though dejected by his loss, Mentzer was pleased with what he accomplished overall. “I'm happy I did as good as I did,” he said. “I think he (Byers) tried to wrestle a smart match instead of an aggressive match, and I think he got caught with the flow.”

Boyer, whose overall record is now 33-3, was Mentzer’s opponent Saturday afternoon. “The toughest overall. third time’s a treat.”

McCollum, the head coach, said, “The bottom line is this: You can’t get out of it.”

Byers, who made it to the quarter-finals, has not been 100 percent.

headline: Canners’ Stars Shine at Tournament
summary: Local wrestlers from Biglerville and Bermudian Springs dominated the tournament, with stars like Bucher and Mentzer achieving significant victories despite injuries and future plans.
cleaned_text:
The Canners’ Championship: Local Stars Shine at Tournament

The atmosphere at the tournament was electric, with wrestlers competing to prove their mettle. Jeff Showers of Bermudian Springs, wrestling at 126 pounds, started out against Bob Williams of Susquenita in the Class I final. Showers, who has no plans to continue competing at a high level, joked that he might become a lumberjack and move up to the woods after high school.

“I’ll miss a lot of things about competing in the sport,” noted the Canners’ 105-pound Bucher while summing up the emotions of the wrestlers on Thursday. Bucher, who plans to attend Indiana University of Pennsylvania next fall, dropped a 15-4 decision to Wes Mendez from Solanco. Mendez, who has a record of 26-7, was part of a strong showing by the Biglerville team.

Biglerville provided the Class AA team with more than half of its scoring on Thursday, with Mentzer and Asper each producing big wins. Mentzer, who helped the area secure its only double-A state medalist by taking fifth, worked toward a 7-3 win over West Perry's Larry Fry. “It's better to wrestle here than at states,” Mentzer noted.

Biglerville had a dominant regular season, going 18-0 and winning sectional and district titles. Asper stated that he has no plans to wrestle at the collegiate level.

A special case was Trevor Byers. He was honored as an all-star on Thursday but was unable to wrestle due to a bone chip he suffered before the district tournament. Even with the injury, Bermudian Springs’ 132-pounder ignored the arm pains to earn a title at the districts.

“What he did at districts came through pure ire; the doctors said he not even try to wrestle with his arm as badly as it was. We had to tie him down to carry him,” Byers said Thursday. “I was frustrated with the way my season ended. It's the worst I've had during my career and it really came at a bad time, but it'll make me that much hungrier at college.” Byers plans to wrestle at Millersville University, where his former state champion, Trevor Byers, will also start out with four years of eligibility.

The local guys, who know each other as friends through years of competition, joined forces by wrestling out together past the sectional tournament.

title: Class I
headline: Local Teams Prepare for BML Title Chase
summary: Local high school wrestling teams, including Bermudian Springs, Bingham, Greencastle, and Scotland, are preparing for the BML title season despite varying levels of experience and roster depth.
cleaned_text:
Bermudian Springs is poised to contend for the BML title this season, bolstered by returning lettermen and a transfer student who previously lettered at Littlestown. The team is strong in the light and upper weights but is somewhat thin in the middle weights.

Bingham (7-2, 13-3) boasts a good turnout of 32 kids, five of whom were qualified last year. This group is expected to boost coach Dave McCollum's hopes for a run at the title this season. The Eagles were 7-0 before losing late-season matches to Biggest and Delone in last year's title chase, and the team must be ready for early matchups against those two.

Greencastle (6-7, 7-6) reports that coach Trent Mathews notes that although the team has only five lettermen, the wrestlers are starting to be senior and junior.

Scotland (9-9, 3-17) has only two returning men and no one to wrestle at the 185 weight class. Coach Sam Brand does not expect the Cadets to contend for the BML title this year.

title: The Evening Sun
headline: Bermudian Springs Upsets Biglerville at Invitational Wrestling Tournament
summary: Bermudian Springs defeated two-time champion Biglerville in a major upset at the South Western Invitational Wrestling Tournament, while Susquehannock and South Western also secured multiple titles.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Mon, Dec 19, 1988

In a major upset, the Bermudian Springs team outpointed two-time defending District III Class AA champion Biglerville, defeating them 95-85. Gettysburg finished sixth in the tournament.

South Western was powered by six first-place finishers, including defending PIAA Class AAA champion Joey Wildasin. Other South Western winners included 103-pound sophomore Brandon Wingert, 145-pound Frank Lecrone, 160-pound Steve Elicker, 189-pound Jim Novak, and sophomore heavyweight Matt Hladio. Lecrone was noted as the most efficient wrestler of the day, picking up falls in just 1:33 and 0:24. Hladio also won both of his bouts by fall in 3:08 and 3:57.

“Going into it, on paper, we were looking at six titles, but we didn’t know which six,” South Western head coach Mark Bowersox said.

For the Bermudian Springs teams, the season was successful. Eric Sowers led the way for the Eagles, taking first place at 140 pounds with a pin and a decision. Bermudian Springs head coach Collum stated, “We were pretty good. Two of our better wrestlers, John Scott and Jake Bankert, [performed well]. I think it [is] to say there’s been a change in power in the league. Dual meets are a lot different than tournaments, but it gave us some optimism for the regular season.”

Biglerville’s Don Orner brought home the 152-pound title with a technical fall and a decision. Biglerville head coach Mike Bucher, owner of a 21 dual-match winning streak, noted that while they did pretty well, they suffered an injury to the 171-pounder Jason. He concluded that the BML is "pretty balanced this season" and that the league is looking forward to the year.

Susquehannock, a new power in the YCIAA Eastern Division, finished second in the tournament by taking two titles. Tom Temple captured the 119-pound weight class with a pin and a decision, and Eric Alt took the 135-pound crown with a technical fall and a decision.

The South Western Invitational Wrestling Tournament included winners such as Eric Sowers of Bermudian Springs, Scott Forry of Spring Grove, Joey Wildasin of South Western, Tom Temple of Susquehannock, Sam Metzger of Spring Grove, and Brandon Wingert of South Western.

title: Dec 28, 1988, page 16 - The Evening Sun at Newspapers.com™
headline: Bermudian Springs Wins First Day at Invitational Wrestling Tournament
summary: Bermudian Springs secured a victory on the first day of the Boulder Springs Invitational Wrestling tournament, with key athletes like Riley Green, Shannon Everett, and Jake Baskett contributing to the team's success.
cleaned_text:
Bermudian Springs — Team co-captain Riley Green led her teammates to a victory on the first day of the Boulder Springs Invitational Wrestling tournament. This tournament win marks the first for the team since late last season. The 112-pound Class AA team, according to the Harmonburg Plains Blue Mountain League rival and two-time champion, is scheduled to meet January 11.

The tournament was highly competitive. Southern placed second, while Mechanicsburg finished third. Overall, the competition was even.

McCollum noted that there didn't seem to be a team with the best record on the first day of the Boulder Springs Invitational Wrestling tournament.

While McCollum mentioned that Seed and the team captain should be back, he opened the lineup for his 115-pound starter, John Scott, and his 112-pound wrestler at Boulder Springs.

Also scoring for the team was Shannon Everett, who finished second. The team also saw strong performances from heavyweight Jake Baskett and 120-pound Steve Hook, who secured a third-place finish. Jake Peyron earned a fourth-place finish.

headline: Spartans Win Match Against Carolina

cleaned_text:
**In Loss on Squires**

By MIKE SANDERS
Times Sports Editor

The Bermudian Springs Spartans, with their team up to 20-3 after seven bouts, backed running coach Daniel Deane to a win on Thursday night against Carolina. Kris Sewers, who moved up a weight class, was reportedly born by Deane's Aunt Judy.

During the match, Matt Merrin, who was at 122 pounds, picked Steve Hawk in the final destination. He was surprised by the loss of those two points, even with Dr. Moving and McCain accounted for. "That was all Delaney needed to get back in the match and make it interesting," one observer noted.

Merrin's pin made the score 28-12, and the Squires went on to hit the next three wickets to close within 28-26 heading into the innings. With his team, aided by Dr. Moving, John Banker won the match.

The Evening Sun
Tuesday, January 10, 1989

**Local Powerhouses Face Off at Delone Tournament**

The Delone Tournament featured several highly anticipated bouts across various weight classes.

At 145 pounds, Jason Forsythe (6-3, freshman) faced a bout involving Grant Forsythe (soph., 1-2) and Chris Showers (sr., 1-2). The comment noted the matchup involved "age and little experience against youth and little experience," resulting in an even advantage.

In the 152-pound division, Steve Hoak (sr., 5-3) competed against Don Orner (jr.). Commentators noted that while Orner has natural ability, Hoak was a senior with experience. The advantage was given to Bermudian Springs.

The 160-pound bout was another top matchup between Mike Floyd (sr., 6-3) and Brad Shower. The bout was described as tough, with Floyd being a senior who had previously taken third place at South Western and Shower being a first-year varsity junior. The advantage was even.

Steve Silsley (sr., 5-4) was also noted, with comments mentioning him as a returning district qualifier.

In the Heavyweight division, Jake Bankert (sr., 3-1) faced Robert Shockey (jr., 7-3). The bout was considered make-or-break, especially since Bankert had been sidelined earlier in the year with a knee injury. The advantage was given to Biglerville.

The article also highlighted the coaching staff, noting that Dave McCollum (2nd year) had won the league in previous years after dropping the Delone and [unspecified location]. McCollum, along with Mike Bucher (8th year), was credited with giving the program a solid start, and hopes were high for Bermudian Springs to win the tournament.

From staff reports

Janell Williams made Central
Dauphin East’s bid for a share of
first place in Division II of the
Mid-Penn Conference a personal
thing yesterday.

Williams tied a school single-
game game scoring record with

9 points as the Panthers routed
Hershey 66-48. The win dead-
locks East with Middletown and
Lower Dauphin in first place. All
have 5-1 league records.

The 39 points tied Williams
with teammate Tasha Livingston
—_—_______..

Prep sports

who set the single-game standard
last season. In this scrap Living-
ston contributed a dozen points.

Williams finished strong,
scoring 15 points in the final
quarter. Only a missed shot at the
final buzzer stood between her
and the record. Williams also had
22 rebounds, a pair of blocked
shots and two steals. She drilled
in 13 of 18 shots from the field.

The performance overshad-
owed a fine effort by Hershey's
Heidi Caruso who finished with
23 points.

Cumberland Valley survived
foul trouble in the late going to
edge Palmyra 53-50. It was the
Cougars’ first MPC Division I loss
of the season.

The Eagles’ Angie Menegat
pumped in 25 points, while Deb-
bie Snyder tallied 27 for Palmyra.
Snyder netted 13 points in the
third quarter as the Cougars tried
to rally, but that effort was near-
ly matched by 10 from Menegat.

Jodi Urich ran off three
Straight transition buckets in a
two minute span of the second
quarter as West Perry dropped
Shippensburg, 44-36. Leanne
Kling sparked the losers with 15.

Heidi Babb burned the nets

By Rod Frisco
Patriot-News

BIGLERVILLE — Simply file
this Bermudian-Biglerville wres-
tling opera under “Classic” ...
along with the rest.

When Bermudian Springs
heavyweight Jake Bankert
slipped behind Biglerville’s Rob-
ert Shockey with nine seconds
remaining in their bout, Bankert’s
major decision enabled Bermudi-
an to slip behind the Canners
28-27.

The result: an explosion of
bouncing bodies strewn across
Bankert and the Biglerville mat

Prep wrestling

as the Eagles celebrated their
ees, over Biglerville since

“I know one thing, I don’t
like ’em this close,” gasped Ber-
mudian head coach Dave McCol-
lum.

And so it was.

As a result, Bermudian, 4-0
overall and 3-0 in the BML, is in
good shape to yank the crown
from Biglerville’s head following
last week's victory over Delone
and last night's zinger.

Biglerville, 4-2 overall and 2-1
in the BML, has lost two straight
following a 23-meet winning
streak that was stopped last Sat-
urday by Central Dauphin.

“We weren't disappointed af-
ter losing to Central Dauphin,
considering we didn’t have our
best lineup, but we're a little dis-
appointed after this,” said Bigler-
ville head coach Mike Bucher.

Potriot-News/Stuort Leouk

CD East’s Janell Williams grabs a rebound away from
Steph Spencer of Hershey in first-half Mid-Penn action

with 38 points as 9-2 Fannett-
Metal waltzed past Mercersburg
Academy 68-41. Babb had 15
points in the second quarter alone
as the winners opened a 35-14
lead by intermission. Jennifer
Zintak countered with 19 for
Mercersburg.

Kim Bjornson's 22 points car-
ried Lampeter-Strasburg to a
58-52 win in triple overtime over
Lebanon Catholic.

Darlene Shaffrom and Sue
Hollingsworth combined for 31
points as East Pennsboro stepped
out of conference play to spank
Conrad Weiser 59-28,

Annette Hoffman pounded
home 30 points on her own as
Indian Valley ripped Greenwood
57-37.

BOYS’ BASKETBALL

Chadd Baker's bucket with
two seconds remaining in over-
time gave East Pennsboro a 62-60

non-conference victory over Do-
ver.

Baker ended play with 17
points while teammate Ron Urich
took game honors with 23 and
became the fifth Panther in
school history to tally 1,000
points in his career. The senior
quard now has 1,009 points,

Jeff Young and Mike Wilt
combined for 38 points for Dover.

GYMNASTICS

Jamie Koonradt and Tammy
Jarrett came through with re-
spective vault and bars wins to
lead Red Land to a 13:
win over Central Dauphin.

These two teams are battling
Central Dauphin East for the lead
in the Mid-Penn Conference.

Koonradt won the vault with
a 9.5 score while Jarrett won the
bars with 9.25, Teammate Kerry
Brocious was first on the beam at
8.80. CD's Marisa Dinatale took
floor exercise with a 9.35 total.

NOTE: Records through Tuesday, Jan. 10. Some records are approximate.

Bermudian claims battle of BML titans

Banker's closing decision puts Eagles over the top, 28-27

“I know one thing, | don’t like ’em this

close.”

—Bermudian coach Dave McCollum

“Still,” said Bucher, stoking
his pipe, “you couldn't have cho-
reographed a meet any better
than that one.”

This was a gift from wres-
tling heaven, a meet of unrelent-
ing action — even when points
were just a hope — and some-
times furious outbursts of emo-
tion.

But even better, it was a meet
of exquisite strategy by both
coaches and some amazing results
by the wrestlers wrapped in a
two-hour heart attack.

At 103, Biglerville’s Travis
Byers was well on his way to a
technical fall over Travis Griffie,
leading 13-0 after two periods.

But at the end of the second
period, Bucher instructed Byers
to take the top position and go
hard for the fall on his next turn-
over rather than just settling for
the technical fall.

But the next turnover never
came, Griffie fighting Byers’ bars
and cross-faces and managing to
get out with a major decision.

Even then, the Eagles were
wrestling without sophomore
John Scott, who was ruled aca-
demically ineligible just before
the meet.

The point lost by Biglerville
became a point saved at the next
weight when Steve Szczeniuk
skipped away from Bermudian’s
Brian McCauslin with two sec-
onds left to avoid a major in a
12-5 loss.

Jeff Griest’s fall over the Ea-
gles’ Tom Oberlander at 119 was
matched by Bermudian’s Shannon
Forsythe at 130, then one-upped
by Jason Forsythe at. 140. That
came when his first cousin, Grant
Forsythe, tried to roll through a
bar and half and didn’t quite
make it.

In fact, 140 was the start of
the great chess match between
McCollum and Bucher that lasted
through 189.

The jockeying at 140 provided
@ fabulous bout at 145 where
once-beaten Eric Sowers stopped
Randy Durbin 4-0. A cross-face
cradle from legs for three back
points late in the bout was the
decider.

That victory gave Bermudian
a 22-13 lead, the largest at any
point of the match.

And though the coaching
thrust-and-parry continued un-
abated through the upper
weights, it still came down to
wrestling. And that was Grade-A
stuff.

At 152, Biglerville’s Brad
Showers stopped Mike Floyd 5-1,
hitting a pair of low single-legs
for the key points. Both were
down from 160.

Bucher bumped defending 3-
AA champion Don Orner to 160,
and Orner pinned Steve Hoak in
4:36 after Hoak, in just his sixth
week of wrestling, gave Orner a
third-period wake-up call by
nearly taking him down.

Then at 171, Ron Decker of
Bermudian chose neutral to start
the third period and executed two
takedowns against a dying Group
before Group reversed and rode
out the final 30 seconds in a 4-4
draw, a huge plus for Bermudian.

At 189, Biglerville’s Jason Fis-
sel, wrestling his first bout since
a severe shoulder sprain suffered
on Dec. 17, dumped Steve Silsley
9-3 to give Biglerville a 27-24
lead heading into 275.

Bankert needed a major deci-
sion or better, and he put his
experience edge over Shockey to
work.

“We had to make some ad-
justments tonight and so did Big-
lerville,” said McCollum. “We
were lucky we had everyone
down to weight and could make
the moves we had to.”

Wrong. Luck was a spectator
last night. And it got one heck of
a show.

NOTES — South Western
maintained its domination in
York County with a 31-15
thumping of previously unbeaten
Dallastown. The loss ended Dal-
lastown's state record winning
streak at 77. ... Line Mountain
took command in the Tri-Valley
League following a 48-12 victory
over second place Juniata. Bob
Renn, Larry Straub, Todd Lau-
denslager and Chad Keim record-
ed falls as Line Mountain opened
a 40-0 lead before Don Klingens-
mith edged Troy Erdman 3-2 at
145 pounds to break the ice for
the Indians, Undefeated
Greencastle kept pace with Ber-
mudian in the Blue Mountain,
any disposing of Gettysburg

Raider swimmers start

Shippensburg’s men’s team got off to
a fast start this season, winning [2 of its first 13
meets and qualifying all five of its relays for the
national championship meet in March.

In those first 13 meets, the Raiders’ only loss
was to Division I power Villanova.

Shippensburg also defeated seven teams, includ-
ing defending Pennsylvania Conference champion
Clarion while taking first place at the Buffalo Invi-
tational.

The Raiders’ most impressive swimmer has been
All-American Brady Stauffer. Stauffer, a sophomore
from Governor Mifflin, has broken school records in
the 100- and 200-yard backstroke with times of
52.12 and 1:52.82.

His time in the 200 backstroke was faster than
the winning time at the NCAA Division II national
championships last year.

Included on this year's team are such former
Mid-Penn o—
swimmers as
senior Marvin
Bliss, who
swims the but-
terfly, from
Palmyra; junior
freestyler An-
drew Ellis
from Cham- ——————_________
bersburg and his freshman brother Matthew, who
swims the butterfly; senior Tim Verge from Cham-
bersburg, who swims the butterfly and individual
medley, and freshman Kurt Sprenger from Ship-
pensburg, who swims the freestyle event.

Other midstaters on the team include freshman
Christ Albrecht from South Western, junior Scott
Berger from Greencastle and junior Derek Higgs
from Dallastown.

The Raiders topped Bloomsburg 126-121 in their
first of four consecutive home meets that also in-
cluded Bucknell, Penn State and Washington & Lee
this week before traveling to Kutztown Tuesday.

@ Shippensburg’s women’s swimming
team has taken a cue from the men’s team,
winning its first three meets in November and

December before losing to Bloomsburg

136-107 earlier this week,

The Raiders followed Bloomsburg with a home
meet against Washington & Lee this week and one
at Lock Haven Saturday.

One of this year’s most successful swimmers is
expected to be junior Kelli Brunner from Central
Dauphin East High School.

Brunner has already turned in a time of 2:22.13
in the 200-yard backstroke.

Other former Mid-Penn swimmers on this year's
team include sophomore Lisa Burgess from Cumber-
land Valley, who swims the breaststroke and indi-
vidual medley; sophomore Amy Giroux from Boiling
Springs, who swims the butterfly and freestyle:
sophomore Kelli Hofstetter from Lower Dauphin, in
the breaststroke and butterfly; senior Tiffany Tins-
man from Susquehanna Twp., in the freestyle
sprint; senior Shawn Ulrich from Palmyra in the

fast

Former Camp Hill players Karen, left, and
Michelle Markley are now at West Chester

backstroke and freestyle and sophomore diver Deb-
orah Wells from Trinity.

@ Michelle Markley, a senior co-captain at
West Chester from Camp Hill High School,
has been averaging nearly 10 points and five
rebounds per game for the Golden Rams wom-
en’s basketball team this season.

She was named West Chester Athlete of the
Week in early December by scoring in double fig-
ures for three straight games. She is a 5-11 starting
forward.

In a 71-62 loss to Philadelphia Textile earlier
this week, she scored 13 points and grabbed nine
rebounds for the Golden Rams.

Her younger sister Karen, a 5-11 junior substi-
tute at forward, scored 17 points and grabbed 19
rebounds in the Rams’ first seven games.

@ At Bucknell, where the men’s and women's
basketball teams get most of the winter Publicity at
the school, the indoor track and field teams have
been turning in impressive early performances,

Greg Keller, a junior who was a high school
All-America at Susquehanna Twp., moved to fifth
on the Bison all-time list of weight throwers with a
winning toss of 46-2 of the 35-pound weight.

Jim Bock, a freshman from Central Dauphin,
was second in the 60-yard dash to equal the ninth
fastest all-time performance at Bucknell, and was
second in the 300 with a 34.0.

lum.”

overall and 3-0 in the BML, is in
good shape to yank the crown

in the BML, has lost two straight
following a 23-meet winning

urday by Central Dauphin.

ter losing to Central Dauphin,

appointed after this,” said Bigler-

Kim Bjornson's 22 points car-

58-52 win in triple overtime over

Darlene Shaffrom and Sue
Hollingsworth combined for 31

Annette Hoffman pounded

Chadd Baker's bucket with

ver.

Jamie Koonradt and Tammy
Jarrett came through with re-
spective vault and bars wins to
lead Red Land to a 135.25-113.30
win over Central Dauphin.

These two teams are battling
Central Dauphin East for the lead
in the Mid-Penn Conference.

a 9.5 score while Jarrett won the
bars with 9.25. Teammate Kerry
Brocious was first on the beam at
8.80. CD’s Marisa Dinatale took
floor exercise with a 9.35 total.

“| know one thing, | don’t like 'em this

close.”

—Bermudian coach Dave McCollum

“Still,” said Bucher, stoking
his pipe, “you couldn't have cho-
reographed a meet any better
than that one.”

This was a gift from wres-
tling heaven, a meet of unrelent-
ing action — even when points
were just a hope — and some-
times furious outbursts of emo-
tion.

But even better, it was a meet
of exquisite strategy by both
coaches and some amazing results
by the wrestlers wrapped in a
two-hour heart attack.

At 103, Biglerville's Travis
Byers was well on his way to a
technical fall over Travis Griffie,

But at the end of the second

to take the top position and go
hard for the fall on his next turn-

onds left to avoid a major in a

Forsythe, tried to roll through a

the great chess match between

through 189.

Points late in the bout was the
decider.

a 22-13 lead, the largest at any
point of the match.

thrust-and-parry continued un-
abated through the upper
weights, it still came down to
wrestling, And that was Grade-A
stuff.

Showers stopped Mike Floyd 5-1,
hitting a pair of low single-legs
for the key ate Both were
down from 160.

AA champion Don Orner to 160,
and Orner pinned Steve Hoak in
4:36 after Hoak, in just his sixth
week of wrestling, gave Orner a
third-period wake-up call by

before Group reversed and rode
out the final 30 seconds in a 4-4
draw, a huge plus for Bermudian,

sel, wrestling his first bout since

9-3 to give Biglerville a 27-24
lead heading into 275.

sion or better, and he put his
experience edge over Shockey to
work.

justments tonight and so did Big-
lerville,” said McCollum. “We
were lucky we had everyone
down to weight and could make
the moves we had to.”

a show.

ely disposing of Gettysburg

Walsh may have created
‘monster’ in Sam Wyche

By Jeff Lenihan
Cincinnati Post

SANTA CLARA, Calif. —
Cincinnati coach Sam Wyche
probably doesn't have a bigger
supporter than San Francisco
coach Bill Walsh, his rival in Su-
per Bowl XXIII.

It was Walsh who escorted
Wyche into the coaching profes-
sion a decade ago.

Wyche was dejected because
he didn’t get a high school coach-
ing job in Greenville, S.C., and
thought his only impact on the
sporting world would be as the

owner of a chain of
stores selling ath-
NFL letic equipment.

- And it was
Walsh who defended Wyche
when it appeared the Bengals
coach would find fame as the
master of the cruel and unusual
defeat,

But Walsh couldn't be blamed
if he watched Wyche’s offense
operate without a huddle, then
stared into the mirror and asked,
“What hath thou created?”

Walsh has twice called
Wyche the league’s most creative
offensive coach this week. When
Walsh says that, he essentially is
giving himself a second-hand
compliment.

Wyche learned from Walsh
the value of quick rhythm on of-
fense and the manner in which
the weak spot of a defense can be
exploited most effectively.

But Walsh has never attempt-
ed and is just beginning to under-
stand this no-huddle business.

Walsh has made many contri-
butions to the modern game. He
is a pioneer of situation substitu-
tions, a concept so prevalent it’s
hard to believe it was done any
other way.

Wyche's no-huddle and
quick-huddle offenses are a re-
sponse to that, preventing oppo-
nents from sending pass rushers
and extra defensive backs onto
the field.

In the Super Bowl, Walsh

From Patriot News files

Bill Walsh
Wrestles with ‘no-huddle’

would like to insert defensive
linemen Daniel Stubbs and Pierce
Holt and defensive backs Tom
Holmoe and Darryl Pollard in
passing situations, but the no-
huddle concept could make that
difficult.

Walsh is not averse to the
pupil using his knowledge against
the teacher, but like Buffalo Bills
coach Marv Levy, he is concerned
that the Bengals might gain an
unfair advantage by substituting
or huddling deceptively,

“We still have to do some
more research into it,” Walsh
said, “because we're not sure ex-
actly what the ramifications of it
are. But I do think that a game
like this should be played well
within the spirit of the rules as
well as within the rules them-
selves,

“We're not trying to pass peo-
ple with yellow flags here. We
just feel the spirit of the rules
should be enforced along with
the rules themselves. And we're
confident that will happen.”

Like most successful innova-
tions, Walsh said the no-huddle
offense figures to be used
throughout the league since

From Patriot-New files
Sam Wyche
A master of innovations

‘Wyche has used it successfully,
“We've used the no-huddle
for reasons of our own in the
past,” Walsh said. “But we
haven't made it the focus of any-
thing we’ve done. Maybe we
should. Maybe everyone should. ,

“When I first saw Sam doing
it, I just said, ‘That's creative.’
He's done it just about the entire
time he's been in coaching. There
were bugs in it initially, so it
would backfire and look funny.
Now that he’s got it going, they
are a step ahead of everyone in
the game.”

If anyone can find a way to
counter the no-huddle offense, it
figures to be Walsh.

“We haven't even thought of
anything yet,” he said. “I just
hope right now we're not con-
fused, that we're not walking
around lost out there. That thing
is dynamite.”

Still, Walsh is unsure he will
be able to find a way to defuse it
by game time Jan, 22,

“We could be at a disadvan-
tage all day,” he said. “They may
have found the way [to beat San
Francisco].”

stared into the mirror and asked,
“What hath thou created?”

Walsh has twice called

offensive coach this week. When
Walsh says that, he essentially is
giving himself a second-hand
compliment.

Wyche learned from Walsh
the value of quick rhythm on of-
fense and the manner in which
the weak spot of a defense can be
exploited most effectively.

But Walsh has never attempt-
ed and is just beginning to under-
stand this no-huddle business.

Walsh has made many contri-
butions to the modern game. He
is a pioneer of situation substitu-
tions, a concept so prevalent it's
hard to believe it was done any
other way.

Wyche’s no-huddle and
quick-huddle offenses are a re-
sponse to that, preventing oppo-
nents from sending pass rushers
and extra defensive backs onto
the field.

In the Super Bowl, Walsh

Wrestles with ‘no-huddle’

would like to insert defensive
linemen Daniel Stubbs and Pierce
Holt and defensive backs Tom
Holmoe and Darryl Pollard in
passing situations, but the no-
huddle concept could make that
difficult.

Walsh is not averse to the
pupil using his knowledge against
the teacher, but like Buffalo Bills
coach Marv Levy, he is concerned
that the Bengals might gain an
unfair advantage by substituting
or huddling deceptively.

“We still have to do some
more research into it,” Walsh
said, “because we're not sure ex-
actly what the ramifications of it
are. But I do think that a game
like this should be played well
within the spirit of the rules as
well as within the rules them-
selves,

“We're not trying to pass peo-
ple with yellow flags here. We
just feel the spirit of the rules
should be enforced along with
the rules themselves. And we're
confident that will happen.”

Like most successful innova-
tions, Walsh said the no-huddle
offense figures to be used
throughout the league since

“We've used the no-huddle
for reasons of our own in the

haven't made it the focus of any-
thing we’ve done. Maybe we

“When I first saw Sam doing

He's done it just about the entire
time he’s been in coaching. There
were bugs in it initially, so it
would backfire and look funny.
Now that he’s got it going, they
are a step ahead of everyone in
the game.”

If anyone can find a way to
counter the no-huddle offense, it
figures to be Walsh.

“We haven't even thought of

hope right now we're not con-

around lost out there, That thing
is dynamite.”

Still, Walsh is unsure he will

summary: Bermudian Springs coach Dave McCollum discussed his team's victory, highlighting the strategic moments and the effort required to secure the win against their opponents.

By CHUCK CURLEY
Evening Sun Sports Writer

BIGLERVILLE—Before a near-capacity crowd at the Upper Adams Athletic Center, Bermudian Springs head coach McCollum figured that his team had a chance to win.

“I looked over there at Brian Group and Jason Fissel warming up and thought I didn't know how we were going to beat them with them coming out when they did,” McCollum said.

However, Bermudian Springs gained a needed boost from a quick escape.

“You've got to know when and where to go at them,” McCollum said. “I didn’t know if I had the team points or not. It would have been terrible to tie these up after we worked so hard on them. And I think Robert Shockey could do nothing to him on the bottom, so I took him up and took him down!”

The article also mentions a specific match sequence: With the score leading 27-24, the mat went down to Bankert, who was limited to three escapes. The team took a 10-2 lead with 36 seconds left, forcing Shockey to take him down again, leading to a major decision.

title: I must be honest.
headline: Local Officials and Athletes Shine at Area High School Meet
summary: The article reports on the atmosphere of a local high school wrestling championship, praising the officiating of Bartlebaugh and McCollum, before shifting to praise the athletic performance of basketball player Chris Holmes.
cleaned_text:
I must be honest. Since my arrival in Hanover, I'd been wondering if I'd see an area high school wrestling meet in a gymnasium with the rafters filled with passionate fans. That is an atmosphere most familiar to me. Having previously worked in northern Pennsylvania, I was used to the roar of the crowd spoken in the same breath with religion. District IV is an area from where fans bow toward the Hersheypark Arena each day of the PIAA Championships.

I found my element this week at the Upper Adams Athletic Center, where Bermudian Springs upset two-time defending District III champion Biglerville in the key dual meet for the Blue Mountain League championship. All of the elements were there—a loud crowd and a little controversy to incite the home folks.

A technical point call produced a lively ruckus at the championship by head coach Bartlebaugh. Bartlebaugh, who assigns officials in the Mid-Penn Conference, showed his mettle of five PIAA Tournament appearances, taking the microphone to explain both calls to the crowd and then yelling, "Let's wrestle!" That drew applause, and when the meet was over, Bartlebaugh, who also officiated the BML showdown between the teams in 1987, also drew praise from McCollum for his assertive stance.

“He did a great job, no doubt,” McCollum said. “He was on top of it.”

It's a strange sport in that respect. In most sports, the less noticeable officials usually are the most efficient. But when the sole judge is alone on a mat with two competitors in a loud, sprawling arena, the official who takes charge usually does the best job. Bartlebaugh did.

It's a refreshing change when we can see a basketball fever who clearly enjoys himself on the hardwood, even when his team is losing badly. That observation fits Fairfield High School’s Chris Holmes, who combines Tyrone "Mugsy" Bogues’ diminutive stature with Isiah Thomas’ blinding grin. In Tuesday’s junior varsity game, Holmes was particularly pesky, ducking dribblers to nick off the ball and once executed a perfectly designed play to drill a 12-foot rainbow for two points. But, even after tossing a couple of passes away, Holmes was flashing that smile, and when he left with about two minutes to pass, received appreciative applause from the partisan Squire fans for his hustle.
