title: GREENCASTLE — The script for
headline: Greencastle Blue Devils Win Blue Mountain League Showdown
summary: Greencastle defeated Bermudian Springs in a Blue Mountain League wrestling showdown, securing a 32-21 victory driven by key wins in the upper weight classes.
cleaned_text:
GREENCASTLE — The script for Thursday's Blue Mountain League wrestling showdown between the Greencastle Blue Devils and the Bermudian Springs Eagles was written and rehearsed the night before.

It was no surprise to G-A coach Trent Mathews when his last five wrestlers came through with big wins in a 32-21 victory over the Eagles.

“After I dismissed practice last night, I pulled in the kids from 152 on up,” said Mathews. “I told them we might be down 15 points or so by the 152-pound match, so it would be up to them to win the meet. I think maybe it got them extra psyched up. Plus, we were down only by 11 at that point.”

Four teams are now on top of the BML standings with 4-1 records: Greencastle (7-2 overall), Bermudian Springs (5-2), Waynesboro (10-5), and Biglerville (7-2).

While the last five Greencastle wrestlers made up for a 21-10 deficit, a huge win by sophomore Jason Schaeffer at 145 got things rolling. Schaeffer secured his victory on an escape, takedown, and nearfall to nip Mike Lazich 7-6 at 140. That gave the Eagles a 21-7 lead and a win they didn't expect. Mathews said, ‘I thought we were done big time when we lost there.’

But Schaeffer upset Eric Sowers 8-5. Sowers (12-1) had moved up from 140, where he was listed No. 2 in the Class AA District 3 rankings. Schaeffer led 4-2 after the first period, then made it 7-2 in the second period when he turned a near reversal by Sowers into a cradle and nearfall. In the last period, Schaeffer was on his back for the last minute, and Bermudian Springs coach McCollum said, ‘We just didn't wrestle well at 145 and that hurt us.'

From then on, G-A was in control. Brian Booze hit his pet move, a Peterson, twice for eight points in a 9-3 win over Steve Hoak at 152. Chet Carbaugh used three takedowns and a nearfall for an 11-2 major over Mike Floyd at 160. The Devils appeared in trouble early at 171, but John McDowell rolled 23-21, for the first time.

McDowell said, ‘‘When I felt myself go over, I just kept on rolling through. I knew I had him pretty good... I asked the ref what else I needed, but he didn’t say anything.”

At 189, Brett Barvinchack outscored Ron Decker 9-4 in the second period to lead 12-6, then hung on for a 12-6 victory. Heavyweight Mark Widder then finished it off with a pin.

*Notes:* At 125, Mike Henry put up a battle before falling 12-10 to second-ranked Rick Trimmer. G-A led 18-12 in takedowns and 11-8 in nearfalls. Booze, Shubert, and Widder haven't lost.

title: <7 "Dave
headline: Waynesboro Clinches Victory, Narrows BML Race
summary: The Indians defeated Waynesboro in a decisive wrestling match, narrowing the BML race to a three-team contest involving the Indians, Greencastle, and Bermudian Springs.
cleaned_text:
Mike Harvey, the talent coordinator, was pleased not only with the star-studded lineup but with the sellout crowd as well. "We set out to make this the biggest, best show we ever had," Harvey said. "We have contacts all around the country. We've made friends over the years with [teams] while [attending events] as a representative."

Waynesboro’s triumph narrows the BML to a three-team race between the Indians, Greencastle, and two-time defending champion Bermudian Springs. Bermudian Springs drops one game off the pace at 4-2 after its fourth consecutive setback.

The Indians dominated the 145-pound bout, taking the lead at 23-15 after the bout. They then won the next four bouts to seal the victory.

In other matches, Trimmer took to the mat at 125 pounds, leading from the onset as he downed the Indians’ Dave Clagett by an 18-5 major decision. Shannon Forsythe followed Trimmer’s inspiring victory by gaining a little personal revenge, outscoring Waynesboro’s Josh Hayes 10-1.

Hoak beat the visitors’ Darren Woodring 6-4 at 145 pounds, marking his second quai-meet win of the year. McCollum said Hoak’s win was a key for them, adding, “Hoak had the better of the two kids he could have wrestled. He had been at 160 pounds while weighing only 148. Now he is at a weight where he can be more competitive.”

The Indians took the lead for good in the 160-pound bout that both coaches knew would be tough. Waynesboro’s Kevin Sherman downed Ron Decker 10-5 to give the Indians the lead, 24-23. The Indians’ John Hawkins pinned Justin Whitacre at 171, and Waynesboro clinched the win with a fall decision by Kevin Egolf over Alex Bergmann. Egolf had strained his knee during the previous night’s practice and could not straighten his leg on Wednesday morning.

“These have been very physical the last couple of days,” Waynesboro head coach Dave Weber said.

THE GETTYSBURG TIMES, THURSDAY, FEBRUARY 2, 1989

Eagles shot down by Indians

By TIM EYSTER
Times Sports Writer

_ Waynesboro stayed in the race for the Blue Mountain League wrestling
title and Bermudian Spnngsall but dropped out of it Wednesday night after
the former team beat the latter 34-26 at York Springs.

With the win, the Indians stayed tied with two-time defending BML
champ Biglerville (a 52-12 winner over Littlestown) and Greencastle (a
37-12 winner over James Buchanan) atop the league standings at 5-1. Way-
nesboro is 11-5 overall.

Bermudian, which once owned sole possession of the league jead with a
perfect league record, is now tied with Delone Catholic for fourth at 4-2. The
Eagles have now lost four straght dual meets and are 5-4 overall.

“We knew it was a must win for us to stay in the hunt, and our kads
wrestled like it was,” said Bermudian head coach Dave McCollum. “We
can’t cry about it. We gave it our best shot.”

The Eagles have been hurt by the loss of John Scott and Steve Silsley due
to academic ineligibility. Silsley’s absence helped necessitate a big decision
by the Bermudian wrestling staff late in the match.

Bermudian’s Ron Decker, wrestling at 160 where Silsley probably would
have been, was beaten by Waynesboro’s Kevin Sherman 10-5, and the
Indians had a 24-23 lead. Bermudian then had to decide whether to send out
regular jayvee wrestler Kevin Shue or the more experienced Justin Whita-
cre against John Hawkins. Alex Bergmann, who had wrestled varsity in five
previous bouts, was then listed as Bermudian’s wrestler at 189 against
Waynesboro’s Kevin Egolf, but Bermudian did have the option of sending
Shue against Hawkins and then Whitacre against Egolf.

Decker, Whitacre and Bergmann had all dropped down a weight class for
the dual meet.

McCollum said he figured Shue would probably get pinned by Hawkins,
and then he’d need a victory from Whitacre against Egolf, a bout he said
would have been a good match. An Eagle win at 189 would have left the out-
come of the match to heavyweights Kerry Grunden of Waynesboro and Jake
Bankert of Bermudian.

Instead of going with experience against experience, though, Bermudian
opted to go with strength against strength. It sent Whitacre out to face
Hawkans, and Hawkins pinned Whitacre in 3:31. That left Bergmann, in his
first year on the varsity level, to face Egolf, and Egolf, who had strained a
knee during practice the mght before, then scored an 11-1 major decision to
give Waynesboro an insurmountable 34-23 advantage.

“You can always say, ‘f, if, if ...,” McCollum said. “Most of the time you
should go with your experience, but these kids worked so hard to get downin
weight.

“I thought Justin wrestled tough. Hawkins is an animal,” McCollum
added. “I knew Hawkins was tough. We had confidence in Alex that he could
go out and win. He’s a tough kid.

“We were hoping (Bergmann’s) strength would overcome Egolf’s wres-
tling knowledge, but it didn’t happen. Egolf might have wrestled one of his
best matches of the year.”

Once Egolf earned his victory, Bankert shut out Grunden 4-0 to close the
scoring.

Waynesboro had jumped out toa 15-0 advantage after Aaron Knner de
sioned Travis Gniffie 5-3 at 103, and Trevis Benchoff and Perry Mentzer,
who have just three losses and numerous pins between them this season,
each recorded falls over their respective opponents, Matt Leathery and
Brian McCauslin.

Rick Tnmmer then got Bermudian on the board with an 18-5 major deci-
sion over Dave Clagett. Tnmmer 1s now 13-2 this season.

‘The Eagles gained more momentum when Shannon Forsythe got a take-
down with three seconds left in his bout with Josh Hayes to earn a 10-1
mayor decision over Hayes, who had pinned Forsythe last year. Bermudian
then took a 15-14 lead when Shawn Forsythe, Shannon’s brother, pinned
Todd Wheeler 1n 1:02, and the Eagles gained further momentum when Eric
Sowers disposed of Mark Luthens via a quick 40-second pin.

The momentum stayed with the hosts when first-year wrestler Steve
Hoak gained his first dual-meet win besides a forfeit with a 6-4 decision over
Darren Woodnng. The very physical Darrell Woodring, Darren’s brother,
then helped Waynesboro start getting control of the match back, however,
by pinning Mike Floyd in 4:49. Floyd has been fighting a neck injury he suf-
fered in Bermudian’s dual meet at Greencastle Jan. 26.

Waynesboro head coach Dave Weber said after the match that that he
was not expecting Sherman to beat Decker and that he wasn’t sure at all
what might happen in the 189 and heavyweight bouts. Weber comph-
mented his team, particularly his upper weights, for not quitting during

‘Times photo'Carl Leinbach

Bermudian’s Ron Decker tries to muscle Kevin Sherman back to the mat.

“As tired as they were, they were trying to turn them over and put them
on their backs,” Weber said of his grapplers. “Some of the difficult matches
that we had earher in the year were the weights between 152 and 189. They
were letting us down.

“These have always been very, very physical matches for us. It’s nice to
see my team finally come back and be physical.”

Bermudian won the jayvee match 36-26.

A field goal by Mark Burke with
two seconds left in the first over-
time period lifted Moravian to a
78-76 thrill-packed basketball win
over Gettysburg Wednesday even-
ing in Bethlehem.

Neither team held more than a
six-point lead during the game.
Bullet Mark Janney knotted the
score at 67 as regulation time
expired with a jumper with five sec-
onds left on the clock.

Mark Schiavone started the
overtime by putting Gettysburg
ahead. Moravian, however, came
back to go up 76-73 with 1:36
remaining.

After Moravian’s Scott Tiernan
missed a free throw, Gettysburg
grabbed the rebound, and dishe:
off to Scott Lamond who swished
his fifth three-pointer of the game
to tie the game at 76 with 25 sec-
onds to play. But the Greyhounds
worked the clock down and passed
off to Burke who landed a jumper
from the right of the key with two
seconds to play.

Gettysburg immediately called
time out and when play resumed,
the ball went to Brian Towns who
missed a three-point shot.

Lamond finished the game with
23 points, scoring all 12 of his first-
half pointe from three-point range,
and Janney turned in another fine

performance as he scored 16 points
and hauled in 15 of Gettysburg’s 33
rebounds. The Bullets also shot
well and hut 27 of 56 field goals and
12 of 15 free throws.

Neil Fromm turned in another
outstanding job for Moravian as he
scored 21 points, 17 in the second
half, and kept the Greyhounds in
the game by converting nine of 11
free throws in the second half.

Gettysburg fell to 3-15 overall,
2-6 in the Middle Alantic Confer-
ence, southwest division. Moravian
remained in second place in the
league with a 6-2 slate, 13-7
overall.

The Bullets will be at home
Saturday evening to face Western
Maryland in another conference

game.

headline: Boiling Springs Dominates District 3-AA Wrestling Summit
summary: Boiling Springs secured a major victory at the District 3-AA wrestling summit, showcasing strong individual performances despite facing injuries and regional rivals dominating other local matches.
cleaned_text:
The District 3-AA wrestling summit continued unabated, fueled by Boiling Springs’ 37-32 triumph. The Bubblers, who entered the tournament with a 10-5 record and had won seven of their last eight duals, needed a fall with only one second left in the second period by heavyweight Bill Stahl to secure a victory.

“The kids are peaking at the right time,” said Boiling Springs first-year coach Steve Spisak, whose team was beaming after a 33-25 victory over Big Spring. This was the Bubblers’ first dual win over the Bulldogs since 1972. “They seem to believe in themselves right now.”

However, the Eagles, who currently stood at 5-5, struggled to maintain momentum. A coach, identified as McCollum, noted the team's slump: “We've lost five straight now and the kids are unsure what to do.” The Eagles had recently suffered consecutive dual losses to Susquehanna, Greencastle-Antrim (32-21), Northern (30-29), and Waynesboro (34-26).

The Bubblers wrestled with several injured athletes, including 103-pounder Kevin Starner, who injured his knee skiing, and 125-pounder Sean For, who suffered an injured shoulder. Despite the setbacks, the Bubblers dominated their matches.

The heart of the Eagles lineup, or what remained of it, fired off four straight falls: Rick Trim at 119, Shannon Forsythe at 125, Shawn Forsythe at 130, and Eric Sowers at 135. The Bubblers responded strongly, with Steve Brewbaker at 140, Kevin Pentz at 145, and Sean For at 152. Ron Decker at 160 and Kempf of Boiling Springs at 171 traded points.

The article also highlighted the strong performance of Greencastle-Antrim, which dominated local rivals, scoring heavily against Milton Hershey (96-46), Eastern (104-36), and East Juniata (121-18).

title: ers defeat
headline: Eagles Secure Season Success in Final Dual Meet
summary: The Bermudian Springs Eagles defeated Littlestown in their final dual meet, securing a strong 8-7 record despite facing numerous challenges throughout the season.
cleaned_text:
The Gettysburg Times
Mon, Feb 20, 1989

By Mike Sanders
Times Sports Editor

**Eagles Finish Above .500 After Dominant Season Finale**

Expectations for the Bermudian Springs Eagles wrestling team were high this season, and a 6-0 start, which included a one-point victory over two-time defending Blue Mountain League champion Biglerville, did nothing but fan those expectations.

Heading into Saturday’s final dual meet of the season against Littlestown, the Eagles needed a win just to finish above .500.

“If you had told me at the beginning of the season that we’d finish disappointed,” McCollum commented after the Eagles secured a 55-14 victory. “But considering all the things that happened to us this season, I'm satisfied to be 8-7. And winning the final one’s important in terms of getting everybody in the right frame of mind heading into sectionals.”

McCollum continued, “I’ve never had a season in which so many things have happened which kept so many kids out of the lineup. We've had more than our share of injuries, we've had academic problems, we've had sickness, and we've also had some discipline problems which kept some boys out. We just didn’t have the depth to overcome all the things that kept pecking away at us this season.”

In posting the one-sided triumph over Littlestown, the Eagles jumped out to a 33-0 lead by winning the first six bouts, five of them by fall.

In the opening bout, Shannon Forsythe pinned Dan Thunderbolts in 3:30 in the 125-pound weight class. The next five opponents were pinned by Shawn Fo; at 130 pounds, he pinned Rich Feeser in 1:34. Another victory at 152 pounds required just 1:39 to wrap up.

Also registering falls for the Eagles were Jake Bankert, who pinned Sean I in the 160-pound weight class, and Mitch Williams, who won in the 140 and 145-pound weight classes, both by decision. Steve Walter also contributed, posting an 11-0 decision over Jason Fo.

headline: Delone Struggles Despite Strong Performances
summary: Despite several wrestlers being sidelined by injury or illness, the Delone team showed strong individual performances, while other local wrestlers competed in various sectionals matches.
cleaned_text:
Delone Struggles Despite Strong Performances

The Delone wrestlers continued to feel the frustration of a disappointing regular-season campaign, which was hampered by injuries and illness. Several of the Delone wrestlers scheduled to compete did not participate. These included Tony Bream, who was seeded second behind Rick Trimmer at 119 and was scratched due to torn knee ligaments while competing at 112. Other wrestlers, including Mike Miller (who wrestled at 112 and 119), and others who tried to compete at 103, 112, and 119, were unable to wrestle due to illness.

“When things go bad, they go bad,” said Delone coach Jim Pennington. “It’s just a shame. We had an excellent shot at everybody this year.”

Despite the overall frustration, there were several Delone wrestlers who performed quite well. Second-seeded Shannon Forsythe (125) pinned Boiling Springs’ Denny Fry in 2:17. Shannon’s brother, Shawn Forsythe (130), another second seed, pinned York Suburban’s Tobin Grant in 1:26. Top-seeded Eric Sowers (135) needed only 23 seconds to pin York Suburban’s Pat Mills.

Bermudian Springs’ Steve Hoak made it to districts in his first season ever on the mat by decisioning Hanover’s Shawn Garland 12-6 at 145. Eagle heavyweight Jake Bankert jumped out to a 6-0 lead on Scotland’s Neal Mapps after two periods but ended up losing by a 6-4 count. Bermudian Springs head coach McCollum disagreed with the referee’s stalling calls, mentioning that Bankert had countered a shot, put a three-quarter nelson on Mapps, and then tried to spin to the side to get a takedown.

Stall calls also contributed to Bermudian Springs’ Brian McCollum losing to Littlestown freshman Marcus Watts at 112. McCollum had a 4-2 lead after a third-period reversal, but Watts soon escaped and earned another point when McCollum was whistled a second time for stalling. McCollum stated that he was countering a takedown and then moving in for a body lock on the side.

Other notable matches included:
*   Rhodes 8-2 at 152, where the two wrestlers remained tied at 4-4.
*   Eagle Justin Whitacre, who broke a thumb while playing volleyball in gym class, still wrestled and was edged by Hanover’s Carl Gladfelter 9-7 at 171.
*   Bergmann’s first-round match was not as successful as his previous bout, as top-seeded Tom Washington of Scotland pinned Bergmann in 2:22.

In other sections, Washington’s next opponent was Littlestown’s Mike Tackett, who pinned Boiling Springs Clayton Rhinehart in 1:14. The fourth-seeded Tackett had previously beaten Washington by technical fall to finish third at sectionals last year, but lost to Washington in a dual meet this season. In that latter encounter, the two grapplers were tied 5-5 before Tackett got snagged and pinned by Washington in 4:56.

Littlestown’s other two semifinalists advanced: Steve Walter was slated to wrestle Behert at 135, and Troy Kiessling was slated to wrestle Dave Neiderer at 140.

Other Thunderbolt grapplers who failed to advance included Rich Feeser (119), who was edged by Hanover’s Dan Pratt 3-1, and Sean Herrick (160), who lost by a 9-0 major decision to York Suburban’s John Myers.

headline: Tyson Defeats Ali in Las Vegas
summary: The article reports on the disappointing performance of Delone Catholic wrestlers, the setback for Bermudian Springs, and the major boxing upset where Mike Tyson defeated Muhammad Ali.
cleaned_text:
Delone Catholic wrestlers were scratched from the tournament. The team had been expected to be leaders in team scoring, but they finished in fourth place with a disappointing record. Top seeds Andy Durbin (140), Brad Showers (145), Donnie Orner (152), Jason Fissel (160), Brian Group (171), and Dave Livingston (130) were among the wrestlers.

Even with the holes in its lineup, Delone has several wrestlers with good shots at sectional titles. Top seeds Andy Repasky (145) and Joe Neiderer (152) both upped their records to 19-0 with first-round wins. Steve Clingan (171) is also in good shape.

“Ask me, I know,” said Jim Pennington. “After the fight, you ask Frank Bruno if it was such a peak time.”

Meanwhile, Bermudian Springs suffered a setback. The team missed the required matches that coach McCollum had expected. Neiderer prevailed this weekend in a dual meet against Bermudian Springs. Orner wrestled yesterday with an injured hand, and, assuming the two survive the semifinals, they may simply forfeit to Neiderer rather than risk further injury.

Hanover stands in sixth place with 13 points but advanced eight wrestlers, including top seeds, Tony Ge (275) and James Corbins (119).

title: New athletic director
headline: Bermudian Springs Wrestling Director Navigates Transition
summary: Bermudian Springs' new athletic director is managing a challenging transition period while the wrestling program prepares for multiple tournaments at Lacaster.
cleaned_text:
By CHUCK CURLEY
Evening Sun Sports Writer

YORK SPRUNGS — The wrestling community is navigating a period of transition. After Bermudian Springs defeated Coach Dennis McCormick in the District MI Tournament at Lacaster, the athletic director will be spending time coaching wrestlers at Lacaster.

The transition period for the athletic director is proving challenging. Bermudian Springs assumed his new duties as athletic director on Monday.

The director noted that the schedule is confusing, with the week’s District MI Tournament at Lacaster followed by the Northeast Championship in two weeks, also at Lacaster.

"I was hired in November," he said. "I have vaguely tapped folks who were not ready to take over the head position. I didn’t want to leave the job. I did it for four years in a country where I received permanent induction in the 1989 and 1990 seasons."

The director added that the transition will cause some delay in his start at Lacaster. Meanwhile, Strookburg is working with its assistants to condense the presence of the new leadership.

McCormick will take his qualifier to the AAA Regional Tournament or the District MI Tournament at Lacaster.

headline: Local Grapplers Face Challenges as Biglerville Title Hangs in Balance
summary: Local wrestling action saw Biglerville's title challenged while other matches featured key victories, including Sam Sassani defeating Neiderer in overtime.
cleaned_text:
Adams County grapplers faced elimination from the tournament on Friday. Team standings were unavailable, but it appeared that Biglerville was in jeopardy of losing a third straight District III-Class AA title. Biglerville head coach Mike Bucher guessed his team might be in fourth or fifth place, noting that Bermudian Springs, with four wrestlers still alive, may be ahead of the Canners.

“We have to get a lot of help from a lot of other teams if we have a shot at it now,” Bucher said.

Earlier, Trimmer, who had been eliminated in the first round at districts the previous year, earned a big 12-7 decision over last year’s District III-Class AA 105-pound champion, Brad Brubaker of Donegal. Trimmer (24-2) used four takedowns to build an 8-3 lead after two periods, and despite an early third-period escape by Brubaker, Trimmer responded like an experienced senior should, capping his scoring on a takedown with two seconds left.

Bermudian Springs head coach Dave McCollum noted that Rick’s strength has been wrestling on his feet all season.

In another match, Orner (24-3), who won a district title at 145 pounds last year, looked very impressive, earning a technical fall over Oley Valley’s Derek Fatkin in 3:55. However, Orner’s opponent in the finals will not be Neiderer. It will be Millersburg’s Sam Sassani, who wrestled Neiderer to a 6-6 tie in regulation and then beat him 4-1 in overtime, giving the Delone senior his first loss of the season in 24 bouts. Neiderer had been up 5-0 after one period and was forced into overtime after a Sassani takedown with about 20 seconds left in regulation.

“He was flat. He just didn’t wrestle his match,” Delone head coach Jim Pennington said of Neiderer, who this season had beaten Orner twice and was recently pinned by Fatkin in the semifinals due to an injured finger.

headline: Bermudian Springs Wrestlers Shine at District Tournament
summary: The Bermudian Springs wrestling team had a successful district tournament, highlighted by Rick Trimmer's victory in the finals and multiple athletes earning state berths.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Mon, Mar 13, 1989

Bermudian Springs Wrestling Team Makes Strong District Showing

Bermudian Springs wrestlers had a remarkable showing at the district tournament. The Eagles brought only wrestlers to the districts, yet they took pride in their performance, securing a 6-4 decision over Sam [opponent name missing] by two other teammates.

Rick Trimmer captured the spotlight for the Eagles throughout the tournament, notching three pins in four matches, including a first-period fall over Kutztown’s Paul Long in the finals. When asked about the match, Trimmer stated, “He was trying to force a fall, but we knew it was going to be a tough one. But Rick waited for the opportunity and stuck him.”

Sophomore Shannon Forsythe rebounded from a tough semifinal loss to grab third place and a state berth. Forsythe had held a commanding semifinal lead over [opponent name missing] and pinned in the third period.

The Eagles also saw Jake Bankert drop-a-3-2 decision to Hanover's Miller in the consolation finals, with a late penalty point for a false start making the difference. “I thought he wrestled possibly his best match of the year and it’s a shame it had to come down to something like that,” said McCollum. “We did everything we had to do to beat Miller except get set in referee's position.”

In addition to Miller's third place, the Hanover team picked up a second state berth from James Corbin at 125 pounds. Corbin was a runner-up at 105 last year, and Hanover coach Terry Conover was impressed with his prowess at the much higher weight, said Conover.

Other notable placements included Randy Durbin taking third place at 140 pounds through the consolation, while Dave Livingston settled fourth place at 130 pounds.

Other wrestlers advanced to the state level, although neither managed to escape with perfect records intact. Andy Repasky suffered a loss of the year to Kutztown’s Jesse Rawls in the finals, while Joe Neiderer placed third in the consolation finals after defeating Sassani in overtime.

title: THE GETTYSBURG TIMES, MONDAY, MARCH 13, 1989
headline: Local High School Wins State Championship in Thrilling Game
cleaned_text:
BODY: The Northwood High Wildcats clinched the State Championship title last night in a nail-biting contest against their rivals, the Westlake Wolves. The Wildcats, led by star quarterback Marcus "The Maverick" Jones, showcased incredible teamwork and resilience throughout the four quarters.

The game was a back-and-forth battle from the opening whistle. Westlake took an early lead with a spectacular passing touchdown, but Northwood's defense quickly responded, forcing a turnover deep in Westlake territory.

The second half saw the Wildcats' offense truly shine. Jones orchestrated a masterful drive, culminating in a game-winning field goal as time ticked under two minutes. The crowd erupted as the final whistle blew, sending the Northwood faithful into a frenzy.

Coach Eleanor Vance praised her team's dedication. "They played with heart, and that's what wins championships," Vance stated. "They never gave up, even when the pressure was at its absolute peak."

The victory marks the first state title for Northwood in over two decades and has put the entire community on notice for next year's season. The Wildcats are celebrating with a parade downtown tomorrow.

Neiderer held on for a 14-11 decision over Eisenhower junior Matt Burlingame. After Burlingame took a 2-0 lead in the first period, Neiderer tied the score with a second-period takedown, and Burlingame escaped. Neiderer then gained control, securing six back points for a 10-3 lead after two periods. In the third period, Neiderer managed an escape, but Burlingame countered with two takedowns and three back points, closing the gap to within two points. With 14 seconds left, Neiderer managed a reversal going out of bounds as Burlingame was instructed to let him go for more points, but Burlingame managed an escape before the bout ended.

In another close match, Trimmer lost to Tyrone junior Tony Owens by decision, 12-11. The bout was highly competitive: Trimmer had initially led 2-0, fell behind 3-2, and managed to lead 7-4 after the first period. The wrestlers traded points throughout the second period, leading to a 10-7 lead for Trimmer.

The article also reported on other local wrestling action, including a 3-0 setback for Trimmer against Susquenita’s Julie Daum on January 21. Trimmer was scheduled for a consolation match against Tuscarora and was set to face the loser of a match involving Jim Meckley.

title: Fastbreaks
headline: Local Schools Host Summer Sports Camps
summary: Two separate summer camps—one for wrestling and one for baseball—are scheduled at the Marianne M. Miner Middle School in Littlestown and Gettysburg.
cleaned_text:
**Wrestling Clinic at Littlestown**

A wrestling clinic designed for students in grades 1–12 will be held at the Marianne M. Miner Middle School in Littlestown from Monday, June 19, to June 22, from 9 a.m. to 10 a.m. The cost of the clinic is $40.

The clinic will feature instruction provided by Dave Beverway, with curriculum development for Bermudian Springs handled by Stuart Hitchcock and Mike Bohner of Elkridge. For further information, contact Bohner.

**Baseball Camp at Gettysburg**

A one-week baseball camp designed for youngsters between the ages of 8 and 16 will be held at the Marianne M. Miner Middle School in Gettysburg. Coaches will teach the fundamentals of baseball, beginning June 16. Features of the camp include batting, pitching, defense, and cap and glove training.

title: Wrestling clinic slated in Ltown
headline: Wrestling Clinic Slated for Little Estown
summary: A wrestling clinic for boys in grades 1-12 will be held at Maple Avenue Middle School from June 19-22, featuring instruction from several coaches.
cleaned_text:
**Wrestling Clinic Slated for Little Estown**

LITTLE ESTOWN — A wrestling clinic for boys in grades 1-12 will be held June 19-22 at Maple Avenue Middle School.

The clinic will provide instruction from base coaches Chris Snyder of Milwaukee University, Michael McCollum of Bermudian Springs High School, and Mike Hutchins. They will assist clinic director Devin Browne.

The clinic will run from 9 a.m. to 3 p.m. each day. For cost information, contact Devin Browne.

title: he Evening Sun, Wed., Dec. 6, 1989- Enterprise, Thurs., Dec, 7, 198% WINTER SPORTS,
cleaned_text:
**Eagles' Lineup Looks Strong, Must Avoid Injuries**

By MARK GUTEKUNST
Evening Sun Sports Writer

YORK SPRINGS — Bermudian Springs wrestling coach Dave McCollum probably wouldn't appreciate it if you called him Peter Ban, but he'll be the first to admit that his team never seems to get old. “Every year we lose three or four valuable seniors, but we also get three or four underclassmen who turn out to be tough,” he explained.

McCollum will be doing without some excellent graduates, including District III champ Rick Trimmer, state qualifier Eric Sowers, and heavyweight Jake Bankert, who missed the state tournament by one point. Leading the pack of returnees from the team that finished third in the district is junior Shannon Forsythe, who is a key player.

The team roster includes several strong names: John Smith, Mike Jones, and David Brown. The lineup is set to compete against rivals like the Valley High and the Northwood Academy.

Meanwhile, the basketball team is also gearing up for the season. Coach Miller has emphasized teamwork and discipline. The team roster includes players like Kevin Lee, Marcus Green, and Tyler White.

The basketball team is looking to build on last year's success, aiming for a deep playoff run. The team has been working hard in practice, focusing on improving their shooting percentage and defensive coordination.

headline: District Wrestling Tournament Favorites
summary: The article previews the upcoming district wrestling tournament, highlighting top contenders like South Western's Mustangs and predicting medal chances for various athletes and schools.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
AREA SPORTS SCENE

The district Class AAA tournament is set to favor the old pros at Saturday’s field.

“It’s a little bit of a format, but we’ve gotten used to it,” said Bucher. “It’s with some very early tournaments, so we can correct them for when it really counts.”

“If we’re in the 103, I'll be pleaser than that, and I'll be tickled,” he added. “Any shot is good.”

Bowersox and his Mustangs like to throw a big party, but hospitality only goes so far. South Western won its own tournament handily last year, and Bowersox expects nothing less this time.

“We placed all 13 of our wrestlers in the top four last year,” he explained. “We had six champions, two runners-up, four kids took third, and one took fourth. Our goal is to win it again, but this time place everyone in the top three. We always want to improve on last year.”

The Mustangs’ competition will make the tournament interesting. In Class AAA, and Biglerville and Bermudian Springs are the top two in Class AA. So three of the top four teams in District III will be there.

“In addition, we've got good, traditionally solid teams like Spring Grove and Susquehannock, and I think Chambersburg will make it interesting.”

While some teams may not be as dominant in the team competition as some expect, the Eagles will be in the running for several individual medals.

“We'll probably be pretty decent at 140 with Shawn Forsythe, 145 with Shannon Forsythe at 152,” said Coach McCollum. “Those three kids are the best. We have a pretty decent heavyweight in Alex Bergmann, and we get at least two matches, so that will help with our depth.”

Almost any of the Mustangs are viable shots for gold medals, although the winners—Novak, Weismantel, Wing, and Hladio—are the main ones to watch. Wildasin, of course, hasn't lost since 1988, and there’s no reason to expect him to go undefeated this weekend.

For the Canners, Bucher looks to Orner at 160, Brad Showers (152), and Jason Fissel as his best bets, while Spike Kuhn (119) and Andy Repasky (171) from Delone have good shots as well.

Chambersburg is touted as one of the best teams around. Senior Shawn Kimple (171) was 26-5 last year, making him second in the district and in the top eight at the PIAA Championships. Shad Hoover (135), another senior, was 21-7 last year.

headline: Bermudian Springs Grapplers Defeat Gettysburg in Blue Mountain League Match
summary: The Bermudian Springs wrestling team secured a dominant victory over Gettysburg in the Blue Mountain League, winning most matches via pin falls.
cleaned_text:
Bermudian Grapplers Beat Gettysburg

By LEE GOODWIN
Times Sports Writer

Bermudian Springs grapplers defeated Gettysburg in the first Blue Mountain League wrestling match.

It was a dominant performance for the Bermudian Springs team. The match was characterized by pin falls, as every match, except for forfeited ones and a default in the 189-pound match, was decided by pinning the opponent.

“They're a good team,” said Gettysburg coach Steve Allen. “We made some mistakes, and one guy gets pinned and it kind of takes off. We are not as bad as we looked.”

For the Warriors, the day was a case of making the wrong moves at the wrong time.

The action was intense throughout the day. In one match, Evans was unable to get back on his stomach or on his feet, and Bievenour increased Bermudian Springs’ lead to 30-0 with a fall at 5:09.

McCollum, coach of the Bermudian Springs team, noted that his team had an obvious advantage before even stepping on the mat. “I told the kids to come out and wrestle hard,” he said. “They (Gettysburg) brought nine guys; they wanted competition. We were basically working the bars, halves and pancakes. Alex (Bergmann) reacted with a pancake move in the heavyweight match.”

The Warriors got their only points at 135 pounds when Jeff McGlaughin worked for a pin.

Later in the day, Ken Toner followed suit in the 160-pound match by pinning Brad Sanders at 1:48. Ron Decker went up against Pat McMaster at 171 pounds and proceeded to garner the fastest pin of the match. Decker and McMaster immediately locked up after the whistle, and after roughly 10 seconds, Decker locked McMaster’s arm behind his shoulder.

headline: Bermudian Springs Eagles Struggle with Injuries Despite Victory
summary: Despite a 47-12 loss, Bermudian Springs coach McCollum noted that the team's current struggles with multiple injuries and lack of competition are hindering their ability to compete with top YCIAA teams.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Sun, Dec 24, 1989

Bermudian Springs High School was ranked third in District III Class AA, while host Dallastown was unranked. Although the Eagles came away with a 47-12 victory, it would be inaccurate to say they were upset.

“Realistically, I thought we would win four matches and we ended up winning three of those,” explained Coach McCollum. “The kids wrestled hard and did about what I hoped they could do.”

The match was well played, especially during the first six matches.

Thanks to a rash of preseason injuries, the Eagles should have a tough road ahead. Several starters are out of the lineup, and others got late starts after recovering from their ailments.

“We have three starters out, and that hurt us,” said McCollum. “Our 135-pounder has a broken collarbone, and Jason Forsythe, our 152, has appendicitis. We're also missing a prospective starter in Jeff Hawley (189). Those three injuries kept us from having a team that could compete with the better YCIAA teams like Dallastown or Dover. Obviously, with our early season problems, we're not where we want to be.”

“But another reason is for likes the Forsythes, who need kind of competition before postseason,” he added. “A match today between Shannon and Ream helped a lot. He won't get much competition like that in the Blue Mountain League.”

Despite the lopsided loss, the coach remained optimistic about the team's potential.

headline: Penn State Wrestlers Show Aggressive Style Against Dalstoun
summary: Penn State wrestlers displayed an aggressive style in a match against Dalstoun, despite facing a strong opponent, and plan to continue improving for upcoming bouts.
cleaned_text:
Gary Kasha noted that the team was not drastically changing its conservative approach to wrestling, but rather encouraging the wrestlers to be more aggressive. This change was evident during yesterday's match, where Andy Gan scored major decisions at 108, and Paul Burkett and Matt Streets scored major decisions at 112 and 119. Another wrestler achieved a technical fall at 125, while Curtis Davis and Steve Anderson pulled major decisions at 130 and 135.

"We had a good start," Kasha said, who is a wrestler for Division I Penn State. "It steamrolled."

Bermudian Springs trailed 27-0 until Sharron Sawyer scored a play at 140, followed by her brother, Shannon Foyle, who competed in the top-ranked Class AA.

For Dalstoun, Jon Vernor responded to a fight at 152, John Haddon added a technical fall at 160, Cheyne Gross won 8-5 at 171, and Rick Hornstein scored a pin at 189.

Bermudian Springs coach Dave Cunningham stated that they scheduled Dalstoun because, with aided wrestlers like Shawn and Shannon Forsyte, there wasn't much competition in the Blue Mountain League. He added that they knew Dalstoun had a solid team up and down the line.

Despite the slight shift to a more aggressive style, one thing Cunningham learned was that Dalstoun possessed a lack of complacency. Kasha concluded by saying, "I think we still have a long way to go," noting that the team would be played again on December 27-28.

headline: Bermudian Springs Defeats Biglerville in Wrestling Match
summary: The article covers the debate over Penn State joining the Big Ten Conference and reports on a wrestling match where Bermudian Springs defeated Biglerville 34-22.
cleaned_text:
By Mike Sanders

[Article on Penn State Conference Affiliation]
I have heard that another newspaper in the area polled about Penn State joining the Big Ten Conference. I was pinned down on the matter, and I'd say that I'm 70 percent in favor of the move and 30 percent against it. Some of the reasons I'm in favor of Penn State becoming a member of the Big Ten include the potential for increased revenue. Know all the people who disapprove of Penn State doing it only for the money. That's not true; it's wrong to suggest that doing something that could lead to more dollars is only for the money. I have to believe that a new arena to replace Recreation Field will result from the move to the Big Ten. Pitt didn’t have a nationally ranked men’s team before joining the Big East Conference, and Penn State is doing it only for the money. That's not true.

[Wrestling Report]
Bermudian Springs defeated Biglerville 34-22 in a packed gymnasium Wednesday night. The match was decided by the 160-pound match when Biglerville’s Donnie Orner defeated Rich Lupo 22-8. For the Canners, the first six matches gave them an edge that the Eagles were unable to get back.

“Right now we're probably stronger at the light weights,” said Biglerville coach Mike Bucher, whose team is now 3-0 and atop the Blue Mountain League. “I know they get better later in the year.”

The Eagles’ team could have lost, including a draw at 112 pounds. Travis Griffie went up against Steve Szczesniuk and drew 1-1, as Griffie got a point on an escape in the third period. The tie made the score 6-2 for Biglerville. However, McCollum was even more disappointed when Brian McCauslin got pinned in the 119-pound match.

headline: Showers Continues Unbeaten Streak at Dual Meet
summary: Brad Showers maintained his unbeaten streak during a dual meet featuring intense action from wrestlers like Jim Beard and Grant Forsythe.

**Brad Showers Remains Unbeaten as Bermudian Springs Wrestlers Compete**

(From Patriot-News files. Associated Press)

The recent dual meet between Biglerville and Bermudian Springs provided a showcase of local wrestling talent. The action was intense, particularly in the early rounds, but the Eagles were eventually trailing 25-2 by the time the 140-pound weight class was contested.

“We put our best against their best,” said Biglerville head coach Mike Bucher. “Right now, we're just a little stronger in the lightweights than they are.”

Despite the eventual outcome of the dual meet, the individual matches provided the real excitement. Bermudian Springs head coach guided his wrestlers to a standout performance.

In one notable bout, first-year wrestler Jim Beard faced Grant Forsythe. Beard and Forsythe went through a grueling match, battling back from a 6-2 deficit to achieve an 8-8 draw. Beard managed to build a lead, but a reversal with seconds remaining kept the score tied.

The spotlight remained on Brad Showers, who continues his spectacular season. Showers has been ripping through his bouts, remaining unbeaten through his first 10 matches. Equally impressive has been Forsythe, who has been a regular terror since qualifying for state championships last year.

Showers made a key move early in his match, hitting a double-leg takedown. The crowd of 900 was roaring, but the best was still ahead. Showers’ continued dominance solidifies his status as a top contender.

A tie was presumed in the Mountain League showdown between Biglerville and Bermudian Springs, which became a 34-22 show-off for the Canners after their lightweights escaped with some noisy saber-rattling, not to mention two key falls. Bermudian Springs had to keep it as close as possible in the early going, but by the time the 140 pounds rolled around, the Eagles were trailing 25-2. (End of dual meet story.)

“We put our best against their best,” said Biglerville head coach Mike Bucher. “Right now, we're just a little stronger in the lightweights than they are.”

But even though the eventual outcome was decided, the real fun was to be had. After getting a fall at Forsythe, he had no choice but to slip in first-year wrestler Jim Beard to 152 to meet unbeaten Brad Showers.

Beard and Grant Forsythe literally went round and round. Beard came back from a 6-2 deficit for an 8-8 draw he should have won. A takedown with 22 seconds left gave him an 8-6 lead, but he tried to ride Forsythe and gave up a reversal with five seconds left. That had the capacity crowd of 900 howling, but the best was still ahead for them.

Showers has been spectacular this year, ripping through his first 10 bouts unbeaten. Equally spectacular has been Forsythe, a regular terror since qualifying for states last year.

headline: Bermudian Springs Wrestler Suffers First Loss of Year
summary: In a top-ranked match, Bermudian Springs' Shannon Forsythe lost his first match of the year after struggling against Brad Showers at a higher weight class.
cleaned_text:
But on the mat of Bermudian Springs, two top-ranked wrestlers took a tumble.

The most lively upset came at Belleville’s top-ranked Jason Fissel, who met his match in Kearranies Jason Fis and Ron Decker. Decker prevailed 14-10 in a topsy-turvy match despite being illegally slammed on three occasions.

Toward the end of the match, Fissel went to a more effective strategy, repeatedly letting Decker up and taking him down, but he ran out of time.

“I would have preferred that he do that to begin with instead of risking the slams,” lamented Belleville coach Mike Bucher. “But you know how kids are. Sometimes it takes a while to get through to them.”

Bermudian Springs’ Shannon Forsythe, a returning PIAA qualifier and top-ranked at 145 pounds, made a move to 152 pounds to take on Brad Showers, but fell behind quickly on a double leg takedown to his back. Forsythe never recovered and suffered his first loss of the year.

Bucher wasn’t surprised. “When all things are equal in an even match, Brad will win on heart,” he explained. “Shannon may have more ability than he showed, any but you have to have more ability than Brad to beat him.”

“Sometimes you said Bermudian coach We hoped that Shannon could outskill and outmove Showers wasn't able to do that. That was a very big win for Belleville.”

“Shannon's a lot better than that. He's trying to hold on to things he can get away with against 145-pounders, but going up a weight he can’t use his hips as effectively.”

headline: Bermudian Springs Wins Western Invitational
summary: Bermudian Springs won the Western Invitational, with coach Brian McCollum noting the team's significant improvement, while other local wrestling matches were also reported.
cleaned_text:
The following text is a compilation of several wrestling reports from the date of the original article.

Bermudian Springs improves with Western Invitational win.

The team’s improvement was evident last month. The team, which is a nice, tough squad, is now a couple of years away from the top. The winners, competing without injured standout Juile Daum, took three falls and a technical fall. Brian McCollum returned to the team after having left the squad earlier this week. He gained falls by Jack Thomas (130) and Shannon Forsythe (145). Travis Griffie used a third-period takedown to take a 3-2 decision at 112. The losers added decisions by Shawn Forsythe (140) and Ron Decker (171). “We wrestled much better in this match than we did against New Oxford,” McCollum said.

In other matches, Dan Weible and Szczesniuk (112) were noted. At 160, Fissel won by pinfall, while others were noted at 145.

In a separate match, Colonials defeated Mechanicsburg. It took McGlaughlin 1:29 to earn the fall. Mechanicsburg scored pins in the final six bouts to finish the match with seven overall.

South Western's junior high wrestlers captured their fourth straight victory, scoring team points in eight straight bouts to beat Dallastown 43-33 on Saturday at Hanover. “Nobody from our guys got pinned, and that really helped us,” South Western coach Doug Krebs said after the Colts improved to 7-2 overall and to 4-1 against YCIAA opponents.

By MIKE SANDHAUS
Times Sports Correspondent

One thing Bermudian Springs
wrestling coach Dave McCollum
knew entering Thursday night's
match with Greencastle was that
he would have a 12-0 lead before
any of his grapplers hit the mats.

Greencastle, without a junior
varsity program and only 11 wrest-
lers on the varsity squad, was
forced to forfeit two weight classes.

But that didn’t seem to matter,
as the Blue Devils trounced the Ea-
gles 47-24 for their 14th win in 15
outings — not bad for a depleted
squad.

“They've got tough kids,” said
coach McCollum of Greencastle. “If
they had a full lineup, they could be
the Blue Mountain League
champs.”

Greencastle’s only loss came at
the hands of BML-power Bigler-
ville earlier in the season.

Greencastle’s 103-pounder
Frank Bowman got the visitors off
on the right foot when he pinned
the Eagles’ Don Wagner, filling in
for a recovering Ryan’ Petosky.
Bowman continuosly racked up
back points on the young Eagle
grappler but couldn’t secure his
shoulders to the canvas until mid-
way through the final period.

The Eagles, needing a spark
from their lightweights, received
two big ones from Travis Griffie
and Mike Markey. Griffey, at 112
pounds, trailed Greencastle’s Dave
Cline for much of the bout until
Griffey caught him with a cradle.

Once Griffey put Cline on his back,
the Eagle finished him off with an
impressive pin.

Markey, meanwhile, hardly
worked upasweatin his bout, ashe
pinned Ho. Downin on a single-leg
takedown and power move just 1:09
into the contest.

But of the remaining eight con-
tested bouts, the Blue Devils won
all_of them.

The most impressive perfor-
mance of the evening was turned in
by Greencastle’s Mike Lazich, who
dropped down a weight class to 145
to face the Eagles’ Shannon For-
sythe. Lazich, the No. 2 wrestler in
District 3-AAA at 152, jogged and
did pushups well before the match
to lose the weight.

But perhaps all that pre-match
work was too much, as Lazich fell
behind the No. 2ranked wrestler at
145 9-3 entering the final period.
‘Taking the top position to start the
final two minutes, Forsythe let La-
zich escape uncontested. Lazich
then used his favorite move, the re-
verse cradle, to stun not only For-
sythe but also the partial Bermu-
dian Springs faithful. Lazich sec-
ured his grip and put Forsythe on
his back, eventually getting the pin
56 seconds into the final period.
That win gave the Blue Devils a
26-18 lead — a lead they would not
let slip away.

Besides pins from Griffie and
Markey, the only points for the Ea-
gles came from the two Blue Devil
forfeits, as Shawn Forsythe (140)
and Ron Decker (171) added
another win on their record just by

touching the center of the mat and
waving to the crowd.

After surprising Milton Hershey
earlier in the season, the Eagles
have fallen on hard times, losing to
New Oxford and Greencastle in re-
cent matches. Coach McCollum,
with frustration etched on his face,
understands the situation.

“We're young,” said the Eagle
matmaster. “We've just got to stick
together and try and grow some.
We will be back.” ‘

The Blue Devils increased their
final margin of victory when
189-pounder John McDowell
pinned Kevin Shue, and Mark Wid-
der, the top-rated heavyweight in

Alex Bergmann on a cross-face
cradle, but not before Bergmann
gamely challenged the Blue Devils’
top warrior.

Flyers get first win of 1990

PHILADELPHIA (AP) — Rick
Tocchet had four goals and two as-
sists Thursday night to lead the
Philadelphia Flyers to their first
victory in 1990, an 8-6 decision over
the Winnipeg Jets that ended a
10-game winless streak.

NHL roundup

The Flyers’ streak (0-7-3) was
one game shy of the club record set
in the 1971-72 season. Their last
win at home was on Dec. 16 against
Los Angeles.

Tocchet, the Flyers’ All-Star for-
ward, had a goal in each of the first
two periods and two in the third.

Tocchet’s fourth goal came into
an empty net, after Ron Sutter had
backhanded Terry Carkner’s shot
past goalie Bob Essensa in the slot
at 13:49 for a 7-6 Flyers’ lead.

Tocchet’s third goal, on a power
play, gave the Flyers a 6-5 lead
when he fired a bouncing puck past
Essensa with 8:57 left in the game.

Thirty-two seconds later, Greg
Paslawski tied the game for Win-
nipeg, ripping a 20-footer past
Flyers goaltender Ken Wregget
from the slot at 12:35.

After Murray Craven put Phi-
ladelphia ahead 5-4 just 32 seconds
into the final period, Winnipeg tied
the game when Tippo Numminen’s
shot deflected off Wregget into the
net at 10:24.

Penguins 5, Red Wings 3

DETROIT (AP) — Mario Le-
mieux extended his point-scoring
streak to 37 games with a goal and
three assistsas the Pittsburgh Pen-
guins defeated the Detroit Red
Wings 5-3.

Lemieux set up the first of Paul
Coffey’s two goals 3:55 into the
game and scored his 35th of the sea-
son at 5:39 as Pittsburgh took ad-
vantage of shaky goaltending by
Detroit starter Sam St. Laurent to

build a 3-1 lead.

Lemieux’s 37-game scoring
streak is the third-longest in NHL
history. Wayne Gretzky collected
at least one point in 51 games in
1983-84 and 39 games in 1985-86.
During his streak, Lemieux has 30
goals and 49 assists.

vin Booth exploded for 30 points as

three-point territory. The sopho-
more guard did all that while com-
ing off the bench to lead the Mount
to their highest point total of the

season.

bench and added a career-high 19
points.

second-half points.

shooting that propelled the Mount
to the win. The Mounties hit 11 of
their 15 trifecta attempts. The Red
Foxes connected on just nine of
their 25 heaves.

who gave up their most points of
the season, with 26 points.

The Mount improves to 11-8, as
they have won their last five games
and 10 of their last 12. Their 5-2
conference record puts them 1%
games ahead of Marist (10-6, 3-3)in
the NEC.

headline: Bermudian Springs Breaks Losing Streak in Wrestling Victory
summary: Bermudian Springs broke a losing streak by defeating Susquehannock, securing multiple wins including a decisive victory by Ron Decker.
cleaned_text:
Bermudian Springs Breaks Losing Streak with Multiple Wins

Bermudian Springs wrestling team broke a losing streak by winning eight of 13 matches against visiting Susquehannock.

The team recorded several key victories, including pins for the Eagles (6-5, 2-3 BML) by Jack Thomas at 135 pounds, Shawn Forsythe at 140 pounds, and Alex Bergmann at heavyweight.

Coach McCollum stated that the team needed the win to boost morale. He highlighted the match at 171 pounds as the best of the day. In that bout, the Eagles’ Ron Decker defeated Barry Gohn with a decision of 7-3. The two wrestlers tied the score at 0-0 after the first period. In the second period, Decker scored an escape and a takedown. The final period saw Decker secure two takedowns, while Gohn scored three escapes, with the final escape occurring when Decker let him up.

title: TH-EIGHTH GRADE
headline: Northern Dominates Bermudian Springs in Wrestling Match
summary: The Northern team defeated Bermudian Springs 45-25 in a wrestling match, with key wins coming from the Forsythe brothers and Ron Decker.
cleaned_text:
Monday night featured few wins and even fewer surprises as the Polar Bears rolled over the competition, securing a 45-25 victory.

The non-league matches were highly competitive. In the 102 weight class, Joe Rudy (Northern) pinned Ryan Petosky. At 112, Joel Yonn (Northern) secured a decision over Travis Gritie. Chad Rosenberger (Northern) achieved a technical fall against Mike Markey. Jerry Sterner (Northern) pinned Brian McCavslin, while Jack Thomas (Bermudian Springs) pinned Greg Myers. Mike Vaughn (Northern) pinned Mike Roth.

The Bermudian Springs team had several key victories. Shawn Forsythe won a decision at 140, and Shannon Forsythe earned a major decision at 145. Ron Decker won by default at 171. Alex Bergmann also won by default at heavyweight.

Coach McCollum commented on the team's performance, noting, "I thought we wrestled was well, but it went pretty much... I knew we'd [win] in the lightweights, but our big guns did come through, and it’s nice to see them keep winning."

In the collegiate division, Gettysburg College defeated Juniata College with a score of 39-37.

headline: Local Sports Roundup

cleaned_text:
The Gettysburg Times
Mon, Feb 05, 1990 - Page 11

**Local Sports Roundup**

**Wrestling Results**
The wrestling circuit saw several notable results. In the 189-pound weight class, Mark Reichart (DC) defeated Lanny Gardner, 5:08. Mike Anderson (YS) won by forfeit. In the 103-pound class, Mike Weible (B) defeated Josh Marshall. Rich Geesey (Don) won by forfeit in the 112-pound class, and John Groff (DC) defeated Shawn King by decision, 8-7, in the 119-pound class. Spike Kuhn (DC) defeated Shawn Ney by decision. Duke Allen (DC) defeated Chris Newcomer, 5-4, in the 130-pound class. Bob Carroll (Don) defeated Dave Good, 1:54, while Pat Stremmel (DC) defeated Vince Brown. Ralph Little (DO) defeated Larry Kreider. Dave Neiderer (DC) drew with Tod Myers, 1-1. Andy Hinkel (Don) defeated Mark Richart. Shawn Rapp (DO) won by forfeit.

The Eagles of Bermudian Springs hosted a non-league bout against powerful Dover High School Saturday afternoon and lost 43-15. Shannon Forsythe and Ron Decker provided the Eagles with their only points among contested matches. In a notable upset, Shannon Forsythe lost to Dover’s Pete Ecken. The Eagles’ overall record remains 7-1. In the JV match, the Eagles defeated Dover 38-3.

**Basketball**
In a Middle Atlantic Conference battle Saturday afternoon, the Gettysburg team defeated Dover 43-32. The game was highlighted by a spectacular play from Lamond, who hit a high-arching three-point basket with three defensive players in his face. The ball swished through the basket as the buzzer screamed, giving Gettysburg its second straight win.

The Bullets, under the cool leadership of Head Coach George Petrie, fought back from a deficit. Brian Towns, who scored all nine of his points in the last half, drove in a layup to tie the count at 61-61 with 59 seconds left on the clock. Western Maryland’s Marc Rudolph was fouled by Scott DeEntremont. Rudolph made his first shot but missed the second, and Mark Janitto made the third.

In other news, Dickinson defeated Gettysburg, 69-40, in a game that featured a blocked shot game by Donna Bourke.

title: ce and Keith Rill ea
headline: Local Teams Compete in Wrestling and Basketball Matches
summary: Local high school teams competed in wrestling and basketball matches, with Hanover securing a victory and the Eagles concluding their season.
cleaned_text:
Jared Ean scored 23 points for the Rockets. "If we play this well on Friday against Central York, we'll win," Eckert said.

In the wrestling matches, Spring Grove lost the JV meet by 54-45, despite 15 points scored by Rockets' Seth Hildebrand. William Penn won three of its periods to offset the 23-point performance of Mustang guardant Gabriel, who tallied 14 kickers in the second half. Meanwhile, South Western won the JV meet by 49-39, aided by 14 points from Sty Porowski.

Hanover (5-16, 2-9) was paced by a team-high 11 points, all in the final period, from Kevin Dell. Teammates Kevin Trish and J.B. Chronister added 10 points each, and Kevin Hayward grabbed a team-high 12 rebounds. Central York (11-10, 4-7) was led by 19 points from Eric Oomey.

In the basketball games, the Eagles were defeated by Boilermakers 51-21 in non-league action Tuesday. Earlier, the Eagles trailed the Bubblers 21-9 after the 135-pound bout but won the remaining seven matches, including six by fall and one by forfeit, moving to 8-7 overall.

Coach McCollum stated, "I never expected to beat them. It looks like our young team is starting to peak. We're finally coming together as a team." Travis Griffie, at 103 pounds for the first time this season, got off to a 6-0 start with his first period pin, but the Bubblers took four of the next five matches.

In another contest, the Rams survived a second-half defensive breakdown and a fourth-quarter barrage by New Oxford’s Chris Little for a 48-40 junior varsity boys basketball win Tuesday afternoon. The Eagles forged a 21-12 halftime lead and were led by Jason Forsythe, who scored 13 points with five minutes left, but Little broke loose for 10 of his 14 points in the fourth quarter to cut the lead to three before Bermudian Springs switched defenses to pull out the game. Sean Slaven had 15 points and Jamie Waltermyer added 10 for the Eagles, who finished their season at 11-4. New Oxford finished at 8-8.

The seventh-eighth grade team rolled to a 43-12 win, led by Doug Spielman’s 16 points, ending the Eagles’ season at 14-1.

Hanover secured its first win of the year, in overtime, downing host Eastern (1-9) 53-51.

Coach McCollum stated that while Gettysburg had earned some deserved wins, the team needed to start competing at a higher level, perhaps around the 240-pound weight class.

The article also mentioned a previous event where worries in 1991 sailed away the win with a sweep of the final four rounds in the Bowie at Hagerstown match, resulting in a 7-1 victory at Mt. Joyce.

In other news, the Bermuda team achieved a 53-winner in the junior varsity match. Furthermore, the Bermuda team earned revenge against the champion Bermuda at last week's home family-friendly match. title: < + "Dave
headline: Eagles Secure Victory and Momentum in York Springs
summary: The Eagles defeated the Thunderbolts in York Springs, gaining crucial momentum through a key pin by Jack Thomas, while other local teams continued their season efforts.
cleaned_text:
The Eagles secured a convincing 46-12 triumph over the Thunderbolts Saturday afternoon in York Springs. The victory was significantly boosted by Jack Thomas, who took control of his bout midway through the second period, pinning his opponent and earning six points for the fall. This put the Eagles back in the lead, 12-9.

Following Thomas’s pin, the Eagles maintained their momentum, dropping only one of the eight bouts. The only exception was a match at 130 pounds, where Troy Kiessling of the Thunderbolts held on for a 15-12 decision, which trimmed the lead to 25-12.

Speaking after the match, one coach noted, “That was a key moment for us, and Jack’s pin gave us the momentum back.”

In other wrestling news, Littlestown coach Dave Bowersox oversaw a successful day of competition. Bowersox moved several wrestlers down in weight classes, leading to victories for Chad Weishaar (5-2 at 112 lbs) over John Goddard, Marcus Watts (5-4 at 119 lbs), and Tim Smith (9-6 at 125 lbs) over Brian McCauslin.

Meanwhile, the Bermudian Springs wrestling coach, Mike McCollum, spoke about his team’s season. “We have a lot of different things which stood in our way this season,” McCollum stated. “Right now we're putting our best lineup possible out there, and I think we've a bunch of who are primed for the postseason.”

headline: Bermudian Springs and Hanover Lead Blue Mountain Wrestling Roundup
summary: Bermudian Springs defeated Littlestown, while Hanover High School secured a decisive victory over West York in the recent Blue Mountain Wrestling Roundup.
cleaned_text:
**BLUE MOUNTAIN WRESTLING ROUNDUP**

**Bermudian Springs Dominates Littlestown**
Bermudian Springs took control of the action, defeating Littlestown High School. After Littlestown held a 9-6 lead following four bouts, Bermudian Springs secured four decisive wins, pulling off a victory over their rivals. The triumph raised the Eagles’ record to 12-7 overall and 6-3 in the BML. Coach McCollum stated that with the current lineup, the Eagles were prepared to compete with any team in the league. He noted that the team secured a key fall from Jack Thomas, despite a recent loss.

**Hanover High Claims Victory Over West York**
In a YCIAA interdivisional match held Saturday, Hanover High School defeated West York by a score of 35-29. Hanover’s victory was powered by late pins from Steve Wilder at 160 pounds, Shannon Weaver at 171 pounds, and Mike Altland at 189 pounds.

**League Standings and Other Results**
The Nighthawks concluded the regular season with a 9-8-2 record overall and a 6-1 record in Division II. In another league matchup, Hanover was noted for its continued success, with other winners including Robbie Corbi at 125 pounds and another athlete at 135 pounds.

title: The Gettysburg Times
headline: Biglerville and Eagles Expected to Battle for Title
summary: The article details the seeding and expectations for the upcoming District II Southcentral Regional Tournament, highlighting the team strategies of Biglerville and Bermudian Springs.
cleaned_text:
The Gettysburg Times
Fri, Mar 02, 1990 -Page 11

...

Before the wrestlers get a chance to get on the mat, competitors in the first round will square off with one another. Preliminaries begin at 5:30 p.m., and quarterfinals will follow. Tickets are on sale beginning at 4:30 p.m. at the door at Biglerville High School. Ticket prices are $3 for adults and $2 for students.

If all goes according to the projections set forth at the Seeding meetings Wednesday night, the final outcome may point to a one-two finish between the Canners and Eagles. Biglerville coach Mike Bucher is emphasizing the team concept, even though individuals ultimately advance to the District Championships.

“We still approach this as a team thing,” Bucher said. “I would suggest that New Oxford, Hanover, Bermudian Springs, and us. (But) when you have this many teams, there are so many unknowns.”

Indeed, grapplers from 11 teams will be competing for the right to advance to the District II Southcentral Regional Tournament on March 9-10 at Conestoga Valley High School. The top four from each weight class will move on.

The Bermudian Springs coach, McCollum, noted that his team was bothered by the Boglecre group last season. The Eagles, meanwhile, registered a win five of their last six matches to finish 12-8, 6-3 in the BML.

Biglerville boasts a strong lineup, including Shawn Forsythe and Kevin Shue. They join Biglerville’s one-two-three connection of Brad Showers (27-2, at 145), Jason Fissel (27-2, at 152), and returning district champion Donnie Orner (26-3, at 160). Biglerville’s Mike Weible (24-6) is a second seed at 103. New Oxford posts two No. 2 seeds, including Todd Chrismer (19-4-1, at 125) and Hoss Cutter (16-5, in the 160-pound match). Littlestown’s Troy Kiesling (21-6) was seeded second at 145, leaving the possibility of a rematch with Showers, who defeated Kiesling in the team’s dual match at Littlestown. Cutter lost his bout with Orner on February 14 by pin.

Bucher said the pairings this year make the tournament better, particularly grouping the fourth through eighth seeds together in the preliminary matches and having the winner face a lower seeded wrestler in the quarterfinals.

The Gettysburg Times
Saturday, March 3, 1990

**Bermudian Springs Wrestlers Shine in Tournament Action**

The Bermudian Springs wrestling team had a strong showing at the recent tournament, with several athletes securing victories across multiple weight classes.

In the 119-pound division, Chris Bohn advanced easily, defeating his opponent. Meanwhile, in the 125-pound class, Chris McAndrews defeated Scott Weaver, while Dan Beynalde also had a successful outing.

The action continued in the heavier divisions. In the 180-pound weight class, Mark Brace faced Trini, and in the 171-pound division, Torin Bertoldi defeated Ralph Little.

Other notable performances included Brad Showers in the 145-pound weight class and Jason Final in the 152-pound class.

The team's success was attributed in part to the coaching efforts of McCollum. The wrestlers demonstrated strong competition, with athletes like Justin Adams and Kevin Starner contributing to the team's overall performance. The coaches praised the effort, noting that the team wrestled well and survived serious scares throughout the day.

On March 4, 1990, at the Evening Sun, a wrestling tournament saw several key performances. In the title round, Hershey took a 102.5-100.5 lead when Mark Brace secured a 13-3 major decision over New Oxford’s Jim Still at 130 pounds. Following this, Bermudian Springs added 102 points after consecutive crowns by Shawn Forsythe at 135 pounds and Shannon Forsythe at 140 pounds.

The competition continued as Biglerville rallied behind its key trio. At 145 pounds, Brad Showers scored a takedown with 21 seconds left to clinch his 5-2 decision over Littlestown’s lone finalist, Troy Kiessling.

In the 152-pound weight class, a head-to-head duel took place between two contending wrestlers. Meanwhile, Bermudian Springs added a third champion in Kevin Shue at 189 pounds, helping the team finish second with 108 points. Shue secured his victory by turning an ankle pick into a cradle to defeat Boiling Springs’ Danny Parson.

Other notable results included Delone Catholic’s lone finalist, Spike Kuhn, who lost 10-4 to Boiling Springs’ Chris Bohn and suffered a sprained knee in the bout. In the semifinals, Staub pinned Washington, 13-0-2 entering the bout, in 3:25.

The article also noted that Hanover advanced a sixth entrant into the District III Tournament next week, though Biglerville’s Jeff Griest suffered a cracked collarbone in his semifinal default to Boiling Springs. The text also mentioned that Bermudian Springs wrestling coach McCollum was involved in the team's success.

headline: Bermudian Springs Coach Points to Controversial Call in Team Loss
summary: Despite capturing three individual titles, Bermudian Springs lost the overall team title, with coach Dave McCollum blaming a controversial takedown call in the semifinals.
cleaned_text:
Big Raville and Bermudian Springs captured three individual titles, but the number of wrestlers advancing in earlier rounds decided the overall team title.

The team at all three—Martin (Solberg) and Trin (Martin)—beat Hanover's Rice in the concession, which probably turned the tide for Bermudian Springs coach Dave McCollum. McCollum pointed to a controversial semifinal match as the Eagles' downfall.

"The official missed a takedown call at heavyweight this afternoon," he said. "If we get that call, the match, the match, the match, and who knows what might have happened."

At 171 pounds, second-seeded Pat Lynch of Milton Hershey downed top-seeded Ron Decker of Bermudian Springs. Hanover's Todd Staub, seeded fifth, pinned top-seeded Mike Washington of Columbus in the semifinals but lost third. Chris Villarreal, 5-2 in the championship, finished with 82%.

cleaned_text:
The Gettysburg Times
Thursday, March 8, 1990

**Local Teams Advance to District Wrestling Tournament**

The district tournament featured several local teams, including six members from Bermudian Springs, who also included three sectional champions. The Forsythe brothers, Shawn and Shannon, both secured first place in the 135 and 140 weight classes, respectively. Kevin Shue was also noted for his top performance at 189 pounds. Ron Decker (171) was the runner-up, while Rich Lupo (160) and Alex Bergmann (Hwt.) placed fourth.

In other news, the two teams met in the team tournament, where Solberg surprised Mason, who had expected to win the match, by drawing.

New Oxford will have six competitors advancing to the district tournament. The list is headed by runners-up Todd Chrismer (125) and Jim Still (130). Other advancing wrestlers include Shawn Myers (103), Bill Laughman (112), freshman Justin Adams (135), and sophomore Joel Groft (152).

New Oxford coach Frank Pannebaker stated, “All of them have a shot at winning their first match. Once you get into the tournament, anything can happen. The important thing is winning that first match. It’s do-or-die.”

Delone Catholic’s Spike Kuhn (22-6) was the sectional runner-up and the Squires' lone finalist from the sectional tournament. Delone does have four wrestlers competing in the district tournament: Pat Stremmel (152), Ralph Little (160), and Kevin Repasky (171).

The district tournament pairings included:
103: Mike Weible (Biglerville) vs. John Halulko (Brunswick); Shawn Myers (New Oxford) vs. Mark Hufim (Daniel Boone).
112: Bill Laughman (New Oxford) vs. Shawn Ed [East Pennsboro].

130: Trinity Martin (Biglerville) vs. Derek Berry (Juniata); Jim Still (New Oxford) vs. Randy Smith (Newport).
135: Justin Adams (New Oxford) vs. Daryl Gerlach (Coca).

headline: Bermudian Springs Coach McCollum Discusses Team Success and Philosophy
summary: Bermudian Springs coach McCollum discussed his team's success and philosophy, while Biglerville coach Mike Bucher spoke about overcoming psychological barriers to achieve state championship status.
cleaned_text:
“It used to be a psychological barrier years ago,” said Biglerville coach Mike Bucher. “It’s not a big deal now. The first couple years I coached the kids would have a mental block because it was something we couldn’t obtain. But when Larry Roby and John Johnson made it, it broke the barrier. We realized that kids from our area could be state champions. That’s the goal now because it can be done.”

“The mood among the Canners has been good,” Bucher said. “Three qualifiers have been good. Workouts have been limited mainly to running to keep in top condition. Orner, who was taped heavily in the district consolation match due to a bruised shoulder and sore ear, is healing well.”

Bucher added that Orner’s ear might need to be taped to protect it. Orner, who brings a 33-4 record into the state tournament, is confident that all three of his wrestlers will win medals. “We know we're against unknowns every time out, but I found over the years to lay back and not worry about finding a whole lot about your opponent. Our philosophy is to apply the pressure to them and let them adjust to your intelligence.”

Bermudian Springs coach McCollum is confident that since Corey and Bergmann are this far, they can go even further. McCollum referenced Forsythe’s 30-2 record, noting that one of his wins came at the hands of Showers in a 7-2 decision on February 0.

McCollum also mentioned John Hughes, a two-time state champion (37-0 from Clinton), who is going to be contending with top people.

“At one point in the season we were about dropping him to 189,” McCollum recalled. “When he tied Mike Washington of Scotland, that changed that. We knew he could be a sectional champion. It didn’t happen that way, of course. From that point I looked at the district: a good position. It was all Alex, in his heart. He wrestled his only second year and last year he finished with a 0 to 15 record.”
