headline: Bermudian Springs Wrestler Forsythe Discusses PIAA Championship Strategy
summary: The PIAA Class AA Wrestling Championships saw several key matches, including Shannon Forsythe's bout and the elimination of Donnie Orner, highlighting the competitive nature of the tournament.
cleaned_text:
Shannon Forsythe, of Bermudian Springs, spoke about the importance of rhythm in wrestling, noting that "you want to win the first one... get into a rhythm." Forsythe, who faced Toby Allen of Redbank Valley, described his bout, mentioning that Allen led with his left leg, forcing him to change his stance.

In other matches, Brad Showers of Bigler defeated Tony Bono of Bristol at 145 pounds, recording two takedowns in a 6-2 victory to advance to the quarterfinals. Showers advised, “If you can take people down first here, you can win your bouts.”

The article detailed several other bouts from the PIAA Class AA Wrestling Championships. Two-time District III champion Donnie Orner was eliminated after two straight losses. Alex Bergmann of Bermudian Springs was pinned by Jim Slomka of Richlands in the consolation round, though he had previously come close to victory against Steve Jones of Avon Grove at heavyweight.

In the first round, Kyle Werkheiser of Wilson defeated Orner at 160 pounds. Meanwhile, Corbin of Hanover struggled, dropping a 6-1 bout to defending 103-pound runner-up Mark Miller of North Star. Despite this, Corbin later secured a 4-3 victory over Shane Stright of Conneaut Lake in the first consolation round.

Coach Terry Conover of Hanover praised Corbin, stating that while Miller was better, Corbin "wrestled a good match."

title: HERSHEY — Then there were
headline: Local Wrestlers Advance at PIAA Tournament
summary: Two local wrestlers, Shannon Forsythe and Brad Showers, advanced to the quarterfinal round of the PIAA tournament after defeating their opponents.
cleaned_text:
HERSHEY — Then there were two.

While five Adams County wrestlers went to the Hersheypark Arena with the hopes of capturing the victory of victories, only two were able to advance past the preliminary round, and one will grapple for a third-place finish.

In the competition at the PIAA tournament, Shannon Forsythe and Biglerville’s Brad Showers won their respective matches and advanced to the quarterfinal round.

Jason Fissel won’t be able to vie for a state championship, but the senior averted early elimination by winning the first-round consolation match to move on in that bracket.

Forsythe, the District II Southcentral Region champion, started out slow against Redbank Valley's Toby Allen in the 140-pound class, but finished strong to win 7-2. Forsythe scored all of his points in the third period. After tying the score at 1-1 on an escape, Forsythe scored on a takedown to take a lead he would not lose. He added four more points, including a late takedown, to pad the advantage. Forsythe goes on to face Montgomery’s Ken Harer (32-4) in the quarterfinal round, which gets underway at 9 a.m. today.

“He was a little nervous; the first match is the hardest one. He didn’t get rolling until midway through the second period,” said the Bermudian Springs coach, Mike McCollum. “He is definitely improved.”

One weight class up and two mats over was Showers, who took with him the best record of any 145-pounder in the state tournament. At 34-2, Showers was looking to make it 35-2.

Showers secured a match against Bristol’s Tony Bono. Showers used three takedowns and gave up just two escapes to notch a 6-2 victory.

“I was just looking for a win, that’s all I want,” said Showers. “I saw he was pretty strong, so I didn’t want to get in too tight. If you can get a takedown, you'll win most of your matches.”

Showers’ initial takedown was almost all he needed as he took a 2-0 lead into the second period. Bono scored a point on an escape for the only scoring of the second period, but in the third and final period, Showers regained control and rolled up two takedowns.

Meanwhile, Fissel gave up nine points on a headlock move before eventually being pinned on the same move. Mark Moore of Kennedy Christian amassed 12 points and had control of the match. Moore turned Fissel on a headlock just moments into the match for a quick five points, and only other points came on escape by Bono.

title: BY JOHN MICHAELS
headline: Roger Sanders Impressed by UW-Madison Coaching Staff
summary: Roger Sanders observed the experienced former wrestlers and coaches at the University of Wisconsin-Madison during the NHA championships.
cleaned_text:
BY JOHN MICHAELS
Press Enterprise editor

HERSHEY—Roger Sanders was impressed by what he saw at the University of Wisconsin-Madison wrestling coach. The coach was standing and looking down at the participants in the NHA championships at Wisconsin. He was one of the most experienced of the 14 former BU wrestlers who are also coaches at the University of Wisconsin-Madison, along with Randy Watts and others. It is encouraging to see all of them be successful.

The wrestlers, now coaches, stayed in the room and worked hard. “We don’t like to cut people,” Sanders said. He noted that he was in contact with most of his former students.

headline: Showers Advances to Consolation Semifinals
summary: The article recaps several matches from a wrestling tournament, highlighting Showers' victory and discussing controversial calls made by officials.
cleaned_text:
Showers advanced to the consolation semifinals with an 8-4 decision after defeating East Pennsboro’s Ryan Gainor. Showers, who upped his record to 37-3, led the entire match, using two takedowns and an escape to build a 5-0 lead after two periods, and kept Gainor at a distance with three third-period points.

“We are tickled pink to have a place winner,” said Biglerville coach Mike Bucher. “There are a lot of wrestlers with a lot of talent who won't be place winners.”

In another match, Showers locked Seibold in a cradle following a takedown and nearly pinned his opponent. Seibold managed to score a takedown in the third period but ran out of time in his efforts to turn Showers over and garner back points.

Other matches included:
Springs Shannon Forsythe and Biglerville’s Jason Fissel were eliminated in the second-round consolation matches.

During a match, the official ruled a move as dangerous, stopping the action while Forythe was preparing to dump his opponent to the mat. Forsythe went on to lose the match 4-3, as Harer scored the first points of the match on a takedown and added a pair in the third period on a takedown. Forsythe garnered a reversal in the second period and was awarded a penalty point.

“The first period he (Forsythe) got him in a double leg and they (the officials) stop the match,” said McCollum. “They said it was a risk factor. He had a clear takedown. Shannon wasn’t about to slam him, that’s why he was taking his time. That hurt us. It’s the first time I’ve ever seen that.”

McCollum also commented on another bout, noting that it took Anderson around moments to set up the cradle, but Forsythe could not get out of it.

Fissel, a loser in the first round and winner in the first-round consolation match, was pinned in the second period on a cradle.

title: Wrestling camp set for June 18-21
headline: Blue Mountain Wrestling Camp Set for June 18-21
summary: The Blue Mountain Wrestling Camp will take place at Littlestown Middle School from June 18-21, offering basic and advanced instruction for wrestlers of all ages.
cleaned_text:
Wrestling Camp Set for June 18-21

HILSTEWEN — The Blue Mountain Wrestling Camp will be held from 8 a.m. to 2:30 p.m. at the Littlestown Middle School on Maple Ave. in Littlestown.

The camp features "Starry" Hitchcock, a wrestling coach who is an NCAA and NBA champion and a silver medalist in the 1975 World Games. Participants must accompany the set of equipment with an application, or they may pay the full amount. Applications will be accepted at the door, but it is recommended that you register. The balance due will be paid at the first session on June 18.

The camp will provide instruction for wrestlers from elementary up to high school age. Basic and advanced wrestling instruction will be offered.

The coaching staff includes several local figures. One coach is associated with Bermudian Springs, where he is the head coach of the varsity team. Another coach is from Panochek, who is the head coach at New Orford High School. Additionally, the camp features former state qualifier from Haven High School in South Western High School.

title: < + "Dave
headline: Blue Mountain Camp Brings Wrestling Instruction to Littlestown
summary: The Blue Mountain Camp, held at Littlestown Middle School, provides intensive wrestling instruction for students from multiple local school districts.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Blue Mountain Camp Held at Littlestown Middle School

During the middle of June, the Maple Avenue Middle School in Littlestown hosted the Blue Mountain Camp, a wrestling camp providing fine instruction for young wrestlers of all ability levels.

The camp is attended by students from the Littlestown, Bermudian Springs, Biglerville, South Western, and Gettysburg school districts, ranging from elementary to high school ages.

The staff includes head coach McCollum, along with coaches like Rick Trimmer, a Bermudian Springs High School graduate, and Willi Metzger, a veteran of the Spring Grove and Lock Haven programs.

“One of the best things about this camp is the staff,” said Dave Bowersox, head coach at Littlestown and director of the camp. “Young wrestlers learn a lot at the same time.”

Bowersox explained that the schedule is carefully set up to give the kids a good workout while preventing them from overdoing it. He added that many of the kids are participating in summer tournaments, and the camp helps them stay in shape.

headline: Bermudian Springs Wrestling Team Expected to Challenge for League Title
summary: Bermudian Springs is predicted to be a strong contender for the Blue Mountain League championship, boasting a roster of returning starters and experienced wrestlers.
cleaned_text:
The Eagles return 13 letter winners, including nine starters, from last year, and the team is considered a solid force. Bermudian Springs is expected to be highly competitive, and many believe the team is the best contender for the Blue Mountain League championship.

The roster features several top returning players, including McKinney Gambie (103), sophomore Jake Tin Prentice (104), Blair Anderson (130), South Livingston (140), Kobe Brown (141), Jee Beagher (171), and Chris Branley (183). Other returning starters include Tresor Cuffa (173) and Mike MacKee (125), alongside Brian McKinney (130).

headline: Bermudian Springs Claims Blue Mountain League Crown
summary: Bermudian Springs defeated New Oxford 39-26 in a wrestling match, securing the Blue Mountain League crown and tying them for the conference lead.
cleaned_text:
Bermudian Springs showed why they are a powerhouse in the Blue Mountain League, defeating New Oxford 39-26 in a wrestling match Thursday night at York Springs. With the win, the Eagles, overall and 2-0 in the league, are tied with Gettysburg and Green-castle for the conference lead.

Coach McCollum stated, “We're right where we want to be. We knew New Oxford would be competitive early, and then win the last four weights.”

The day began with a pleasant surprise for McCollum when sophomore Bob Yingling pinned Shane Rutters at 103 pounds in 1:43. McCollum noted, “Bob has very little varsity experience, but he’s a solid competitor.”

The team continued its winning streak with Bert Claybaugh pinning Ryan Petosky at 112 pounds in 3:12. Next, Shawn Myers defeated Bermudian Springs’ Travis Griffie at 119 pounds by decision, 13-10.

The match remained back-and-forth as Bermudian Springs’ John Goddard and Mike Markey won matches at 125 and 130 pounds, respectively. New Oxford’s Justin Adams countered with a technical fall decision at 135 pounds.

Later, Jack Thomas and Dirk Bievenour of Bermudian Springs recorded wins at 140 and 152 pounds, respectively. New Oxford’s Brian Lawyer pinned Mike Roth at 145 pounds.

With the Eagles clinging to a 24-20 lead, McCollum’s upper-weight class took over. Shannon Forsythe and Jason Forsythe each recorded pins to seal the victory, and Rich Lupo defeated Mike Markle at 189 pounds, 7-5.

New Oxford coach was visibly frustrated, stating, “It was kind of a yo-yo match there for a while... I didn’t expect losses at 103 and 125 pounds and thought we could have been more competitive.”

headline: Eagles’ Upper Weights Shine at Meet

“The Eagles’ Upper Weights”

“They'll match up all the way through,” he continued. “I’m sure that in the end, one team is going to win seven matches and the other’s going to win six. However, the Eagle squad which makes that early January trip may be much different than the one which won four of the final five bouts to hold off New Oxford. One possible starter, Jim Beard, was academically ineligible, while another, Kevin Shue, wrestled a JV bout Thursday despite a heavily padded injured hand. He’s not over, and that pin got us the lead on Shawn Mye over the Eagles.”

“Kevin Shue is not quite where we want him, but the JV win will boost his confidence. In addition, unlike New Oxford, McCollum noted, the Eagles have not [lousy upper weights]. The sad part is that people will see the upper weights lost and think, ‘They have lousy upper weights,’ and we don’t. It’s just we keep hitting guys like the Forsythes. We just can’t beat those Forsythes.”

During the meet, Bievenour, who was wrestling his first match of the season, posted a 61-second fall over Jarod Willow, also making his debut, at 152. Shannon Forsythe, who received a plaque from Bermudian Springs athletic director Sam Elias prior to the meet to commemorate his District II crown in 1989, then used a half-nelson to pin Joel Groft in 1:24. **The Evening Sun**
**Tuesday, January 29, 1991**

**Babe Ruth League Sweepstakes**
The Babe Ruth League is holding registration for a sweepstakes anniversary prize. Entrants are eligible for a one-week trip to Scotland, a VIP tour of the Drambuie Liquer Co., Ltd., and $1,000. The sweepstakes ran from January 1, 1990, to December 30, 1990, and all entrants will receive a customized Hole-In-One bag tag.

**Controversy Mars Bermudian Springs Wrestling**
Controversy surrounded the Bermudian Springs wrestling team after a recent non-league match. Coach McCollum stated that the team felt "ripped" during the match, noting, "It was pretty controversial. I felt we got ripped and this is the first time I’ve felt that way in our gym for a long time.”

McCollum’s comments followed the team’s decision to Northern in a non-league match held Monday at Bermudian Springs.

**SCHOLASTIC WRESTLING**
In other athletic news, a wrestling match featured several notable performances. At 140 pounds, Dirk Bievenour was leading 9-3 going into the third period before escaping and taking his opponent to his back. During the match, McCollum expressed frustration over a call, stating, “We got called for an illegal which looked legal to me. I thought he did a job taking him down legally. I thought that was the turning point. We needed to get something.” Ultimately, Bievenour lost the match 15-5.

In other weight classes, Shannon Forsythe and Jason Forsythe each recorded pins at 152 and 160 pounds, respectively, for Bermudian Springs in a match that ended 24-22.

headline: Bermudian Springs Wrestlers Control Destiny After Victory
summary: Bermudian Springs High School secured a crucial victory over Waynesboro, boosting their record and giving them control of the Blue Mountain League title race, while also criticizing the officiating of the match.
cleaned_text:
**The Evening Sun**
**Fri, Feb 01, 1991**

**By CHUCK CURLEY**

**YORK SPRINGS —** Bermudian Springs High School's wrestlers know exactly where they stand, especially with their biggest rival looming just behind them.

"I would say the major threat is Greencastle, and that’s on February 13th (at Greencastle). They’re our big threat, and we have to have everybody for rings," said a source.

The recent victory over Waynesboro on Thursday boosted Bermudian Springs to 6-0 in the Blue Mountain League and 8-4 overall. This result drops Waynesboro (8-7 overall)—which had defeated Greencastle 25-24 last week—into a tie with the Blue Devils at 5-1. Therefore, the Eagles now control their own destiny in the bid for their first BML title.

Meanwhile, Waynesboro High School coach David Weber was not a happy man on Thursday night.

"Perhaps his Indians might not have done better than their 32-24 loss to Bermudian Springs indicates, but Weber at least felt his team deserved a better chance to settle the matchup of 5-0 Blue Mountain League records at the Springs High School gymnasium," reported the article.

Weber complained about the officiating, stating, "The last thing I hate to do, all tight, is say something about a referee. You usually bite your lip, but... bring a man in here and do a job like that in a critical situation is appalling."

The official, Ken Shafer, whistled four falls, including one on Indians’ Mark Luthens, but the pins which seemingly went unnoticed were of significance.

McCollum, the team coach, advised his team before the match. "I knew the pinners. I just made of it," he said.

The former coach continued, "Every official... One-1,000," but of as quick as the other.

have two starters out,” said CV coach
Jim Smith. “We are trying to get back
to what we were in the 70s.’

Three straight major decisions
turned the tide for the Eagles,

Brian Barrick rode the strength ofa
six-point third period to beat Luis Ca-
macho 11-3 at 145. Then Ed Allison,
ranked second in District III at 145,
moved up a weight and decisioned
Bill Lybrand 16-8 at 152.

The Eagles’ Eric Durham then
countered Chad Alban’s escape with
five seconds left with a takedown at
the buzzer for an 11-2 win at 160.

“T thought we were in trouble when
we lost at 140, but then we came
through with back-to-back-to-back
four-pointers,” said Smith,

‘That made the score 27-9 in favor
of the Eagles with the Mustangs
needing pins in the last three bouts to
tie the match,

At 171, Doug Scidenstricker gave
the Mustangs a glimmer of hope
when he flattened Scott Kennedy in
57 seconds.

‘Things continued to look bright for
South Western as Matt Hladio took
the mat at 189 and immediately took
down Tony Kokolis and got him in a
cradle. However, Hladio could not
get the fall and Kokolis responded to
every move that Hladio made. Al-

“We're being very cautious and
trying to evaluate what's going to
happen,” he said. “We try to maintain
positive attitude. We don’t talk ab-
out the word ‘recession.’ When
things are bad, we can’t dwell on the
negative.”

That is a lesson which Donnelly
learned at Littlestown, where he said
the Bolts’ Blue Mountain League and
District III basketball championship
in 1978-79 was an extension of the
preceding football season.

In that campaign, Littlestown went
12-0, in€luding a Southern Division
title and a 21-0 victory over Northen
Division champion Shippensburg.

On that squad, Donnelly was a de-
fensive back on a squad which per-
mitted only 33 points all season,

Likewise, Donnelly said, every
member of the Bolt championship
team had a well-defined role, one

which produced a closely-knit team
and life-long friendships.

“I'd say, when we get together,
that the other guys get on me about
turning the ball over. I was never re-
ally a shooter, but I’d remind them of
the Columbia game, in which I hit the
last shot to win the game,” he said.

Donnelly said he learned both mo-
tivation and intensity from his coach,
John Barnhart.

“He also coached me in football
and I learned a lot about how I need to
be prepared and to have intensity,
which follows through today, but to
‘not get emotional,” he said.

Donnelly was planning to apply
such lessons asa coach, his reason for
gaining degrees in secondary educa-
tion and economics from the Univer-
sity of Pittsburgh.

“The original plan was to get out of
college, teach and coach for a couple
of years and then get into the business
world,” he said.

However, when he learned from
his sister, who was residing at Allen-
town, about a radio sales vacancy at
WAEB, an AM and FM radio station

The Search Continues

If you know a former Hanover area
athlete or coach whom you would like
to see profiled and you know his or her
whereabouts, call The Evening Sun
sports department at 637-3736 or write
to 135 Baltimore St, Hanover, Pa.,
1731.

there, Donnelly decided to accelerate
his plan. i

“Somebody once told me that if
you can sell radio, you can sell any-
thing,” he said. Donnelly did the job
well enough to eventually become
sales manager at WAEB, but said,
“After three years, I felt I needed to
move ahead.”

Therefore, he requested twice to
interview for the next available ex-
ecutive position within the firm
which owns WAEB. That position
became his current job and his initia~
tion to Florida, where he directs 21
full-time employees at WZZR-FM.

He said Port St. Lucie, the spring
training home of the New York Mets,
is an active community in which he is,
determined to make WZZR-FM a

bout Saturday afternoon. Barrick went on to take an 11-3 decision.

though he dropped a 13-6 decision, it
gave the match to the Eagles.

“Koko doesn’t intimidate easily.
He was aggressive when he had to
be,” Smith said.

“They countered our offensive
moves very well,” Bowersox said.
“Apparently they scouted us well.”

Brian Cromer closed out the match

“We have a well-balanced team

team, it all depends on how we match

“T have 13 kids and probably 12
different styles,” said Smith. “We at-
tack on our feet and are always going
for the fall. We stress that.”

Cumberland Valley also took a
three-point decision, 33-30, in the
jayvee match.

close.” Matt Markey (125) was wres-

Bievenour (140) was going against
the third-ranked wrestler in the
trict, Mark May. All three lost, but
wrestled well and keep the match

in top five

Waynesboro nipped Biglerville for
the team title at the Blue Mountain
League junior high wrestling tourna-
ment Saturday at James Buchanan,

The Indians scored a total of 178
points to just barely finish ahead of
Biglerville, which amassed 176.

New Oxford wound up third, Get-
tysburg fourth and Bermudian
Springs fifth.

ished seventh,

ished first in the 145-pound division,

yan Cleary at 80, Doug Noel at

ment among the top three or four
teams as compared to other years,”
said New Oxford coach Tom Swope.
“Tt was good competition, We placed
11 of 17 wrestlers, so I’m pleased.”

Hanover (8-3) overcame a forfeit
at 70 pounds with 17 consecutive
triumphs, including three pins in 16
seocnds or less, to rout Northeastern,

The Falcon pinners were Nate
Becker at 85, Jeremy Reck at 90, Joe
Weaver at 95, Tyke Conover at 105,
Josh Conover at 110, Dave Maus at
125 and Keith Hess at 154.

The Falcons added seven forfeit
triumphs.

Northern Tourney

Jason Dell captured the 110-pound
championship to help South Westen
toa fourth-place finish in the 1 1-team
Northem Tournament.

The event was won by Manheim
Central, with Shikellamy second and
Solanco third.

Earning a second-place finish for
the Colts was Tyson Rebert at 90.
Thirds went to Mike Lybrand at 100,
Jason Snook at 105 and Brian Hart at
165. Heavyweight Aaron Utermah-
len placed fourth.

Raiders 37, Eagles 35

Craig Laughman, unbeaten against
YCIAA opposition, gained a pin at
154 ein, boosting Spring Grove
past Dover when the Raiders com-
pleted the meet with two straight for-
feit wins.

Spring Grove (6-2) trailed 35-19
entering Laughman’s bout. The
Raiders’ leading scores to that point
were Matt Helwig’s technical fall at
85 and major decisions by Jonathan
Lloyd and Dan Lockner, who also
owns an unbeaten YCIAA mark,

Bullet wrestlers
post trio of wins

GETTYSBURG — The Gettys-
burg College wrestling team had only
one win to-its credit heading into a
quadrangular match Saturday after-
noon at Hen Bream Gymnasium. But
the Bullets earned victories over Sus-
quehanna, Swarthmore and York to
hike their record to 4-5,

_ Gettysburg opened the competi-
tion with a 25-14 victory over Sus-
quehanna, In that match, Steve Le-
vine recorded his team-leading fifth
pin of the season, The Bullets got
ins from Narin Sihavong at 118 and

ete Scacheri at 134 in their 27-16
win over Swarthmore, and from Trij
Scott at 190 in their 28-17 telus
over York.

Sihavong and Steve Barley each
produced three wins for the Bullets
during the competition,

Western Maryland defeated Ursi-
hus 23-22 and Lebanon Valley 25-22
while losing 22-14 to Mansfield.
Triple winners were Tony Spagnola
at 158 and Chris Madden at 167.

title: Feb 03, 1991, page 25 - York Sunday News at Newspapers.com™
headline: Chronister's Win Brings Standing Ovation to Bermudian Springs
summary: Curt Chronister secured a 3-2 win for Bermudian Springs, leading to a standing ovation, despite Dover's overall victory over Woodbury.
cleaned_text:
When the final bout was over, a 3-2 win by Bermudian Springs heavyweight Curt Chronister, the Bermudian fans erupted and gave their team a standing ovation.

Dover head coach Charlie Jacobs, while on the short end of the stick, could appreciate the waiting—there's no doubt about that, Jacobs said. Dover did not end the day with a victory, having been defeated by Woodbury, N.J., 52-18. But the focus quickly returned to Bermudian Springs.

One of the reasons the schools haven't met often is that...

"It feels great," Chronister said, referring to the same heavyweight who put Sushi close meet last Saturday. "I knew it would probably come down."

With Dover strong in the lighter weights and Bermudian Springs strong up high, the Bermudian head team was not out of it. "When we were down 11, I told him if he could go and win (at 145), we were still in this thing," McCollum said. "I knew our heavyweight people we could score on."

For Dover, the main problem was that six of its seven wins came from a waayabie victory.

Scotland’s Closing Could Affect Area

The only thing constant in life is change. Of course, some things change slowly, so slowly, in fact, that the last time they changed is forgotten. A vivid reminder of that occurred last week when a regional institution, the Scotland School for Veterans’ Children, was targeted for closure over the next two years by Governor Robert Casey. The school, established for children of Civil War veterans, has been operating for more than 100 years, but change, in the form of a padlock, is planned to slice some of the deficit off the proposed state budget. The certain lobbying by veterans groups to keep the school open has been ongoing.

**Bermudian Springs Wins Blue Mountain League Wrestling Championship**

By Skip Hutter
The Patriot-News
Thursday, February 14, 1991

GREENCASTLE — The little guys did the job here last night, and it rewarded Bermudian Springs with the Blue Mountain League Wrestling championship.

The Eagles won five of the first seven weight classes and went on to a 25-24 victory over Greencastle. The Blue Devils fell to 6-2 in the league.

“I knew we had some good kids coming back,” McCollum said, “but I didn’t know what they would do.”

Trent Matthews called the turning point of the match, noting that Brian McCauslin won 15-8 over Brad Etter at 130, and Jack Thomas surprised Brad Heffner 10-5 at 135, putting the Eagles in front to stay.

“They were the difference,” Matthews said. “We beat them both last year.”

McCollum was especially pleased with the performance turned in by McCauslin. “He is a four-year starter,” McCollum said. “Some days he’s there and some days he isn’t. Tonight, he was there.”

Looking back, Matthews agreed with McCollum’s assessment of the lighter weights. “I knew whichever team won four matches before 140 would win it all,” Matthews said. “We only won two.”

The Eagles opened a 18-6 lead when Dirk Bievenour pinned Manahan at 1:22 of their pound match.

**The Evening Sun**
Fri, Feb 15, 1991

In recent area wrestling action, the Canners secured a significant victory, defeating Gettysburg with a score of 28-25. The Canners built their lead by having key wins from Travis Byers at 103, Glendon Forsythe at 112, Chad Charles at 119, Trin Martin at 130, Matt Taylor at 135, Jeff Greist at 140, and Tom Solberg at 145.

Gettysburg coach Steve Allen noted that the team was challenged, stating, “We were down big. They (Biglerville) are pretty good up top and neutralized our lighter weights. They have a solid team.”

Gettysburg, which had previously maintained a five-match win streak, managed to stage a comeback with decision wins from Tim Coscia at 152 and Mike Schultz at 160, along with a fall from Chad Heffner at 171. Josh Martin of Biglerville provided the winning points.

In other athletic news, the Bermudian Springs team had a successful night. Recording pins for Bermudian Springs were Bob Yingling (103), Mike Markey (125), Jack Thomas (135), Dirk Bievenour (140), and Shannon Forsythe (152). Forsythe improved his record to 23-0, while Jason Forcuthe continued his strong performance.

New Oxford coach Frank Pannebacker, whose team is 7-6 overall and 5-3 in the league, stated, “Hopefully we’ll end the season on a winning note like we did tonight.” Also winning for New Oxford were Burt Clabaugh, Tom Wright, and Dustin Muller, who won by technical fall. The Colonials also received four forfeits.

In the YCIAA DI action, Spring Grove defeated Central York. The top-ranked 171 weight class saw Shawn Holden pin Brian Becker. The Blue Devils secured falls from [unspecified wrestler] at 160, Brian Booze at 189, and Travis Pups at heavyweight, ending the match.

Many of the fans who showed up at Saturday’s 11th annual YCIAA Swimming Championships at William Penn High School came to learn who the best swimmers in York County would be. However, there is another tale to tell about this meet and others like it. It is a setting such as the William Penn Natatorium where, standing on the pool deck, we saw dozens of individual seasons come to completion. For some of Saturday’s senior competitors, the meet ended careers.

Yet swimming also is a sport where underclassmen can excel almost immediately, even though times on Saturday were generally slower than the seeded times. That was a surprise because of the new layout of the pool itself. In past years, competitors dove off a squared-off rise in the pool deck. New individual platforms installed at the other end allowed swimmers to take a more traditional starting position in Saturday’s meet.

cleaned_text:
**SBURG TIMES, FRIDAY, MARCH 15, 1991**

**Forsythe and Byers in the Hunt for State Wrestling Glory**

State Wrestling preliminaries were held Thursday, allowing several top competitors to advance to the quarterfinals. Ataqua’s freshman, Jonat Reed, had no trouble in the junior 145-pound weight class, securing a technical fall victory.

For both Forsythe and Byers, the sense of relief after having the initial match behind them was overshadowed only by the thrill of victory in their respective state appearances. Forsythe, who made it to the quarterfinals for the second consecutive year, noted, “It feels good to get the first match over with. It takes the pressure off me now. I felt really good.”

For Byers, the focus was on the next round. “If I win my next match, I get a medal,” Byers stated. “I want to be able to do that next year, too.”

Forsythe and Byers are among the top eight in their weight classes in Pennsylvania, but both aim to go higher. Coach McCollum of Bermudian Springs mentioned that while the team was strong, they had not reached the top eight in the same situation last year.

In other preliminary matches, John Hughes, Benton’s three-time state champion, won easily to advance to the quarterfinals. Hughes, with a career record of 35-0 and 143-2, is in the opposite bracket of Forsythe.

The quarterfinal matchups were set: Forsythe faces Rick Hagemeyer of Montoursville, a District IV team. Byers takes on undefeated Brian Rose of Crestwood (District 1). In Byers’ match, the junior man maneuvered for a reversal less than a full period into the down position, which he later described as the best situation of the match.

headline: Bermudian Springs' Forsythe Wins South Central Region Championship
summary: Bermudian Springs' Shannon Forsythe secured the South Central Region Championship after a strong state tournament showing, despite being eliminated in the semifinals.
cleaned_text:
Shannon Forsythe, a 145-pound wrestler from Bermudian Springs, coasted through the quarterfinals of the state tournament but was eliminated in the semifinals by Hertzog. Hertzog emerged victorious, scoring a reversal in the third quarter and putting Forsythe on his back to close out a five-point move for an 11-7 win as time expired.

Forsythe, who will receive a medal for finishing in the top four, wrestled for fifth place against Bruce Gearhart of East Pennsboro and defeated him to win the South Central Region Championship.

The tournament marked the farthest Forsythe had advanced in his three appearances at the state tournament, and it was the best showing of an Eagle wrestler since Trevor Byers won the title in 1986. Forsythe had also hoped to challenge Benton’s unbeaten John Hughes for the championship.

“I think he’s happy to get a medal out of the tournament,” said McCollum. “Coming away without a medal would be disappointing.” He continued, “He's come a step closer every year, and has improved. It’s a credit to him. He put himself in a position where he could have won the match. He’s had a great career.”

Meanwhile, Biglerville’s Travis Byers advanced to the quarterfinals. After losing in the quarters, he won his first consolation match but then lost to Brandywine’s John Haulko 11-9 in the third round, ending a brilliant junior season. Byers finished the season with a record of 27-9-2.

There were three teenage girls from suburban Philadelphia's
Prospect Park hovering behind the home plate fence three hours

dium back on April 12.

“We're here to take pictures of our hero — Lenny Dykstra,”
beamed Michele Sinick. “If you talk to him, make sure to say we
said hello... OK, will you do that?” she eagerly told this reporter.

Michele and her fanatical friends may have seen Dykstra deliver
at the plate that night with a single, double and two-run home runto

’. lift_the Phillies to an 11-4 win over the Cardinals.

They, however, weren't on hand after the game to see the un-
flinching fellow with the mohawk take some long drags from a short
roll of finely cut tobacco wrapped in paper — in front of the press.

They also weren't there to give Dykstra some advice on when to
fold ‘em. Seems as though Lenny rang up a $78,000 invoice to
some gambling sharks in Mississippi.

Nor were they slamming Whiskey Sours, taking shots of Pepper-
mint schnapps or downing Budweisers at John Kruk’s bachelor
party at Smokey Joe's Restaurant last Sunday night. And they we-
ren't in Dykstra’s $92,000 Mercedes Benz at 1 a.m. when it
rounded a bend in Radnor Township and smashed into two trees.

Surely, the three young ladies follow their favorite player on the
. basepaths but they don't know him very well.

Dykstra’s blood alcohol content level at the time of the 1 a.m. ac-

0.10 percent. That's roughly the equivalent of twelve beers in one
hour or eight shots of hard liquor.

Dykstra is assumed to have been driving around 50 mph ina 25
mph zone on the winding road.

Radnor Township police officially filed charges against Dykstra
on Tuesday for driving under the influence, speeding and reckless

title: “Dave
headline: Bermudian Springs Discusses Conference Future; Lupo Family Continues Racing Legacy
summary: Bermudian Springs is navigating potential conference changes while the Lupo family continues a multi-generational tradition in local drag racing.
cleaned_text:
**Bermudian Springs Discusses Conference Future; Lupo Family Continues Racing Legacy**

**Bermudian Springs Conference Talks**
Russ Greenholt of Bermudian Springs discussed the ongoing discussions among Blue Mountain League (BML) schools regarding potential conference changes. He noted that the discussions were mixed between the Mid-Penn and the YCIAA. Greenholt mentioned that the cross country coach, Mike Spangler, who is from Southwestern, was a factor in the debate. He stated that the BML's future would be "real interesting" if the league dissolved. However, he added that Bermudian Springs was in an ideal position, as the school appeared to be well-received by either the Mid-Penn or the YCIAA.

**Drag Racing Update**
The article also covered local racing news, noting a subtle change in the Super Sprint car program: the insertion of the letters "JR" on both the helmet and the car. For Bermudian Springs High School senior Rich Lupo, this change was a radical one, spanning generations. Richard Lupo, Rich's father, had been a leading driver in the Hanover area since arriving from Florida. Richard had raced locally and on the World of Outlaws tour, driving for fellow Florida natives like Bobby Allen and Bob Weikert. Rich Lupo, 47, continued this tradition. His son, who spent the last winter as a member of the Springs Blue Mountain League champion team, now pilots the No. 59 Super Sprint car owned by New York City artist Sal Scarpitta.

headline: Local Wrestlers Dominate Multiple Tournaments
summary: The article reports on the success of local wrestlers across several tournaments, highlighting individual champions and team efforts in the Blue Mountain League and other regional events.
cleaned_text:
Local Wrestlers Achieve Success Across Multiple Tournaments

"The total team effort was key," said first-year coach Mark Kuntz. "Every single kid scored points for the team, and that’s what it takes to win a team title in a tournament like this. You need everyone scoring points, and another difference in the scoring was the number of pins we had."

At one event, Mike Sterner (103), Eric Lawyer (145), and Aaron Glatfelter (152) brought home gold medals for Spring Grove. Glatfelter was a particularly pleasant surprise for Kuntz, winning a 9-5 decision over Springs’ Mike Roth. Kuntz noted that Aaron had a very good match, stating that in his senior high career, he had never won a title.

In the Littlestown Quad tournament, the Eagles topped the field with four champions—Travis Griffie (125), Dirk Bievenour (160), Jason Forsythe (189), and Curt Chronister (heavyweight)—and placed all 12 of their wrestlers in the top four. The Blue Mountain League’s prime contender finished a close second with 162 points to the Rockets’ 166.

The host Mustangs regained a measure of confidence, placing third after winning the event the last three years. Senior Jason Homan (119) was their lone champion and the only returning champion from last year to repeat. (Biglerville’s Trinity Martin, at 135, did not place.)

"I think we grew a little bit in this tournament," said South Western coach David Cox. "Some kids got their first wins and some good experience. In a few instances, we performed beyond what I expected."

Elsewhere, Hanover’s junior high wrestlers dominated the Dallastown Invitational Tournament on Saturday, sending 11 wrestlers into the championship.

**Controversial Calls Plague Bermudian Springs Wrestling Victory**

CHUCK CURLEY
Evening Sports Writer

NEW OXFORD – Bermudian Springs wrestling coach McMCollum was hit by a controversial call on Thursday, which created trouble for the team's ensuing operations.

After New Oxford stunned the visiting Bermudian Springs team with a 30-20 victory, it was determined that the loss could almost immediately be turned into a gain. McMCollum gave a chance to the heavyweight wrestler, Curren (Charter), to go beat. "But I will do right now. I will wake up our team because I have really enjoyed our attitude. It goes back to being good, and the Eagle (2-1) will be 1-1."

**Gettysburg, Carlisle, and Delone Catholic Wrestlers Shine in Local Matches**

On December 22, 1991, local wrestling saw several key matchups, with Gettysburg, Carlisle, and Delone Catholic emerging as strong contenders.

In one notable bout, Gettysburg defeated the Thundering Herd. Key wins for Gettysburg included Brent Wineka at 130, Chris Evans at 140, Brian Selby at 145, Chad Heffner at 189, and Wade Leedy at heavyweight. Meanwhile, Carlisle secured pins from Tim DeAngelo at 152, Scott Fisher at 160, and Gerard Slusser at 171.

Another highly contested match involved Bermudian Springs. The Eagles struggled against the visiting team, losing key bouts. Coach McCollum noted that the turning point was at 149, when a wrestler was caught in a cradle, leading to the loss of points and a team point. Bermudian Springs managed falls from Keller at 145 and Jason Forsythe at 189.

At a separate venue, the Squires faced challenges, losing a fall to Doug Stremmel at 103 and decisions to Fred Perez at 119 and Blaine Reichart at 140. Separately, Wess Klunk won the 112-pound match for Delone Catholic by default. Delone coach Bob Gano praised his team's effort, stating, "Our team wrestled well enough to win; they wrestled our hearts out. Morally and ethically, we had the better team." Gano also criticized the opponent for weighing in a wrestler after the match had already started.

In another regional contest, South Western defeated the Mountaineers, winning the jakevee match by a 24-15 score. The Mustangs (South Western) received wins from Jason Homan at 119, Aaron Sullivan at 125, Jason Dell at 135, and others.

The article also noted that the Shippensburg Tournament saw Biglerville send 17 wrestlers, with 16 advancing to at least the semifinal round.

Martin at 140, Tim Zeigler at 160, and Gerrett Forsythe at 171 for the Canners are all in the semis heading into today’s matches, which get underway at 9 a.m. “We're at the middle of the tournament,” said Biglerville Coach Ike Bucher. “Things are just about where we want them to be. I think they all have a good shot at winning.”

Meanwhile, New Oxford Coach Frank Pannebaker said his squad is “right about as far along as expected.”

For the Colonials, Dave Cajas at 103 lbs., Russ Hartman at 140, Justin Adame at 145, Joel Groft at 160, and Mark Markle at heavyweight are all in the semifinals. “I think Justin Adame and Joel Groft have good shots at winning and possibly Dave,” Coach Pannebaker added.

At Boiling Springs, Bermudian Springs put eight wrestlers in the semi-finals, including top-seeded Travis Griffe, Jason Forsythe, and Curt Chronister at 125, 189, and heavyweight, respectively.

Also in the semis, which start at 11 a.m. today, are Bob Yingling at 112, Brandon Dowell at 119, Mike Markey at 135, Mark Keller at 145, and Dirk Bicvenour at 160. “We had a good night, and there are some [wrestlers] on the team,” said a coach. “Although John Goddard (at 130) and Mike Roth (at 152) both lost close matches.”

The eight-team tournament concludes today.

title: “Dave
headline: Burt Clabaugh Sparks Colonial Middieweights
summary: Several high school wrestling teams competed in local duals and roundups, with reports detailing wins, losses, and coaching commentary on team performance.
cleaned_text:
Dave Burt Clabaugh started the group with an 8-1 decision at 119 pounds. Jason Snook (125), Hector Ramos (130), and Dustin Muller (135) added pins. Justin Adams (152) and Nate Updyke (171) added technical falls for the Colonials (2-1), while P.J. Groft, Joel Groft, and Mark Markle all added decisions.

**SCHOLASTIC ROUNDUP**

**Mustangs 40, Knights 17**

South Western snapped a two-match losing streak by winning at Eastern York. Recording pins for the Mustangs (2-4) were Jason Sierdzinski (112) and Tim Schimp (140). Jason Homan (119) added a technical fall, while Aaron Sullivan, Jason Dell, Bill Lybrand, Chuck Topper, and Paul Ritter scored decisions.

“The kids wrestled well,” said South Western coach David Cox. “They were pretty aggressive. That's something I've been pushing. They're improving with each match.”

The Mustangs won the match 52-0.

**Burt Clabaugh Sparks Colonial Middieweights**

Mark Keller (45), Dirk Bievenour (152), Jason Orwick (171), and Curt Chronister (hwt.) notched decisions while Scott Lemin drew at 135 for the Eagles, who fell to 2-3. West Perry won the first four bouts (there was no match at 103) for an 18-0 lead.

Meanwhile, Bermudian Springs’ Jason Forsythe lost his first bout of the season at 189. Forsythe was called for an illegal hold—apparently, a twisting hammer—and when his coach couldn't continue, the match was awarded to Hertzler.

**CEDAR CLIFF DUALS**

Mounties 32, Rockets 23
Rockets 46, Knights 18
Cardinals 43, Rockets 12

Mike Sterner (103) and Brent Scott (189) were triple winners as Spring Grove won against Reading, while losing to the Mounties.

**York County Coaching Insights**

Mike Bucher, coach of Biglerville, was asked who he thought was the top team in York County. “Hanover, definitely,” he said. “Dover is a very good team. They are probably right now second best to Hanover in York County, and I'm sure they could give Hanover a good run.”

When asked what accounted for the vast difference in scores, Bucher replied, “Well, for one thing we didn’t wrestle as well today as we did against Hanover. But probably the big difference was that we didn’t match up nearly as well against Dover as we did with Hanover.”

Biglerville (2-3) started off on a strong note with a decision by John Weible at 103 and a major decision from Travis Byers at 112. But the Eagles (4-1) claimed the next four weights to pull ahead for good, including a center match at Kerry Ferguson.

Bad weather
helps ’Skins

By DAVE GOLDBERG
AP Football Writer

WASHINGTON — Rain, wind and the Washington
defense turned Atlanta’s “Red Gun” offense into a water
pistol Saturday.

The Redskins, whose 14-2 record was the best in the
NFL during the regular season, used a steady down-
pour and winds gusting to 30 mph to help force six tur-
novers. That was the key as they beat the Falcons 24-7,
sending Atlanta slip slidin’ home from their first
sojourn into the playoffs in nine years

“The run and shoot in the rain is like a loaded gun
with one bullet,” said Deion Sanders, Atlanta’s corner-
back, kick returner and sometimes receiver, whose
main contribution to this one was a holding call that
prolonged Washington’s chnching touchdown drive.

The Redskins, on the other hand, had a fully loaded
gun as they qualified to meet the Detroit Lions —a 38-6
pinner over Dallas Sunday — at RFK Stadium next

unday.

Ricky Ervins ran 23 times for 104 yards, including a
16-yard touchdown run, and Earnest Byner rushed for
57 more. Ervins ran 17 yards for a touchdown 3:11
before Gerald Riggs went over from the 2-yard-lne,
then Riggs clinched it with a l-yard dive in the final
quarter.

Ervins noted that in Washington’s 56-17 win earlier
this year over Atlanta, most of the Redskins’ yardage
came on Rypien’s passing because the Falcons rushed
eight men.

“Last time their defense controlled the line of scrim-
mage,” Ervins said. “This time our offense did.”

But Atlanta, missing Mike Rozier and Steve Brous-
sard with injuries, could manage only 43 rushing yards
and spent most of the game in the shotgun, allowing the
Redskins to lay back. two of Washington’s touchdowns
were set up by fumble recoveries, and the Redskins had
four interceptions against Chris Miller, who was 17 of
32 for 178 yards.

“Everyone talks about offense,” Gibbs said. “Defense
is what wins champinships for you. Today they created
turnovers.”

It was a lesson in playoff football for Atlanta, which
last made it to the postseason in 1982 and won indoors
in New Orleans 27-20 last week.

“Typical playoff weather,” said Washington coach
Joe Gibbs. “We like to say 1t was Redskins weather.
Muddy and a wet field.”

Indeed it was.

Even though Miller was 14 of 17 in one stretch of the
third and fourth quarters, he could get only one score
out it. And the 14th completion was fumbled away by
hae] Haynes, leading,to Riggs’ Layard TD run with
2 left inthe fourth quarter. ~

“We knew that with today’s conditions, if we could get
pressure on Miller, he would get frustrated and put up
some balls that we could come down with,” said defen-
sive tackle Tim Johnson, who had one of the sacks. “For
the most part it worked.”

“The wind didn’t bother me. Not being able to stand
up bothered me,” Miller said, and coach Jerry Glanville
added: “I saw Miller’s feet sliding everytime he threw.”

“ think that was probably our biggest problem on the
turnovers,” Glanville said.

After a muddy, sluggish first period, the Redskins
jumped to a 14-0 lead on its two scores early in the sec-
ond period. But they led only 14-7 at halftime as Chip
Lohmuller, who led the NFLin scoring with 149 points,
missed three field goals — from 47, 37 and 44 yards, the
last as the half expired.

The first TD came on Ervins’ 16-yard run at the end
of an 11 play, 81-yard drive, the second was set up by
Pat Chaffey’s fumble that was recovered by Washing-
ton’s James Geathers at the Atlanta 39. Mark Rypien,
who was 14-of-28 for 170 yards, hit Ricky Sanders for 26
yards to the 2 to set up Riggs’ first TD run.

But Atlanta, stymied without a first down after its
first series, finally got a drive working, going 80 yards
in 11 plays Tracy Johnson going in fromn the
1-yard line with 57 seconds left in the half. The key play
was a pass interference call on Washington’s Martin
Mayhew that negated a Darrell Green interception.

Lohmiller finally hit from 24 yards with 6:54 left in
the third period, increasing Washington's lead to 17-7.
It was set up by Brian Mitchell’s 26-yard punt return to
the Atlanta 18 after the Washington defense had
pushed the Falcons back to their own 10 on their first

Kramer, Lions roll, 38-6

Backup QB throws for 341 yards

By DAVE GOLDBERG
AP Football Writer

PONTIAC, Mich. — Nearly
80,000 fans left the Pontiac Silver-
dome with the same words on their
lips: “I saw it but I don’t believe it.”

Believe it.

Erik Kramer, the quarterback
from nowhere, took the Detroit
Lions, the team from nowhere, to
within on victory of the Super Bowl
in a game that even he conceded
“was unimaginable for me.”

Kramer, who replaced the
injured Rodney Peete at midsea-
son, passed for 341 yards and three
touchdowns Sunday as the Detroit
Lions, who hadn’t won a playoff
game in 34 years, beat the Dallas
Cowboys 38-6 to advance to the
NFC title game. It was Detroit’s
seventh straight win and ended a
six-game Dallas win streak.

“Today was just something spe-
cial,” Kramer said. “From the first
series on I was in that zone every.
one hopes and dreams he can bein.”

He was helped by a Dallas
defense that was stacked to stop
Barry Sanders, who was limited to
22 yardsin 11 carries until he broke
lose for a 47-yard touchdown run in
the fourth quarter for the final
Detroit score.

He was also egged on by the Cow-
boys, who taunted him, particular-
ly early in the game.

“I took it as a challenge. They
kind of called me out,” Kramer said.
“They didn’t have too much respect
for me. They said ‘This is the guy
who’s got to beat us.”

So beat them he did, leading the
Lions to their mnth win without a

loss in the Silverdome. The Lions,
the only NFL team to go unbeaten
at home, won the NFC Central title
with a 12-4 record. It was Detroit’s
first winning season since 1983.

This victory sends the the Lions
to a far less friendly place for next
Sunday’s NFC title game — RFK
Stadium in Washington. That’s
where they lost the season opener
45-0 to the Redskinsin a game they
played without Sanders and with
Kramer sitting on the bench.

On this Sunday, the quarterback
who first played in the NFL as a
replacement player for Atlanta in
the 1987 strike and joined the Lions
from the Canadian League last
year, was as good as any Montana,
Marino or Elway. Or as good as
Bobby Layne, the Hall of Fame
quarterback for the Lions’ title
teams of four decades ago.

Kramer threw a 31-yard TD pass
to Willie Green on Detroit’s first
possession and then, with the Lions
leading 17-6, hit Green from mne
yards. Sixty-four seconds later,
after Victor Jones’ recovery of Troy
Aakman’s fumble on a snap, Kram-
er threw seven yards to Herman
Moore for another TD.

“He had one of the finest games
reading I've ever seen,” said Dallas
safety Ray Horton, a nine-year vet-
eran who was beaten by Green on
the first touchdown. “We'd change
our defense. He’d wait until the last
minute to snap the ball and he
knew what we were in.”

Aikman, injured six weeks ago,
replaced Steve Beuerlein late in the
first half after Beuerlein had
thrown an interception that was
returned 41 yards for a touchdown

Section |

Super Bowl
Bills’ defense

snuffs Chiefs

By BARRY WILNER
AP Sports Writer

ORCHARD PARK, N.Y. — Everyone knows about
their atomic offense. Guess what — the Buffalo Bills
can dominate with defense, too.

Sure, they routed the Kansas Ciy Chiefs 37-14 on
Sunday to move back into the AFC championship game.
And dim Kelly threw for three touchdowns and Thur-
man Thomas, the NFL’s most valuable player, rushed
for 100 yards.

But it was defense that set the tone. Sparked by Kir-
by Jackson's two interceptions and strong line play by
Cornelius Bennett and Jeff Wright, the Bills yielded
just 213 yards. That was in sharp contrast with the
Bills’ No. 27 league ranking in total defense.

“Rankings are ndiculous,” Wright said. “People tell
you the defense is worth nothing. We showed what we
can do today. We made our statement on the field.

“We're tired of hearing how we had the second from
lest defense. They were loaded for bear and we stopped
them .”

Stopped them dead.

Kansas City, which routed Buffalo 33-6 this season
and gained 397 yards in the process, never was in the
game. Steve DeBerg completed only five passes for 29
yards before leaving midway through the second quar-
ter with a sprained right thumb. Mark Vlasic replaced
him and was 9-for-20 for 124 yards and four
interceptions.

“We forced them into a passing situation, something
we want to do to every team,” Bennett said. “We were
very determined not to let them run on us. We really
concentrated on stopping the run across the board.”

In doing so, the defense complemented the usual
supply of big plays from the top-rated, no-huddle attack
led by Kelly. Andre Reed caught two TD passes and
sames Lofton had another as Kelly hit 23 of 35 for 273
yards.

“It seemed like everything worked out perfectly
today,” Reed said.

Buffalo, 13-3 during the season in winning its fourth
straight AFC East crown, will be host to Denver for the
conference championship next Sunday. The Bills, 20-19
lesers to the New York Giants in last year’s Super Bowl,
have lost only once in their last 19 home games — a
17-14 overtime loss to Detroit in the season finale, when
Kelly, Lofton and Thomas did not play.

While their offense was stalled early, the defense was
unrelenting. Kansas City never could establish any
ground dominance, which meant the Bills offense got
lots of chances to work out the kinks after a week off.

“I think we're one of the best defenses in the league,
not one of the worst, when we're all here,” Bennett said.

Buffalo’s offense got going late in the first quarter.
Kelly capped the first scoring drive with a 25-yard scor-
ing pass to Reed.

The Bills held and took over at their 35. A 5-yard
holding penalty on Jayice Pearson kept the drive going
after a third-down incompletion, but Keith McKellar
had a pass deflect off his knee. The ball tipped off Lon-
nie Marts’ hand to Eric Everett, who returned it to the
Kansas City 40.

Again, however, the Chiefs went nowhere and
punted.

And again, Reed victimized Pearson. The Bills’ star
receiver broke free down the middle in single coverage
to haul in Kelly's pass for a 53-yard score, making it
14-0.

“They were different calls, audibles, ust man-to-man
patterns,” Kelly said. “It was just a matter of him beat-
ing them.”

Which was no problem for Reed.

“Those routes are 1n the game plan every week,” he
said. “Jim saw a look out there that was perfect.”

Scott Norwood’s 33-yard field goal with two seconds
left in the half made it 17-0.

The Bills chnched it by marching 36 yards after Kir-
by Jackson’s interception to start the second half. Kelly
hit Lofton with a 10-yard TD pass

Thomas's 100 yards was his fourth straight playoff
game with at least 100 yards. Norwood also had 47 and
20-yard field goals. Ken Davis scored on a 5-yard run
with 4:57 left in the game to close out the Bills’ scoring.

It was 24-0 before the Chiefs got Barry Word’s 3-yard
TDrunlatein the third period and Vlasic hit Fred Jones
with a 20-yard scoring pass with 2:07 left in the game.

Bermudian overwhelmed by West Perry

By RYAN RAFFENSPERGER

‘Times Sports Correspondent

Ifthe Bermudian Springs Eagles
wrestling team had been as succes-
ful in the first six matches as they
were in the final six, the outcome of
Saturday’s match with West Perry
would have been in doubt.

But the most Bermudian could
manage in the first six bouts was a
draw at 135, and the visting Mus-
tangs waltzed to a 32-14 victory
Saturday afternoon.

“West Perry got some key wins
early on,” Bermudian head man
Dave McCollum explained. “They
got close wins at (119), (125), and
(130) That got them on a roll, and
very enthused. We were never able
to recover.”

West Perry jumped out toa quick
lead by virtue ofa fall at 112. Corey
Leigh pinned Bob Yingling in a
matter of 44 seconds and West Per-
ty had a quick 6-0 advantage.

The Mustangs picked up their
third straight win, the second by
fall, at 125. Adam Smith, who led
3-0 heading into the final period,
gave John Goddard no hope of com:
ing back as he pinned him 37 sec-
onds into the period.

The Mustangs kept the express
on at 130. Jay Thoman came within
an eyelash of pinning Mike Markey
in the first period. However, Mark-
ey hung tough and closed the 5-0
cushion to 5-4 early in the third per-
iod. Thoman dug deep and scored
seven of the final nine points of the

WRESTLING

match to give West Perry an 18-0

lead.

The Eagles finally got on the
scoreboard at 135 but didn’t make
up any ground in the process. Ber-
mudian’s Scott Lemin and Steve
Miller dueled to a 4-4 tie. Lemin
reversed Miller in the third to knot
the score at three. Miller took the
lead back moments later with an
escape, but was penalized for stall-
ing with just four seconds left forg-
ing the tie.

Bermudian finally got a victory
and seized some momentum with
succesive wins at 145 and 152.

Mark Keller broke the ice with
an entertaining win in the 145
pound class. Keller trailed 3-2 after
the first two periods. He gained the
win on a reversal with only 38 sec-
onds left and nearly got the pin
eight seconds later. He held on for
6-3 decision.

Dirk Bievenour kept things on
the upper hand for the Eagles as he
rallied for a 3-2 win over Brian
Peters. Bievenour scored all his
points in the final eight seconds of
the match. He escaped for one
point, and got a takedown with two
seconds left to steal the win.

Any shm hope that the Eagles
had at rallying was dashed as Ted
Shenk took care of Mike Roth, 9-2
giving the visitors an 18-point
cushion heading into the final three

matches.

Bermudian managed to win two
of the three remaining matches and
it took a “very, very, questionable”
decision, according to McCollum,
by the referee at 189 to deny Jason
Forsythe a win.

Forsythe was disqualified for
using an illegal hold that injured
Joe Hertzler 48 seconds into the
match.

“He described it as a twisting
hammer lock,” McCollum said. “I
think it 1s disheartening that he
lost that way.”

The Eagles fall to 2-3 overall and
McCollum is concerned that his
team 1s not wresthng up to their
capabilities

“This team 1s wrestling OK, but
not to their full potential,” he noted.

BY DAN CHRIST

The Bermudian Springs-Hanover wrestling match held Wednesday night at Bermudian High School was a tough outing for the Eagles. Although Bermudian Springs was expected to do well this season, coming off a Blue Mountain League championship and having the lowest wrestling score, they struggled against the Hanover team.

McCollum conceded to the team 2:40 in the second round, but he managed to salvage some of his pre-season hopes. "We've nowhere been better than the team," McCullough said.

The season has been bumpy for McCullough. He noted that the team has faced defections from his program. He started with 12 defections and 12 wins, but since Rip Lopez left the team this week, and following an annual match at Bermudian, this marks the first season at Bermudian that McCullough has had in the last 22 weeks.

headline: Eagles Overpower Warriors in Key Pins
summary: The Bermudian Springs Eagles defeated the Gettysburg Warriors in a wrestling match, securing key pins and ending a five-game losing streak.
cleaned_text:
The Gettysburg Times
Thursday, January 16, 1992

**Roth and Forsythe Record Key Pins in Match Against Eagles**

Gettysburg continued its wrestling season with a close match against the Bermudian Springs Eagles. The Warriors (5-4, 2-2) held onto the lead until the 152-pound match, where they were overpowered by the strong and highly skilled Eagles.

Aside from Mike Schultz’s win at 171 pounds, Bermudian Springs won every bout from pounds up to heavyweight, including critical pins by Keller, Roth, and Forsythe.

Gettysburg Coach Steve Allen was satisfied with the performance despite the slim defeat. “Overall I am happy with the way we wrestled,” said Allen. “Our guys wrestled well, and we haven't been this close to beating Bermudian Springs in years.”

Bermudian Springs Coach McCollum was relieved to end a five-straight-loss streak and was confident his team still had a winning attitude. “We lost five straight and losing can become a habit,” said McCollum. “Our kids could have easily folded in, but they showed a desire to win. It was a really good match, and we went out and got some pins which was really important.”

The teams have key matches next week—Warriors face a solid New Oxford squad (4-2) while the Eagles host Biglerville (5-3).

**Results**
*   **103 lbs:** Randy Rohler (G) won by forfeit.
*   **112 lbs:** Blaine Sanders (BS) pinned Bob Yeing (G).
*   **119 lbs:** Travis Griffie (BS) won by forfeit.
*   **125 lbs:** Justin Kerchner (G) dec. John Goddard (BS) 3-2.
*   **130 lbs:** Mike Markey (BS) dec. Bronte Wineka (G) 6-1.
*   **135 lbs:** Brian Keller (G) dec. Scott Lorin (BS) 6-4.
*   **140 lbs:** Chris Evans (G) dec. Ron Garey (BS) 16-3.
*   **145 lbs:** Mark Keller (BS) pinned Brian Selby (G).
*   **160 lbs:** Mike Roth (BS) pinned Chad Fouchie (G).
*   **171 lbs:** Mike Schultz (G) dec. Jason (BS) 7-4.
*   **189 lbs:** Jason Forsythe (BS) pinned Chad Hotiner (G).
*   **Heavyweight:** Curt Chronister (BS) dec. Wade Leedy (G) 7-2.

title: “Dave h
headline: Susquehannock Warriors Defeat Bermudian Springs; Trojans Overwhelm Suburban
summary: Susquehannock defeated Bermudian Springs in a league match, while other local teams including Gettysburg and Dover recorded significant victories.
cleaned_text:
Susquehannock Warriors Defeat Bermudian Springs in League Match

The Susquehannock Warriors rolled to their 10th win of the season yesterday with a 44-15 pounding of perennial Blue Mountain League power, Bermudian Springs. The Warriors, who own first place in the YAIAA Division at 4-0 (10-3 overall), secured pins from Steve Bilbie (112), Neal Bechtel (135), and Kari Ait (171). They also achieved a 13-9 victory at Bermudian Springs, where Bermudian got a fall from Jason Forsythe at 189. Susquehannock also won the junior varsity match, 45-27.

In other local action, the Mustangs were unable to score a pin as Waynesboro notched a non-league victory over the host team. Meanwhile, three Mustangs continued to improve on already impressive seasonal stats against the Indians last night. Jason Seirdzinski improved his record to .15-3.

In a separate matchup, Dover rallied to the Eagles in a battle between Northern County neighbors. Dover built a 25-14 lead on the strength of major decisions recorded by Larry Pequig (135) and Shawn Moul (145). An injury and a forfeit at 140 aided the Eagle's comeback. Northern's comeback started at 171 with Litzel winning a decision, followed by Dave Habowski at 171. Dover had to match and the heavyweights fought to a draw. The Eagles fell to 6-4 overall on the day.

The Trojans couldn't pace with the Warriors in this non-league fest. Gettysburg overwhelmed Suburban with ten pins while Brennan Binford (125), Tom Hauser (140), and Tobin Grant (160) all scored falls for the Trojans.

The Evening Sun / Tuesday, January 28, 1992 B—7

— Braves edge Bolts
in overtime, 55-53

Williams’ foul shots
provide difference

Jason Williams’ two foul shots
with 13 seconds left in overtime
lifted Gettysburg to a 55-53 victory
at Littlestown in junior high boys
basketball action Monday evening,

Littlestown got off a final shot in
an effort to force a second overtime
but it fell short.

Brian Fiene’s layup with 20 sec-
onds left in regulation sent the
game into the extra period for
Littlestown.

“We had our opportunities,” said
Littlestown coach Darrell Crabbs.
“We missed some layups and open
passes. We did some good things
too, but we just didn’t do it at the
end.’
Brian Lindsay led the way for
Gettysburg (10-3) with 18 points.

For Littlestown (11-5), Darrell
Crabbs had 19 points before fouling
out with 2:30 left in the overtime

period.
Gettysburg cruised to a 61-35
win in the seventh-eighth grade

ALL ROUNDUP

game. ony Jones scored 17 points
for the winners and Mike Rucker
had 12 for Littlestown.

quarter.

way.

Randy Hanson and 13 from Steve
Kuhn,

game.

Carbaugh with six points.

and Erin Rohrbaugh scored her 100th
point to help the Lady Canners to a
26-24 victory Monday in a junior
high girls basketball game.

Biglerville (6-9) led 23-12 enter-
ing the final quarter before Bermu-
dian made its run.

Sue Hull led the winners with 10
points while Kristy Thomas matched
that total for Bermudian (4-10).

In the seventh-eighth grade game,
Biglerville won 29-25. Brandy Wilt
led all scorers with 11 points for the
winners and Mary Peters had nine for

bs Littlestown (6-10) with eight.

ening and Terrance Jacobs combined

take its first lead at 21-20 and then led
at 33-29 with 4:33 remaining. From
that point, the homestanding Tigers
scored the first half’s final 11 points
for a 44-29 advantage. Five players

Attorneys expose Tyson’s strategies

By LISA LEVITT RYCKMAN
AP National Writer

INDIANAPOLIS — Attorneys in Mike
‘Tyson’s rape trial cracked the lid on their case
during jury selection Monday, making it clear
one key will be whether the boxer’s teen-age
accuser ever said “no” to sex.

“No’ means ‘no,” special prosecutor Greg Gar-
rison told prospective jurors. “If a woman says
‘no’ and a man forces her anyway, that’s rape. A
woman is in charge of her body at all times.”

Tyson, who was cheered as his arrived at the
courthouse, is charged with rape, criminal devi-
ate condiict and confinement. If convicted, the
25-year-old former heavyweight boxing champ-
ion’ could be sentenced to 63 years in prison.

Five jurors — one woman and four men —
were seated before court was adjourned Monday.

Among the men were a truck driver, a T-shirt
printer, a body shop parts manager and a spe-
cialist who works with learning disabled and
emotionally handicapped children. Two of the
men said they were sports fans who don’t follow
boxing, although they had seen Tyson fight on
television.

“Do you believe Mike Tyson is violent?” Tyson
attorney Vincent Fuller asked one man. “In the
ring, he is,” replied the man, who eventually was

Efficiently, Northern
disposes Bermudian

From staff reports

DILLSBURG — The Northern
Polar Bears won only six of 13 bouts

seated.

Judge Patricia J. Gifford estimated that jury
selection would take about two days, with open-
ing statements as early as Wednesday afternoon.

Tyson’s accuser, an 18-year-old Miss Black
America contestant, told police she met Tyson at
a pageant rehearsal on July 18 and went with
him to his hotel room, where in the pre-dawn
hours of July 19 he restrained her on the bed,
penetrated her with his fingers, performed oral
sex and raped her.

“Do you think rape is less acrime if the woman
used bad judgment?” Garrison asked the parts
manager.

“J don’t think she should be punished for bad
judgment,” the juror replied.

‘Tyson, who has often been accused of fondling
women, insists his accuser consented to sex.

“The woman knows what happened. I know
what happened. I’m innocent,” the boxer said
after an earlier court appearance.

During jury selection Monday, Tyson’s attor-
ney, Vincent Fuller, asked jurors about their
understanding of implied consent — the idea
that behavior could signal willingness, even
though the word “yes” had never been uttered.

Fuller, who successfully defended John Hinc-
kley in the shooting of then-President Reagan,
also questioned jurors about how they would

handle conflicting medical testimony, explicit
discussion of male and female anatomy and the
use of vulgar language.

‘Tyson stifled yawns, doodled on a pad and
occasionally conferred with his attorneys. Most-
ly, he stared ahead impassively or locked down,
leaning away from the defense table out of range
of a closed-circuit camera that carried the pro-
ceedings to reporters in overflow seating in a
nearby room.

Fight promoter Don King told reporters Tyson
did roadwork before going to court because “he is
getting prepared to do what he do best — that is
fight.”

‘Applause greeted Tyson as he sauntered into
the City-County Building on his way to court.

“This ain’t Mississippi, so you ain’t guilty yet!”
shouted a black woman, who refused to give her
name.

The trial has drawn news media from around
the world. Reporters were allocated half the
seatsin the 50-seat courtroom, leaving the rest of
the press corps to watch on the closed-circuit
television.

Despite the closed-circuit camera, state law
prevents the trial from being televised.

Some of the other seats in the courtroom are
reserved for the public. One spectator was in
place Monday at 6 a.m.

title: matches
headline: Local Wrestlers Shine in Jan. 31 Matches
summary: Local wrestlers achieved several victories and improved their records during the January 31, 1992, matches.
cleaned_text:
The Gettysburg Times
Jan 31, 1992

**Wrestling Action Highlights**

The wrestling matches featured several key performances. Jason Forsythe improved his record to 18 wins against only two losses, securing a pin over Waynesboro’s Kevin Fortney at 189 lbs with only seconds left in the bout. Meanwhile, Andy Basehoar upped his overall mark to 20-0 with a pin over Curt Chronister at heavyweight.

Mark Keller improved his mark to 16-4 for the Eagles with a fall at 3:52 over Steve Weagley at 145 lbs, and Mike Markey pinned Brian McLeamore in 2:48 at 130 lbs.

In other bouts, the Bolts picked up a forfeit win, and Gamble secured a pin in 3:06 at 112 lbs. At 119 lbs, Delone’s favored Fred Perez was nearly upset by Troy Rang but hung on for a tough 3-1 win. The Thunderbolts built a 27-3 lead with pins from Justin Phillips and Ti Smith at 130 and 135 lbs, respectively. Delone won five of the final seven bouts, including pins at 145 lbs, 171 lbs, and heavyweight from Tim Kuhn, Joe Elliot, and Jake McMaster, respectively. The Bolts wrapped up the day with a 7-5 decision win by Bryan Sentz over Jaimie Hoffman at 152 lbs. Marcus Watts then upped his mark to 17-2 overall with a pin over Steve Shupe at 160 lbs.

Earlier in the week, the Canners were noted to have had a win over Bermudian Springs on Thursday night.

title: “Dave
headline: Gettysburg Defeats Shippensburg in Wrestling Match
summary: Gettysburg defeated host Shippensburg in a wrestling match, while other local teams like Bermudian Springs and South Western also recorded key victories and losses.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Sun, Feb 02, 1992

York Vo-Tech (0-15) suffered a fall loss to Shawn Tate at 145 and an 8-2 decision loss to Garth Warfel at 130.

Dover copped five straight bouts between 130 and 145 to help defeat host Bermudian Springs (4-10). Bermudian Springs was helped by decisions from Travis Griffin at 119 and Brandon Dowell at 125, as well as a draw by Jason Orwick at 140 and a forfeit win from Jason Forgythe at 189.

In another match, Dover's Pat Warner scored a 5-1 win over Mike Hearney. Bermudian Springs was called out for an illegal slam by Mike Roth while leading Jim Smith of Dover by a 2-0 score.

South Western (4-10) got a 2-1 decision from Chuck Topper at 171 and a fall from Jon King at 189.

South Western coach David Cox stated, “They are ranked tenth in the state in Class AAA for a reason and that's why. They are tough. I knew that coming in after they had held both Dallastown and Northern down earlier this season.”

Cox continued, “We had some close ones. Aaron Sullivan had a tough 3-2 decision at 125, Jason Sierdzinski and Cumberland Valley's Chad Bear had a good bout at 112 (which Bear won 14-7), and Jason Homan was losing only 4-0 when he got barked and pinned in the 119 bout.”

Meanwhile, Gettysburg defeated host Shippensburg with the help of five falls.

Earning pins for the Warriors were Randy Rohler at 103, Brian Keller at 135, Justin Updyke at 171, and Matt Sprenk at 140.

title: Grove coach
headline: Local Teams Achieve Victories in February Wrestling Matches
summary: Multiple high school wrestling teams, including Bermudian Springs, Greencastle, and Spring Grove, recorded successful days with multiple pins and victories in February 1992.
cleaned_text:
**High School Wrestling Highlights: Multiple Teams Secure Victories in February 1992**

In several local wrestling matches reported on February 6, 1992, several high school teams achieved notable victories.

In a match involving Spring Grove, coach Kuntz noted that the team was hoping to score in the upperweights, stating that Dover wrestled them very tough. Kuntz highlighted the 130-pound match where Steve Smyser of the Rockets pinned Pat Warner. Smyser initially led 2 points into the last period, but a takedown by Warner tied the score at 3-3. Kuntz recounted the pinning sequence: “Steve escaped and when Warner went for a takedown, Steve stepped over him and planted him right on his back... The match was going back and forth, then he made one fantastic move and got the pin.” This was noted as the lone pin for Spring Grove. Other pins included John Lloyd (103), Ben Sterner (119), Brent Scott (160), and Robert Bair (189).

Elsewhere, the Hawks secured pins for Nate Becker (103), Jon Beckner (125), Matt Hart (160), and Todd Staub (heavyweight). In a key bout, Josh Conover edged out Gettysburg’s Blaine Sanders 2-1 at 112. Conover entered the match with an 18-3-2 record, while Sanders was 17-2. Hanover also took the JV match 6-4.

In the Blue Mountain League, Bermudian Springs had a successful day. Jason Forsythe improved to 19-2, and Mark Keller improved to 17-5 during the team's romp over winless James Buchanan. Coach McCollum pointed out that the best bout of the match occurred at 160, where the Eagles’ Jason Orwick held off Buchanan's Eric Horsh 6-4. After a scoreless first period, Orwick's reversal and Horsh's escape made the score 2-1. Additionally, Bermudian Springs' John Goddard (135), Ken Toner (171), and Curt Chronister (heavyweight) all recorded pins. Jay Claycomb secured a technical fall against Buchanan (0-12, 0-7).

In another matchup, Greencastle defeated Delone Catholic. The Blue Devils secured falls from Teddy Rice at 103, Sam Witmer at 130, Brad Etter at 135, Rich Downin at 145, and Jeff Reed at 152. Delone Catholic recorded wins from Fred Perez at 125, Tim Kuhn at 140, Steve Shupe at 160, Dave McCleary at 189, and Jake McMaster at heavyweight. Delone Catholic coach Bob Gano praised the effort, noting that "Jake had a heck of a match with Greencastle's Norman Hahn (a 4-2 decision) and Perez put a hurting on his opponent."

title: “Dave
headline: District III Wrestling Begins for PIAA State Tournament
summary: District III wrestling begins this weekend, sending 416 wrestlers to compete for 78 spots in the PIAA state tournament in Hershey.
cleaned_text:
The Gettysburg Times
By Jim Coleman, Sports Editor

There's only time for last-minute adjustments as District III wrestling gets underway this weekend at two sites. Four hundred sixteen wrestlers will be competing for 78 coveted spots in the PIAA state tournament next week in Hershey.

“You have to be ready to go mentally at this point. That plays a big edge. If you're not ready to go mentally, you'll be out quick,” said one coach.

In Pennsylvania, perhaps the high school hotbed of the country, it’s safe to say that most wrestlers are raring to go, and the talent level is at a peak.

The only returning state qualifier from last year is Biglerville’s Travis Byers, who won the AA section title at 103 lbs. Byers, a state quarterfinalist, faces solid competition, but he, along with everyone else, cannot overlook the early bouts.

“There is no easy road,” said Gettysburg Coach Steve Allen, who has three of his Warriors competing at AAA. “To get to states is a great accomplishment at this point.”

Bermudian Springs was the only area wrestling program to advance to states last year, but the field is loaded with possibilities this year. Among the top contenders is Blaine Sanders of Gettysburg. Only a sophomore, Sanders coasted through the AAA sectionals, taking the title with a variety of solid moves and heading to districts with a 23-4-0 mark at 112 lbs.

“Blaine is really resting well now,” said Allen. “He has the total package and the desire.”

Other notable competitors include Travis Byers and Shannon Forsythe of Bermudian Springs.
