title: “Dave h
headline: Bermudian Springs Wrestlers Qualify for State Championship
summary: Bermudian Springs wrestler Jason Forsythe and another athlete qualified for the PIAA State Championship after a successful District III AA finals weekend.
cleaned_text:
**The Gettysburg Times**
**Mon, Mar 09, 1992 - Page 8**

**LANCASTER —** District champion Jason Forsythe of Bermudian Springs, along with another athlete from the school, earned a ticket to the PIAA State Championship this weekend in Hershey. Five other Adams County wrestlers also concluded their seasons on the final day of the District III AA finals at Conestoga Valley High School on Saturday.

Martin earned his District gold medal at 140 pounds with a tough 3-0 win over senior Justin Shaffer of Daniel Boone. The Canner junior handed Shaffer only his second loss of the season.

Martin found himself in an unfamiliar position after the second period, tied at 0-0. The 29-2-1 Canner junior admitted, “I'm not used to that situation. Usually, I've gotten a takedown in the first or second period.”

Meanwhile, Forsythe had to win all three of his matches. Due to a stunning loss Friday night, the Bermudian Springs senior had to wrestle back from consolation rounds to qualify for states by capturing third place.

Forsythe was so distraught over his head coach that he was talking about the upcoming track season Friday night at the hotel. McCollum admitted that some of the emotional letdown was his fault.

“I think Friday night Jason was just a little nervous and uptight,” McCollum said. “He didn’t wrestle his style last night. Maybe we put too much pressure on him as coaches. But you can’t take anything away from Corey Gumby, who defeated Forsythe.”

title: 25 honor Hawk mentor
headline: Coach Conover Honored; Local Wrestlers Gear Up for Tournament Action
summary: Multiple high school wrestling teams and individual athletes are preparing for major tournaments, with Strasburg High's coach receiving a prestigious award and local competitors facing intense competition.
cleaned_text:
**Hanover Coach Conover to Receive Wrestling Honor**

Strasburg High coach Conover was named the Class AA Coach of the Year. The award, which is named after the late T. C. Hall, recognizes Conover for directing the team to a 25-0 dual-meet record, the District III Class AA Team Tournament championship, and the District III Class AA Section I Tournament crown. Conover has guided Hanover to a 25-10-2 dual-meet record over the past 21 seasons. Having already won two previous District III Class AA Coach of the Year honors, Conover is expected to receive his latest award at the District III All-Star Meet on March 18 at Manheim Central High School.

**Local Athletes Prepare for Tournament Action**

In other wrestling news, local athletes are gearing up for upcoming competitions.

At one event, spectator Beckner noted the intensity of the matches, stating, “It’s a little more excitement to have to push yourself in past months.” Head Coach Frank Pannebaker, who previously coached PIAA qualifiers at Rockwood Area High School in District V, advised his team, “You have to be ready to shoot bullets and let the chips fall where they may.” Despite the pressure, both he and Adams remain confident. Adams stated, “I think I can do pretty good up there if I just keep my head and wrestle because there is no person who has been to my weight class.”

Meanwhile, Biglerville Head Coach Mike Bucher praised Martin, noting, “He's doing all of the right things — going to bed early, and working hard.” Bucher added that Martin has a “real good mental attitude,” which will be bolstered by the presence of senior Travis Byers, who competed at the PIAA tournament last year and can familiarize Martin with the setting.

At the Bermudian Springs wrestling program, McCollum stated that the goal for his team is simple. Because Forsythe faces a defending PIAA runner-up, Jeff Abbott, in the first round at Burgettstown, the goal is to “get through Thursday, either by upsetting Abbott or beating a loser in the next round.”

**Upcoming Meet Pairings**

**Blue Mountain League-YCIAA Challenge Meet**

title: < + “Dave h
headline: Path to Gold: Wrestlers Head to State Championships
summary: The article profiles several wrestlers heading to the Pennsylvania State Championships, discussing their preparation, opponents, and the challenges of the double-elimination tournament format.
cleaned_text:
The Gettysburg Times
Wed, Mar 11, 1992 -Page 9

The road of experience has many twists and turns, and for each of the wrestlers heading to the Pennsylvania State Championships in Hershey, those twists and turns will be the springboard to a path of gold. For Forsythe, a 189-lb senior from Harings; for Trinity Martin, a 140-lb wrestler from Biglerville; and for Justin Adams, a wrestler from New Oxford, just finding the doorstep of Hershey Park is an achievement.

At this level, there isn’t much that separates the standouts from those who go home early. All three standouts share the extra edge and most can drift past it with past experiences as motivation.

“I was mentally ready to go at the kicks,” said Forsythe. “I learned that wrestling all the way up to the bout.”

Forsythe (30-4-0) shrugged off an early district upset and rallied to secure a spot in the state tournament.

His first round opponent in states is Burgettstown’s Jeff Abbott, a senior who enters the tournament with an unblemished 34-0 mark, along with sectional and district gold medals from the powerful Pittsburgh region. Abbott’s last loss was in the state finals last year.

“We're expecting the best,” said Jason’s coach. “I think Jason is in good spirits, and it’s always better to go up against a topnotch wrestler like that early because they usually get stronger as the tournament goes on, so that’s an advantage.”

Forsythe, however, has wrestled West York’s Jeff Wolfe (27-0) twice this season, and although he lost both times in close decisions, that experience should once again be a boost.

“I think Abbott has everything to lose being undefeated,” said Forsythe. “He has a lot more to lose than me.”

Jason’s brother Shannon, a freshman wrestler at Central Connecticut State, competed in states last year, and even getting a taste of the Hershey experience from the bleachers should again help out.

“Jason knows what he is up against,” said McCollum. “Going through the wrestlebacks at districts was a good experience for him, and that should help him at states. We're just hoping to get him through Thursday, but even the wrestlebacks are brutal. I mean, you could be facing a regional champ in the consolations.”

Forsythe and Biglerville’s Trinity Martin (29-2-1) wrestle in the Class AA tournament, which commences Thursday at 9 a.m., while Justin Adams (31-2-1) starts his day at 4:30 p.m. on Thursday with the Class AAA first round.

First round winners do not wrestle until Friday, while the losers head to the consolation bracket for another Thursday bout. It is a double elimination tournament continuing through...

along with sectional and district gold medals
from the powerful Pittsburgh region. Abbott’s
last loss was in the state finals last year.

“We're expecting the wor; e
best,” said Jason’s coach, “I
think Jason is in good spirits, and it’s always bet-
ter to go up against a topnotch wrestler like that
early because they usually get stronger as the
tournament goes on, so that 1s an advantage.”

Forsythe, however, has wrestled West York’s
Jeff Wolfe (27-0) twice this season and although
he lost both times in close decisions, that experi-
ence should once again be a boost.

“I think (Abbott) has everything to lose being

undefeated,” said Forsythe. “He has a lot more to
lose than me.”

Jason’s brother Shannon, a freshman wrestler
at Central Connecticut State, competed in states
last year and even getting a taste of the Hershey
experience from the bleachers should again help
out.

“Jason knows what he is up against,” said
McCollum. “Going through the wrestlebacks at
districts was a good experience for him and that
should help him at states. We're just hoping to
get him through Thursday, but even the wrestle-
backs are brutal. I mean, you could be facing a
regional champ in the consolations.”

Forsythe and Biglerville’s Trimty Martin
(29-2-1) wrestle in the Class AA tournament,
which commences Thursday at 9 a.m., while Jus-
tin Adams (31-2-1) starts his day at 4:30 p.m. on
Thursday with the Class AAA first round.

First round winners do not wrestle until Fri-
day while the losers head to the consolation
bracket for another Thursday bout. It1s a double

headline: Forsythe Advances at 189 After Showing Increased Confidence
summary: Jason Forsythe advanced in the 189-pound weight class by demonstrating increased confidence and strategic patience during his matches.
cleaned_text:
Jason Forsythe Advances at 189

The Gettysburg Times
Fri, Mar 13, 1992 - Page 15

Jason Forsythe advanced at the 189-pound weight class. His performance was marked by a newfound confidence, which he noted was key to his success.

“I felt a lot more confident,” Forsythe said, entering the second day of competition with a 31-5 record. “I was in a little more control than the first time. I had more confidence.”

Forsythe’s improved attitude was evident in the second period. When tied at 0-0 and in the down position, he used patience to gain a winning position. He noted that his opponent, Kurt Snyder, came with legs, which he found to be a problem. Forsythe explained that he had to wait for Snyder to loosen his legs before he could work on any move.

headline: Local Sports Update: Youth Leagues Grow and PIAA Wrestlers Face Setbacks
summary: The Recreation Board reported increased enrollment in youth sports leagues while local high school wrestlers faced early elimination at the PIAA Championships.
cleaned_text:
**Recreation Board Youth Sports Registration and PIAA Wrestling Update**

**Youth Sports Registration**
The Recreation Board's youth baseball and softball programs added 121 more players for its 1992 season after Thursday's registration session. The program added 29 players to the Small Fry (ages 12-13) League, 24 to the Pee Wee (ages 10-11) League, 18 to the Junior Girls (ages 8-11) Softball League, 16 to the Senior Girls (ages 12-17) Softball League, 13 to the Morning (ages 8-9) League, and 11 to the Teener (ages 14-15) League. Program totals now stand at 122 for the Small Fry League, 107 for the Pee Wee League, 77 for the Junior Girls Softball League, 64 for the Senior Girls Softball League, 56 for the Morning League, and 26 for the Teener League. Registration ends today with the 6:30 p.m. to 8:30 p.m. session at the Hanover Municipal Building. The cost is $5 per borough resident and $7.50 for all others.

**Frees Award**
WESTMINSTER, Md. — Western Maryland College senior tailback Eric Frees of Ephrata was recognized for his outstanding collegiate career with a Special Achievement Award.

**PIAA Wrestling Championships**
HERSHEY — The bad news from Thursday’s first two rounds of the PIAA Championships was that none of the five Hanover area high schools’ entrants won in the first round. However, some optimism emerged.

“I don't want to lose my head,” said one source. “But if we come back through (the consolation bracket), it’s better to lose the first day, the first round.”

This was the fate suffered by every Hanover area grappler: Hanover’s Jon Beckner at 119 pounds and Steve Wilder at 189; Biglerville’s Trinity Martin at 140; Bermudian Springs’ Jason Forsythe at 189; and New Oxford’s Justin Adams at 140.

The Gettyburg Times
Fri. Dec. 18, 1985

**Local Sports Highlights**

**Basketball**
In a recent contest, James secured a win for his team, though Trinity fell with a final score of 57-53. One player earned two points after being on the two time-point stripe. During the second quarter, he hit a 10-foot jumper, earned three points in the paint, and converted a three-point shot. By halftime, Ruffenberger scored a dooset, and the Warmer’s team maintained a lead. Although the team was held without points in the third period, the Warmer’s team managed to add to the score and not contest the quarter. The Shaunocks, the defending III Class champion, were not present for the game.

**Wrestling**
The wrestling matches featured several standout performances. In one bout, Gano and Ait were noted for their efforts, and Kane Warmer had what appeared to be his best outing. In another match, Mark Twain defeated Miller in the first match of the evening after two periods, and subsequently caught Miller in a rebound relation to pin him at 52 points. The wrestling action also featured McCullough, who provided strong protection for a 115-pound man. McCullough successfully put Haddon on his back, and the Warmer’s team maintained a lead in the competition.

The Patriot-News
Thu, Jan 07, 1993

Division III is next-door neighbor to Biglerville, another Blue Mountain League castaway, plus Northern, which has moved down from Division I. Gettysburg wanders into Biglerville on Jan. 20; usually a blowout, this could be a blowup.

The curious thing about Division III is that none of this year’s members were in the division last year before the conference expanded.

Here are the Division III capsules, in predicted order of finish:

**Wrestling and Sports Roundup: Local Teams Compete in 1993**

**Wrestling Highlights**

In one wrestling matchup, Trinity Martin and Donny Asper scored falls for the Canners, while Tony Johnson and Glenndon Forsythe recorded decisions, sealing the victory for Cumberland Valley over Biglerville. The Canners also secured a pin from Garrett Forsythe at 189 pounds. Biglerville was a 33-0 winner in the JV match.

Hanover Nighthawks swept the first seven bouts in YAIAA Division II action, defeating York Vo-Tech. The Nighthawks obtained falls from Jeremy Reck at 103, Nate Becker at 112, Josh Conover at 125, Jon Beckner at 135, Mike Corbin at 140, Matt Gilpin at 152, Eric Trimmer at 160, Mike Chance at 171, Mike Mummert at 189, and Matt Gass at heavyweight. York Vo-Tech was led by Garth Warfel, who edged the Hawks’ Brian Wilder at 152.

South Western struggled against the middle of Cumberland Valley’s lineup, as the Eagles won eight straight matches after Jeren Seidenstricker's win at 103 in a no-league matchup. The Littlestown upperweights also contributed, with Bryan Sentz (171), James DeGroft (189), and John Beachtel (275) all getting pins.

After a loss to Hanover on Wednesday, the Eagles (7-2) rebounded from a deficit, earning pins from Nathan Stock (125) and Mike Markey (145), and a technical fall from Mark Keller (160). Bermudian Springs won the next five matches, helping them get back on the winning track. The Bubblers’ Kevin Sanders, who had pinned a rival earlier in the season, needed a pin to win the team matchup.

“It was a rough match today,” said wrestling coach McCollum. “There were some surprises.”

Gettysburg rebounded from an early deficit to top Susquehanna Twp., bouncing back from a close loss to Biglerville on Wednesday.

**General Sports News**

Hanover’s junior high team raised its unbeaten streak to 24 matches with a 93-15 drubbing of York High on Saturday. After losing the first bout, the Falcons (5-0-1) won the next six, including pins from Duane Hare (75), Pea Long (85), and Josh Perkins (90).

The Hanover Rotary honored local high school athletes on Friday. Those honored included Penn State fullback Brian O'Neal, former Penn Staters Scott Fitzkee and Brad Scovotti, PSU quarterback Kerry Collins, and local athletes.

The Hanover Rockets (6-0) got pins from Derrick Henning (75), Kevin B (80), Craig Ayers (90), Marty (100), Josh Zeigler (130), Drew Little (165), and Chris Umbarger (heavyweight). Spring Grove remained undefeated, setting up a matchup with New Milford, also unbeaten, on Wednesday.

headline: Bermudian Springs Wrestlers Secure Key Wins
summary: Bermudian Springs wrestlers achieved notable victories, while other local teams, including the Canners and the Warriors, also had successful wrestling outings.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Bermudian Springs Rebounds with Pair of Wins

In a strong showing, Bermudian Springs secured a pair of victories with wrestlers Sean Slaven and a heavyweight who defeated John Beachtel. Slaven achieved a pin, while the heavyweight used a near-fall and takedown in the third period to rally for a 13-11 triumph.

Meanwhile, the Littlestown head coach, Dave Bowersox, noted that James DeGroft held a 16-5 lead when he was pinned by the Eagles’ Sean Slaven in the 189-pound bout. Bowersox also mentioned that Jason Bentzel nearly gained a fall at 130 pounds for Littlestown (8-10) before losing a 10-5 decision.

In other news, McCollum was concerned after his Eagles lost a close showdown with Adams County rival Biglerville. However, he expressed happiness after his Eagles swept Central York, 51-18, and Littlestown, 52-10, in a triangular meet at Littlestown. McCollum stated, “We didn’t have two real tough teams, but they wrestled real well. They came into practice hungry on Friday and we had a real practice day, and that was the same way the game was.”

The Nighthawks, who previously defeated Schuylkill Valley 128-16 and Wane Dauphin 84-52 in the season-opening match on December 12, got to the final. The Canners, in the other hand, had a tough final match, as 275-pounder Beau Kint’s pin sealed a 77-58 win over Mid-Penn Division II's Milton Hershey. The Canners topped Hershey 101-37 in the quarterfinals, a win that helps the Canners in their quest for one of the two automatic qualifiers.

Elsewhere, Mike Schultz remained undefeated with a 37-second pin at 171 pounds as Gettysburg crushed a visit from Chambersburg. The Warriors, who face Bermudian Springs on Thursday in a show-case for second-place in Mid-Penn Division II, also secured falls from Justin Kerc (135), Brian Keller (140), Andy Bi (145), and Chad Heffner (189).

headline: Local Sports Roundup: Grief, Football, and Wrestling Action
summary: The article covers multiple local sports events, including a discussion of grief and support, football praise, and detailed wrestling match results.
cleaned_text:
Lewkowicz spoke about the emotional toll of the recent events, stating, “This is a time to grieve... It’s like a time bomb has hit this school. It’s overpowering.” He expressed that his role as a coach has expanded to that of a friend and guidance counselor in lieu of the tragic news. Shue echoed this sentiment, noting, “As a coach, losing a ballplayer is like losing one of your own... This is where you have to go beyond being just a coach. You have to be a friend.”

In other sports news, Barry Streeter, head coach, praised a player: “Shannon’s a good runner, a tough kid. He fits well into our offense and I think he has a shot at much more playing time next season.” Forsythe, who has three years of eligibility remaining, admitted, “I have to admit that I thought I'd do better my first year... I never expected the players at [the opposing school] as good as they are. They are better than I imagined and a lot faster, too.”

The wrestling team also saw action. Hudnell reversed from a cradle and put Shue in a front headlock, but Shue escaped with the win. Randy Rohler notched a pin at 112 pounds when he caught Mark Irvin at the edge of the mat, rolled him onto his back for the fall. McCollum stated that the team felt strong at 112 pounds, so they took a wrestler at 125 pounds. At 119 pounds, Blaine Sanders pinned Jess Dowell, giving Gettysburg a 16-0 lead. McCollum noted that the Eagles did not expect to lose six points here either. Nathan Stock gave the Eagles something to cheer about when he pinned Rich Robinson in 51 seconds. The hoopla was short-lived, as Justin Kerchner won a major decision over Scott Heltzel.

headline: Championship Battles Heat Up for Berks Wrestling Teams
summary: The article details the intense competition and bracket predictions for the Class AA and Class AAA high school wrestling championships, highlighting the strong positions of Biglerville and Oley Valley.
cleaned_text:
The championship brackets for both Class AA and Class AAA wrestling are heating up, with several teams fighting for at-large berths. In the Class AA division, Biglerville (13-2-1) is in a strong position, having recently defeated Bermudian Springs and Milton Hershey. Meanwhile, Milton Hershey head coach Jim Weidman highlighted victories over Central Dauphin and the team that beat Palmyra, noting that the recent tie with the Cougars was a significant athletic achievement. For the at-large spots, Oley Valley is guaranteed a berth after defeating Tulpehocken. If Palmyra can beat Lower Dauphin on Thursday, they secure a spot; otherwise, Milton Hershey is next in line, provided they win against Newport, Lancaster Catholic, and CD East. Lampeter-Strasburg, last year's runner-up, is also in contention, needing to sweep Garden Spot and Cedar Crest. The Class AA quarterfinals are scheduled for Tuesday, February 23, and the Class AAA quarterfinals for Wednesday, February 24, with the semifinals and finals set for two weeks later at Hempfield High School.

headline: Bermudian Springs Dominates Mid-Penn III Meet
summary: Bermudian Springs secured a decisive Mid-Penn III victory over Mechanicsburg, with key wins reported by Mark Keller, Ben Stine, and Sean Slaven, keeping the team's hopes alive for the District III tournament.
cleaned_text:
The Gettysburg Times
Fri, Feb 19, 1993

At Biglerville, the Canners solidified their spot for a district berth in Class AA, either as the top spot in the Mid-Penn or the runner-up, with a 52-6 pounding of Shippensburg on Thursday night.

Meanwhile, at Mechanicsburg, the Eagles picked up two pins, a technical fall, and a forfeit victory on the way to a 39-17 Mid-Penn III victory over Mechanicsburg, Thursday night.

The Bermudian Springs team continued its strong run, securing a 39-17 Mid-Penn III victory over Mechanicsburg. Mark Keller improved to 23-1 on the season with a 7-2 win at 152 pounds, while Ben Stine (171) and Sean Slaven (189) both notched falls for the Eagles (12-4, 4-2).

At the wrestling meet, the coach, McCollum, was pleased with the team's performance. The team was still with a shot at an at-large berth in the District III Class AA tournament, which hosts Biglerville and Phillipsburg-Osceola, later that day.

In other matches, the following results were reported:
*   Matt Oberholzer (Gp) pinned Bran Dean (Ge) at 140 pounds.
*   Andy Borning (Ge) defeated Sam Witmer (Gr) 10-7 at 152 pounds.
*   Selby (Ge) pinned Chad Horst (Gr) at 160 pounds.
*   Zimmerman (Go) pinned Jake Myers (Gr) at 130 pounds.
*   Mike Schultz (Ge) pinned Matt Widmer (Gr) at 112 pounds.
*   Chris Robinson (Gr) defeated Chad Rinehart (112) by decision.
*   Norm Hann (Go) pinned Dusty Patterson (Ge) at 135 pounds.

Sectionals Grab the Weekend Spotlight

The weekend promises action across multiple sports. In baseball, action starts today at 8:30 at the Baltimore High School, featuring first pitches by Mindy Martin (25-4) at 140 and Chad Charles (25-4) at 120. Chad Charles is particularly noted for his strong performance.

The Baltimore High School will host several events, including competition from the Maryland High School Strippers and Delaware Catholic. The schedule includes Strippers at 8:30, followed by wrestling at 8:30, and consultations at 8:30. For the wrestling portion, a team of four wrestlers will participate at each weight class. The top four wrestlers at each weight will compete in the District III runner-up bid for Baltimore High School. The Strippers will meet next Tuesday to prepare for the team event.

Other key area wrestlers to watch for this weekend include Dalen Cathleen Cole (22-1) at 114.

In other sports, New Oxford's David Cajas (23-1, 100), Mark Cajas (22-1, 100), and Troy Huffman (24-1, 100) are expected to compete.

title: <> 1 of 6 mate
headline: Area Wrestlers Face High Stakes at District Tournament
summary: Area wrestlers are facing high expectations at the district tournament, where veterans and top performers will compete for medals and berths to the state championship.
cleaned_text:
Split Second Mistake Can Be Costly for Area Wrestlers

There isn’t any room for error, coaches preach, but for area wrestlers, 37 is a nice big number. With seven sectional winners heading to the mats, expectations are high.

“Obviously, I'm pleased with what we have going to districts,” said Biglerville coach Mike Bucher, who has eight wrestlers competing at the Class AA tournament at Conestoga Valley High School. “I think this year’s district field is extremely talented, and a lot of that is from our sectional. I think we'll see some state medals from this district.”

Along with Bucher’s Canners, New Oxford and Gettysburg also have eight wrestlers competing at districts. The Colonials and Warnors compete in the AAA field at Shippensburg University.

In the AA field, which gets underway at 5:30 p.m., wrestlers are immediately eliminated after a single match. While this can be difficult for younger wrestlers who haven't been here before, participants must go in with plenty of intensity. That is what separates the district champion from the district qualifier.

In both district fields, the top four wrestlers from each weight class receive medals, and the top three advance to next weekend's state competition at Hersheypark Arena.

Of the 37 area wrestlers, 12 are making return trips. Two of those—New Oxford's Justin Adams at 145 pounds and Biglerville’s Trinity Martin at 140 pounds—advanced to state competition last year.

“I think it’s a big mental advantage for those that have been here before,” said the Bermudian coach. “They know what to expect.”

The coach’s contingent includes district veterans Brandon Dowell (130 pounds).

title: The Gettysburg Times
headline: Old Dominion Wins National Tournament; Bermudian Springs Wrestlers Compete at State Level
summary: The article reports on Old Dominion's basketball victory over Virginia Commonwealth and details the performance of Bermudian Springs wrestlers, including Donny Asper's successful bout at the state tournament.
cleaned_text:
**The Gettysburg Times**
**Fri, Mar 19, 1993**

**Old Dominion Dominates Virginia Commonwealth in National Tournament**

NORFOLK, Va. — Keith Jackson scored 26 points Thursday as Old Dominion never trailed on its way to a 74-68 victory over Virginia Commonwealth in the opening round of the National Invitation Tournament. Old Dominion also saw freshman Odell Hodge contribute 16 points and 10 rebounds.

Virginia Commonwealth, meanwhile, trailed by as many as 11 points in the second half before cutting the margin to 70-68 with 39 seconds left on a layup by Eugene Kissourine. Kenny Harns scored five points in the 14-5 run. The Rams then fouled Jackson, who hit both free throws with 26 seconds left. After a turnover, the Rams fouled Hodge, who also made both foul shots for the final score. Sherron Mills, a senior playing his final college game, scored 24 points. Virginia Commonwealth’s McCoy had 16 points.

title: Buchner’s resignation is a step down after 12 years as Biglerville coach
headline: Buchner Steps Down as Biglerville Wrestling Coach
summary: Mike Buchner announced his resignation as head coach of the Biglerville wrestling team after 12 years, a move that will be followed by a managed transition.
cleaned_text:
Buchner’s Resignation Marks End of 12-Year Run as Biglerville Coach

By JIM Coleman

Mike Buchner’s decision to step down as head coach of the Biglerville wrestling team is a significant move for the area. Speaking to me Tuesday night, Buchner initially told me he planned to coach for three or four more years. However, after more than a year of deliberation, he made the announcement.

Buchner, who has worked with the school for 12 years, made the announcement alongside director Bill Ciole. They stated that the transition would be managed carefully to ensure everything runs smoothly. Buchner also noted that he and the team had achieved a tremendous amount of success this year. The last thing Buchner did was sit down at the annual medal show competition.

JIM Coleman
Sports Editor

Matt Finch made a head-to-head meal of some instant recognition, something that will be missed throughout the winter season.

Local Sports Roundup: Wrestling and Collegiate Highlights

The local wrestling scene remains highly competitive. At the regional level, the success of athletes like Mark Keller and Mike Markey, who both qualified for states after placing second at the district tournament, highlights the dedication required in the sport. Coach Dave Conaway noted that both men exhibited the qualities of hard work and desire. Similarly, Brian Keller, who rounds out the middleweights, had a strong season, including a gutsy mid-season overtime win. The wrestling program at Bermudian Springs continues to produce talent, exemplified by the coaching expertise of McCollum.

In local action, Camp Hill dominated its non-league matches, defeating Biglerville before rain halted play. Key victories included Zeb Grace defeating Biglerville’s Jason Rose in two tiebreaker sets, and George D’annunzio securing a win over McLain. Later, Camp Hill continued its winning streak against Littlestown, with victories recorded by Josh Shelienbe, Josh Kotzman, and Chris Firestone.

In other sports, the NCAA tournament saw Michigan's Wolverines continue their run, while the New York Knicks secured a victory. The article also noted the suspension of Detroit’s Alvin Robertson and the strong performances of players like Patrick Ewing.

The Eagles Should Be a Contender for the State Championship

By Ed Rigs

In high school wrestling, the ultimate goal is to compete in the state championship. The local team, the Eagles, is preparing for a tough season. While some athletes, such as Leah McCarthy, a sophomore at Springfield High School, have multiple goals for the State Championship, the team remains focused on its preparation.

The team is currently undergoing intensive training, ensuring that the 20 selected athletes are ready for the big game.

The schedule for upcoming matches includes:

In other matches, Branden Dewell earned a workmanlike 11-6 major decision at 160 pounds. Jeremy Alkerson competed at 145 pounds and defeated the Eagle.

title: the Los An;
headline: Bermudian Springs Coach McCollum Praises Team After Championship Finals
summary: Bermudian Springs coach McCollum praised his team's performance at the wrestling championship, which featured key victories and notable upsets, including Justin Shue's loss to Jeremy Seidenstricker.
cleaned_text:
**The Gettysburg Times**
**Monday, December 20, 1993**

*(Note: The original OCR text contained unrelated sports scores (football) alongside the wrestling results. The following text focuses on the wrestling coverage, which is the primary subject of the corrections.)*

**Wrestling Finals See Key Victories and Surprises**

At the recent wrestling championship, the Canners struggled in the consolation finals. While Glenndon Forsythe took third place at 152 pounds on an injury default, 119-pound Victor Oyola and 135-pound Tony Kehr were pinned in their final bouts.

McCollum, the coach from Bermudian Springs, was pleased with the team's performance, noting, “I thought we did real well. I was real happy to get five guys in the finals. We had some real tough competition.”

One of the tough competitors was Blaine Sanders of Gettysburg. The Warrior used his superior takedown abilities to win his section, and worked a bear hug to trip for his final two points in the 10-6 championship win.

The Eagles also captured two bronze medals. Freshman Joel Tallman defeated Jonathan Maley of Spring Grove 13-4 in his consolation final, and Tim Orwick captured his medal with a 9-2 decision over Lampeater’s Jeremy Sheaffer.

However, one of the biggest surprises of the day was Justin Shue’s loss to Jeremy Seidenstricker in the 119-pound final. Although Seidenstricker’s victory was not considered incredible, the way he dominated Shue, scoring seven points in the second period en route to a 7-1 win, was noteworthy.

“Seidenstricker is pretty tough,” said Smith. “I think they’re kind of back and forth. Really, Shue wrestled well in accepting the team trophy.”

In other news, the Bermudian Springs head coach, Mike Buck, assembled a strong lineup for the Canners, who may have more celebration ahead of them this season.

title: ‘ ont't] 3
headline: Bermudian Springs Struggles After Loss to Northern
summary: Bermudian Springs suffered a significant loss to Northern in the Mid-Penn II race, forcing the team to rebound to salvage its season.
cleaned_text:
**THURSDAY NIGHT FEVER**

**Bermudian Springs Struggles After Loss to Northern**

**By DAN CHRIST, Times Sports Correspondent**

Gettysburg—The wrestling action continued Thursday night with a significant bout between Gettysburg’s Blaine Sanders and Boiling Springs’ Chad Wivell, who tangled in a senior 130-pound match at Gettysburg High School.

The loss was a major blow for Bermudian Springs, knocking the team behind the eight ball in the Mid-Penn II race. Northern handed the Eagles a harsh wakeup call in York Springs, delivering a 42-13 drubbing. Bermudian Springs managed to win only four of 13 bouts.

“We have a good team,” said coach McCollum. “If Bermudian Springs is to salvage its season, it will have to rebound from last night's beating.”

The team struggled to score points early in the day’s action. Bermudian Springs did not score a single bout point until 1:01 into the 125-pound matchup. Prior to Nathan Stock’s 8-3 decision, Joel Tallman, Mark Irvin, and Scott Hudnell fell in lackluster efforts.

McCollum noted that a key to a Bermudian Springs victory was the middleweights. “We really have to wrestle a really balanced team,” he said. “We had to wrestle for thirty minutes the match.”

Meanwhile, Northern head coach Clair Rudisill stated that the team had been prepared for a tough outing. “We thought it was going to be a real dogfight,” Rudisill said. “We just thought we had to wrestle tough as a team. Our kids just wrestled exceptionally well.”

The Evening Sun
Fri. Feb 10, 1994

**Bermudian Springs Wrestling Team Has Wild Season**

The wrestling program at Bermudian Springs has been a wild affair, marked by strong performances and victories. The team recently secured a win over Bolling Spring, during which Eugene scored points across multiple weight classes, including 152 pounds.

The program continues to compete at a high level, with the team participating in various district matches. The coaching staff, led by McCollum, has guided the squad through a challenging season, demonstrating the depth of talent available to the school.

Orwick Helps Lift Bermudian Springs to Victory

By TERENY DAY
Evening Sun Spot Correspondent

YORK SPRINGS — Bermudian Springs High School wrestler Tim Owrick secured a win for the Eagles, marking coach Dove McMahon’s maiden victory in 158 pounds. Wrestling with an injured knee, Owrick defeated Livia Nielsen in Saturday’s tri-meet, which also included Central York. For the 16-year-old Owrick, this was his fifth victory of the season.

“I really don’t expect Owrick to pin. Timmy is not a big wrestler yet, but he has been found mentally strong. He works hard and has been very consistent with his attitude at Bermudian Springs,” a coach stated.

Although James “Joe” Gledcov’s pin at 189 pounds made it a five-point match, Tim was a number of times more experienced. “I know it was going to be the best Little Union team,” he said.

Other notable results included Bermudian Springs coach Dove McMahon defeating Marion Nathal by pinning him at 119 pounds. At 125 pounds, Bermudian Springs linters defeated Flury in the third pin. Jose Dewell, a defender of Two Pounds, also won at 125 pounds. Additionally, the Bermudian Springs coach defeated Dove Goodman at 100 pounds.

In other matches, Littleton State defeated Allison Doug Weigle (15) for Bermudian Springs at 160 pounds.

Time Sports Correspondent

In their latest dual meet test of the season, the Indiana University men's basketball team faced a challenging opponent, securing a 35-12 victory that included a 12-point win over New York Springs. The team gave Hanover its best effort, making the match highly significant for both sides.

In another contest, Nate Sanders and White Green won an eight-point game, which was also won by Chambly College.

headline: Local High Schools Compete in Wrestling Championships
summary: Multiple high schools competed in various wrestling matches and championships, with Cedar Cliff and Oley Valley highlighted in the local coverage.
cleaned_text:
The Patriot-News
Sun, Feb 20, 1994

**Team Championships Quarterfinal Action**

The Bear Bears could only find two wins against the Division I champions. In a 145-pound match, Jeremy Brent Johnson secured a 6-1 decision against Tony Carricato.

In other news, it was all Cedar Cliff. They can turn it around in a hurry, and they'll be in for a tough day at Cedar Cliff in the 3-AAA finals.

Oley Valley's semifinal triumph was achieved by Wilson, who knocked off Oley Valley in a 30-27 victory, helping the puzzle of the District 3-AA championships fall into place.

The Patriot-News also reported on several other local matches:

Lower Dauphin tuned up for its 3-AAA Team Championships match-up with Wilson by shutting out Carlisle 62-0. Carlisle missed two good chances to snap the shutout, but Steve Poleshak stopped Adam Greenway 7-6 at 135, and Jeromy Burroughs picked up an overtime triumph over Kevin Patton at 189.

Waynesboro nipped Red Land 30-28. The key match was at 140, where Waynesboro’s Dana Weber knocked off Red Land's Steve Abraham 4-1 in a battle of twice-beatens.

In a wild affair at Newville, Big Spring beat Springs 32-25. During the match, a coach was ejected for the first time after he exploded at an official for two stalling calls in the final 16 seconds. Those calls—the first a warning, the second a point—forced a 4-4 overtime that Orwick eventually lost 5-4 when Finkey was awarded an escape in the second overtime period.

Regarding the incident, McCollum stated, “There didn’t seem to be any loss of control [on the part of Orwick]. It just came to a head, and I erupted. But I think I had a good reason to be angry.”

title: Evening Sun Photo by Scott Holloway
headline: Biglerville Wrestlers Achieve Upsets and Advance to Semifinals
summary: Biglerville wrestlers achieved several upsets in the quarterfinals, advancing to the semifinals and facing West York in crucial head-to-head matches.
cleaned_text:
**Evening Sun Photo by Scott Holloway**

**18s Pops Through the Legs of Biglerville’s Victor**

**The Evening Sun**
**Sat, Mar 05, 1994 - Page 17**

With 71 points, the Bulldogs have a solid second place. Bermudian Springs is in third with 48 points.

Bermudian Springs pulled a few upsets in the quarterfinals when Joel Tallman and Scott Hudnell advanced at 103 and 112, respectively. Tallman knocked off fourth-seeded Matt Goode of Greencastle, while Hudnell, with a 2-10 mark entering the tournament, defeated 20-match winner Ben Miller of DeLone 9-8.

“The early rounds are usually where the upsets happen,” said the Bermudian Springs coach. “Scott didn’t care about this kid. He didn’t even know his record. He didn’t know a thing about him. He just went out and wrestled real hard.”

Biglerville’s Victor Oyola pulled one of those upsets at 112 when he pinned Littlestown’s Nick Kress, the fourth seed. That was the only weight where a two-seeded wrestler lost.

Oyola led a charge of 11 Canners into the semifinal round as the host squad lost just one time. Todd Kint (171) was forced to default in his opening match. With the exception of byes to Chris Marshall (125) and Donny Asper (145), every Biglerville wrestler won by fall.

“Our kids were aggressive going for the pins,” said Canner coach Wes Bard. “I wonder if we spent a whole six minutes on the mat (combined). We wanted to get to districts and not show West York too much.”

West York, who defeated Hanover in the first Class AA Team title round, logged 11 wrestlers alive with 160 points. Josh Clouser was a late withdraw from the event.

“We've got some head-to-matches tomorrow against West York,” Bard said. “We have to win the head-to-heads.”

Other matchups include Biglerville's Tony Kehr vs. West York's Dave Gal, and Canners’ Brian Pitzer vs. Billy Feeser at 140.

title: “Dave h
headline: Bermudian Springs Stock Wins District Title
summary: Bermudian Springs' Nathan Stock won the District title at 119 pounds, while Biglerville wrestlers secured multiple victories in the team competition.
cleaned_text:
Nathan Stock Wins District Title; Biglerville Wrestlers Shine

The State Championship tournament saw several wrestlers advance to the States. For Biglerville, John Weible, Chris Marshall, Brian Pitzer, Donny Asper, and Tony Johnson all moved on to the next round. Meanwhile, Bermudian Springs’ Nathan Stock extended his championship run.

Despite an impressive showing, the Canners fell 1.5 points short of Hanover’s 102.5 team points, finishing in second place in the team competition. Banden compiled 28 team points.

Hanover’s team win rankled some of its competitors, especially considering a move which Section 1 Tournament Director Mike Bucher said was made at the District III level. Biglerville coach Wes Bard said he knew as early as last summer that Hanover wanted to move out of Section 1 in order to improve its chances of winning the District title. Hanover’s request was granted, and wrestling out of Section 2, the Nighthawks advanced their entire 13-man team to the tournament—one reason: to win this tournament. And they won the tournament.

“That’s okay. We've got another goal. We're aiming for some high places at States,” Stock said.

After a disappointing fourth-place finish at last week’s sectional, Stock felt inspired to get fired up this week. He went on to capture his District title at 119 pounds, a remarkable achievement after having to face his coach in the safeating room last week.

Stock’s semifinal match saw him dismantle Oley Valley’s Mike Calcagno 16-9, utilizing eight near-fall points. In the final, Stock faced Hanover’s Nate Becker. Stock had defeated Becker in a dual meet earlier this season, and he duplicated the result again on Saturday. Stock’s victory was attributed to his excellent speed and footwork, particularly when he hit two third-period takedowns on spins, including the clincher with four seconds remaining.

Meanwhile, Biglerville sophomore Brian Pitzer repeated his success, proving to be the 130-pound District I-AA champion. Chris Marshall needed the full six minutes to defeat West York’s Matt Sprenkle in the 125-pound championship rematch. The Canners stunned Sprenkle in the first-period pin, but Sprenkle learned from the loss and came out with a new strategy. Marshall countered Sprenkle’s attack with a hard-time generating counter-takedown, securing the victory.

title: there,” Agassi
headline: Bermudian Springs Junior Stock Makes Tournament Comeback
summary: Bermudian Springs junior Nathan Stock overcame a poor sectional performance to achieve a comeback victory in the South Central Regional Tournament, while other wrestlers like Michael Chang and Agassi also competed successfully.
cleaned_text:
Nathan Stock, a junior from Bermudian Springs, entered the South Central Regional Tournament after a disappointing sectional tournament, where he finished fourth. Stock, who had previously been the top seed and top-ranked wrestler in the district, stated that the experience was a major mental challenge.

“I had a real bad weekend last weekend and I didn’t want to have another one this weekend. It was more mental, more emotional,” Stock said.

In the tournament, Stock started out emotional but quickly gained momentum. In the semifinals, he jumped out to an early lead against Mike Calcagno before the Lynx wrestler mounted a comeback. Stock maintained his composure and secured a 16-9 victory, setting up a match against Nate Becker.

Stock recalled that his previous win over Becker in the dual meet did not give him the impression that the title was guaranteed. He focused on his performance, noting that while he had opportunities to score, he was able to complete his offense.

Meanwhile, other wrestling action was reported. Michael Chang, the No. 6 seed, joined Agassi in the fourth round by rallying to beat Alberto Berastegui of Spain with a score of 1-6, 6-4, 6-2. Chang overcame three break points in the second set, which he was trailing 0-3, 15-40 in.

In another match, Agassi completed a near-perfect performance, defeating Boris Becker with a score of 6-2, 7-5.

The Gettysburg Times
Sat, Mar 19, 1994 -Page 13

...competition with an 8-4 loss to North Star’s Brad Kimmel in the second round of consolations. Jarrod Ellis squeaked by Stock 8-7 in their quarterfinal match.

After his fourth-place finish at sectionals, though, it didn’t appear Stock would be making the trip to Hershey. The Eagle junior, who finished the year with a 28-6 mark, bounced back and took first place at Regionals to earn his way to states.

“Stock was really disappointed after the sectional tournament, and I wasn’t sure he was going to be able to bounce back,” Springs head coach McCollum said. “He set his sights on the NCAA. He came back and he worked real hard. He showed a lot of improvement.”

The article also mentions a wrestler who spun for a takedown with 55 seconds left to wrestle but came up short and bowed out of his first state tournament.

McCollum continued, “I think he’s frustrated with himself, because he probably expected to do better. It’s a taste of states. I hope this leaves a sour taste in his mouth and makes him work harder for next year, and gives him the determination he needs to come back next year and get in the medal round.”

“I think he had a great tournament. It would have been nice to get into the medal round and take something home. But I think he’s taking something home with him now that might mean a little bit more.”

title: Dec 09, 1994, page 13 - The Gettysburg Times at Newspapers.com™
headline: Squires Defeat Eagles 45-18 in Big Win
summary: The Delone Catholic Squires defeated the Bermudian Springs Eagles 45-18, securing a big win despite the Eagles struggling with injuries and lineup issues.
cleaned_text:
The Delone Catholic Squires defeated the Bermudian Springs Eagles with a decisive 45-18 victory on Thursday night, dropping the Eagles' record to 1-2.

The Squires established a significant 24-0 lead early in the match, securing pins on the first four boards before adding another pin to take a 10-4 lead. The Eagles managed some points in the quarter through a jay-wing weight and a forfeit by Dohne at home. However, the Eagles were caught off guard by a Dohne line change, which was exacerbated by the unavailability of a new Squire starter.

Squire coach Bob Gane spoke about the team's performance, noting that the Squires had faced challenges, particularly regarding injuries to wrestlers like Freeman Besson and Daniel Besson. Gane stated that without these injuries, the team would not have had to forfeit a weight.

The match was the Eagles' second in as many days, and they also struggled with missing key members from the lineup. Eagles coach Dohne commented that the Squires had a genuinely good team and were not the type of opponent they had expected, nor were they able to maintain the necessary intensity.

"I think the kids really trained hard," Dohne said, acknowledging the effort shown by the team, particularly after the difficulties faced on Monday night.

title: The Gettysburg Times
headline: Eagles Edge Out Victory in Close Wrestling Tournament

The article highlighted the heavyweight bout, which was expected to be a close contest. Bob Itterly, a senior, won the match with a 28-26 decision, defeating Mat Robinson.

“Every match counted, and we knew it was going to be a real barnburner,” one coach stated. “Bobby doesn’t really have anyone to work with in the training room. You wonder how Bobby’s condition is going to be, but he came through tonight.”

Itterly was down 2-0 early in the bout, but he managed a quick escape and takedown, bringing the first period to a 3-3 tie. In the second period, Itterly gained another escape, securing the victory.

The most action was played out by the lighter weight classes. Coach Dave Conaway moved Ben Harrington down to 112 pounds in hopes of a sure win, but Eagle Scott Hudnell changed those plans by earning a major decision victory of 14-6.

Conaway reflected on the competition, stating, “I think I made mistakes in a couple of my matchups. I thought the move with Harrington and 119-pounder Jarrod Adams would be a good one, but it backfired.”

McCollum expressed his satisfaction with the team, noting that it was great to have both his 112-pounder and 119-pounder, Joel Thallman, back in the lineup. He added, “It’s nice to have Hudnell and Thallman back because we are very weak without them. Hudnell had a key win. He made it to…”

title: The Gettysburg Times /
headline: Northern Springs Downs Eagles, Remains Undefeated
summary: Bermudian Springs Northern defeated the Eagles 47-15 on Thursday night, maintaining their undefeated record.
cleaned_text:
The Gettysburg Times

**Northern Springs Downs Eagles, Remains Undefeated**

By OWEN HOFECKER
Times Sports Correspondent

Bermudian Springs Northern hosted Bermudian Springs on Thursday night and remained undefeated after downing the Eagles, 47-15. The Polar Bears are now 3-0, while the Eagles fall to 2-3.

Northern took the lead in the match on the first bout when Nate Sheaffer secured a first-period pin against Nick Stock. They never relinquished their lead.

The second bout, at 112 lbs, proved to be one of the most exciting of the match. Northern coach Clair Rudisill had his strategy working well when he dropped Todd Yohn, who usually wrestles in the 119-pound weight class, down to 112 pounds to wrestle Eagle Scott Hudnell. Yohn was victorious, 6-5.

“Sullivan and Dowell had a classic bout that went right down to the match,” said a coach. “Jess Unought thought he had the match 3-2 after the third period, but he didn’t realize he only had two points in the third period. He thought he had three. Then it went to the overtime period where he lost.”

Northern won the next three bouts at 140, 145, and 152, putting more points on the board, as well as getting their only win in the 160-pound class when Doug Weigh competed.

title: Thunderbolts Allen Shipley and
headline: Thunderbolts and Eagles Shine at Wrestling Meet
summary: The article details the results and highlights of a local wrestling meet, featuring victories for the Thunderbolts and the Eagles, and noting the strong performance of Bermudian Springs.
cleaned_text:
Thunderbolts Allen Shipley and Adam Hawk picked up two pins at 171 pounds and 189 pounds, respectively.

The results included: 103 pounds - Chad Roser (L) forfeit; 112 pounds - Paul Mummert (L) forfeit; 119 pounds - Tim Jones (L) forfeit; 125 pounds - Andy Basehoar (L) forfeit; 130 pounds - Stevie Jackson (ST) dec. Brian Miller (L), 5-2; 135 pounds - Vanani Ogwa (ST) maj. dec. Bryan Christ (L), 18-5; 140 pounds - Chad Masemer (L) forfeit; 145 pounds - Tom Collins (L) forfeit; 152 pounds - Shawn Smith (L) forfeit; 160 pounds - Grady Brown (L) forfeit; 171 pounds - Allen Shipley (L) pin Justin Mountz (ST), 5:49; 189 pounds - Adam Hawk (L) pin James Colston (ST), 2:38; and Hut. - Ron Hal St) pin Cit Guise {1}.

Mummert’s teammate Ryan Wentz made shorter work of his opponent, pinning the Bobcats’ Jason Barry in 1:12. New Oxford’s Brian Mayers completed the trio when he pinned Matt Mitchell of Northeastern at the 4:45 mark in the third period. With just five matches remaining, the Colonials led 40-5.

Sam Elias accepted the plaque as the coach of the league champion Eagles.

In another match, Joe Stambaugh took a 4-0 first-period lead and stayed out of danger the rest of the match, coming away with an easy 11-3 victory.

“This was the second time this year that Nathan had to wrestle him [Stambaugh],” said a coach. “It’s tough to kid more than once, but Nathan responded to that challenge and wrestled a very, very good match.”

Springs’ Josh Hinkle continued the rout in the 130-pound weight class with a pin at 3:03 of his match. Jess Dowell did not land a pin, but he was able to rack up enough points to score a technical fall in the closing seconds of his match, 7-0.

The final match of the night took place at 189 pounds, and it appeared at first that the matchup might provide the evening’s most exciting bout. Ben Price of Bermudian and Mechanicsburg’s Nathan Pigott closed the first period tied 4-4. Pigott worked Price to his back and scored the first two points of the second period. The tables turned quickly, though, as Price escaped and forced a takedown. Price nearly pinned Pigott with a cradle at the end of the second period and at the start of the third, then manhandled his way through to victory.

With the victory, Bermudian Springs evened its record at the top. Coach McCollum seemed confident that the Eagles are headed in the right direction.

“Our kids worked hard all week. I was very glad to see the kids wrestle so well tonight.”

**'Hungry' Bermudian Springs Wrestling Team Prepares for Season**

By DAN CHRIST, Sports Correspondent

The Bermudian Springs wrestling team, nicknamed The Eagles, is reportedly "hungry" as they prepare for the season. The team recently wrestled against the Rockets last Thursday, followed by a match last Saturday that was postponed due to area weather.

During a recent visit, the team was observed working hard in practice. Coach McCollum noted the intensity, stating, "They're hungry to get right out of it in York Springs. Bermudian was whipping itself into a frenzy in the practice room."

The team has been working diligently, having secured its hold on third place in a recent tournament. Coach McCollum also highlighted the team's overall readiness, noting that the wrestlers "haven't had any for his 103-pound opening bout as [they are] in the best shape of the year."

The team's success is attributed to individual efforts, such as the superior footwork displayed by wrestler Craig Snyder. Freshman Eagle Nick Stock was also mentioned as part of the team's roster.

Dave Began His Career Late

“Beau has come a long way having wrestled only four years,” said Bard. “As a freshman, he was only 4-18, but he improved his record each year until he was 23-7. He’s very strong, and is a typical heavyweight in that he’s mild-mannered and very coachable.”

Three Gettysburg Warriors made the All-Times Team.

Junior Justin Shue finished the year 25-5 and has a shot at breaking the century mark next year. “Justin wrestled well for 90% of the season,” said coach Perry Smith. “He dropped a bit in the post-season. In February, he was rated number one in the District. I think he peaked just a little too early.”

Senior Dusty Patterson ended the season at 21-5. “He did a great job this year,” said Smith. “He was only 3-18 as a sophomore, but after working out a lot with Ken Haines, Ken absolutely sets the standard in everything he does,” Smith continued. “He has a great work ethic and knows how to focus on his goals. He’s the hardest worker I’ve ever coached, and I'll miss him tremendously.”

The Bermudian Springs Eagles have three wrestlers on the team.

Nathan Stock, a 25-4 senior, is the first Eagle named to the team. He was one of the Eagles’ tri-captains and the class president. “Nathan,” said coach McCollum, “loved to stand up. He's the hardest worker on the team and was very coachable. He was a three-year starter and led the team with 11 regular season pins.”

Junior Jess Dowell ends the season at 27-5. He has been to Districts three times and ended up fourth in the District this year. “We were happy to see him become the section champ,” said McCollum. “He was named to the District II All-Star Team. He has the biggest heart, and by that, I mean he’s the most determined of all the team members.”

Itterly plans to Shippensburg University.

Senior Pat Hofmann of Delone is the Squires’ entry on the team. The 130-pounder made his way to Districts but did not qualify for the State tournament. He ended the season at 22-4.

New Oxford’s Bob Topper, a 171-pound junior, was also named to this year’s All-Times squad. He ended the season at 30-3 and has come a long way toward developing his potential. “Bob is a good student and a hard worker,” said coach Dave Conaway. “He found himself this year and realized he can wrestle with the best.”

The final member of the All-Times Team is New Oxford sophomore Jarrod Adams. He is 26-7 for the season, and 48-17 in his short career.

title: The Gettysburg Times
headline: Eagles Face Youth and Inexperience as Season Begins
summary: Bermudian Springs wrestling coach McCollum anticipates a difficult start to the season due to the team's reliance on inexperienced newcomers.
cleaned_text:
**The Gettysburg Times**
**Wed, Nov 29, 1995**

**By Tim Lambert**
*Times Sports Writer*

**Editor's Note:** The Times will preview Adams County high school teams in its next three issues.

As the wrestling season approaches, the Bermudian Springs Eagles are facing a challenge between youth and inexperience. Looking at his roster, coach McCollum noted that while the team has eight returning lettermen from last year’s 28-5 squad, they must fill five weight classes with newcomers, two of whom may be freshmen.

“We have to fill those holes and hope those younger kids come along quickly so we can compete,” McCollum said. “Our first month is going to be tough. I think this year’s team will be a lot like last year’s. We will start out slow and hopefully come on in January and February.”

The team is coming off a 27-5 season, which included a fourth-place finish in the District I Tournament. One standout athlete is Dowell, who competed at 140 pounds after barely missing out on the PIAA tournament.

title: te"We were more fortunate than some
headline: Warrior Team and Bermudian Springs Win Key Wrestling Victories
summary: Multiple local wrestling teams, including the Warriors and Bermudian Springs, secured significant victories and pins in their respective matches.
cleaned_text:
Warrior Team Wins Big; Bermudian Springs Takes Mid-Penn Title

Warrior coach Perry Smith stated that his team was "more fortunate than some." He noted that while he was happy with getting in three times, compared to some schools, they were "kind of fortunate."

During practice sessions, the team demonstrated effective training. Pat Riggs at 112, Justin Shue at 140, Chris Haines at 145, Jim Temchock at 160, Kevin Keller at 171, and Mitch Weikert at 275 all won by fall in the first period of their respective bouts. Justin Shue and Chris Haines seemed to be competing for the quickest pin; Shue pinned his opponent in just 20 seconds, but Haines beat him out, getting his pin in 19 seconds.

Jesse Martin at 152 and Drew Leedy at 139 also secured pins for the Warriors. Martin was winning his bout 26-8 when he pinned Luke Hammond at 152. Similarly, Leedy was ahead 11-1 before pinning Dave Clever at 139. Wes Winters at 103 and Seth Hurlbert at 119 won by forfeit.

The Rockets picked up two victories by pin. At 125, Mario Angehni pinned Chuck Stockman at 135, and at 135, Jon Molchan pinned Scott Angell in just 57 seconds. In the only bout to go the full six minutes, Warrior Mark Dutrow defeated Aaron Hurunan at 116.

"In every bout that was decided by fall, the wrestler who got the first takedown emerged the winner," Smith said. "That’s something we really stress. I think in high school wrestling, about 92 percent of the time, the guy who gets the first takedown usually wins. We put a lot of emphasis on that, and we hope it pays off."

Meanwhile, the Bermudian Springs wrestling team picked up its first Mid-Penn IIT victory on Thursday with a 37-21 win over Greencastle. The Eagles now stand at 3-4 overall and 1-2 in the league. Coach [Name not provided] said, "Losing hike we did last night (to Gettysburg) was good for us. To up a win here."

During the match, Jess Dowell wrestled at a weight [weight class not specified], beating Mike Ridge, whom the coach felt was one of Greencastle's best wrestlers. Along with Dowell’s major decision, Eagles Shawn Gebhart, Matt Nickey, and Joel Thallman all picked up pins.

In other local action, the Littlestown Maine team won its match. Chad Masemer won to move to 10-0 on the year, and Adam Hawk had to come back to win in overtime at 189 pounds.

In junior high wrestling action, Lattlestown (2-2) beat homestanding York Suburban 86-9, as Dan Sponseller picked up a pin in just eight seconds. Also getting pins for the Thunderbolts were Tim Bowersox, Chad Weaver, Jessie Dickinsheet, and Bobby Whiteford.

**After Ugly Loss to Hanover, Bermudian Springs Rolls Past**

By Andrew Peterman

headline: Nighthawks Win District Championship; Henson Twins Secure Perfect Records
summary: The Hanover Nighthawks won the District III-AA Section 1 championship, led by individual titles from Jeremy Reck, Joe Henson, and Josh Henson, who all achieved perfect 30-0 records.
cleaned_text:
Hanover Nighthawks Win District Championship; Henson Twins Secure Perfect Records

Hanover, PA — The Nighthawks wrestling team soared to a District III-AA Section 1 championship at Delone Catholic High School on Sunday, February 25, 1996. The team finished with 183 team points, securing first place and advancing to the next sectional championships at Conestoga Valley.

The Nighthawks' success was highlighted by three individual champions: Jeremy Reck (119), Joe Henson (140), and Josh Henson (145).

Jeremy Reck, who defeated York Suburban’s Chad Groover in the finals, captured his 133rd career win, eclipsing the previous record held by former South Western grappler Joey Wildasin. "It feels great and it’s overwhelming," Reck said. "I can't believe it. I had more confidence tonight knowing I could break the record."

The Henson twins dominated the event, both winning their sectional bouts en route to titles. Joe Henson defeated Green Castle’s Mike Ridge via technical fall, while Josh Henson earned a pin in his championship bout. The twins now share identical 30-0 records this season.

Other notable performances included:
*   Ji McDowell (130), second-seeded, defeated West York’s Chad Luckenbaugh, winning 4-3 in the championship bout. McDowell is now 27-0.
*   Ben Price, fifth-seeded, captured the title at 171 with a second reversal in a 4-3 victory. Bermudian Springs coach McGee said, "Ben's amazing. He’s only been wrestling two years in high school, and he’s just starting to come into his own."
*   Delone Catholic captured third place in the team standings with 108 points, featuring Ben Miller, who won the 135-pound finals with a 13-6 decision.
*   Other sectional champions included Littlestown’s Chad Massemer at 160, and West York's Eric Lehman at heavyweight.

Coach McCollum expressed satisfaction with the team's performance. "We weren't outclassed. I'm pleased with the performance," he stated.

Mike Dowell Advances to State Finals After Quarterfinal Victory

Mike Dowell advanced to the state quarterfinals after defeating a tough opponent in the semifinals. Dowell, who is making his first trip to the state tournament after finishing fourth at the previous year's district meet, is aiming to become the Bemudian Springs state champion.

In another match, Cassidy Shults, who was trailing Pitzer 5-1 late in the third period, reversed the Biglerville star onto his back for a five-point move. Pitzer, the only four-time District 3-AA champion, was later eliminated from medal contention in the consolation round.

Regarding other matches, Shingara stated, “I don't know what I have, but I've had it since the morning quarterfinals.” When asked what was bothering him, he replied, “I just hope I feel better today. It feels good to be back in the finals, but it'll feel better if I win.” Shingara, who was a runner-up to Biglerville’s Chris Marshall at 125 pounds, had a strong showing.

In a separate bout, Dowell controlled the action from the start, taking down his opponent six times. The Eagle, who has allowed just five points, was unable to counter the takedowns.

Coach McCollum commented on the wrestling style of one competitor, noting, “The thing about Jess is that he’s very hard to score on and he’s always in good position on his feet. Very seldom do you see him make a mistake.”

Other matches included Conover’s first-period takedown standing up against Dimm in a quarterfinal round. Weidman, who will compete for third in today’s consolation final round, built an early 8-4 lead on Walker but could not hold it. Walker eventually prevailed on an overtime takedown. “I don't know what it is, but I've been behind in most of my bouts here. I fall behind, but I'm never out of it,” Walker said.

East Pennsboro’s Tim Martz dropped his quarterfinal at 135 pounds but rallied with three wins in the consolation rounds to earn a shot at third against Line Mountain’s Mike Martz.

title: The Gettysburg Times
headline: Bermudian Springs Eagles Aim for Championship Title
summary: Bermudian Springs Eagles coach McCollum and head coach Bob Gano are focused on developing the team to win the Adams County championship title in the Mid-Penn conference.
cleaned_text:
The Gettysburg Times is the first newspaper in the area to issue a multi-day conference title. The rebuilding process continues at Biggerville and Littleton. The eight-year head coach, Bob Gano, has been tasked with powering the area, and this could finally be the season. The Squires defeated Hanover. Gano's top priority is developing a team that peaks at the end and gets past North Penn.

The Bermudian Springs Eagles compete in the Mid-Penn conference. Gano led the Bermudian Springs Eagles to the Northern title. Here are capsule reviews of the Adams County high school championship.

The Bermudian Springs Eagles, under Coach McCollum, finished the 1990-06-8 season with an overall record of 4-2 in the Mid-Penn. Key issues include the state runner-up, Jess Dowell.

Coach McCollum has been kept busy. The Eagles still expect to end this season strongly. McCollum stated, "We have been kept busy. We have a positive attitude. If we can get that part of our team right, we can win."

headline: Local Teams Compete in Wrestling Tournaments
summary: Local high school wrestling teams competed in various tournaments, with Delone Catholic winning the Eastern York team title and Bermudian Springs athletes achieving individual success.
cleaned_text:
“I’m really excited,” said the jubilant Romero. “I’m a freshman and nobody really expected me to wrestle as good as I did. I gave it my all. My dad told me to keep an even head and not get down or be scared. I just went out and wrestled the way I like to wrestle—hard. I’m not used to this level of competition, but I’m really glad I wrestled in this tournament.”

Josh Hinkle, at 145, and Keith Wickline, at 171, each captured third-place finishes for Bermudian Springs.

“I’m really pleased with the progress,” said the Eagles coach. “We've come a long way. This is just what we needed at this point. Some of the kids had individual success today and got a lot of confidence in themselves. Because of that, they will wrestle a lot harder in the upcoming dual meets.”

Biglerville’s Tim Weible, at 189, took third place for the Canners.

“This is a real good tournament,” said Canners coach Wes Bard. “There are a lot of good quality teams here. We saw a couple of teams we'll be facing in the future.”

The coaches noted the impact of the flu on their teams. “We only brought nine guys. We lost some out sick. We would like them to be at this point. It would be nice to have them all healthy. Then maybe we could have a few more wins in our pocket. The flu is killing us, and there’s no let up in sight.”

Smith and McCollum were quick to echo this assessment of the tournament and the impact flu was having on their teams.

“There are a lot of tough kids who come out here each year and do well at the district level,” said McCollum.

In other local wrestling news, Delone Catholic took the team title at the Eastern York Tournament with 168 points and had three weight-class champions: Chad Kileen (130), Bed Miller (135), and Josh Boyer (189). Taking seconds for the Squires were: Zach Maloff (103), Dustin Riggs (119), and Steve Spicer (160). Third places went to: Tom Hoffman (125), Clint Bronsene (145). Fourth-place finishers were Paul Arigo (112) and Tom Miller (140).

Separately, New Oxford defeated Kennard-Dale 64 to 12 in a YAIAA inter-divisional match Saturday. New Oxford won by pins, with contributors including Brian Abell, Eric Cole, Jared Hughes, Mike Shultz, Craig, Justin Aiello, Brian Landi, and Zin.

Greencastle defeats Bermudian Springs in Mid-Penn III Struggle

Greencastle defeated Bermudian Springs Thursday night with a final score of 36-34. The victory was secured by the Eagles in the upper weight classes, which propelled them past the Bermudian Springs team in a back-and-forth Mid-Penn III tussle.

The results from the evening included several key victories for Greencastle. At 125 pounds, Steve Bohn defeated Matt Kirkland via technical fall (15-0). Jason Trask pinned Sam Jatfer at 130 pounds (5:22). Steve Koontz won by forfeit at 135 pounds, and Bryan Shue also won by forfeit at 140 pounds. At 152 pounds, Bryan Christ pinned Andy Vinson (1:54). Chad Masemer pinned J.A. Musselman at 171 pounds (:27), and Jason Wagner pinned Adam Hawk at 189 pounds (2:40). Finally, Jason Myers won by technical fall at 275 pounds, pinning Sean O'Brien (2:21).

In another match reported, Josh Hinkle pinned Tom Lane at 145 pounds (:40). Mike Ridge pinned S. Sullivan at 152 pounds (1:05). Jesse Hessong pinned Keith Miner at 160 pounds (1:25). Mike Ci defeated Keith Wickline at 171 pounds (4-3). Matt Jansen defeated Dan Weigle at 189 pounds (14-8). Waylon Trimmer pinned Brandon Ors at 275 pounds (5:57).

In a separate match reported for Saturday, Rich Polifka pinned Ricky Marchi at 103 pounds (1:38). Josh Chrismer pinned Heather Polifka at 112 pounds (:22). Dusty Wolff pinned Kurt Hermansen at 125 pounds (1:13). Chad Earnst pinned Tony Rettig at 130 pounds (5:44). Walt Medina pinned Shawn Gebhan at 135 pounds (:57). Joel defeated an opponent at 140 pounds.

Following the matches, McCollum, the Bermudian Springs coach, stated, “We finished out strong, we just came up a little short,” noting that his team fell to 2-3 in the league and 3-9 overall.

headline: Bermudian Springs Wrestling and Gettysburg Swim Team Shine at Jan. 1997 Meets
summary: The Bermudian Springs wrestling team had a successful meet, highlighted by wins in multiple weight classes, while the Gettysburg swim team also achieved significant victories in several events.
cleaned_text:
The Gettysburg Times
Mon, Jan 27, 1997

**Wrestling**
The Bermudian Springs wrestling team had a strong showing at the recent meet. The Eagles coach, Katie Teeter, noted that the team had a lot of close matches and was gaining confidence. The team faced tough competition, including a victory over Boiling Springs, 33-29. The Eagles were able to rally past their opponents, securing a win. The team also achieved significant success in the upper weight classes. Notable victories included:
*   112 lbs: Josh Chrismer (Bermudian Springs) pinned Maslowski.
*   119 lbs: Scott Bohn (Bermudian Springs) pinned Rajotte.
*   125 lbs: Justin Rose (Bermudian Springs).
*   140 lbs: Chip Vogel.
*   The team also won the 200-yard medley relay.

**Swimming**
The Gettysburg swim team had a successful meet, winning the final four events. The team secured multiple first-place decisions. The girls' 200-yard fly event saw Debbie Rajotte make history with a time of 1:59.61. In the girls' 200-yard backstroke, the team also performed well. The boys' 200-yard medley relay was another highlight.

**Basketball**
The Warriors placed first in the co-ed 200 medley relay. The Canners are now 1-4. The article also mentions that the Warriors only scored two totals, with Ciliotta scoring 3-pointers.

title: BY DUNCAN TRUSSELL
headline: Bermudian Springs Defeats Gettysburg in All-Adams County Showdown
summary: Bermudian Springs defeated Gettysburg 40-32 in an all-Adams County wrestling showdown, overcoming early momentum swings to secure the victory.
cleaned_text:
BY DUNCAN TRUSSELL
Times Sports Writer

Bermudian Springs defeated visiting Gettysburg Thursday night 40-32 in an all-Adams County showdown that featured pins in all but two of the matches.

Having won its last six matches, Gettysburg, the Warriors, appeared to be on a winning streak. They jumped out to a quick start with pins from Jason Amnor at 105 pounds and Wes Winners at 112 pounds. The Warriors took the next bout, winning at 122 pounds against the high school at 119 pounds. The Warriors bowed out of the 125-pound bout, but they pinned the Eagles' Kurt Hermanson in a hard-fought contest.

Momentum shifted in the 129-pound class. The Eagles ran off four straight victories by Chris Gabbart (150), Sean Gibbart (140), Josh Hinkle, and a former wrestler.

Warriors coach Perry Smalley said the Warriors were not finished, noting that they earned pins from Craig Hines (103) and Brad Shaffer (160). Bermudian Springs quickly regained momentum when Keith Walters joined in at 147 pounds, giving the Eagles the win.

Eagles coach Dave McCollum stated that the match was in front of experienced wrestlers, which provided the edge the Eagles needed to win.

The premier match of the night came in the 182-pound class, featuring Bermudian Springs and Drew Leedy in a classic back-and-forth scramble.

LITTLESTOWN — Bermu:

Dave McCollum didn't panic
and he didn’t show any signs
of nervousness,

Yesterday afternoon,
McCollum’s Eagles were

“There was no lack of confi-

Waylon (Trimmer) left, I

towel in.”

gave Bermudian a 34-24 non.
league wrestling victory.

Littlestown needed a pin at

the way. Trimmer went on to

picked up a decision.

up a notch,” the senior said.
“The guys stuck together. I
wasn't worried about wins or
losses — it’s just a team
thing.”

Ginter liked his chances at

Jeff Mehle.

him to have to go out and pin,

sonable shot for a victory. I

just didn’t fall for him.”

man.

beat up all year,” said Littles-
town coach Mike Ginter. “I

feit, but he kind of typified

working harder today.”

## The State of Wrestling in Central Pennsylvania

The wrestling scene in Central Pennsylvania remains vibrant, with several schools competing at the high level of the Mid-Atlantic region. The sport continues to draw strong local interest, with coaches and athletes dedicated to improving their skills and achieving success at the state level.

One of the most notable programs is Northern York, which has established itself as a powerhouse in the area. The program is led by dedicated coaching staff, who are instrumental in guiding the athletes through the rigorous training required for competitive wrestling.

The competition is fierce, with schools like Delco also fielding highly competitive teams. The dedication shown by the coaches, such as those at Northern York, speaks to the deep commitment to the sport.

The state of the sport is one of continuous improvement. Athletes are constantly working to refine their techniques, and the local community provides a strong support system for the teams. The focus remains on developing well-rounded athletes who can compete successfully against rivals from across the region.

The Evening Sun
Wed, Nov 26, 1997

**Catholic Teams Compete in Tip-Off Tournaments**

The Catholic League saw several teams compete in tip-off tournaments this week. In one contest, Lancaster Catholic faced Lebanon Catholic. The game was highly competitive, with the score eventually reaching 59-43.

In other sports news, the Gettysburg team was highlighted for its strong performance. Megan Larkin led all scorers with 22 points. The team was noted for its ability to play with anyone, suggesting a strong season ahead.

In collegiate men’s basketball on Tuesday, Will Marshall, a senior forward, posted game highs of 23 points and 11 rebounds while playing for Western Maryland.

title: AP Photo
cleaned_text:
**The Evening Sun**
**Sunday, November 30, 1997**

**McCollum Wins No. 200**
By BRIAN SHEA
Evening Sun Sports Writer

McCollum, in his 21st year as head wrestling coach at Bermudian Springs, won his 200th career match on Saturday as the Eagles opened the 1997-98 campaign with a 66-9 drubbing of Camp Hill.

“It means a lot,” he said between visits from well-wishers after Saturday’s match. “Back when I first got hired in 1978, I committed myself to Bermudian Springs and Bermudian wrestling, so it means a lot.”

Since then, he has engineered the team to a 200-128-3 record. McCollum has coached one state champion and one national champion.

In other matches, West Ferry registered victories across the final three weight classes, including pins secured by Joe Kolar at 110. Bermudian Springs crushed Camp Hill, winning the first victory of the day. The victory was noted as the 20th win for the coaching staff, and the first of ten victories, five of which came via fall.

title: From staff reports
headline: Bermudian Springs Eagles Secure 200th Win in Wrestling Victory
summary: The Bermudian Springs Eagles defeated the Camp Hill Lions 66-7 in a wrestling match, allowing coach McCollum to reach 200 wins, while Susquehannock also secured a narrow victory over Littlestown.
cleaned_text:
The Bermudian Springs Eagles Wrestling Team Dominates Camp Hill

The Bermudian Springs High School Wrestling team had a successful day on Saturday, culminating in a decisive victory over the Camp Hill Lions. With Camp Hill forfeiting five of the 13 weight classes, the Eagles defeated the Lions 66-7, securing a major win for coach McCollum.

McCollum, who is in his 21st year as head coach, has now compiled a 200-128-3 record and previously coached state champion Trevor Byers in 1986.

The day’s action began with sophomore Ricky Marchi (103), who quickly scored Bermudian Springs’ first points by pinning his opponent in 48 seconds. Following two Camp Hill forfeits in the district qualifier, sophomores Adam Weigand and Corby Himes also pinned their opponents. After three more Lion forfeits, the score reached 54-0 in favor of Bermudian Springs, making the match virtually over.

The weight class results included:
*   112-Jason Spahr (BS) won by forfeit
*   119-Tim Bolden (BS) won by forfeit
*   125-Nick Stock (BS) pinned Bran Rhoades
*   130-Adam Weigand (BS) pinned Matt Sandusky
*   135-Corby Himes (BS) pinned Dan Khck
*   140-Shawn Boyers (BS) won by forfeit
*   145-Josh O'Dell (BS) won by forfeit
*   152-Chad Eamst (BS) won by forfeit
*   160-Justin Hotfacker (BS) pinned Matt Sadler
*   171-Gavin Miller (CH) defeated Keith Miner (BS) 4-3
*   189-Kerth Wickline (BS) pinned Aaron Piscionen
*   275-Brandon Miller (CH) pinned Jason Weigie

Other Mustangs who secured pins included Brian Alwine (125), Josh Dull (140), Jeremy Sterner (145), Andy Campbell (160), Brock Hostetter (171), and Josh Galemore (275).
