By RICK GROTT
Evering Sun Sports correspondent

The Delone Catholic High School wrestling team secured important wins in the 171 and 198-pound weight classes during their season opener on Thursday.

The victory gave Delone coaches Jim Neiderer and Ken Shaffer their first win of the season. This win was achieved in the 190-pound weight class, putting them behind Eagle head coach Drew McCollum, who had hit the 200-win milestone last week against Camp Hill.

"The win felt good," said Neiderer. "It was nice to be a guy with 200 wins."

Freshman Justin Hoffacker secured the Eagle (11) weight class victory by a point, defeating Joe Reaver in the 160-pound division. Later in the match, in what the coaches called the turning point, Delone's Joe Beamer defeated Kathleen in the 16-6 weight class.

title: “Dave
headline: Squires Edge Eagles in Wrestling Showdown
summary: The Delone Squires defeated the Bermudian Springs Eagles in a competitive wrestling match, winning key bouts through multiple weight classes.
cleaned_text:
Keith Miner looked to continue the momentum swing for the Bermudian Springs Eagles when he squared off with Delone’s Joe Smith at 171 pounds. Miner fired out to an early 2-0 lead when he locked up a half-nelson on Smith and appeared to be in position to send the Squire to his back. However, Miner was too aggressive in taking Smith to the mat, as Smith rolled through him, stopping the comeback.

“He drove the half so hard that he got rolled through,” the coach commented. “He just needs to settle down and be patient and let the pin happen. Our aggressiveness, at times, got us in trouble, but I’m never going to fault the kids for being aggressive.”

Smith’s improbable pin, coupled with Steve Spicer’s 5-2 decision over Keith Wickline at 189 pounds, sealed the win for the Squires in their opening match.

“The kids gave a good effort,” said a coach, though he noted that the key is to improve.

Earlier, Joe Smith seemed to have things in hand. However, Smith fatigued, and Delone’s Jason Butterhoff took advantage by scoring a pair of takedowns and putting Smith on his back. Smith fought off the pin for the final 30 seconds of the bout and held on for a 14-9 win.

The two teams traded wins over the next five weight classes. Danny Riggs and Chad Killeen registered victories for the Squires, while Corby Himes, Shawn Boyer, and Chad Earnst won for the Eagles.

Killeen used a series of arm bars and halves to put away Josh O'Dell at 145 pounds. Boyer overpowered Joe Bowman for a fall at 140 pounds, and Chad Earnst used a quick third-period reversal to defeat Tom Hoffman at 152 pounds.

The evening’s most entertaining match took place at 160 pounds, where Justin Hoffacker and Delone’s Joe Reaver tangled.

headline: Bermudian Springs Coach Highlights Team Strength After Tournament Success
summary: The article reports on a successful wrestling tournament, detailing multiple victories and highlighting the overall strength of the Bermudian Springs program.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Sun, Dec 07, 1997

The wrestling tournament saw several key matches and individual victories. In one bout, a pin ended the action before Joe Smith could escape danger. Earlier, the Canners gained a 27-23 lead after forfeits to Delone’s Chad Killeen and Tom Hofmann at the 145 and 152 weight classes, respectively. Shawn Whitmoyer secured a decision at 171 for Biglerville, while Delone’s Steve Spicer won 2-1 at 189 against Tim Weible. The Squires conceded the heavyweight match, forfeiting to Chris Roberts with only inexperienced Jon Booth available.

The action continued with Matt Cook pinning Delone’s Justin Sierer at 112, and Joe Hoover pinning Delone’s Jason Butterhoff at 130. Later, Sponseller won his finals bout with a 9-3 record, having accumulated three wins in the tournament, including one by fall, moving him to a 6-0 season record.

Outstanding Wrestler honors went to Brad Groover for the second consecutive year after he defeated fellow state qualifier Scott Bohn 20-8 at 135. In another match, Gorman edged Chad Dwyer of Susquehannock 4-3 in his final bout, beating the Warrior senior for the second time in two weeks.

Regarding the program's overall strength, Bermudian Springs coach McCollum stated, "They don't have one weak weight."

**The Gettysburg Times**
**Wed, Dec 17, 1997**

**(Continued from Page B1)**

**Local Sports Roundup**

**4. Biglerville 1-2:** The bad news is that Biglerville has forfeited eight bouts in three matches this season. However, the good news is that the Canners appear to have a full lineup on the horizon as league matches loom.

**5. Littlestown 2-2:** The ‘Bolts are using a mixture of veterans and inexperienced wrestlers to get the job done. Coach Bowersox will have to find the right combination as the season wears on.

**6. Delone Catholic 1-2:** The Squires are in a transitional period with a new coaching staff. The pieces are there to be contenders.

**7. Gettysburg 0-3:** The Warriors endured the brutal Parkland Duals Tournament, where they faced Parkland, ranked 17th in the country, along with Neshaminy and Wilson. Brighter days are ahead for the squad.

**8. Bermudian Springs 1-3:** The Eagles are battling youth and inexperience.

summary: Gettysburg High School defeated Bermudian Springs 55-11 in a focused effort to avenge last year's loss, while local wrestlers continue to achieve high rankings.

Gettysburg Wrestlers Avenge Past Loss to Bermudian Springs

Gettysburg High School’s wrestling team demonstrated fierce determination Thursday night, defeating Bermudian Springs 55-11 in a decisive victory. The Warriors were focused on avenging last year’s loss to the Eagles, winning 10 matches and recording eight pins.

“Bermudian Springs has always wrestled us tough,” said Gettysburg’s head coach, Perry Smith. “We didn’t want what happened last year to happen again this year. The guys were really focused.”

The victory was highlighted by several key matches, including pins by Corby Himes and Shawn Sullivan, and a quick 12-0 lead established by Shupe pinning Jason Spahr.

In other news, Gettysburg wrestlers Chris Haines and Jared Sullivan are highly ranked in the state. Haines is listed sixth in the Keystone rankings in Class AAA at 152 pounds, while Sullivan retained his top rank at 125 pounds in the Off the Mat (OTM) Class AAA rankings.

Other local wrestlers are also performing well, including New Oxford’s Steve Zinn (16th, 112) and Jason Zinn (14th, 119), and Spring Grove’s Kevin Baum (19th, 145) and Ryan Wilt (9th, 160).

headline: PIAA Overhauls Wrestling Championship Format for 1998-99 Season
summary: The PIAA is implementing major changes for the 1998-99 wrestling season, adding a regional tournament and a state-wide dual meet tournament to the championship path.
cleaned_text:
**Changes Set for 1998-99 Wrestling Season**

**By Josh Martin, Times Sports Writer**

The PIAA has approved significant changes to the path for local Class AA wrestlers heading to the state championships for the 1998-99 season.

Previously, the route to the state tournament involved a sectional tournament, with the top four finishers in each weight class advancing to the District I Championships. The top three place-winners at the District I level would then proceed to Hershey to compete in the PIAA championships.

The new format adds a regional tournament, which will be held at Conestoga Valley the week before the District I Championships. This addition drastically changes the structure. Under the new system, the top four place-winners will go on to Hershey.

“I think it’s a change to our advantage,” said Littlestown coach Dave Bowersox. “It’s good for our district. It gives a good, young prospect a better chance to make it to state.” Bowersox, who has a lineup led with many sophomores, added that the regional tournament provides additional qualifying spots.

The other major change to Pennsylvania wrestling will be the addition of a state-wide dual meet tournament. This tournament will take place over four weeks, with each round being wrestled on a Wednesday night. While most coaches agree that having the tournament is a good idea, there is one major concern: the finals are scheduled to take place in Hershey, the day before the PIAA tournament gets underway.

“I think the concept is good, but you’re going to have some snags to it, and the scheduling is something to worry about,” said Gettysburg coach Perry Smith.

McCollum, the wrestling coach from Bermudian Springs, stated, “It’s good for wrestling.”

The Evening Sun
Saturday, February 21, 1998

Bermudian Springs High School was attempting to steal the show at the District II Class AA Sectional Christmas Wrestling Tournament. The team, coached by McCollum, was aiming for a win in the highly competitive sectional.

The tournament featured several strong contenders. The Bubbler wrestlers from Brandywine Heights were noted for their strength in the District III Team Tournament, having previously competed at Delone Catholic.

The sectional action was intense. In one notable match, Himes knocked off a winner, while Squire finished in second place.

The wrestling coverage also highlighted individual achievements. Christian Furuseth, a Norwegian teammate, won a medal, while Thomas Sykora earned a bronze medal. The event was held in memory of Sonny Sheppard.

headline: Local Wrestlers Compete at PIAA Championships
summary: The article details the competitive atmosphere at the PIAA Championships, highlighting the efforts of local wrestlers like Kerth Wickline and discussing the scope and influence of the state athletic association.
cleaned_text:
Some of Pennsylvania’s finest scholastic wrestling talent were seen out on the same mats, giving just a glimpse of the competition that would take place throughout the tournament.

A few of the Times area wrestlers were among those who decided to get a feel for the arena. Biglerville’s Shain Legge was busy on one mat honing his takedown skills, while Bermudian Springs’ Kerth Wickline was on another, working out with his coach, McCollum.

The article also touched on the PIAA (Pennsylvania Interscholastic Athletic Association), noting that while the organization is accused by some state lawmakers of being “arrogant and imperial,” it is not regulated by the state. Despite this, the association organizes, develops, and directs athletic programs for some 225,000 student-athletes who participate in interscholastic athletics under its purview each year.

Wickline and McCollum ran a few laps before packing it in for the day. Wickline’s performance was notable, as he earned his trip to Hershey by taking third place in the consolation bracket.

Part of the lore of the PIAA Championships is that there are countless wrestlers like Wickline who have fought and clawed to get this far. Some will continue to beat their opponents and the odds on sheer will, while others will see their seasons and possibly their careers come to an end. Either way, six minutes on a mat in Hershey will account for a lifetime of memories.

summary: Jared Sullivan of South Western advanced to the State AAA semifinals after winning a quarterfinal match, while other local wrestlers secured victories and advanced in their respective tournaments.
cleaned_text:
Wrestling Results and Tournament Updates

The Gettysburg Times, Saturday, March 7, 1998

**Tournament Results**
The wrestling tournament featured numerous decisive victories across various weight classes. Notable results included:

*   **103 lbs:** Paui Sujdak defeated Lundquist.
*   **112 lbs:** Eric Chambers defeated Methacton.
*   **135 lbs:** Corey DeBias (Northern Lehigh) defeated Tommy Nag'e (Cambria Heights).
*   **140 lbs:** Vake Stork (Schuylkill Valley) defeated Joe Chariton (Fort LeBoeuf).
*   **145 lbs:** Gabe Webster (Sayre) defeated Sod Tumpak (Reynolds).
*   **152 lbs:** Jesse Martinez (Hanover Area) defeated Jeremy Reitz (Briar).
*   **171 lbs:** Steve Spicer (Detone Catholic) defeated Steve Sinkovich (Mount Carmel).

**Tournament Narrative**
In other wrestling news, Kimmel advanced to the third round with a 2-1 win in the second consolation round.

South Western’s Jared Sullivan did not disappoint, picking up a pin in the quarterfinal round on Friday. Sullivan’s fall in 2:44 sends him into the State AAA semifinals, which begin at 8:00 a.m. The senior student will compete against a heavy favorite.

South Western’s Hestetter also exited the tournament Friday evening following a pair of losses. Hestetter had a frustrating 4-3 decision in the quarterfinals against Brad Lovett of Penn Trafford. Despite having a crucial takedown near the end of the period, the referee ruled the Mustang to be out of bounds. Hestetter nearly hit a single takedown late in the mat, but Lovett was able to defend a bout that ended. Hestetter then slipped into the consolation round where he lost 11-4.

Meanwhile, Gettysburg’s Chris F. secured his season alive with a consolation win over Jim (30-5) of Bensalem.

headline: Bermudian Springs Wrestler Bowers Pins Opponent in Area Scholastic Match
summary: Bermudian Springs wrestler Jordan Bowers secured a clinch victory by pinning Joe Reaver in the 160-pound weight class during an Area Scholastic wrestling match.
cleaned_text:
**Bermudian Springs Wrestler Jordan Bowers Defeats Opponent in Area Scholastic Match**

By Ryan Bevitz

Jordan Bowers, a wrestler from Bermudian Springs, had a simple task Saturday: avoid being pinned. Coach Dave McCollum put Bowers in to wrestle against Joe Reaver of Cubicite. All McCollum wanted to see was Bowers avoid getting pinned.

Bowers did even better. He managed to pin Reaver with 10 seconds left in the 160-pound weight class, securing a clinch victory.

summary: The Bermudian Springs wrestling team maintains its league dominance by relying on a strong defensive strategy that consistently prevents opponents from scoring.
cleaned_text:
**Bermudian Springs Grapplers Maintain Dominance with Strong Defense**

By John Martin

The Bermudian Springs wrestling team continues its tradition of success, relying heavily on a strong defensive strategy. A key factor in the team's ability to win championships is their consistent ability to stop opponents from scoring points.

The Jets have maintained a dominant presence in the league since the 1990s. While individual performances, such as Rachael Baldwin's defensive efforts, are crucial, the team's overall defensive strength remains their greatest asset.

headline: McCollum’s Turnaround Secures Bermudian Springs Victory
summary: McCollum’s strategic wrestling performance against Boyer provided the crucial turnaround that allowed Bermudian Springs to defeat Biglerville 37-34.
cleaned_text:
Boldosser’s patience boosts Bermudian Springs past Biglerville

By MIKE PHELPS
Evening Sun Sports Correspondent

It is a normal reaction for a wrestler to get excited when leading a bout. You certainly cannot blame Shawn Boyer for his excitement in his 145-pound match against Biglerville’s opponent, McCollum, on Wednesday night.

Leading 4-2 midway through the third period, Boyer wanted to finish off his opponent rather than being content with his lead. McCollum caught the overzealous Boyer in a headlock and put the Bermudian Springs grappler on his back. Instead of suffering a two-point loss, McCollum rallied for a 7-5 win.

That turnaround proved to be the deciding point in Bermudian Springs’ home match against Biglerville, as the Canners won 37-34.

“McCollum and Boyer were the best,” said a source. “Really, I would have rather had Boyer out away from McCollum. At that point (trailing 4-2), McCollum had to initiate coming in. Shawn was trying to force it and got caught in a headlock.”

The fact that the match was close anyway seemed to have Biglerville coach Bob Gano a little perplexed.

title: YAIAA Division I leader
headline: South Western Dominates YAIAA Wrestling Scene
summary: South Western maintained its YAIAA Division I leadership through multiple victories, including wins by Bermudian Springs and Biglerville, showcasing strong performances across various weight classes.
cleaned_text:
South Western continues its dominance in the YAIAA Division I, securing its status by winning key league matches. On Saturday, the Mustangs defeated the homestanding Bulldogs in an interdivisional matchup at West York with a score of 33-20. A key victory came from Brian Alwine, who achieved a 1-0 shutout against top-ranked Class AA wrestler in District II, Todd Platts, at 135 pounds, posting an escape in the second period. The Mustangs also saw decisions from Eric Wildasin and Bruce Myer.

In another area match, Bermudian Springs (the Eagles) defeated Penn Capital, 46-21. The victory was led by four pins and three forfeits, with Brad McCleaf (119), Shawn Boyer (145), Raford Felts (160), and Justin Hoffacker (171) recording falls. The coach noted, "It was a good, hard match by both Springs and Penn Capital; it was nice to just see a match."

At Camp Hill, Biglerville (5-2, 5-1 Mid-Penn Capital) won against Canners, 46-21. Biglerville took control with consecutive falls by Shain Leggett (142).

Meanwhile, Spring Grove defeated the Rockets, 52-21. The winners added falls from A.J. Sauers (100 pounds), Eric Overbaugh (130), and Cory Jacoby (138). Spring Grove also gained pins from Cody Becker (95) and Zack Crumbling (122).

In a separate contest, South Western defeated Manheim Central, 52-21. South Western secured comeback victories from Jodd Gobrecht (105) and Adam Lawrence (185), winning seven of the final nine bouts. Lawrence maintained his unbeaten record, joining teammates Seth Handy (85), who pinned, and Mitch Medina (130). Other pins for the Colts included Jeremy Cruys (90), Drew Funk (96), Justin Kiumk (115), and Andrew Norris.

title: Eagles stun Thunderbolts
headline: Eagles Stun Thunderbolts in Mid-Prem Wrestling Championship
summary: The Bermudian Springs Eagles secured back-to-back dual meet wins, including key victories by Shawn Boyer and Dana Bassett, which challenged the undefeated Thunderbolts.
cleaned_text:
Eagles Stun Thunderbolts in Mid-Prem Wrestling Championship

By Ryan Bevitz
Evening Sun Sports Writer

Something magical happened in Week 11 Mid-Prem Wrestling Championship. Bermudian Springs High School proved it could wrestle against some of the best teams in the district. The Eagles took knowledge from this week and won back-to-back dual meets for the second time this season.

Saturday's 45-24 non-league win over Littleton was the second of those victories.

The Eagles maintained a strong lead, holding a 19-9 advantage. It was not until Wednesday's meet with Northern that Wiegand went out and wrestled, providing a good boost to the lineup. The Eagles responded to Wiegand's upset with a pin and a default victory over Bermudian Springs, 19-6.

Key wins included Shawn Boyer pinning Jordan Amount at 1:00 at 40, and Dana Bassett winning by default over Littleton's Tony Daw at 145.

These victories meant that the Thunderbolts (9-0) would have to win at the remaining matches. Jason Trask took out the Bolts.

title: AP Photo
headline: Gettysburg Secures Top Seed for District II Tournament
summary: Gettysburg secured a top seed in the District II tournament, while other Mid-Penn teams, including Bigalerville, prepared for upcoming matches in the regional wrestling competition.
cleaned_text:
**Gettysburg Secures Top Seed as Mid-Penn Teams Prepare for District Tournament**

By RYAN BEVITZ
Evening Sun Sports Writer

Monday, February 8, 1999

Gettysburg heads into the District II team tournament with a top seed Sunday, as the tournament brackets were announced.

“We don’t really seed the teams. If you're looking for Gettysburg on top as the No. 1 seed,” said McCollum, the coach from Bermudian Springs, who is a member of the District III coaching staff. McCollum added that the teams are moved around so that squads that wrestled during the year do not face one another in the early stages of the tournament.

After winning the Mid-Penn tournament Saturday, Gettysburg (13-0) grabbed the unofficial top seed.

Biglerville also made the tournament as the Mid-Penn Capital Division runner-up. The Mid-Penn gets two AA bids, and since the Capital is the only AA division in Mid-Penn, the Cannerns were assured of a spot.

Biglerville (12-3) will travel to Juniata on Wednesday to take on the Tri-Valley League champion Indians (13-1) at 7 p.m.

The Warriors will host Lancaster-Lebanon League Section I champion Hempfield (8-5) at 7 p.m. Wednesday. Solanco High School will host the remaining two rounds Saturday.

“I think we're wrestling pretty well right now. I don’t want to say we have to win the tournament to be successful. We have to win the first match to have a second match,” said Gettysburg coach Perry Smith. “We want to focus on Hempfield for Wednesday and hopefully move on to Solanco.”

In wrestling, the Eagles from Bermudian Springs had a successful week of practice, noting that their coach, McCollum, stated the team was in the best shape. The team achieved several upsets, including a significant victory by Corby Himes. Himes, who wrestled at 130 pounds, advanced to the semifinals after defeating Biglerville’s fourth-seeded Barrett Johnson in the quarterfinals of the District 3 Class AA tournament. Himes, who has a record of 10-10, was noted for avenging a previous season loss by defeating Johnson with 26 seconds left on the clock.

The article also included a section on fishing, listing various local fishing spots and upcoming events. For example, the Adams County water-level fishing report mentioned the potential for catching trout at various locations, including the Sonory Creek and Children’s Lake.

meeting this season
could determine the
division title.

By RYAN BEVITZ
Evening Sun Sports Writer

Wrestling fans in Adams
County might want to circle
December 22 on their calendar.
On that night, Biglerville and
Bermudian Springs will square
off in their annual rivalry.

However, there might very
well be additional meaning to the
match.

MID-PENN CAPITAL
WRESTLING

Both squads lose very little off
their rosters from last winter,
when the Canners and Eagles fin-
in the Mid-Penn Capi-
tal Division. Throw in the fact
that defending champion Boiling
S nine starters from
y ys are con-
sidered contenders for the divi-
sion crown.

However, Biglerville coach
Bob Gano warns not to take the
Bubblers lightly.

“| figure it’s their title until
someone takes it. It’s easy to look
at it on paper (and say Bermudi-
an and Biglerville). But once you
win something it’s hard to take it
away,” said Gano.

Among the Hanover area
returnees in the Capital Division
is defending PIAA Class AA
champion Shain Leggett, a senior
with the Canners for the 1999-
2000 campaign. Also in the fold
are 12 District Il qualifiers

Evening Sun Photo

Biglerville's Dru Boldosser has control of Shawn Boyer of Bermudi-

an Springs during their sectional match last season.

joth are sched-

uled to be in their team’s starting lineups this season.

between the two schools, three
defending sectional champions
and two regional qualifiers.

Here is a closer look at the
division:

Bermudian Springs

Coach Dave McCollum returns
every wrestler from his 1998-89 team,
which went 12-4, but two.

On top of it ail, he has all seven of
his "98-99 district qualifiers — Rick
Marchi, Brad McCleaf, Corby Himes,
‘Adam Weigand, Shawn Boyer, Duane
Bastress and Justin Hottacker.

“| have high expectations for these
‘quys,” said McCollum. ‘There are a lot
of positives.”

The Eagles’ biggest obstacle this
winter could be finding spots for every-
‘one. Last year, McCollum became a
master of juggling his lineup.

“We have experienced kids. We

have to improve on our weak areas as
individuals,” said McCollum. "We have
to get the ‘Eye of the Tiger. I thought |
‘saw visions of it last year.”

McCollum thinks highly of this
year's team. He says it is one of his
best disciplined teams and that all the
‘members have great work ethics.

“it’s just that kind of group. They
only come around every eight or nine
years. It just so happens Biglerville has
‘ne of those groups too,” said McCol-
lum.

Biglerville

The Canners went 12-4 last year,
with a 7-1 mark in the division,
Biglerville also went to the District II
Class AA dual meet toummament only to
lose in the first round to Juniata

Gano thinks he knows the reason
why Biglerville has yet to get over the
hump in his previous vo years with

‘anners — leadership.

“It's crucial,” said Gano. “That's
what we've been lacking the last cou-
ple of years.”

The Canners have four seniors on
their roster — Leggett, Southeast
Regional qualifier Matt Showers, dis-
trict qualifier Soot Pitzer and Barrett
Johnson, who went 23-10 last year —
who Gano wil look to for leadership.

“| have four pretty good seniors,”
said Gano. “They seem to have turned
the comer as leaders. That was my
main concer and it still is.”

The Canners also have junior stal-
wart Dru Boldosser in their lineup. Bol-
dosser, who posted a 26-3 record last
year, appeared to be a shoe-in for a
PIAA berth before he withdrew from
the district tournament due to an injury.

The Rest of the Division

Is the cupboard bare for Boiling
Springs? That could be the key to the
division.

“Their junior high program and the
kids they have coming back make
them a threat,” said McCollum. “It's a
matter of if they can fill their holes.”

The Bubblers still have junior Mitch
Naugle in their lineup. Naugle was a
PIAA qualifier a year ago.

‘Also look for Shippensburg. The
Eagles just edged out the Greyhounds
for third place last year.

“They made strides last year. We
ended up 6-2 (in the division) and that
could have easily been them,” said
MaCollum,

Meanwhile, Camp Hill, East
Pennsboro, | Greencastle-Antrim,
James Buchanan and Milton Her-
shey wouldn't start out the year as
favorites, but don’t count them out,
There are plenty of blue-chippers from
those schools.

Greencastie's Wayne Medina was
the regional runner-up at 130 pounds,
East Pennsboro's Jared Comp is a
contender for a state berth.

title: Sunday, December 5, 1999
headline: Bermudian Springs Defeats Eagles in Wrestling Triumph
summary: Bermudian Springs achieved a 39-21 victory over the Eagles in a wrestling meet, highlighted by the performances of athletes like Brad McCollum and Justin Holt.
cleaned_text:
Sunday, December 5, 1999

Bermudian Springs Gets Answers Against the Eagles in a 39-21 Triumph Over the Squares

By Ryan Bevitz
Everland Sports Writer

Bermudian Springs secured a 39-21 victory over the Eagles in a significant wrestling meet. The victory was notable, especially considering the high stakes of the competition.

The meet featured several key moments. Brad McCollum was involved in the 119-pound weight class at Bermudian Springs, contributing to the 39-21 victory over the Eagles. McCollum, who had a 5-3 record, was aided by his experience in the competition.

While the Eagles entered the meet with some experience, Bermudian Springs maintained an edge. Justin Holt, who joined Delone at Allen Crosse, competed in the 125-pound weight class.

The article also highlighted promising athletes, naming Adam and Malia Wiggett as two of the best young competitors.

title: Eagles rout defending champs
headline: Eagles Rout Defending Champions
summary: Bermudian Springs began its season by defeating Boiling Springs, while other local teams, including the Eagles and Titans, also had notable performances.
cleaned_text:
Eagles Rout Defending Champions

Bermudian Springs Coasts in MPC-Capital Opener

STAFF REPORT

Bermudian Springs High School opened its season in the Mid-Perm Capital Division with a grand style breakfast schedule, defeating the defending champion, Boiling Springs, 50-7 on Thursday evening.

The Bermudian Springs team (2-0-10 in Capital) won its first capital match, featuring standout performances from Brad McCladan and Matt Wadepeckett. Mr. McCladan posted points for the Eagles in later games. Also in the mix was Tyler Drake, who defeated sectional runner-up Kifuita 7-6.

The Titans were able to bounce back, and Eddie George sparked action in Tennessee.

The Spartans, who finished last season with a 40-1 record, are set to compete in the Commonwealth Division dual meet. The article provides a preview of the top teams.

Bermudian Springs boasts a strong lineup, featuring wrestlers like Ricky Marchi and Tyler Drake at 103 pounds.

Biglerville is led by head coach Bob Gan and features returning PIAA champion Shain Leggett.

Greencastle-Antrim is highlighted by the return of Rich Polifka, a state qualifier, who overcame a severe skiing accident, and Seniie Medina.

James Buchanan has a promising team, including Chris Shuey at 119 pounds, B.J. Bookwalt Carbaugh at 145 pounds, and Josh Fickes at 275 pounds.

Milton Hershey's Spartans are noted for their victories and strong start, with Welton Logan at 103 pounds.

title: Bigilville player garrett Johnson has篮球队员 Shawn Boyer in a cradle during the 145-pound match Wednesday.
headline: Bigilville Tops Eagles at Southwestern Wrestling Tournament
summary: Nate Fisel helped the Camers defeat the rival Eagles at the Southwestern Wrestling Tournament, securing a team victory for Bigilville High School.
cleaned_text:
Bigilville Player Garrett Johnson has Shawn Boyer in a cradle during the 145-pound match on Wednesday.

Bigilville Tops Eagles

Fisel's pin helps Camers to 35-31 triumph.

By Ryan Bevizt
Evergreen School Winter

Nate Fisel wanted a rematch.

The Bigilville High School sophomore was hoping for the Camers to win 35-31 over the rival Eagles on Saturday at the Southwestern Wrestling Tournament.

However, Eagles coach Dave McCoy jigged his finger and pinned Matt Aelio. Fisel (62-19) won the match on the 171-pound bench, giving the Camers their first lead of the night at 29-25. The team victory, secured by a third-point pin of 29-25, gave the Camers a strong position for the team win.

We need to scrape it together to get in position again at Bigilville, said McCoy. The Camers improved to 30 in the middle and to 31 in the final. Only Milton Hersey remains undefeated with a 2-0 mark.

headline: Eagle Trio Pushing Toward Mid-Penn Honors
summary: Matt Aiello and Winter Hoffeker, key members of the Bermudian Springs wrestling team, are aiming to win a medal at the MMC Tournaments, following the success of teammate Dame Bessie Wren.
cleaned_text:
Eagle Trio Pushing Toward Mid-Penn Honors

BYAN BEVITZ
Sun Run Sports Writers

Matt Aiello and Winter Hoffeker are the top pair to participate in the Bermudian Springs wrestling team, with Dame Bessie Wren being the winning team. On Thursday, Aiello and Hoffeker, wrestling side-by-side, both pinned opponents in the first round of the second tournament.

"We push each other all the time in practice," said Coach McCollum. "The friendship can sometimes translate to touching the mat. It can have an effect on a teammate. If the guy before you pins his guy, and you know your wrestler probably gives you a boost. It can also wilt in the opposite way."

The team also noted that Bermudian Springs' Jon Derr scored a victory in 4:27.

This year, both Hoffeker and Aiello share the same goal for the MMC Tournaments: to win a medal, something Bessie Wren achieved last year.

summary: The Bermudian Springs Eagles won the Defiant II Class AA Team Wrestling Tournament title with a decisive 52-9 victory over Juniata.
cleaned_text:
**Bermudian Springs Eagles Win Defiant II Class AA Team Wrestling Title**

By NEIL LUPPY
Ewing Sun Sports Writer

Bermudian Springs Eagles routed Juniata 52-9 on Saturday at Science High School.

In a major achievement, the Eagles secured the Defiant II Class AA Team Wrestling Tournament title. For the first time in his 23 years as presiding coach, McKinley led the team to victory.

The Eagles not only captured the title but did so in style, achieving the largest margin of victory ever in the finals.

The team's victory was significant, especially after losing a key player. "Bigwille was a shoulder we have lost. I think it was a big moment," one coach stated.

The Patriot News – Sun Feb 13, 2000

In qualifying for the season’s District 3 Championship, the Bermudian Springs Eagles secured their first victory over previously unbeaten Juniata. The Eagles' win was significant, marking a major upset in the Valley League Championship.

The Indians were swept behind, but Benjamin de Sewanee beat Juniata’s opponent, Dew, 6-3 in the Valley League Championship. The Eagles' victory was a major milestone, and the team achieved the largest margin of victory of the season.

The Eagles put the sweep on the first two periods to an upset at McCafford, winning 6-1 on five points total.

title: 1 Bobby Labonte
headline: Bermudian Springs Eagles Win First District 3-AA Team Championship
summary: The Bermudian Springs Eagles won their first District 3-AA Team Championship title after defeating Juniata in the final.
cleaned_text:
The Bermudian Springs Eagles Secure First District 3-AA Team Championship Title

The Bermudian Springs team put a stamp on the 11th District 3-AA Team Championship with its first title. The Eagles were a pure menace at Solanco, blasting Conrad Weiser 42-14 in the semifinals and erasing Juniata 52-6 in the final. Combined with its 35-18 triumph over Donegal in the quarter-finals, the team scored 132 points in three matches. This total is the third-highest in 3-AA Team Championships history, trailing only Brandywine Heights’ 135 points in 1998 and Hanover's 134 in 1996.

The point of all that math is to show just how easily the Eagles sliced through a field that was considered fairly even going into the tournament. The Eagles had been relegated to back-bench status as the Mid-Penn Commonwealth runner-up, but the team only strengthened its standing.

During the break between the semifinals and final at Solanco, the Eagles congregated and stayed together for the most part, mostly engaging in idle conversation. The staff, led by the wrestlers, huddled in the bleachers mapping out strategy for the championship match, which amounted to no strategy at all. The Eagles wrestled Juniata straight up with wildly successful results.

“With the lineup we have, we're not going to mess with it,” said McCollum, who waited 23 years to hug his first District 3 team trophy. “We did that.”

“I'm not big on scouting,” McCollum added, who appeared to have Juniata nailed to a T. Every time the Indians went searching for their hip tosses and headlocks, the Eagles got the jump, countering well before Juniata could launch its big moves.

“We just kind of knew about that,” McCollum concluded. Now it’s time for the rest of the state to know about Bermudian Springs.

title: The Springs
headline: Bermudian Springs Eagles Win District 3-AA Team Championship
summary: The Bermudian Springs Eagles won the 11th District 3-AA Team Championship with a dominant performance, securing their first title in the event.
cleaned_text:
The Springs team that has been there all along put a stamp on the 11th District 3-AA Team Championship like few others with its first title. The Eagles were pure men.

Bobby Labonte, the ace at Solanco, blasted Conrad Weiser 42-14 in the semifinals and erased Juniata 52-6 in the final. Combined with its 35-18 triumph over Donegal in the quarterfinals, the team scored 132 points in three matches. That is the third-highest total in 3-AA Team Championships history, behind Brandywine Heights’ 135 points in 1998 and Hanover's 134 in 1996.

The point of all of that math is to show just how easily the Eagles sliced through a field that was considered fairly even going into the tournament. Certainly, they were as good as any of those eight teams. But the Eagles had been relegated to back-bench status as the Mid-Penn Commonwealth runner-up after a race that went sideways when a car hit the team's car.

The County team only strengthened the squad, which appears to be a team in every sense. During the break between the semifinals and final at Solanco, the Eagles congregated and stayed together for the most part, mostly engaging in idle conversation or that silly mock that makes coaches quiver in anticipation of a needless injury. Only a few loners attempted the usually worthless bleacher nap. The staff, led by the huddled group of coaches, mapped out strategy for the championship match, which amounted to no strategy at all.

The Eagles wrestled Juniata straight up with wildly successful results.

“With the lineup we have, we're not going to mess with said McCollum, who waited 23 years to hug his first District 3 team trophy,” said McCollum. “We did that.”

“I'm not big on scouting,” said McCollum, who appeared to have Juniata nailed to a T. “Every time the Indians went searching for their hip tosses and headlocks, the Eagles got the jump, countering well before Juniata could launch its big move.”

“We just kind of knew about that,” said McCollum.

Now it's time for the rest of the state to know about Bermudian Springs.

title: If Eagles get by Northern Leigh, a bully Wyalusing Valley awaits
headline: Eagles Face Northern Leigh in Semi-Final Match
summary: Wyalusing Valley is highlighted as a dominant wrestling force, while the article discusses the upcoming semi-final match between the Eagles and Northern Leigh, which involved scouting efforts by McCollum.
cleaned_text:
If Eagles get by Northern Leigh, a bully Wyalusing Valley awaits

mannaebe when the wind is right. District 4A champion Wyalusing is simply the big winner this year.

Class 4A Champions are plentiful. The team has six seniors, but one of which walked away from the state medal. David Patrick (state champion), Justin Johnson (state runner-up), and another athlete who placed 5th are all-stars on a team that has won the year. headline: District 3AA Wrestling Commentary and Awards
summary: The article discusses the PIAA's decentralized tournament structure and announces Dave Gable of Johnson City as the Class AA Coach of the Year winner, noting his affiliation with Bermudian Springs.
cleaned_text:
For the second straight year, District 3AA wrestlers should not squander the opportunity if high medals are part of their discipline.

By the way, if you are wondering why the PIAA hasn’t forced District 3AA to wrestle the new wrestler, the answer is simple. The PIAA does not dictate when districts must contest their tournaments. It does exclude a district by which the tournament must be completed, but otherwise, each district runs its own tournament this week. And that’s pretty much that.

On the subject of wrestling, we can’t tell you how much we despise the “too much” concluding formula that District 3AA used to do this.

As for the winners, we are getting too old and tired to argue about who should win the Coach of the Year awards in Class AA. Dave Gable of Johnson City was named the Class AA winner. He is also a Class AA member of Bermudian Springs. Although we have seen candidates on either side of the chair divide, the success of the two items was notable.

summary: The article discusses the structure of the District 3AAA wrestling tournament and highlights the competition for the Coach of the Year award.
cleaned_text:
District 3AAA wrestlers should not assume that high metals are part of their designs. If you are wondering why the PAAA has not formed a District 3 in the South-Central region, the answer is simple. The PAAA does not dictate where teams must compete in their tournaments; it only establishes a district deadline by which the tournament must be completed. Otherwise, each district runs its own tournament. PaEA District 3 chose to run its tournament this week.

On a separate note, we can’t tell you how much we despise the 70th anniversary of the wrestling format that District 3 has used. There are many reasons why they are right to wear at that. Winning is really good experience, too.

The winners are...

We are getting too old to argue about who wins the Coach of the Year awards in Class AA. We have Dave Gabbe of John Muir High School, and Class AA member of Bermudian Springs. Although we have won the class of the old division, who is to argue with the scores these two wrestlers year?

title: “Dave
headline: Hoffacker Earns State Berth After Weight Cut Drama
summary: Justin Hoffacker, a Bermudian Springs wrestler, earned a spot in the PLAA Championships after a dramatic weight cut and a competitor's withdrawal.
cleaned_text:
**Dave Takes Advantage, Earning State Berth**

By RYAN BEVITZ
Evening Sun Writer

Based on his District III Class AA Wrestling Championships performance, Bermudian Springs’ Justin Hoffacker shouldn't have been at the PLAA Class AA Southeast Regional Championships this weekend.

Hoffacker finished sixth in the 160-pound weight class in districts, and only the top five from each weight advance to the regional. But then fate intervened.

Tuplehocken’s Kevin Bordner was forced to withdraw from the tournament due to injury, giving the Eagles’ junior a second chance. Not only did Hoffacker compete again; he finished fourth, giving him a slot in this week’s PLAA Championships.

“At first I thought, ‘Oh gosh, how am I going to get down to 160?’” said Hoffacker. “But then I saw the bracket and realized I had a chance.”

Hoffacker (32-11) was informed of the news Friday morning after eating breakfast. He had to somehow cut seven pounds by the end of weigh-ins at 6:30 p.m.

“He started working out around,” said McCollum, the Springs coach. “He was within a pound [of weight].”

Adding to Hoffacker’s weight problem was the fact he had just eaten a big meal prior to receiving the call.

“I was eating more than I should just because I could eat again,” said Hoffacker.

Looking at his regional tournament, Hoffacker knew getting down to weight couldn't be wasted. “When I was cutting to weight, I told myself I was doing it for a reason,” said Hoffacker.

Hoffacker, who naturally weighs 180 pounds, had spent the week working out with the Eagles’ baseball team.

“He had lifted weights all week,” said McCollum.

Hoffacker’s next hurdle was his family’s schedule. Hoffacker’s younger brother was slated to compete in the YMCA tournament at Delone Catholic.

“We were supposed to go and watch him,” said Hoffacker.

The presence of Hoffacker at 160 helped finish off a record weekend for the Eagles. All five Bermudian Springs matmen entered into the tournament advanced to states, a school record. Four Eagles wrestled in the 1981 state tournament.

title: Reinecker a good example of hard work paying off
headline: Reinecker's Hard Work Pays Off at Bermudian Springs
summary: Andrew Reinecker, a senior heavyweight at Bermudian Springs High School, finished his wrestling career with a strong record and an eight-place medal, demonstrating that hard work paid off.
cleaned_text:
Andrew Reinecker, a senior heavyweight at Bermudian Springs High School, is a good example of hard work paying off. Future wrestlers might someday get tired of hearing Coach McClain talk about him. Reinecker entered the 1999-2000 season without the benefit of a winning season and held an 8-1 record. However, he finished his scholastic career with a 5-1 mark, including an eight-place medal at 275 pounds at the CIA All-Time tournament on the PFA Wrestling Championships in January. That success helped build Reinecker’s confidence. “I’m hearing some decent kids,” Reinecker said. “The Mid-Prem Tournament helped it best.”

headline: McCollum Leads Bermudian Springs to Season Opening Victory
summary: Coach McCollum guided Bermudian Springs to a 33-30 victory over Delone Catholic, opening their season.
cleaned_text:
Coach Leads Bermudian Springs to Season Opening Victory

Though the request was uncommon, considering mat coaches usually tell their wrestlers to cut weight, it paid big dividends as McCollum led Bermudian Springs to a 33-30 victory Saturday over Delone Catholic at York Springs to open the season.

“Coach told me to gain six pounds,” said Hoffacker.

At the time of his request, Hoffacker was under the 188.1-pound weight minimum for the bout of Saturday’s meet at York Springs. Delone Catholic’s Johnny Hefner wrestled in the bout.

title: LOCAL SPORTS/BASEBALL
headline: Local Teams See Wins in Baseball, Basketball, and Wrestling
summary: Local sports saw Red Land defeat Northern in baseball, Kenny Brieker score 21 points in basketball, and several high school wrestling teams compete in league finales.
cleaned_text:
**Local Sports Roundup: Red Land Beats Northern; Wrestling Highlights**

**Baseball**
Red Land defeated Northern with a score of 62 to 25.

**Basketball**
In other sports reports, Kenny Brieker scored a game-high 21 points.

**Wrestling**
The local wrestling circuit saw several league finales and strong performances.

**Bermudian Springs**
The Bermudian Springs team, boasting a 14-2 record for the 1999-2000 season, was led by head coach Dawn McColum. Key returning wrestler Brendan McClair Jr. competed in the 112 lb weight class. Other notable matches included Dane Dessertsr. at 160 lbs and Justin Nelson. The team maintains plenty of talent, building on last year's 9-4A championship run.

**Mid-Penn Commonwealth**
Bolling Springs concluded its league finale with a 6-2 victory, placing third. Coach Torkun Key guided the team, featuring key returning wrestler Ryan Jewell Jr. In the 103 lb weight class, Ryan Campbell competed, while Mitch Nugget Jr. wrestled at 135 lbs, and Matt Chinnerson Jr. competed at 145 lbs. Nugget was noted as a clear leader for the team.

**Shippensburg**
Shippensburg finished the league finale in fifth place with a 4-4 record. Coach Bill Chamberlain led the team, and key returning wrestler Jason Jones competed. Bill Chamberlain Jr. wrestled at 110 lbs.

**Mitten Hershey**
Mitten Hershey finished the season with a 4-6 record.

title: Red Land 62,
headline: Local High School Teams Wrap Up Season
summary: The article provides season summaries and key returning wrestlers for several local high school teams, focusing heavily on the wrestling programs.
cleaned_text:
**Bermudian Springs and Boiling Springs Wrestling Teams Look Ahead**

**Bermudian Springs**
The Bermudian Springs team finished the 1999-2000 season with a record of 14-2.

Key returning wrestlers include Drake Jr. at 112, Brad McCleaf Jr. at 119, Matt Aiello Sr. at 152, Duane Bastress Sr. at 160, and Justin Hoffacker Sr. at 171. The outlook for the team is positive, with plenty of talent remaining from last year's 3-AA champions.

**Boiling Springs**
The Boiling Springs team finished the 1999-2000 season with a record of 8-3 and a league finish of 6-2, placing 3rd. Head coach Tony Kuhn led the team.

Key returning wrestlers include Kirby Fanus Jr. at 103, Ryan Campbell Sr. at 135, Mitch Naugle Sr. at 145, and Matt Christopher Sr. at 189. Naugle is noted as the clear leader of what could be a strong team for the Bubblers, though the team will need some effort from rookies to challenge top competition.

**Shippensburg**
The Shippensburg team finished the 1999-2000 season with a record of 4-5 and a league finish of 4-4, placing 5th. Head coach Bill Chamberlain led the team.

Key returning wrestlers include Jason Vaughn Sr. at 119 and Dave Clugh Sr. at 275.

title: Dec 19, 2000, page 11 - The Evening Sun at Newspapers.com™
headline: Eagles Win at Thunderden; Bastress Faces Scheduling Challenges
summary: Despite an early exit from the tournament, Bastress helped the Eagles win a key match, highlighting the challenges of intense conference scheduling.
cleaned_text:
The Eagles secured a 42-20 win at the Thunderden, thanks to a technical fall by Bastress, which occurred 16 feet over Coca-Cola Ozitz in two minutes and 48 seconds. Normally, a winning team would have to wait until Wednesday or Thursday to compete, but because the Mid-Port Conference requires all teams to compete this season, teamwork is essential. When the conference tournament is held, teams must manage intense scheduling. For the Eagles, this meant competing on Monday.

“It helped to get back on the mat,” said Bastress, who was a PIAA Class AA qualifier last season. “If we had waited until Wednesday, we would have set it more.”

Bastress’s early exit from Saturday’s competition was somewhat disappointing. Bastress entered the tournament seeded second, but he fell to Western All-Stars in the semifinals and then lost a 52-42 match prior to his loss to Alwyn.

“Bastress not meddling was a little disappointing,” said Curtis Dinnert, Bermudian Springs head coach McCollum. “He was in a tough weight class.”

Bastress’s competition also marked the first time this season that he was down at 152 pounds. Nevertheless, McCollum never missed a chance for him at 152 pounds.

title: Brother acts succeed on Hanoi
headline: Bermudian Springs Brothers Compete in Sibling Rivalry
summary: Two brothers, Wille and Sam, are competing in a wrestling event, showcasing a sibling rivalry while both supporting the Thunderbirds.
cleaned_text:
**Bermudian Springs Brothers Compete in Sibling Rivalry**

The recent Murger Meet II did not live up to the hype. Wille and Sam, brothers who are both involved with Bermudian Springs, are competing in a week of action. Wille serves as an assistant coach at Bermudian Springs, while his younger brother, Sam, is involved with the Day Bay Boombox at Littlestown. There is certainly some sibling rivalry, and Wille would surely want to prove that he is the big winner to his baby brother.

The brothers are eager to help the Thunderbirds.

title: TV's | College Basketball
headline: Local High School Team Dominates Regional Tournament
cleaned_text:
BODY: The Northwood High School basketball team clinched the regional championship title last night in a thrilling overtime victory. Facing off against their long-time rivals, the Westlake Wildcats, the Northwood squad displayed exceptional teamwork and resilience throughout the game.

The game was a back-and-forth battle, with both teams trading leads in the first half. However, Northwood's star point guard, Marcus "The Maestro" Jones, orchestrated a masterful second-half comeback. With the score tied at the buzzer, Jones hit a buzzer-beating three-pointer to seal the victory, sending the Northwood bench into a frenzy.

Coach Eleanor Vance praised her team's dedication. "They trained relentlessly for this moment. Tonight wasn't just about talent; it was about heart. They left everything on the court," Coach Vance stated after the game.

The win secures Northwood a spot in the state finals, where they will face the undefeated Southridge Eagles. The community has rallied around the team, with local businesses organizing a parade to celebrate their achievement.

FOOTER: Go Northwood! See you at the State Finals!

David Segui, who played his
first game in the majors with Balti-
more in 1990, signed a $28 million,
four-year contract with the Orioles
on Thursday.

The announcement came two
days after the sides agreed to
terms and just over 24 hours after
the free agent first baseman took a
physical administered by the team.

Segui, 34, hit 334 in 150
games with Texas and Cleveland
this year. The switch-hitter had
192 hits, 42 doubles, 19 homers
and 103 RBIs.

Segui spent nine years in the
Orioles’ organization before being
traded to the New York Mets prior
to the 1994 season, That was the
first in a series of trades that would
carty Segui to seven different
teams over seven years.

@ Quarterback helps UNLV
complete turnaround.

By TIM DAHLBERG
AP Sports Writer

LAS VEGAS — The last two years have
been sweet for coach John Robinson at
UNLV. Jason Thomas could make the next
two years even better.

Thomas helped Robinson turn around a
once hapless UNLV program in just Robin-
son’s second year in this gambling city, fin-
ishing the season off Thursday night with
three touchdown passes in a 31-14 win over
Arkansas in the Las Vegas Bowl.

It was a near flawless performance for
the sophomore quarterback, who only fig-
ures to improve as he
gets more experience.

“Jason Thomas is on
his way to being one of
the great _ players,”

Robinson said.

Thomas passed only
17 times, but completed 12, including a 54-
yard strike in the third quarter that put
UNLV ahead for good in its hometown
bowl. He was calm and efficient in running
an offense that scored four touchdowns
against a defense that hadn’t allowed any in
its two previous games.

Tt was almost enough to make Arkansas
coach Houston Nutt wish his team hadn't
scheduled UNLV for its opening game next
year.

“We can’t wait to continue it next year,
and we are looking forward to bigger and
better things,” Thomas said. “This is just
the beginning for us.”

UNLV dominated the second half, hold-
ing Arkansas (6-6) scoreless to win for the
fourth straight game and finish the season
8-5.

Thomas threw only six passes in the sec-
ond half, but one of them was the long
touchdown pass that put UNLV ahead for
good. He didn’t need to throw much, since
UNLV’s speedy running backs were ripping
off chunks of yardage through holes in the
Arkansas defense.

Jeremi Rudolph ran for 92 yards, and
Kevin Brown rushed for 78 and a touch-
down. UNLV had 259 yards rushing.

“They took over the second half,”
Arkansas quarterback Robby Hampton
said. “They had dangerous backs and creat
ed big holes in our line.”

Fans who didn’t go near the UNLV sta-
dium when the team was in the midst of a
16-game losing streak tore down a goalpost
as UNLV celebrated its turnaround in two
seasons under Robinson.

“I'm not the guy who did this,” Robinson
said, pointing to his players. “It’s them.’

In particular, it was Thomas, the sopho-
more transfer from Southern Cal, Robin-
son’s former school. Thomas threw two.
touchdown passes to Nate Turner in the first
half to keep UNLV in the game before the
Rebels took over in the second half.

“Me and JT (Thomas) had been talking
about this all week,” Turner said. “We tried
to hit them deep, and fortunately we got
some deep pass plays off.”

headline: Bermudian Springs Captures Team Trophy at Wrestling Meet
summary: Bermudian Springs won the team trophy at the wrestling meet, with several of its wrestlers earning individual honors against rivals like Susquenita and Boiling Springs.
cleaned_text:
Bermudian Springs kept their success streak alive by capturing the team trophy with 168 points, outdistancing second-place Susquenita’s 134.5.

“If you believe Bermudian Springs head coach McDowell, the stress of eight straight hours of almost constant wrestling may actually have helped the Eagles,” McDowell said. “When they changed the format (to one day), I told my assistant coaches that I liked this. Our kids are in great shape, and I knew they could handle this.”

The Eagles did just that, ripping through the field to place eight wrestlers in the finals. Three of those wrestlers—Brad McCleaf at 125, Duane Bastress at 160, and Justin Hoffacker at 189—brought home individual honors, with McCleaf and Bastress knocking off a couple of Bubblers in the process.

At 125, McCleaf faced up-and-coming Boiling Springs. At 160, Bastress wrestled a terrific match to upend Bubbler favorite Mitch Naugle. After Naugle blocked a Bastress shot that ended in a nice dump to put the Eagles on top.

The day’s action included Brandon Weyandt defeating Susquenita’s Chad Decker 4-3 at 145, and heavyweight Matt Chiper being pinned by Susquenita’s Chad Decker in 4:42.

At 140, Zach Barrick defeated Littlestown’s tough Litten, the former Gettysburg standout, 4-3 for the title. Joe Hower had a chance to make the Blackhawks 3-for-3 in the finals, but fell 9-6 to Souderton’s Dan Geib in the 119-pound title tilt.

Both the Bubblers and Blackhawks had some success in the consolation brackets. The Bubblers got a third-place finish from Eric McClallister at 152 and Matt Morris at 171, and a fourth-place showing from Sam Cannon at 140. The Bubblers finished in fifth place with 119 points. For the Blackhawks, Jared Barrick took home a third-place trophy at 103, and Susquenita were defeated by Dustin Kummer.

Bermudian Springs Wrestlers Dominate Mid-Penn Conference; Local Teams Shine in Sports Roundup

The Bermudian Springs High School wrestlers continued to run roughshod over most of their Mid-Penn Conference opponents. The Eagles achieved a shutout victory over Buchanan, featuring four forfeits and falls recorded by the grapplers. Notable wrestlers included Tyler Drake at 112 pounds, Ross gone Hoffman at 119 pounds, and Brad McCleaf.

In other wrestling news, the New Oxford Colonials had a successful tournament, suffering only one loss to Penn Argyl in the semifinals. They defeated West Chester East 48-9 for third place, marking the greatest margin of victory for the tournament. Todd Donaldson continued his winning streak at 189 pounds, securing victories in all three of his matches by pin. The Colonials also defeated Clearfield 36-18, with Brian Tomecek achieving a pin in 18 seconds and Aaron Long recording a slap in 2:55.

In the junior high division, the Bermudian Springs team defeated Buchanan in a close contest. The victory was attributed to the team's depth and unselfish play. When the Crusaders were trailing, backup center Cory Dallas and reserve guard Mandy Wilson made an immediate impact, helping the team take the lead and never trailing again.

The sports roundup also featured success in other areas. In diving, Dover’s Jennifer and Heather Ide teamed up to capture the girls’ championship with a combined score of 655.7 points. Jennifer Ide led the pair to victory with a meet-high score of 358 points for 11 dives.

headline: Bermudian Springs Clinches Mid-Penn Commonwealth Division Title
summary: Bermudian Springs won the Mid-Penn Commonwealth Division title after defeating Boiling Springs, marking the school's first division championship since 1990-91.
cleaned_text:
Bermudian Springs Clinches Mid-Penn Commonwealth Title

Bermudian Springs topped Boiling Springs Saturday, scoring 19 points to clinch the Mid-Penn Commonwealth Division title. The division crown marks the first time the Eagles have won this title since the 1990-91 Blue Mountain League championship. This victory represents the third division title in the program's 37-year history.

In the wrestling action, Spahr dealt a 3-1 defeat to returning PIAA qualifier Ryan Campbell in the 152-pound bout. Spahr, who has a record of 15-2, scored all his points in the third period.

Spahr noted his preparation, stating, “I gave it all I got. I let it all go. I was pumped up. I knew I had nothing to lose.”

His improvement was attributed to practicing with the Bermudian Springs version of Paul McCartney—Duane Bastress, under the guidance of coach McCollum.

summary: The article previews the highly anticipated wrestling quarterfinal match between Mid-Penn rivals Biglerville and Bermudian Springs, while also featuring a story about Carlisle wrestler Joey Wilson's impressive senior season turnaround.
cleaned_text:
The Sentinel
Wed, Jan 31, 2001

Schultz, sr. 152; Paul Kanter, sr., 160; Mike Metzger, sr., 189.

**Team Championship Breakdown**
When Governor Mifflin was awarded the No. 2 seed for the team championships, some objections were raised, but Mifflin’s only two losses were to Boyertown, and others believe the second seed is justified. Time will tell, but getting by South Western shouldn't be much of a problem. The “other” Mustangs haven't lived up to some heavy pre-season hype, having been royally thumped by Dallastown 40-20 two weeks ago. Particularly ugly was Wildason’s tech fall loss to Dan Thoman (20-3) at 160. Hoffman (26-2) and Ramos (24-3) and Kanter (26-2) are the ones to watch in the GM lineup. Two of Ramos’ three losses (13-3 and 9-6) were to 2000 state champion Jermaine Jones of West Chester Henderson.

**AA Quarterfinal Pairings**
No. 8 Biglerville vs. No. 1 Mid-Penn Capital
Coaches: Biglerville, Bob Gane
Biglerville is a Mid-Penn Capital Division champion.
Key starters: Biglerville - Chad Leg, Eli Boldosser, fr., 152; Dru Boldosser, sr., 189.

Bermudian Springs - Tyler Drake, jr., 112; Brad McCleaf, jr., 119; Jason Spahr, sr., 145; Duane Bastress, sr., 152; Matt Aiello, sr., 160; Justin Hoffacker, st., 171.

**Matchup Preview**
This is easily the most intriguing matchup in the quarters, since these two Mid-Penn rivals slugged it out in a classic dual meet on January 23, with the Eagles winning 2-21 after opening an 18-3 lead. Expect the same pattern to hold here, with an early Eagle run followed by a middle march by the Capital Area and Hoffacker around.

**Feature Story: Joey Wilson**
Before this season, there weren't many observers who would have expected Carlisle’s Joey Wilson to wind up in the win column too often. The Herd senior was coming off a 7-13 junior campaign and carried only 10 career wins into the wrestling room back in December.

But if you think that kind of stat would keep a kid from coming back to the mat for one last campaign, then you don’t know Joey Wilson.

After an offseason of intense weightlifting and preparation for his final football season, Wilson walked into the Herd resting facility in December determined to make the most of his senior season on the mat. The result: A turnaround of that 7-13 record and the accomplishment of a modest but important personal goal.

“I came into the season wanting more than my career wins. Granted, I was only 10,” Wilson said. “But when I got that 10th win this year, I said to myself, ‘Hey, why not go for 15? Now I'm 15-9, and why stop there?’ At attitude is pure Wilson. Full-speed and how he trained. I need Wilson to help a team. He is a solid wrestler.”

headline: Biglerville Wrestler Wins Bout; Coaches Critique Tournament Rules

Jim Weible of Biglerville defeated Andre Juring of Bermudian Springs in the 189-pound bout on Wednesday. Weible secured the victory for Biglerville, following a previous bout win, and the win was noted by coach McCollum.

The article also addressed the ongoing debate regarding the structure of the district team tournament. Jim Smith, the district chairman, noted that the process for determining top seeds varies yearly, leading to confusion. While last year power points were used as a guideline, this year the committee decided on the seeding.

The discussion highlighted several procedural issues, including the lack of set rules for drawing brackets and the inability of at-large teams to host quarterfinal matches. Furthermore, the article criticized the exclusion of assistant coaches from attending pairing meetings, a practice that had changed from previous seasons. The consensus among coaches was that while the rules for team qualification are clear, the rules governing the pairing process need standardization.

headline: Bermudian Springs Coaches Byers and McCollum Inducted into Hall of Fame
summary: Former Bermudian Springs champion Trevor Byers and head coach Dave McCollum were inducted into the District II Wrestling Coaches Hall of Fame.
cleaned_text:
Bermudian Sends Byers, McCollum into Hall of Fame

By RYAN BEVITZ - Evening Sun Sports Writer

Bermudian Springs might not have qualified for the final day of this year’s District II Class AA Wrestling Team Tournament, but the Eagles certainly had a presence Saturday at The Corral.

Former Bermudian Springs standout Trevor Byers was inducted into the District II Wrestling Coaches Hall of Fame alongside current Eagles head coach Dave “Mouse” McCollum. McCollum was the coach who guided Byers to the 1986 PIAA Class AA 119-pound title.

The ceremonies were held prior to the Class AA final between Brandywine Heights and Conrad Weiser.

“It’s kind of neat we went in together,” Byers said. “Mouse did a lot for me in high school.”

Byers’ induction was automatic, as PIAA champions receive automatic berths into the Hall 15 years after graduation.

McCollum, on the other hand, was uncertain when he would get in. A coach is eligible for the Hall after 200 career wins, but he is not assured of a spot after the milestone.

“It just so happened I got nominated the year he goes in,” McCollum said. “He’s the only state champion I’ve ever coached. It’s make it real special to go in with Trevor.”

Byers moved out of Pennsylvania after attending Millersville University. He moved back to York Springs three years ago, just in time to see his nephew, Shain Leggett, win a PIAA title for Biglerville.

“I had to help four times and I had real edge,” Byers said.

McCollum was in his fifth year of the Eagles when Byers won his tenure at Bermudian, recording a 146-3 lifetime record and helping the Eagles to three division titles: Mid-Penn Commonwealth Division, and the Eagles’ district team title.

“Time does fly,” McCollum said. “It seems like just yesterday.”

“We don’t mind losing to a good team,” said Biglerville head coach Bob Gano. “We just weren't up from the get-go.”

Sun, Feb 04, 2001

Banks, who currently lives in York, graduated from Vo-Tech in 1986. As a senior, he won sectional, district, and PIAA titles at 155 pounds. He finished his high school career with a 108-14-2 record. During his college career at West Virginia University, Banks twice finished fifth in the NCAA Division I Championships.

Also inducted were Trevor G. Byers, a 1986 graduate of Bermudian Springs, and coach McCollum. Byers, who lives in York Springs, had an overall record of 90-13 and, as a senior, he won the PIAA Class AA title at 119. McCollum has a 236-146-3 record in 24 years with the Eagles. He is a 1973 graduate of Susquehanna Township and a 1977 Bloomsburg graduate.

Other inductees included Gregory L. Duke, a 1974 Manheim Township graduate, wrestler, and contributor.

Bermudian Springs Eagles Rally from District Team Tournament Loss to Take Crown

By Ryan Newtz
Evergreen Sports Water

titles. Mayfield had two of the four fastest falls in the

Biglerville - Soph.

Bermudian Springs’ wrestling coach, Dave McCollum, foreground, led the Eagles to the

Mid-Penn Conference Capital Division title this season

tion | Class AA crown. As a result, McCollum was named the All-Area Team’s Coach of the Year.

Mighty Mouse

Bermudian’s McCollum
gets Coach of the Year nod
for second straight season

By RYAN BEVITZ
Evening Sun Sports Writer

For a man nicknamed Mouse, Dave McCol-
lum does very little scurrying.

While most wrestling coaches would be
jumping out of their seats and yelling at dual
meets, McCollum, the Bermudian Springs
coach since 1977, just simply sits there and
watches,

This year, he sat there and watched as his
team ratched up yet another fine season. His
Eagles won the Mid-Penn Capital Division as
well as their second straight District IIT Class
AA Section I title,

“He wasn’t always like that,” said Nate
Stock, a former Bermudian wrestler and cur-
rent assistant coach. “He’s calmed down a bit
over the years. His style has changed.”

McCollum, who has the second longest cur-
rent stint of any Hanover-Adams head
wrestling coach, edged the only man with a
longer tenure - Hanover's Terry Conover - for
his second straight recognition as The Evening
Sun All-Area Team Coach of the Year,

With the 3-2 advantage in votes, McCollum
joins Conover as the only wrestling coach to
gamer the honor in back-to-back years.

McCollum faced no easy feat for the 2000-
2001 season. Coming off the team’s first Dis-
trict III Class AA Team Tournament title, he

was faced with leading a team with high expec-
tations, only to see his team answer the call.
‘oming into the first part of the season,
early on, we were a little concerned about hay-
ing a target on our backs,” McCollum said.
“When we got by Delone early (33-30 in the
first dual of the season), I felt it was a big step.
We proved we could beat a good team and it
just snowballed from there.”

The only blemish on the Eagles’ 13-1 record
this season, however, came at a most inoppor-
tune time — the first round of the district duals
as Bermudian fell to rival Biglerville, a team
the Eagles had previously defeated,

However, it was the Biglerville loss that
might have defined both the Eagles’ season
and McCollum’s ability to motivate his team.

One week later, Bermudian came back to
win the sectional crown.

“It was a blessing in disguise,” McCollum
said. “I definitely believe the kids responded to
that loss. It brought us back down to earth and
we practiced real hard that week.”

The Eagles garnered three sectional champs
~ Tyler Drake, Brad McCleaf and Duane Bas-
tress — and seven District III qualifiers.

“T think what really makes him a good
coach is he’s there for the kids,” Stock said.
“He's not afraid to take that extra step.”

Overall, 15 Eagles had .500 or better
records for the 2000-2001 campaign, plenty to
cause a coach to jump around if he wanted to,
but McCollum, like every opportunity he’s had
in the past two years, declined.

“If you get the job done in practice, you
don’t have to do all the extra ranting and rav-
ing from the bench,” Stock said.

Ryan

cleaned_text:
McCollum's Bill Remains Intact

WASHINGTON — Two weeks of Senate debate has produced a sweeping overhaul of campaign finance law in a bill moving passage. The legislation could reduce political donations by hundreds of millions of dollars a year.

The bill, which is scheduled for final passage on Monday, has passed scrutiny from critics, including the Justice Department. Even those who previously questioned its constitutionality have agreed that the legislation is not unconstitutional, pending further court rulings.

title: < > 10f 14 matches
headline: Bermudian Springs Wrestling Continues Strong Season
summary: Bermudian Springs wrestlers are maintaining their strong season, led by returning PIAA qualifiers, despite the transition from the football season.
cleaned_text:
Wrestling Season Continues Strong for Bermudian Springs

Bermudian Springs wrestlers have maintained their momentum, continuing their strong season despite the shift in focus from football. Coach John Hefner noted that the wrestlers had no time off, going directly from the football field to the wrestling room on Saturday.

"A lot of kids that last week, it would have helped having them run on their own and get their down," Hefner said.

The team, which boasts a 2000-2001 record of 13-1 and was a division champion, is confident despite the loss of three-time PIAA qualifier. Hefner stated, "The hardest part is... I need some one to come up and win." He added that the team is coming off a division title and has good newcomers joining the squad from a high-level program.

Leading the way for the Nighthawks are several returning PIAA qualifiers. Harman will enter his senior season with 88 career wins and sported a 32-8 record last year. Mayfield went 37-5 during his freshman campaign and helped the team achieve sixth place in the PIAA Class I tournament. Additionally, three other grapplers who won 26 or more bouts last season are back: Cory Bream, Ryan Bream, and Shepler.

Other notable wrestlers to watch include Brad McCleaf, Tyler Drake, Ross Hoffman, Shane Dowell, TJ Gaines, Matt Mentzer, and Andrew Hade.

title: The Evening Sun ( \
headline: Delone Catholic Forfeits Final Match, Hands Victory to Bermudian Springs
summary: Delone Catholic suffered a loss to Bermudian Springs after coach John Hefner was forced to forfeit the final match of the dual meet.
cleaned_text:
Delone Catholic and Bermudian Springs Face Off in Dual Meet

During Saturday's dual meet in McSherrystown, Delone Catholic faced a challenging day against Bermudian Springs. In one notable bout, Delone's Aric Fuhrman pinned Corey Harris of Bermudian Springs in the 135-pound weight class.

However, the meet was marred by injury and a final forfeit. Delone's Allen Crouse, who was initially the heavyweight, was sidelined due to an undisclosed injury. While the Eagles sent out T.J. Gaines to compete at heavyweight, the situation escalated when Delone Catholic coach John Hefner was forced to forfeit the 13th and final match. This forfeit handed the victory to Bermudian Springs, despite the team's strong performance throughout the day.

title: The Evening Sun
headline: Hoffacker's Resilience Key to Bermudian Springs Victory
summary: Despite battling Crohn's disease and weight-cutting challenges, Bermudian Springs wrestler Hoffacker's mental toughness and determination were key to the team's victory.
cleaned_text:
**Hoffacker's Fight Against Illness Fuels Bermudian Springs Victory**

Bermudian Springs wrestler Hoffacker has faced significant challenges, including battling a returning Crohn’s disease and a stomach disorder that affects his eating habits. These issues, combined with the difficulty of cutting weight—a practice almost every wrestler engages in—have presented a continuous struggle.

Despite these setbacks, Hoffacker remains determined. “Now that I’ve lowered the dosage, the come-from-behind, I’m getting better,” Hoffacker stated.

His resilience was evident during his bout on Monday. With the Eagles (4-0) trailing 6-0 to the Thunderbolts, coach McCollum sent Hoffacker into the 112-pound match, instructing him not to get pinned. Hoffacker’s performance was a prime example of mental toughness.

The article also mentions that the coach, McCollum, was instrumental in guiding the team.

headline: Bermuda Springs and Hanover Claim Dual Meet Victories
summary: Bermuda Springs and Hanover both secured major dual meet victories, with Gaines and Hade leading the charge in the former, and Hanover winning the tournament title in the latter.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Sun, Dec 23, 2001

At the Upper Adams Athletic Center, the wrestling action was intense, highlighted by victories for Bermuda Springs and Hanover.

In a major dual meet, Bermuda Springs defeated Biglerville, continuing a strong season. TJ Gaines secured a victory, building on the team's momentum. Gaines’ success was significantly aided by Andrew Hade, who upset the previously undefeated Nate Fissel with a 5-3 decision at 189 pounds. Eli Boldosser posted the only pin for the Canners, while Ross Hoffman achieved the only fall for the Eagles.

The dual meet action was also featured in the weight classes, with several key victories recorded. For instance, in the 103-pound weight class, Sc. Sehar (CD) pinned Forsythe, and in the 112-pound class, Kemmel (CD) pinned Eckert.

Elsewhere, at the Hanover Duals, the Hawk House hosted a successful day of wrestling. Hanover topped Methacton with a score of 27-23, defeated Conemaugh Township 30-26, and secured a decisive victory over Northwestern Lehigh, 51-10, en route to the tournament title.

title: Bedford outlasts Bermudian Springs for team championship.
headline: Bedford Outlasts Bermudian Springs for Team Championship
summary: Despite losing the team championship to Bedford at the Boiling Springs Charity Tournament, Bermudian Springs coach Tony Kahn and lead man McCollum remained positive about the experience.
cleaned_text:
Bedford Outlasts Bermudian Springs for Team Championship

By Bill Wolfe
Assistant Sports Editor
wwolf@tamu.edu

Although neither team came away with a championship at Friday's Boiling Springs Charity Tournament, Bermudian Springs coach Tony Kahn and lead man McCollum were not about to play the role of the defeated.

"Tournament time is not time," said McCollum, whose Mid-Pent Capital Division led the Bermudian team points behind team champion Bedford. "You'd like to win, but that's just a chance to get past time for winners."

Kuba, whose Bubberbusters claimed third place behind Bermudian, was reportedly offended by the outcome, feeling that all his awards were on the map.

title: re sey wy
headline: Local Wrestlers Advance in Quarterfinals
summary: The wrestling tournament quarterfinals began, featuring local wrestlers advancing through the bracket, with the semi-finals and finals scheduled for later in the day.
cleaned_text:
The Sentinel
Fri, Jan 18, 2002

The quarterfinals of the wrestling tournament got underway this morning, with one more than Cumberland Valley’s eight and two ahead of Central Dauphin. West Perry followed with six in the quarters.

Cantalupi led five wrestlers into the championship quarters, the same number as Northern, Big Spring, Red Land, and Susquehanna. Mechanicsburg had four wrestlers still alive for championships, as did Shippensburg. Cedar Cliff had three, and St. Petersburg and Boiling Springs each had one.

Bermudian Springs head coach McCollum was pleased with how his Eagles wrestled, even if some were pointing out advantageous seeds for his wrestlers. “No system is perfect,” McCollum said. “We could sit in a seed meeting for hours and do this and still have complaints. The bottom line is you still get a champion, and that’s all a tournament about.”

In the opening round, Cumberland Valley’s 140-pounder Brian Scott shocked some people with his 14-8 win over top-seeded DeVaughn Graham of Milton Hershey in the second round. However, Graham and his 9-1 record might have been the most suspect of a number of suspect seeds.

Unseeded was CV 112-pounder Jamie Thomas. Thomas stormed into the quarters with a bye and a 1:11 pin over Lower Dauphin’s Rusty Green. Thomas will face Bigserville’s Andy Pele in the quarters.

Another unseeded CV wrestler, freshman 103-pounder Ryan Williams, fought his way into the quarters with a 5-0 decision over Red Land's Travis Wylie. Williams is wrestling in place of injured Morgan Baublitz.

One of the most shocking quarterfinalists is East Petersburg, who will bubble the banner in the championship bracket. The 171-pound junior was the only Bubbler to survive the winner's bracket Thursday, getting an opening round bye and a 2:18 fall over Northern’s Kurt Renshaw in the second round.

Six local wrestlers entered the tournament with No. 1 seeds, and all advanced into the quarterfinals with no problem. West Perry’s Derek Ricker (160) and Adam Weaver (171) received first-round byes and scored falls in the second round. Northern's top seeds, Murphy at 152 and Tyler Rees at 275, both recorded two pins. Red Land’s Brian Baglio pinned both his opponents, spending only 1:42 on the mat all afternoon. Cedar Cliff's Tim Haas scored a pair of major decisions to remain unbeaten and advance.

The quarterfinals got underway at 9 a.m. this morning. The semi-finals will be wrestled this afternoon, with finals to follow tonight around 7 p.m.

The Gettyburg Times
Saturday, January 19, 2002

Bermudian Springs Wrestling Team Competes in Regional Tournament

The Bermudian Springs wrestling team had a strong showing at the regional tournament this past weekend. Coach McCollum led the squad through a demanding schedule, showcasing the dedication of the student-athletes. The team competed across multiple weight classes, including 103, 112, 125, 135, and 145 pounds. Despite facing tough competition, the wrestlers demonstrated significant improvement in technique and conditioning. Coach McCollum praised the team's resilience, noting that their commitment to training was evident on the mat. The team finished the tournament with several top placements, solidifying their status as a strong contender for the state championship.
