Bermudian Springs Was Annoyed After Loss, But Coach McCCollum Leads Team to Victory

By Rop Frisco
OF THE PATRIOT-NEWS

Bermudian Springs was a little annoyed. After a difficult loss this past Thursday, a loss that might have wrinkled the Eagles’ smooth ride to a high seed for this week's District 3-AA Team Championships, the team couldn't wait to exorcise its frustration.

The Eagles (10-1) will move into Wednesday's District 3-AA Team Championships quarterfinals, despite the seven-point defeat to Hanover earlier in the week.

“That loss on Thursday hit home to some of our kids,” said Bermudian Springs head coach Dave McCCollum. “But we didn’t pick a very good time to lose.”

McCCollum continued, “It would have been an absolutely terrible time to lose if the Eagles hadn't righted themselves. Taking two losses into the Team Championships would have been devastating.”

The team found its momentum with a sharp 9-0 start from lightweights Kirk Hoffacker at 103, Jon Hade at 112, and Tyler Drake at 119, putting the Eagles on the fast track.

The article also noted that Susquenita (14-4) was also moving into the quarterfinals.

“We knew we'd have to be tough from 103 and 145 and get as many wins as we could. Susquenita has some good upper weight wrestlers, but we are better than they were,” said McCCollum.

The match saw Bermudian Springs build a significant lead, eventually reaching 23-0. Susquenita managed to break through, but Bermudian Springs maintained its edge. The victory was secured by Nick Storm, who turned in a pin at 171, curing the victory.

“I was really pleased,” said McCCollum. “That was an important win.”

**The Patriot-News**
**Thursday, January 31, 2002**

**Bermudian Springs Wrestling Highlights**

In a recent bout, Conrad Weiser secured a victory over Kirk Hoffacker at the 103-pound weight class. The match required overtime, as Hoffacker lost traction on a front headlock roll-through in the final ten seconds of the period. Hoffacker ended up on his back and lost 7-2, giving Conrad Weiser a 3-0 lead.

Bermudian Springs head coach McCollum commented on the effort, stating, “They put us on our heels right away at 103. We didn’t expect to lose. Hoffacker is a pretty decent wrestler. He normally doesn't make a bad decision like that. That worried me right away. You want to go to the 30-second rideout when you're that close.”

The third-seeded Eagles (11-1) advanced to Saturday's Class AA semifinals against second-seeded Brandywine Heights at South Western High. While No. 6 Conrad Weiser actually won more bouts (7-6), he had the edge where it counted most.

**Dual Meet Results**

In another dual meet, host Brandywine Heights won a thriller against Susquenita. The victory was powered by heavyweight Cody Young, who prevented Susquenita’s Chad Decker from getting the pin or technical fall, which was crucial for the Blackhawks to win the dual. Decker secured a 19-7 major. Following the Decker-Young bout, Susquenita had a team point deducted when an assistant coach ran onto the mat. The final score was 29-28, with Brandywine Heights winning by virtue of a 7-6 bout edge.

Separately, Delone Catholic, the fourth seed, defeated Hanover with a score of 34-22, powered by two pins.

cleaned_text:
The Gettysburg Times
Mon, Feb 4, 2002

**SQUIRE KING: Delone Catholic Wrestlers Lift Head Coach John Hefner on Their Shoulders After Winning the District 111 Class AA Team Championships**

Delone Catholic wrestlers celebrated a major victory, lifting head coach John Hefner after winning the District 111 Class AA Team Championships Saturday evening at South Western High.

The Squires defeated Juniata in the semifinals. "Nobody thought we could beat them, but we knew we could," said Jacoby, referring to the win over Juniata. "We were pumped up for it."

Delone dropped a 26-22 decision to the top-seeded Indians a week earlier, where they won nine bouts on the way to a mind-bending 40-18 rout.

The Indians were without star 12-pound junior Mike Bryan, a state medalist from the previous year, who was suspended for violating school policy. Bryan's absence was compensated for when freshman Luke Jethas pinned his replacement, Lay Shoop, in 1:36.

Following Tyler Gilson's decision at 119, the Squires won three straight bouts to put the Indians in a difficult spot. Dana Gingerich got a fall at 125, followed by a major bout win that sealed the Squires' trip to the finals.

Meanwhile, while Delone was celebrating its semifinal win, Bermudian Springs looked to have booked its trip to the finals as well. The Eagles (11-2) led Brandywine Heights (12-4) 25-20 before the roof caved in at 189. Justin Betz took senior Andrew Hade down with four seconds left in the bout to snatch a 4-3 win, bringing the Eagles lead to 25-23.

At McCollum’s top big man, senior TJ Gaines, was still battling the effects of pneumonia. While backup Matt Munson had just three varsity bouts under his belt, McCollum went with Gaines. Gaines led 2-1 going into the third period but began to lose his battle with fatigue, allowing Cody Young to stay in the bout. Young picked up a point on a Gaines’ stall call and later escaped to take a 3-2 lead. Gaines made a valiant effort but couldn't prevent a takedown by Young, losing 5-2. Young’s win gave the Bullets a 26-25 victory.

"We felt TJ. could wrestle and he felt he could wrestle," said McCollum. "We'll never know if Munson could have beat him or not. It's a decision we made, and now we have to live with it."

title: Dynamic Duo
headline: Bermudian Springs McCarthy Brothers Target PIAA Success
summary: The McCarthy brothers of Bermudian Springs are competing in the PIAA Class AA Southeast Regional Championships, aiming for state titles and personal career milestones.

**Dynamic Duo from Bermudian Springs Aim for PIAA Glory**

By Nryan Britz

In an era where postponements force high school wrestlers to travel extensively to find practice partners, the Bermudian Springs team has been exceptionally successful. For the past four seasons, the McCarthy brothers, Davis and Brend McCarthy, have built a formidable reputation, qualifying as top contenders for the state tournament.

The brothers are set to compete in the upcoming PIAA Class AA Southeast Regional Championships. Their goals are ambitious: not only are they aiming for top finishes in the PIAA tournament, but they are also vying for major personal milestones, including 100 career wins.

"They sort of fed off of each other for four years," Davis McCarthy noted.

The action for the PIAA Class AA Southeast Regional is scheduled to begin at 4:15 p.m. on Friday. The brothers are competing across multiple weight classes, aiming to secure top spots and continue their quest for state championship success.

headline: Local Wrestlers Shine Despite State Meet Disappointment
summary: Despite missing the ultimate goal at the state meet, local wrestlers like Medina and Ortiz achieved significant victories and celebrated the end of a season marked by athletic success.
cleaned_text:
A Gains Redemption at State Meet

Ryan Bevitz
The Evening Sun

Medina had to watch the state meet from the stands. “States was the ultimate goal, but you can’t always get everything you want,” Medina said.

Medina had two chances to go out a winner during Tuesday’s event at Central York High, and earned the victory both times. He was a 6-5 winner over Kennard-Dale’s Andy Reiterer at the beginning of the meet, and then pulled out a last-second 2-1 victory over Susquehannock’s Matt Kelly in the last bout of the night.

Later in the tournament, Medina wrestled twice at 140 pounds due to a late scratch by Spring Grove’s Andy Zeigler. Medina led the West squad to a 26-21 win over the East. “It felt good to finish with a win and not have that loss lingering,” Medina said. “It’s a good way to end the season.”

Other local successes included Ortiz, who was a 9-1 winner over Delone Catholic’s Allen Crouse at heavyweight. Meanwhile, Harman was a 5-3 winner over Spring Grove's Mike Polashuk at 160 pounds.

The article also noted that many local matmen competed, including Littlestown’s Alex Leib, Hanover’s Nate Klunk, Ed of Hershey, New Oxford's Nate Biles, and Bermudian Springs’ TJ McCollum.

Medina reflected on his wrestling career, noting that while he didn't have much time to mourn his losses in the district tournament, his dreams of grappling at the college level have helped console him. “Just going to the college level has helped me get back on track,” Medina said. “It will be the last time competing in the sport for many who participated, although Medina plans to continue to compete in college.”

**SPORTS**
**SATURDAY, AUGUST 17, 2002**

**NFL Rule Changes Proposed**
The New York Times reported that the New York Giants have been working on a rule change for a potential playoff game against the Chicago Cubs. The proposed change would allow the Giants to score a touchdown on a pass to a receiver in the first half, and then score a touchdown on a pass to a receiver in the second half. Furthermore, the Giants would be allowed to score a touchdown on a pass to a receiver in the third half, while the Cubs would be allowed to score a touchdown on a pass to a receiver in the fourth half.

**PGA Championship**
The PGA Championship is facing uncertainty due to impending weather conditions.

Mid-Penn Capital champion.

Wrestlers to Watch: Shane Dowell (sr.), Kevin Flickinger (sr.), Cory Harris (sr.), Ross Hoffman (sr.), Jonathan Lehr (sr.), Gavin Leppo (sr.), Mitch Megonell (sr.), Nick Storm (sr.), Kirk Hoffacker (jr.), Jason Marshall (jr.), Jared Wolfe, Kyle Flickinger (soph.), Jonathan Hade (soph.).
Outlook: The Bears are coming off a Capital Division title and are optimistic. Bowersox feels that if the team can get a strong showing from its upper weights, the squad can contend for the division title. To do that, Bowersox will look to Sam Collins (11-11 in 2001-02) to anchor the upper weights. The lower weights will be led by Mike Anderson, a returning state qualifier, along with returning wrestlers like Cory Gorman (20-15), Shannon Weaver (18-8), and Conover (16-13).
Quote: “We're going to have to fight, scratch and claw for everything.” - Dave Bowersox

**Cannern**
Head Coach: Bob Gano
2001-2002 Record: 13-3, 8-0
Mid-Penn Capital champion.
Wrestlers to Watch: Brian Campbell (sr.), Pat Crawford (sr.), Chad Leggett (jr.), Andy Pele, Dustin Trostel (jr.), Gregg Reber, Jared Heckenluber (so), Elijah Paterson (soph.), Jake Showers (soph.).
Outlook: With a roster of only 15, the Cannern are going to have to stay injury-free and keep everyone eligible to contend for the Capital Division crown. They'll also need some key wrestlers to step up to fill the shoes of those who graduated or who chose not to go out for the team. Pelc, a two-time state qualifier, is the biggest name among the returnees. His 30-15 record last year will shoulder some of the load. Also look for returning district qualifiers Leggett (24-7 in 2001-2002), Rebert (16-14), and Trostel (26-11) to contribute.
Quote: “We're going to have to fight, scratch and claw for everything.” - Bob Gano

**Spring Grove**
Head Coach: Tony Miller
2001-2002 Record: 13-4, 7-1
YAIAA co-champion.
Wrestlers to Watch: Joe Bresnahan (sr.), Zach Crumbling (sr.), Kevin Henning (sr.), Zach Abel (jr.), Cody Becker (jr.), Brendan Swope (jr.), Kyle Sprenkle (jr.), Kelin Bidelspach (jt.), Andrew Sheridan (soph.).
Outlook: Throughout Miller's tenure at Spring Grove, which began with the 1995-96 season, Miller has built a program that has achieved success.

The Gettysburg Times reported on the intense quarterfinal action at the high school wrestling tournament. The article details the results across various weight classes, noting several upsets and strong performances.

In the 103-pound division, Mark Gioin (Gettysburg) received a bye, while in the 112-pound division, Andy Peto (Big Springs) and Kirk Hoffacker (Bermudian Springs) also received byes. The 125-pound division saw Troy Lien (Gettysburg) advance, and the 140-pound division featured Gaines (Bermudian Springs) defeating Dits (EP).

The action was particularly intense in the upper weight classes. In the 135-pound division, Jake Kump (Gettysburg) defeated Owen (Ship), and in the 118-pound division, K. Flickinger (Bermudian Springs) was involved in high-stakes matches.

The report highlighted several standout performances, including Shane Dowell, who put together two solid wins, and Joel Brookens, who appeared to be in contention for multiple periods. Other notable matches included Bozart of Cedar Cliff defeating Jon Hade (Bern) and Moore (EP).

The article concluded by noting the depth of talent, with several wrestlers advancing to the semifinals, showcasing a highly competitive field across the state.

The Evening Sun
Fri, Jan 17, 2003

The night’s match was indicative of the rivalry between Biglerville and Bermudian Springs.

“That match took a lot of wind out of our sails,” Biglerville coach Bob Gano said. “But it happened, and hopefully we can learn from it.”

“You just throw the records out and let the kids go at it,” McCollum, Bermudian Springs coach Dave, said regarding the rivalry.

The intensity was evident in the 130-pound match between Biglerville’s Brian Campbell and the Eagles’ Shane Dowell. Campbell entered the match with a 2-7-11 record, while Dowell was competing in the 140-pound match.

Other notable bouts included Mitch Megonnell pinning Pat Crawford at 4:46. Biglerville secured the next two matches with Gregg Rebert earning a 7-2 decision over Gavin Leppo and Justin Trostel, and an 11-3 major decision over Nick Storm.

The overall scoring saw Biglerville’s 10-7 deficit grow into a 22-7 deficit, forcing the Canners to forfeit their matches at 171 and 189.

In other results, Andy Pele won an 11-3 major decision for Biglerville over Kevin, and Jon Hade secured an 11-0 major decision over Landon McCleaf.

Biglerville, coming off of a tough 32-25 loss to Littlestown, its first loss to the Bolts since God created whizzers or at least the technical fall, dropped to 5-2 in the division and 8-6 overall. While a 3-AA Team Championship is almost assured for the unbeaten Eagles now, Biglerville’s spot in the 12-team field is decidedly uncertain.

“But all we were tonight was our 10th win. The good things can’t happen until we get our fifth win,” said the Bermudian Springs head coach.

In a separate story, Duke held off Wake Forest. Heather Miller, left, reached to knock the ball away from Duke's Mistie Bass last night during the second half of the No. 1 Blue Devils’ 71-61 win.

Down 28-11, the Canners were turning into the strength of their lineup, starting with Leggett. title: “Dave
headline: Bermuda Springs Defeats Hanover in Wrestling Showdown
summary: Bermuda Springs defeated Hanover 39-19 in a wrestling match, relying on the depth of its roster to secure the victory.
cleaned_text:
Bermuda Springs High's Ryan Enyeart faced off against Hanover High School on Wednesday night. The match was a close battle, with both teams maneuvering for extra points. Ultimately, the depth of the Eagles proved decisive as they defeated the Hawks 39-19 on their home mats.

"They played conservative at the weights where we were better," said Conover.

The article details several key moments of the match. Cory Bream scored four points for Hanover with a 9-0 major decision at 130 pounds, but the Hawks lost six points at 135 pounds when Bermuda Springs’ Ross Hoffman pinned Matt Redding. Hanover also lost an opportunity at 140 pounds when Thomas Slaugh dropped his first lead of the year.

The Eagles secured the win by accumulating three pins and a decision in the next five matches. Jason Marshall at 171, Nate Cookerly at 189, and Tim Eyler at 275 all earned pins, and Nick Storm won by decision at 160 pounds. Chip Rupp’s 4-0 decision at 215 pounds was the Hawks’ only win at the upperweights.

On the other side, Hanover’s Gary Mayfield remained unbeaten with a 14-7 win over Kirk Hoffacker at 103 pounds. Despite the loss, Hoffacker managed to save his team a point when he scored an escape in the final seconds of the match.

The Patriot-News
Thu, Jan 30, 2003

The Eagles secured a crucial victory, defeating the Nighthawks 25-17. This win was vital, giving the undefeated Bermudian Springs Eagles (15-0) life in the semifinal match to defend their 3-A Association champion title.

Semifinals begin at noon. In the other Class AA semifinal, top-seeded Juniata (23-1) will take on Brandywine Heights (11-3), also at noon.

"I can't say enough about how these kids stuck together," said the head coach, "and I'm glad we're moving on in the team championship."

The No. 3-seeded Eagles faced their second close match in consecutive seasons. Last year’s quarterfinal match came down to the final bout and ended with a 24-23 victory for the Eagles.

"But I've never been in a situation where it came down to me. Actually, there was earlier this year against Greenville, but that bout was different," said Bermudian Springs coach McCollum.

Bermudian Springs scored a big victory over Ryan Kopenhaver at 130 pounds, which gave the Eagles a significant advantage with two bouts remaining. At 130 pounds, Cory Bream scored a victory over newcomer Ryan Enyeart.

"I'm really proud of Ryan," McCollum said. "He's been thrown into this situation, and a varsity match is a tough wrestler. We asked him to have a tough time and keep it close."

The team looked to Ross to get a pin done. In a dual meet, Bermudian Springs scored key pins at 171 pounds and 103 pounds, besides Hoffman, who downed Mitchell.

title: 1e way I did all year,” Hoffman said.
headline: Local Teams Shine in Dual Meet Action
summary: Local wrestling teams, including Bermudian Springs, secured key victories and pins during a competitive dual meet.
cleaned_text:
“It was the way I did all year,” Hoffman said. “I was told to do the same things that worked this season.”

Hoffman scored a reverse early in the bout and caught Redding in another cradle, scoring the pin. “I pinned him earlier this year,” Hoffman said. “I just stayed with the game.”

“I’m nervous?”
“No,” Hoffman quickly replied. “I kept my eye on the bout before me just to see how it went. I didn’t give up any more than three points, but I’ve never been in a situation where it came down to me. There was one earlier this year against Biglerville, but that bout was forfeited—we won.”

Jon Hade scored a big 6-4 victory over Ryan Kopenhaver at 125 pounds, giving the Eagles a 25-22 advantage with two bouts remaining. At 130 pounds, the Nighthawks’ Cory Bream secured a 6-0 victory over a newcomer.

In a dual meet where every point counted, key pins were scored by Jason Marshall (171 lbs) and Kyle Flickinger (103 lbs), besides Hoffman. The Nighthawks earned a pin from Ryan Shipler (145 lbs), who downed Mitch MeGonnell at 145 pounds. The Eagles were trailing 15-13 after 215 pounds, but Tim Eyler scored a tight 8-7 win at 275 pounds, putting them ahead 16-15.

“I can’t say enough about how these kids stuck together. Things were on the line, and I’m glad we're moving on in the team championship,” said Ryan Enyeart.

“I’m really proud of Ryan,” McCollum said. “He’s been thrown into this situation, and Bream is a tough wrestler. We asked him to hang tough and keep it close. Then we looked to Ross to get the job done. He came through for us.”

“It was that kind of battle all night,” McCollum added. “A few weeks ago we beat Hanover pretty handily, but you can never count out that squad.”

Hanover advanced to the quarterfinal contest after a beating Biglerville 39-36.

Of the Rockies Round.

In the world of high school wrestling, Juniata defeated Bermudian Springs last night in the District 3-AA Team Championship at Ft. Western High School, winning its first team title.

"Juniata's just better," said a 19-year-old Baltimore resident. "Hofman broke boats against Brandt. He was right. Juniata broke boats against Brandt. He was right."

Michael Tennenbrook, Tula

Juniata's 189-pound Philip Bomber received ululations after his pin closed the match.

headline: Eagles Beaten by Juniata in Final
summary: The Bermudian Springs Eagles lost the District II Class AA Team Tournament final to the heavily favored Juniata Indians, despite their hopes of avenging a previous loss.
cleaned_text:
Eagles Beaten by Juniata in Final

By RYAN BEVITZ
Evening Sun Sports Writer

Perhaps in a storybook, Bermudian Springs High would have won.

Denied an opportunity to wrestle for the District II Class AA Team Tournament title a year earlier, the hungry Eagles would have avenged 2002's disappointment with a set of shiny new gold medals in '03.

Those thoughts will have to remain in a storybook. Any thoughts of a Hollywood ending for the Eagles were quickly squashed by the cruel facts of reality. In the end, the Goliath known as Juniata is just better.

"They're a flat-out better team with better athletes and better wrestlers," said McCollum.

The Indians (Juniata) were ranked No. 1 and proved too much for Bermudian Springs. "We knew they were going to be tough," McCollum said.

David, as the Eagles fell to the Indians 47-17 Saturday in the final at South Western High School.

While the Eagles may have been upset with their loss to the heavily-favored Indians, they did relish their opportunity.

title: <|> serremars
headline: Juniata Claims First Team Title in District Championships
summary: Juniata defeated Bermudian Springs 47-17 in the District 3-AA Team Championships final to secure its first team title.
cleaned_text:
In the testosterone-filled world of high school wrestling, few instances are as definitive as a champion-ship final. Last night, Juniata defeated Bermudian Springs 47-17 in the District 3-AA Team Championships final held at South Western High School, securing its first team title.

Juniata dominated the final, taking seven of the eight available bouts to pull away from the Eagles.

“Juniata’s just better. They have better wrestlers. To only give them 47 was no bad,” said the Bermudian Springs coach.

Despite the lopsided final, Bermudian Springs (16-1) earned high marks simply by qualifying for the championship match, as both finalists had to scratch their way through the afternoon semi-finals.

Juniata survived early trouble to defeat Briar Heights 34-30, and Bermudian Springs’ strong finish carried it past defending champion Delone Catholic 34-33.

The final itself was one-sided. Juniata’s 14-man lineup matched up well against McCollum’s gritty Eagles. After the teams split the first four bouts, beginning with the 215 pounds weight class, the bonus points came in big flurries for the Indians. Juniata quickly extended their margin, going from 9-8 to 22-8, and finally to 41-17 with a pin at 171 pounds, courtesy of Phil Bomberger pinning Eric Aumen.

“He was right,” said Hart, referring to the matchup. “Juniata lost all bouts against Brandywine, trailed 30-21 with one left—160, 171, and 1. But with upper-wrestler Bomberger lurking, the Indians needed to win 160 and 171 to win, and they were fine both.”

headline: Area Squads Battle for Team Title at Wrestling Tournament
summary: Two area squads are expected to compete fiercely for the team title as the wrestling tournament progresses through the semifinals.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Sat, Feb 15, 2003

The tournament schedule has been adjusted, moving today’s start time to 11 a.m.

Once the tournament resumes, two area squads will be in the thick of the team title chase. Wrestlers who received a bye in the quarterfinals will not get any advancement points unless they win in the team standings.

Hanover coach Terry Conover stated that a strong start is crucial because it sets up the team for Saturday. “That's where you really have to rise to the challenge,” he said.

In other news, Spring Grove’s Joe Bresnahan (275), Gettysburg’s Ben Orendorff (275), South Western's Will Mechalske (275), and New Oxford’s Ashley Boyers (275) were among the wrestlers who competed. The Hawks pulled out two consolation wins, placing them in second place with 24 points. Hanover currently has nine points in the semifinals.

“We're all right. We didn’t get upset,” Conover said. “We're trying to win this thing and we're hoping to score a lot of points yet.”

Littlestown sits in third place with only one wrestler eliminated. Coach Dave Bowersox’s young team pulled off two wins over higher-seeded wrestlers, including a 10-1 victory by 152-pounder Nate Hawn over third seed Jeremy Cannon of Boiling Springs.

Host Delone Catholic sits in fourth, but coach John Hefner knows the tournament is far from over. Like Hanover, the Squires only have 13 entrants, but 10 made the semifinals, and two more are alive in the wrestlebacks. Hefner noted, “As a team, the kids did as well as they could. I would have liked to get another pin or two though.”

Biglerville is in sixth with 14 points. Six of the nine wrestlers Coach Bob Gano brought to the tournament are in the semifinals.

The semifinal round is heavily populated by Hanover-Adams matchups, with 44 of the 56 wrestlers coming from those two schools. The semifinals begin at noon.

title: “Dave h
headline: Gettysburg Wrestling Program Avoids Aberration
summary: Gettysburg wrestling coach Perry Smith discusses his team's successful season, noting that the program's resurgence was highlighted by senior Brett Miller's performance.
cleaned_text:
An Aberration for the Once Mighty Gettysburg Program?

Thanks to this year’s 13-4 season, the answer is a definitive no.

“I’d like to think we never left,” Smith said.

The result was Gettysburg earning its fourth berth in the District II Team Tournament, and Smith winning his third All-Area Coach of the Year nod. Smith beat out Hefner for the honor.

“Last year was a tough year, and people were wondering if Gettysburg would be down for a while,” Smith said. “So this year was very important for our program. There was a responsibility on the part of the kids this year to maintain the respect and regenerate what we had, both on and off the practice room.”

PERRY SMITH
Coach of the Year

“At the beginning of the year, we knew this could be one of our most balanced teams,” Smith said. “We didn’t have any weak spots.”

Perhaps no wrestler better illustrated Gettysburg’s turnaround than senior Brett Miller. The 215-pounder entered this season with a modest 33-56 career record, but went 34-10 en route to a berth in the PIAA Championships.

**Friday, November 28, 2003**

**Wrestling Season Previews: Local Teams Look Ahead**

The Evening Sun featured a roundup of local high school wrestling teams preparing for the 2003 season, offering previews and outlooks for several Pennsylvania schools.

**Catholic**
The preview highlighted several wrestlers, including John Hefner (130 lbs, 17-2, 8-0), Dana Gingerich, Justin Miller, Aric Leeper, Dustin Thas, Justin Newman, Curtis Hard-Hocker, Dan Mentzer, and Jeff Miller. The article noted that the team would struggle to assess due to a small roster size.

Under Head Coach Dave Conaway, New Oxford entered the season with a 2002-03 record of 12-5, 6-2. Key losses included Ryan Anderson, Ashley Boyers, Barrett Clabaugh, Mike Ferrer, Kyle Feldner, and Wayne Laughman. Wrestlers to watch included Phil Grillo, Gared Chrismer, Nate Emet, Tyler Smith, and Matt Topman.

**Bermudian Springs**
Coach McCollum led the Bermudian Springs team, which had a 2002-03 record of 12-5, 6-2. Key losses included Shane Dowell, Gavin Leppo, Nick Storm, Mitch Megonnell, and Kevin Flickinger. McCollum reported that he had 30 wrestlers on his roster, with nine returning starters. Wrestlers to watch included Nathan Cookerly, Tim Eyler, Kirk Hoffacker, Jason Marshall, Adam Staub, Ryan Enyeart, Kyle Flickinger, Scott Ford, and Jon Hade.

**Biglerville**

**Gettysburg Warriors**
The Gettysburg Warriors were highlighted for their successful 2002-03 season, which included a berth in the District III Team Tournament, ten District III qualifiers, and a PIAA Tournament qualifier in Miller. Despite losing five wrestlers to graduation, including Miller, the Warriors aimed for another strong season by returning eight of the ten District II qualifiers from the previous year. headline: Local Teams Shine at Mid Penn Tournament
summary: Several local high school wrestling teams, including the Eagles, Gettysburg, and Biglerville, achieved notable success and multiple medals at the Mid Penn tournament.
cleaned_text:
The Gettysburg Times
Mid Penn Tournament Highlights

The Eagles featured two of the area's finalists: Kirk Hoffacker (112) and Jon Hade (152). While both Hoffacker and Hade had strong tournaments, neither captured the elusive Mid Penn gold. Hoffacker was pinned by Cumberland Valley’s Morgan Baublitz, and Hade dropped an 8-1 decision to Chris Murray of Central Dauphin.

Hade noted that carrying extra weight wasn't necessarily a disadvantage, referencing his silver effort at 152 pounds. "I'm not a big fan of cutting weight," said Hade, who had wrestled 3-2 at 130 pounds in last year's Mid Penn tournament. "I wanted to stay healthy and keep all of the muscle that I had. Plus, I wanted to get a lot bigger than I had been." Hade was certainly bigger, and perhaps even better than the previous year. He blanked second-seeded Brett Christopher of Boiling Springs 7-0 in the semifinals before running into Murray. "My goal was to make it to the finals and show people that I've come a long way," Hade stated.

The Eagles achieved significant success in just two days with a new-look lineup, grabbing eight medals. Jason Marshall and Kyle Flickinger (125) bounced back for third-place finishes. Other medalists included Bryce Marshall (6th at 103), Rickey Schmelyun (5th at 119), Nathan Cookerly (4th at 189), and Tim Eyler (6th at 275). "Eight medals up here, I can't complain about that," said a coach. "It’s a weekend to grow up real fast, and it’s our opportunity to wrestle the AAA boys."

The local AAA bunch, Gettysburg, bounced back from a rocky consolation round Saturday morning to produce five medalists. Joe Martin (145) and Joel Brookens (215) led the charge by placing third at their respective weights. Martin's run included four straight consolation wins, three of which came against seeded wrestlers. Brookens dropped a semifinal heartbreaker to Tribbitt in overtime but recovered nicely to finish third. Troy Litten (6th at 130), Zack Kump (4th at 135), and Colby Haller (4th at 171) also picked up medals for the Warriors.

Biglerville's Gregg Rebert (3rd at 160) and Jared Heckenluber (6th at 145) were the area's final medalists. Rebert gave a gutsy effort in a 1-1 rideout win over Cumberland Valley’s Tim Cramer for third, while Heckenluber dropped three straight bouts after reaching the semifinals.

Coach McCollum commented on the overall quality of the tournament. "I thought Jut Trostel had an extremely good tournament," he said. "He wrestled extremely well and I'm proud of him. Gregg wrestled back very well. He showed a lot of desire in that third-place match. I also think Jared wrestled better than his record indicated. It was a great tournament for us."

summary: The article reports on Lance Armstrong's cycling ambitions, details successful wrestling matches at Bermudian Springs, and covers a victory for Detone Catholic in the Columbia Christmas Tournament basketball consolation game.
cleaned_text:
**Armstrong Eyes Cycling Glory; Local Athletes Shine in Wrestling and Basketball**

**Cycling**
Lance Armstrong, 32, stated that while he is eyeing a place in cycling history, he is aware that age will limit his future racing career. He noted that the only other five-time winners of the Tour de France are Eddy Merckx, Miguel Indurain, Bernard Hinault, and Jacques Anquetil. Armstrong said, “It's not like they suddenly stopped at five. They all tried and intend to do the same. I'm excited about it and I'd be very upset if I lost.” He believes his love of the ride, whether up a mountain or on a straightaway, still gives him the edge.

**Wrestling**
The wrestling circuit saw strong performances across multiple levels. At Bermudian Springs, champions included Kirk Hoffaker (112), Ricky Schmelyun (119), Scott Ford (130), Dustin Eyler (135), Jon Hade (152), Jason Marshall (171), and Jason Cookerly (189).

Coach McCollum praised the team's efforts, highlighting several key victories. He noted that Dustin Eyler had a significant win when he defeated Brett Rhodes, a boy he had previously lost to in the Mid Penn Tournament, calling it a "get even" match. Similarly, Scott Ford secured a crucial win against a competitor who had previously taken fourth place in the Mid Penn Tournament.

In other events, New Oxford won five matches by forfeit, and coach Davé Conaway picked up his 100th career victory when the Colonials defeated Northern Bedford.

**Basketball**
In the consolation game of the Columbia Christmas Tournament, Detone Catholic defeated Living Word, 69-48. Alex Smith scored 19 points, and Ryan Knox added 18 points in the Squires’ victory. Delone was trailing 14-11 after the first quarter but outscored Living Word 58-34 in the final three quarters.

title: SDAY, JANUARY 14, 2004
headline: Bonus Points Key to Bermudian Springs Win
summary: Bermudian Springs defeated Hanover 35-21, securing the victory through key bonus points and strong individual performances, despite some struggles in the middle weight classes.
cleaned_text:
**Bonus Points Key to Bermudian Springs Win**

By Josh Martin
Times Sports Editor

Call it a case of cleaning up after yourselves a little better than your opponent. Bermudian Springs made sure to pick up every point it could find at Hanover on Tuesday, leaving nothing behind. Hanover, on the other hand, was a little sloppy with its points. The Nighthawks left several of the prized but unseen markers on the mat in a 35-21 loss to the visiting Eagles.

“Bonus points,” said Hanover head coach Terry Conover. “Bonus points went our way and not our way. There were a couple of matches we thought we could win and we didn’t. That was big tonight.”

The Eagles (11-1) got a leg up early when Rickey Schmelyun was able to stave off Gary Mayfield for six full minutes in the second bout of the evening. Hanover opted to send Mayfield on Schmelyun instead of senior Kirk Hoffacker, who started the match with a second-period fall at 112. Schmelyun held a 2-1 lead after a period but sent it late in the third, but had to settle for a 10-point victory.

Bermudian Springs continued to build momentum in three of the next four bouts to ease in, fronting 14-9. The good fortunes stopped there when Adam Staub floored Thomas Slaugh, 9-2, in a mild upset at 145. Staub pulled the rug out from under Slaugh right off the whistle, racing to a 7-1 lead after two lopsided minutes. There was little scoring the rest of the way, mostly because of Staub’s ability to control the pace and his opponent.

“Adam Staub got a key win that kept us in the match,” said McCollum.

The teams swapped major decisions at 152 and 160, with Tyler Paden picking up the four spot for the Hawks. Paden, like Mayfield, was in excellent position to post a fall in the second period but couldn’t close the deal. The Eagles had no problem in that department at 171 and 189, getting mat falls in hand when he put Chris Bowlin away with just a save.

title: C—2 The Evening Sun/ Sunday, January 18, 2004
headline: Keefer Scores 1,000 Points; Mustangs Win Area Showdown
summary: The Mustangs defeated the Warriors 54-49 in a major area sports victory, highlighted by Adam Keefer scoring 1,000 points in his high school career, while other area schools held successful sporting events.
cleaned_text:
**Keefer Scores 1,000 Points; Mustangs Win Big in Area Sports**

By TOM SIXEAS
Evening Sun Sports Correspondent

On a night in which Adam Keefer achieved a milestone, scoring 1,000 points in his high school career, the Mustangs defeated the Warriors 54-49. The Warriors, who were led by a strong effort from Gettysburg, were unable to stop the Mustangs.

The game was a back-and-forth affair. The Warriors initially jumped out to a 26-16 lead in the first half. Gettysburg led 32-23 at the half. However, the Mustangs rallied, scoring 14-3 in the third quarter and taking the lead.

The Mustangs' scoring was keyed by Pete Shovlin, who hit a pair of big shots for the team. Keefer, who has narrowed his college choices to Marywood College and Slippery Rock University, scored 17 points and grabbed seven rebounds for the Warriors.

Gettysburg head coach Adam Keefer said, "I'm glad I was able to get it done for us. We had a lot of energy provided by Keefer’s impending milestone, jumped out to as much as a 10-point lead in the first half before the Mustangs put it away. Gettysburg played with a lot of energy—they knew they couldn't give up any bonus points."

Flaherty, Gettysburg head coach Bill Flaherty, noted, "We noticed something and told our kids to force him left. Dave Paul and Matt Schmidt really did a good job of forcing him left, because he's better when he goes right."

South Western opened its lead to 50-39 with 3:03 left. Three-point plays by Rodney Edmond and Derrick Sisirunk, and a jumper from Edmond, got the Warriors back into it at 50-47 with 1:02 left. The Mustangs, meanwhile, hit four of five free throws in the last minute.

title: The Patriot-News
headline: Eagles Triumph Over Boiling Springs; Upper Dauphin Honors Sporting Legacy
summary: The article reports on two local sports events: the Eagles' wrestling victory over Boiling Springs and the celebration of Upper Dauphin High School's athletic history and community.
cleaned_text:
The Patriot-News
Sun, Jan 18, 2004

**Eagles Triumph Over Boiling Springs in Heated Match**

Last night, the Eagles secured a 43-21 triumph over Boiling Springs in a match that was far more heated and intense than the final margin would suggest. Pins in three of the final four bouts turned what was a close 22-21 chest-pounder into a memorable event. After all the excitement, the Eagles could certainly use a break, especially with a home dual scheduled against Spring Grove again tomorrow.

“Boiling Springs came here to wrestle,” said McCollum, the Bermudian Springs coach, noting that his team was virtually impossible to beat.

title: if
headline: District MI Team Tournament Rescheduled to Thursday
summary: The District MI Team Tournament was rescheduled to Thursday, providing a much-needed break for the Eagles and other area teams like Gettysburg High.
cleaned_text:
Monday's lack of practices and school on Tuesday wiped out a complete slate of basketball games and swim meets. The District MI Team Tournament, originally scheduled to begin Wednesday night, has been pushed to Thursday, according to chairman Randy Umberger.

While athletic directors have been forced to scramble, the Eagles, the top seed in the Class AA tournament, have taken a much-needed breather. They are scheduled to face either Delone Catholic or Boiling Springs at 7:30 p.m. Thursday at Springs.

"This was a good time for us," said the Eagles' head coach, whose team won the tournament silver medal last season. "We had some kids recently who came down with sickness. I talked to the boys and they're all feeling a whole lot better."

The delay was also crucial for other area squads. For example, Gettysburg High's girls swim team was scheduled to swim against Cumberland Valley on Tuesday night with the Mid-Penn Colonial championship on the line. That meet has been moved to today, allowing the teams time to recover from the two days of inactivity.

headline: Local Teams Compete for Glory in Wrestling Tournament
summary: Local high school wrestling teams, including Bermudian Springs and Delone, competed fiercely on January 30, 2004, securing key victories in their quest for tournament success.
cleaned_text:
Bermudian Springs and Delone Shine in Local Wrestling Action

The wrestling action continued on January 30, 2004, with local teams competing for tournament success. Bermudian Springs, the Squires, are focused on achieving a second appearance in the tournament's medal round. Coach McCollum stressed the importance of securing major decisions and pins, noting that the team needed to build momentum.

Earlier in the day, the Squires faced a tough challenge from Juniata. Meanwhile, the visiting Delone team dominated their matches. Delone defeated Boiling Springs, winning a bout at 152 pounds, with Delone's victory helped by major decisions from Chris Comstock and Alex Brown, allowing Aric Fuhrman to clinch the 27-21 victory.

Delone also secured a secondary victory against Latetown High, where the Squirettes rolled to a 60-40 win. Delone Catholic coach Gerry Eckenrode praised the efforts of Jana Zinn, Christine Schleich, Erica Livelsberger, and Amy Topper in defending the team.

Bermudian Springs continued its strong showing, with Nathan Cookerly and another wrestler scoring falls in the second period, while 275-pounder Tim Eyler recorded an early victory. The team's coach noted that the squad was motivated to get back to the medal round, despite the long road ahead.

headline: Cumberland Valley Headed to PIAA Championships After Dominant Season
summary: Cumberland Valley is heading to the PIAA Team Championships after defeating District 10 champion Erie Prep and having a spotless season.
cleaned_text:
The Patriot-News
Fri, Feb 6, 2004

**Cumberland Valley Dominates Season Leading to PIAA Championships**

While it is unlikely that the wrestlers for Cumberland Valley and Erie Cathedral High Schools will mail graduation cards to each other this spring, these two programs, separated by 300 miles of Pennsylvania hills, will meet for the fourth time this season at the PIAA Team Championships at Giant Center in Hershey.

Cumberland Valley is making its second state team duals appearance, something that was high on the Eagles’ to-do list when the season began in November. The team recently clubbed Erie Prep (15-3), the District 10-AAA champion, 47-14 during the Cumberland Valley Winter Duals on January 10. This victory, which was shocking in its margin, might have been the Eagles’ best match of a spotless year.

The two teams had previously met at the Top Hat Tournament in Williamsport on December 5-6 and at the Manheim Holiday Tournament on January 29-30. Cumberland Valley beat Prep by 5/2 points at the Top Hat and trailed the Ramblers, who finished behind them, by 14 at Manheim.

“I don’t know whether that,” said Cumberland Valley head coach Roger Barrick, who is 219-93-1 in a 19-year career that ends this year. “I'll tell you [tomorrow] night. As a coach, the Eagles have virtually no clue about the other District 10 champion and quarterfinal opponent, Fort LeBoeuf (14-1). They have to be tough to have knocked off the No. 1 team in the state.”

headline: Eagles Aim for First Team Title at District 3 Wrestling Tournament
summary: The Bermudian Springs Eagles are heading into the District 3 wrestling tournament with hopes of winning their first team title, featuring several key matchups and individual title defenses.
cleaned_text:
The Patriot-News
Thu, Feb 19, 2004

The Eagles are looking to build on a strong season, having advanced one of 14 wrestlers to the districts. This gives the Eagles a decent shot at their first team title when the tournament kicks off at 8:30 a.m. tomorrow. While the team wants to do well, the focus is also on individual success.

“We want to be competitive and make a run at the title. It’s a challenge for us,” said Bermudian Springs coach McCollum.

Several wrestlers are making adjustments for the district run. Mike Anderson (Littlestown) and Aric Fuhrman (Delone Catholic) moved up a weight class for this year’s competition. Anderson will be competing at 119, up from 103 last year. Fuhrman is vying for another championship at 145 after competing at 140 a year ago.

Defending title holders are set to compete in the same weight classes as last year. Juniata’s Greg Jensen (130), Hamburg’s Leland Wood (152), and Juniata’s Phillip Bomberger (171) will be competing in their respective classes.

Gary Mayfield, a three-time defending champion at 103, is seeking a fourth title. Only seven District 3 wrestlers have performed this feat in 67 years. The Hanover senior has 155 career wins, tied with Bomberger, going into districts. This is five behind Evan Sola’s district record of 160.

Other notable matchups include:
*   The 152 draw, where Hamburg’s Leland Wood is the defending champion.
*   The 160 draw, which could prove the most interesting of the day, featuring spotless marks from Brandywine Heights’ Mike (36-0) and Halifax's Brett Wiest (25-0).
*   The 171 class, where Halifax’s Bryan Wiest (29-3) defeated Conrad Henning (28-4) to get major consideration.

The District 3 Tournament features several wrestlers with 20-plus wins, topping the 25-win mark.

cleaned_text:
The PIAA Considers Reducing High School Wrestling Weight Classes

The Pennsylvania Interscholastic Athletic Association (PIAA) has proposed a significant change to high school wrestling by suggesting a reduction in the number of weight classes. The PIAA has thrown its hat into the ring, proposing a set of 12 weight classes for the 2004-05 season, down from the current number.

While the exact details are still being finalized, the proposal aims to streamline the sport by consolidating the current weight structure.

In local wrestling news, Bermudian Springs continues to be a strong program. Coach McCollum is noted for his success, and the team remains a contender in the regionals. Meanwhile, other local programs, such as Cumberland Valley and Dallastown, are also highly competitive, with teams like Red Lion showing significant improvement.

title: “Dave h
headline: District Champions Advance to PIAA Southeast Region Tournament
summary: Several wrestlers, including Bomberger, Jensen, and Lamboy, won their district matches and advanced to the PIAA Class AA Southeast Region tournament.
cleaned_text:
The Patriot-News
Sun, Feb 22, 2004

The top six wrestlers at each weight move on to next weekend’s PIAA Class AA Southeast Region tournament at Wilson.

In the district final, Bomberger trounced Schuylkill Valley's Patrick Walsh 18-7. Meanwhile, Jensen settled a week-old score with Upper Dauphin’s Drew Barder, hammering him 12-0.

Other district champions included Brad Kummerer of Hamburg at 103 lbs and Mike Anderson of Littlestown at 119 lbs.

In the Section IV final, Lamboy edged Kauttman. Lamboy spotted the Blackhawk's first takedown but hit a four-point move late in the first period for a 4-2 lead. Trailing 5-3 late, Kauffman was edged through a whizzer to score the tying takedown and force overtime. After a scoreless period from neutral, Kauffman had the option and chose down for the 30-second rideout. He never got close to escaping, and Lamboy put him on his back late.

“I didn’t think I would run out of gas, but it was a tough bout,” Lamboy said. “It’s always tough when we wrestle. But I had enough at the end.”

In another match, Rush sported a nasty welt above his right eye after he and Nuding clashed heads. However, it was the only damage Nuding inflicted. Rush sandwiched takedowns around a hard second-period ride, then sealed the win with a two-point nearfall.

Barder won his match in overtime by one point. Jensen, who was coached by McCollum, stated, “Last week, I guess wasn't really in it, and I thought, because of it, I would lose my head.” He added, “But my coach worked real hard, and I have to give credit to him.”

title: Great weekend for Bermudia
headline: Bermudian Springs Sweeps Regional Wrestling Title
summary: The Bermudian Springs High Eagles team won the overall team title at the Gary Mayfield Regional Wrestling Championships, qualifying all eight wrestlers for the PIAA Championships.
cleaned_text:
**Bermudian Springs Dominates Regional Wrestling Championships**

By GREG SWATKEN
Ewing Sun Sports Writer

The Bermudian Springs High Eagles team achieved a clean sweep at the Gary Mayfield Regional Wrestling Championships, securing the overall team title with 108.5 points on Saturday at the Woods Shooting Classic.

Even without an individual champion, the team’s performance was outstanding. The victory will send all eight regional wrestlers to the PIAA Championships, which begin at Hirschy in Glen Center.

“You cannot ask for a better tournament,” said Hirschy Springs Head Coach Tommy Iannetta. “I don’t know how to put it into words.”

Kyle German, a standout for Hanover, helped lead the team to the best team ever, setting a new record of 36-10. German stated that he must keep training hard and hardcore in preparation for four more matches.

title: The Gettysburg Times
headline: Bermudian Springs Wrestlers Shine at PIAA Tournament
summary: Bermudian Springs wrestlers, including Jon Hade and Nathan Cookerly, had a successful season, earning multiple medals and advancing eight wrestlers to the regional championship.
cleaned_text:
The Gettysburg Times
Thursday, April 1, 2004

The sophomore showed no signs of slowing down when he went 32-11 and grabbed a fifth-place medal at the PIAA tournament. Schmelyun placed second at both the sectional and district tournaments before settling for third at regionals. Schmelyun’s style is eerily similar to that of Littlestown’s two-time state runner-up, Mike Anderson.

Jon Hade (152) of Bermudian Springs answered any questions about a big jump in weight class when he turned in a 36-7 season. He flawlessly bridged the gap between the top and bottom ends of the lineup in a season that saw the Eagles win nearly every team trophy out there. Hade was a sectional champion, district runner-up, and regional eighth-place finisher at states.

Nathan Cookerly (189) of Bermudian Springs was perhaps the biggest surprise in the area this season, turning in a 33-10 mark to make his first appearance on the All-Times team. The senior gave the Eagles some extra pop at the top end of the lineup. Cookerly placed second at sectionals before slipping to fifth at districts, but he rebounded nicely with a silver medal at regionals. Cookerly was the only one of eight Bermudian state qualifiers that reached the regional finals.

Coach McCollum spoke about the team's performance, noting, “We really had it going there. And at the district tournament, we came out real strong and had a good first day. Schuylkill Valley came back on the second day, and it would be close, but we fought for every point.”

McCollum added, “We felt that was a big step. It’s been a goal of mine and our assistant coaches for some time. It was really special.”

The Eagles advanced a whopping eight wrestlers to the regional championship, a major accomplishment for the Bermudian Springs program.

**Bermudian Springs Wrestling Team**

Under Head Coach McCollum, the Bermudian Springs wrestling team, which was in its 28th season, boasted assistants Nathan Stock, Troy Sauer, and Ted Mannes. The team finished the 2003-04 season with an 18-2 record and a 9-0 record in the Mid-Penn Capital League.

The team highlighted several key losses, including Kirk Hottacker (33-8), Jason Marshall (35-10), Nathan Cookerly (33-10), and Tim Eyler (30-16). Key returnees included Bryce Marshall (36-12) and Rickey Schmelyun (32-?).

Sun, Dec 12, 2004

Wrestlers advanced to the finals at the Carlisle Holiday Tournament. Only the lightweights were able to secure top medals. Sphinxons Schh Hoffman and Bryce Marshall claimed the top medals at 103 and 112 pounds, respectively. On Saturday, the team finished second out of 16 teams overall with 205 points.

Big Spring won the team championship with 215 points, and Owen J. Robert finished third with 164. David McCullum expressed immense pride in the wrestlers from Bermudian Springs. "We really worked hard," he stated. "They came here with one purpose, and that was to win it all. We were pretty much in the lead for the whole tournament until the end of the association round, which is where Big Spring more or less caught up with us. We and our kids wanted to get to the finals, and we knew we would."

cleaned_text:
**Biglerville, Littlestown Split at Canner Duals**

By BRIAN SHEA
Evening Sun Sports Correspondent

The high school wrestling teams from Biglerville and Littlestown both emerged from the first day of competition in the Canner Duals with the same 1-1 record, though they did not travel the same road.

At Biglerville, the Bolts took a 55-20 win over Harriton in their first match in Pool A. Later, the team faced tough competition from Norristown in a 45-18 victory.

Meanwhile, the article also featured comments regarding the recovery of wrestler Schmelyun. Schmelyun, who is expected to wrestle at 130 pounds upon his return, expressed his anxiety about returning to the mat. Coach McCollum, however, stated that his biggest concern would be Schmelyun's conditioning. McCollum added that they were not rushing him back and hoped to give him three or four, maybe five, good days of practice before he competed again.

cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Thu, Feb 17, 2005 - Page 14

**Hanover Area Wrestlers Dominate Sectional Seeds**

The Class AA Sectional Championships begin Friday evening at South Wester High School. Red Lion has the most top seeds with four, while Spring Grove and South Western each have a pair of top seeds. Overall, Hanover area wrestlers claimed 11 of the 14 top seeds in Class AA Section I and five of 14 in Class AAA Section IV.

Several wrestlers are looking to repeat championship success. Jon Hade (26-2) is the top seed at 160 pounds and will look to win his second consecutive sectional championship, having claimed the title at 152 pounds last year. Kyle Flickinger and Scott Ford are also returning sectional champions.

In Class AAA Section IV, Spring Grove’s Kelin Bidelspach (33-3) is the top seed at 130 pounds. He is joined by a PIAA medalist from last year, Rickey Schmelyun, who is the top seed at 135 pounds. Schmelyun, who missed most of the season with a broken leg, has won all four of his matches since returning to the Eagles’ seed.

Another favorite to win his weight class is Delone Catholic’s Alex Despit, who is the top seed at 135 pounds (171). He will face a preliminary-round match in his first bout. He is joined as a top seed by teammate Andrew Sheridan (34-?) at 140 pounds.

Gettysburg’s Adam Gonzales (1-?) is the top seed at 135 pounds. He will face a preliminary-round match.

South Western’s Cory Wonder (27-3) is the No. 4 seed at 135 pounds. He will seek his undefeated third consecutive sectional championship.

Meanwhile, Uestown’s Greg Conover (24-6) is in a first-round match. Spring Grove coach Tony Miller stated, “They know this is the end of the year, and this is what you have trained all year for.”

**Weight Class 140**
The Favorite: Littlestown’s David Macy is at 140 after winning the 135 crown last season. Macy has shown very few chinks in the armor, winning 27 of 30 bouts this season. A return trip to the finals is in the cards for the "Bolt senior."
The Field: Delone senior Justin Neiderer (25-3) has been bigger and better than ever. Neiderer’s losses have come against Macy, Pennsbury powerhouse Ben Rees (who also clobbered Macy), and Brandywine’s Brandon Gery, who caught Neiderer in the third period of a one-point match. In order to get another shot at Macy, Neiderer will likely have to defeat Macy in a series.

**Weight Class 119**
The Field: Littlestown’s Mitch stepped in their dual a month ago. He has as much fight as anyone, stopping Fe O of knawing off his own arm before giving up the fall.

**Weight Class 160**
The Favorite: We're going to take a pass on this one. Hanover’s Alea Despines (22-10) edged Delone’s Curtis Hardnack (19-7) for the top seed, but the two didn’t wrestle in their dual. Hardnack has a decided edge in postseason experience, but Despines faces an easier path to the finals.

**Weight Class 135**
Biglerville’s Dan Campo is the No. 4 seed despite a 7-19 record, based mostly on his head-to-head win over Northeastern freshman Shawn Wagner (13-12). They'll meet in the quarters, while Littlestown’s Kenny Roth (2-16) is in this class.

**Weight Class 112**
The Field: Bermudian Springs’ Seth Hoffman (14-3) gets back into the mix after being knocked out of the Eagle lineup by fellow sophomore Brian Ford for the stretch run of the regular season. Ford came up a few weigh-ins shy of qualifying at this weight, giving Hoffman another shot. Hoffman opens with sixth-seeded Jusun Mitchell (7-11) of Littlestown. Hanover’s Justin Boyers (17-16) and Delone Catholic’s Brandon Smith (14-14) put in a nice 4-5 matchup. Boyers clipped Smith 5-3 at their dual.

**Weight Class 145**
The Field: York Suburban’s Ron Cohen (20-5) got the No. 2 seed based on a close win over Brown. Those two will play out a sequel should Brown beat Biglerville freshman Jeff Foster (14-13) and Cohen take care of Pitt winner between Nathan Rill (1) and Fairfield’s Jason Roberts (1-12).

**Weight Class 171**
Meanwhile, back at the ranch, Nate Murren (19-9) and Thomas Slaugh (28-5) lock horns in a nice 4-5 match. Slaugh edged the feisty Murren 3-2 just after we flipped the calendar to a new year.

title: “Dave h
headline: Bermudian Springs Wins District II Wrestling Title
summary: The Bermudian Springs wrestling team secured the District II Class AA championship after key victories from wrestlers including Bryce Marshall, Kyle Flickinger, Rickey Schmelyun, and Jon Hade.
cleaned_text:
**Bermudian Springs Wrestling Team Wins District II Class AA Championship**

Springs High’s 11-year wait for a District II Class AA wrestling championship is over. The Eagles went 4-for-4 in championship matches Saturday at Hersheypark Arena, securing the overall team championship with 139.5 points. Schuylkill Valley finished second with 130 points.

“These kids worked so hard this week after sectionals. They really came through for us,” said the coach, McCollum.

Sophomore Bryce Marshall got things rolling for the Eagles in the 103-pound final with a 3-1 decision, marking Bermudian Springs’ first district victory in years. Marshall, who improved to a 32-4 record this season, stated, “They wanted to get one this year. I figured I would be the first one.”

Marshall was followed by teammates Kyle Flickinger (119), Rickey Schmelyun (130), and Jon Hade (160), who each won their first district championships, giving the Eagles a firm grasp on the team title. Flickinger won the 119-pound final with a 6-3 decision over Upper Dauphin’s Braxton Ditty. Two bouts later, Schmelyun won the 130-pound championship by technical fall over Bast Pennsboro’s Pat Conrad in 3:54.

Schmelyun’s victory was significant, as it was his 11th match of the season after returning from a broken leg. Hade, who raised his record to 33-1, noted the team’s momentum, saying, “After all of those guys got gold. I didn’t want to be stuck with silver.”

Overall, seven Hanover area wrestlers earned district titles, and 22 wrestlers moved on to the Class AA Southeast Regional Championships, beginning Friday at Wilson High School in West Lawn.

In other individual victories, Biglerville’s Jake Showers (215) won his second consecutive title with a 7-0 victory over Columbia’s Joe Williams. Delone Catholic secured a pair of champions: Justin Neiderer (140) and Alex Brown (152). Neiderer won his final match with a 4-3 decision over Littlestown’s David Macy.

**High School Athletes Compete at PIAA Championships**

In the wrestling events, local athletes are competing for state medals. One wrestler was noted for his performance in a close match against Octorara’s Garth Ladley, where he previously beat Ladley at the regional tournament.

title: “Dave h
headline: Bermudian Springs Claims NCAA National Wrestling Title
summary: Bermudian Springs graduate Duane Bastress won the NCAA Division III 184-pound championship, while McDaniel College's Tony Howard finished fifth in his weight class.
cleaned_text:
Duane Bastress, a graduate of Bermudian Springs, capped off a stellar season on Saturday by capturing a national title.

Bastress, the No. 2 seed, defeated No. 8 seed Ryan Summers of John Carroll University by a 10-7 decision to claim the NCAA Division III 184-pound championship. The championships concluded on Saturday at St. Olaf College in Northfield.

“To have him in the national finals, our kids look up to that,” said McCollum, the Bermudian Springs coach. “They all know him, and that’s what they were talking about here tonight. We have Rickey (Schmelyun) here for a regional championship, and they all want to know how Duane is doing in the national finals. They want a national champ.”

Bastress finished his junior campaign with a 38-2 record.

In another notable performance, McDaniel College junior Tony Howard finished fifth in the 174-pound bracket. Howard fell in the consolation semifinals by a score of 6-5 to Johnson & Wales’ Tim Ruberg, but rebounded for a 6-1 win over Wisconsin-Lacrosse’ Josh Chelf in the fifth-place bout. Howard closed the season with a 14-3 record, earning an All-American label and marking McDaniel’s best showing in the NCAA tournament.

**HERSHEY, Pa.** — Matt Fisk, a Wyalusing senior, set a new record Friday night, achieving his 174th career victory with a 2-1 decision over Shane Valko of Forest Hills in the Class AA 125-pound semifinals.

“I hate I finally got it,” Fisk said.

Matt Fisk had what he wanted all year long—the PIAA record for career victories. What he was denied, however, was a reception fit for that kind of achievement.

In other news, Wyalusing’s Jake Showers (215) suffered his first defeat of the season after dropping a 9-1 major decision to Line Mountain’s Kelton Balum (46-1) in the 130-pound semifinals.

Schmelyun (17-1), who placed fifth at 119 last year, recovered to beat Donny Clark of West Branch in a consolation bout, 7-4. He will wrestle Ridgway’s Nikko Leitzel for third place this afternoon.

“Seeing Jon win and make the finals made me forget about my loss,” Schmelyun said. “I was so happy for him. That helped me to get my head screwed back on for my match.”

title: a scoreless first period
headline: Bermudian Springs Wrestler Falls to Octorara in Championship
summary: A Bermudian Springs wrestler earned a silver medal at the PIAA championships despite losing the 160-pound bout to Octorara's Mike Letts, highlighting the depth of the team's success.
cleaned_text:
Seated on a rolled-up wrestling mat with his face reddened from blood and exhaustion, the Bermudian Springs senior had just lost another 3-1 decision to Octorara's Mike Letts, this time in the Class AA 160-pound championship at the PIAA championships on Saturday evening at Giant Center.

Despite the loss, the silver medal Hade earned capped off a great state tournament for the Eagles, who brought home a school-record three medals, with Schmelyun finishing third at 130 and Marshall eighth at 140.

“I told him he had four great years and that I was really paying attention at the state finals,” said McCollum, praising his wrestler with a private word at the end of the mat following the loss. “He wrestled a great match.”

The bout was eerily similar to the regional semifinal the two wrestled the previous Saturday morning at Wilson High School. The difference was the first-period takedown by the District 1 junior—who came into the bout No. 1 among the state's Class AA 160-pounders—with just 47 seconds left in the period.

“We were hoping to get the takedown this time,” said McCollum. “Letts is a great wrestler. If either one makes a mistake, then the other gets the takedown.”

The two traded escapes, with Hade getting his in the second and Letts in the third period. The Bermudian Springs wrestler then tried in vain to get a tying takedown, but he gave credit to Letts’ ability to shut down his offense.

“I knew coming in he'd keep his hips down low,” said Hade. “I tried not to get frustrated. He knew how to keep me on my feet.”

This might have been the last bout of Hade’s career, as he has committed to play football at Shippensburg University in the fall. Despite the loss, he was able to appreciate what he had accomplished.

“Making the finals is something I've dreamed about since fourth grade,” said the future college defensive back. “During the Parade of Champions with all the people standing and clapping, I realized how much the sport really meant to me.”

Elsewhere in the Class AAA medal round, the Red Lion duo of Eric Albright (125) and Keith McDonald (189) also earned medals.

title: ortstop Jimmy Rollins
headline: Bermudian Springs Wrestling Team Wins State Honors
summary: The Bermudian Springs wrestling team achieved significant success, earning multiple PIAA medals and individual awards, led by athletes like Jon Hade and Justin Terhune.
cleaned_text:
The Bermudian Springs Wrestling Team Achieves State Success

By TOM HUNT
Daily Record/Sunday News

The Bermudian Springs wrestling team had a highly successful season, earning multiple PIAA medals and the honor of Daily Record/Sunday News Division II Wrestler of the Year.

The team's success was highlighted by the performances of its athletes. Jon Hade, for example, reached the PIAA finals, where he faced the state’s top-ranked Class AA 160-pounder. Hade’s journey included a memorable early match where he reversed his opponent to win, and a previous match where he suffered a 3-1 loss to Octorara’s Mike Letts.

Another standout was Justin Terhune, whose senior season capped off a stellar wrestling career with a state silver medal.

The team’s achievements were credited to the coaching staff, including coach McCollum, who guided the program to its best season yet.

title: York Daily Record
headline: Local Wrestler Hade Falls Short in 160-Pound Championship
summary: Despite winning three straight decisions at the state tournament, Hade lost the 160-pound championship match to Letts.
cleaned_text:
“He didn’t let any pressure from the outside disturb him.”

A self-described troublemaker growing up, Hade was introduced to wrestling by his father, Dennis, as an outlet for his rambunctious energy.

Hade got involved in the Hanover YMCA program, which produced recent state champions like Dana Gingerich of Delone Catholic and Gary Mayfield of Hanover.

McCollum, the coach from Bermudian Springs, was concerned about Hade’s ability to compete live. “I told Nate (Stock, assistant McCollum said. “It bothers me that I haven't seen him wrestle live. I have to see if he can compete.”

“But Jon knew what he could do. He faced the toughest competition of his career back-to-back, even with an injury. It’s hard to find a kid like that in this day and age. He's a different breed.”

At the state tournament, Hade won three straight decisions before meeting Letts in the 160-pound championship match. The match went eerily like the regional bout.

“The opponent got the lone takedown, again in the first period, and then prevented Hade from finishing any scoring opportunities over the next two periods.”

The loss left Hade with a senior season record of 39-4 and his career win mark at 117, a record good enough for the school.

headline: Hanover Nighthawks Wrestling Coach Conover Reflects on Program Tradition
summary: Coach Conover expressed pride in the enduring tradition of the Hanover Nighthawks wrestling program, believing the team can compete with any opponent if they match up weights.
cleaned_text:
Coach Conover spoke highly of the tradition and history of the program, stating that the coach is a big part of the school's identity. He emphasized that the coach has consistently been a winner, never giving up a fight, which is what makes the program special.

Although the Nighthawks did not have their most talented team last season, they still gave everyone in the YAIAA Division II fits. The team finished 18-15 overall (64 in the division), leading Conover to believe he will earn his 500th win at Hanover around the middle of this season.

Conover remains optimistic about the team's potential, noting, “There’s no question, if we can match up weights, we can beat anyone in the league on a given night. We might not win the division, but we should be able to compete with anybody.”

title: illion deal
headline: Boiling Springs Edges Bermudian Springs in Wrestling Match
summary: Boiling Springs defeated Bermudian Springs 28-24 in a wrestling match, overcoming an early deficit through key victories in multiple weight classes.
cleaned_text:
By PATRICK ABDALLA
Evening Sun Sports Writer

The little guy: up big on the mat Tuesday, just not big enough to rally Bermudian Springs High to a victory.

The Eagles (0-1) could not overcome an early 25-9 deficit against Boiling Springs.

Boiling Springs (1-0) took the match, 28-24.

“They came in as the underdog and they came to wrestle,” said the Eagles coach, referring to the effort.

Boiling Springs jumped out to its early lead on the strength of pins from their 160- and 171-pounders.

The first match was a major decision at 140 pounds for Josh Geidel, who beat Guy Fulton 15-4.

However, Bermudian Springs bounced back with a pin from Rick Schmelyun at 145 and a decision from the team.

The Eagles’ 9-4 lead was short-lived. Anthony Wieland pinned Nathan Rill and Jeremy Cannon pinned Tyler Stanbaugh at 160 and 171 respectively. The Bubblers fed off the spark, winning the next three matches to take a 25-9 lead.

“They got a key win at 189,” McCollum said, referring to Josh Cook’s 2-1 victory over the Eagles’ Dan Washburn.

“We thought we were going to give up. We felt we still had a shot at coming back and beating them,” McCollum said.

In the 103-pound bout, Nick Blasone started the rally with an 8-1 victory over Mark Hammond. Derek Schmelyun’s 8-1 victory over Cory Mattila at 112 quickly followed, bringing the team score to 25-15.

It was a must-win match when Bryce Marshall trailed Dakota Kuhn 3-2 in the third period. However, a quick escape with 90 seconds left tied the match. With 35 seconds to go, Marshall took Kuhn down and held on for a 5-2 victory.

Johns Hopkins Wins Wrestling Victory Over McDaniel
Johns Hopkins University won four of the final five bouts, and freshman Chris Floyd picked up a 9-7 decision over Courtney Nightengale in the 165-pound weight class to seal a 21-16 wrestling victory over McDaniel on Tuesday in Baltimore. McDaniel's three-match winning streak was snapped, dropping them to 10-6 overall and 3-1 in the Centennial.

Local High School Wrestling Predictions
In local wrestling discussions, one source noted that the matchup was likely between Bermudian Springs and Brandywine Heights. "I think it’s between them and Brandywine Heights," said Miller. "I think the Nos, [and] 3 are the best teams, but Brandywine’s got a lot of weak spots. They're a little bit more easily upset than Bermudian Springs."

However, to reach a matchup with the top seed, the Eagles will have to get by the lone team that beat them this year: the second-seeded Bubblers of Boiling Springs.

McCollum Praises Wrestler's Improvement
Coach McCollum was particularly impressed with Rodriguez’s effort against Jake Walmer, who was 35 pounds heavier than the 275-pound Rodriguez. "Early on in the season, he was sluggish and not in shape and a little off with his moves and things," said McCollum. "As the year has progressed, he’s gotten in a lot better shape and has done some things better that he was struggling with in the beginning of the year."

Recent Match Action
In another bout recap, freshman sensation Hunter MiDoan got things started at 112 with a pin. Brayden Daigle followed with a pin, followed by a decision at 119. Curtis Hardnack (125) and Dan Leeper (130) both secured pins, putting Jacob Delone on top 21-0. Heavner nearly had to default at 189.

headline: Bermudian Springs Coach Praises Team Focus Ahead of Tournament
summary: Bermudian Springs coach David McCollum notes that his team is highly focused and prepared for the District 3 Class A Team Tournament semifinals.
cleaned_text:
Wrestling Fans

Today’s District 3 Class A Team Tournament wrestling semifinals offer some exciting matches.

Bermudian Springs could hardly wait to get his hooks back on unbeaten Bobbi Jupiter (20) after Babbionight showed up in the first match of the 13-1 Eagles, their only loss being 28-24 on December 7. With the Eagles getting their long-awaited 2-4 win on January 4 in today at the Humber School’s Spartan Center, David McCollum feels it will put a much better fit on the mat this time, and he believes his team has a razor-sharp fight now.

"Let it put this way," the Bermudian Springs head coach said. "When we had our team meeting at the end of Friday’s practice, when we talked about what the next match was, we were the quietest this bunch has been all season. They are focused. Normally, I have to bring them all in the end of practice. Lindsay, I have to do this."

headline: Boiling Springs Defeats Hanover; Bermudian Springs Dominates
summary: Boiling Springs defeated Hanover in a wrestling final, while Bermudian Springs secured a significant victory over Boiling Springs, maintaining their reputation as a top team.
cleaned_text:
Boiling Springs Takes Down Hanover, Bermudian Springs Dominates

Boiling Springs defeated Hanover in the final match with a 2-1 decision fall at 1:31. The victory was secured by Phil Slaugh at 145 and Dan Washburn at 189, which decision by older brother Thomas [presumably Slaugh or Washburn] really put the Bubblers in the seat.

The Bubblers’ upperweight wrestlers are as tough as they come. The team picked up another huge win at 119, as the Bubblers concluded the final by pinning Boiling Springs’ Ryan Park in 1:09 at 140.

The Bubblers’ upperweight wrestlers took the wind out of Hanover’s sails with four consecutive pins, moving from 160 to 215.

Victory with a 2-1 decision marked the second time Bermudian Springs had beaten Boiling Springs this season. The Bubblers walked out of York Springs with a 28-24 victory in the first match of the season.

“Bermudian Springs has been the talk from day one,” said Boiling Springs coach Rod Wright. “Even after we beat them, that’s been the talk. What do we got to do for respect? I hope we don’t have to do anything more to open people's eyes.”

Bermudian Springs Wrestler Bryce Marshall Advances to Semifinals

Bermudian Springs wrestler Bryce Marshall, who raised his high school record to 100-2, will meet West York junior Cody Martin in this morning’s semifinals, which begin at 10:30 a.m. at Delone Catholic High School.

Marshall was on and off the night, needing only three minutes to advance to the District Class AA semifinals.

The wrestling tournament featured several strong performances. The top four wrestlers in each weight class advance to the District 3 Class AA Championships next week in Hershey. The consolation finals begin at 5:30 p.m., with the championship finals to follow.

Marshall is part of a parade of wrestlers, as the Bermudian Springs coach stated. "We want to get two vans full of kids," the coach said. "There will be two consolation [teams]. That was our goal."

headline: Bermuda Springs Wrestling Coach McCollum Leads Team to District Title
summary: Bermuda Springs wrestling coach McCollum's team achieved a strong showing at the district championships, with McMaster winning the District 3 Class A Section 1 title.
cleaned_text:
Bermudian Springs Wrestling Coach McCollum Leads Team to District Title

Bermuda College's high school wrestling program is having a successful season. The team, coached by McCollum, has compiled a strong record, achieving 31 wins and 5 losses, totaling 135 points. The team is one of 14 Bermuda wireless competitors participating in the district championships, which began earlier today.

McMaster won the District 3 Class A Section 1 title in his weight class through crossover teamwork in Delaware. After advancing through the quarterfinal and semifinals, McMaster secured the section 1 title with a 9-1 major decision over Hanover's Alta Despeps.

"It is definitely a strong showing for the Bermuda Springs Conference," McCollum said. "We want to keep him there. With a few more wins, he will no longer have to worry about securing one of the six prime positions for the Southeast Regional Championships in every Class AA weight class."

The team is highly competitive, with Bermuda's Nick Sabal (35-50) serving as the top seed and PIAA 15L. A potential sensational win for McMaster's side of the bracket remains a key focus.

Spectators’ heads, a funny thing happened. Moist caught Schmelyun and put him on his back for a five-point move at the end of the period. When a wrestler got careless on the takedown, he failed to score. Moist, who was ranked fifth in the state, rode him tough the entire second period. At the start of the third period, Moist looked to his corner, and his coaches motioned to take down. Considering Schmelyun’s tilting ability, the Panther senior, who had tech-falled his way through the tournament, decided to go neutral. The move almost worked.

“It was real good for me because it let me know where I am conditioning-wise,” Schmelyun said.

Bermudian Springs went 3-for-3 in fifth-place bouts, the kind of matches that decide whether a wrestler punches his ticket to state or gets to start eating a little more at dinner.

Marshall started the Division II champs off right with a 5-1 decision over Victor Konno of Northwestern Lehigh. Konno had knocked the junior into the consolation bracket with a 4-3 decision in Friday's quarterfinal. This time, Marshall limited Konno's offense while registering takedowns in the first and third periods to earn his return to Hershey, where he worked his way onto last season's medal stand.

Derek Schmelyun followed that performance with a tough 5-2 decision over Zach Wolford of Hanover, who had beaten the younger Schmelyun twice in the ends.

Trailing 4-3, Mendoza nearly turned Eyler into an equalizer for critical back points at the time the Eagle junior fought him off. Eyler then sealed the victory with a late reversal.

McCollum wasn’t sure what got his consolation crew re-energized, but was certainly happy with the results. “I told someone whatever did between the afternoon round and the qualifying we need to do again, know what it was. We did the mall,” he said with a laugh. “We were in positions where we could have folded. That show: a lot of character.”

Success wasn't limited to the Bermudian contingent. Conover was able to erase the disappointment of last weekend’s fifth-place finish in districts by cruising through the consolation bracket, winning four straight bouts to grab the third-place medal. He was one of only four competitors to pull that trick in the grueling two-day event.
