With nine of 14 starters set to retum and a bevy of
eager underclassmen ready to cut their teeth at the
varsity level, McCollum’s eagerness to usher in the
2008-09 season is warranted. But before he dives
into another set of challenges, he has allowed himself
a little time to admire what was accomplished this
season.

“It's finally starting to sink in,” he said. “After a
good month your head starts to clear and you realize
what you did. Score after score and championship
after Championship, and I’m thinking ‘Wow.’ The
only thing to make it a totally unbelievable season
would have been to win that last match against
Burrell. What else could you have asked for? It’s
a dream come true.

“Going into the season we had high exp.
and goals, but never dreamed of winning everything
we entered and winding up in the state finals.”

Not many can honestly claim to have seen a run-
ner-up finish at the state tourney coming, but it did-
n't take a rocket scientist to realize the Eagles were
going to be very, very good. They were returning an
army of talented and experienced wrestlers from
teams that went 32-7 over the previous two seasons,
including last year’s lot that was 3-2 at the state team
tournament.

Bermudian cleared a major hurdle by finally get-
ting the best of nemesis Boiling Springs in the season
opener, then went about carving up every other team
on its schedule. For good measure, the Eagles blast-
ed their Way to team titles at four regular season tour-
naments.

It was during the last of those toumeys when
McCollum began to think big.

“Tt hit me when beat Schuylkill Valley at the
Dallastown Duals,” he said. knew Schuylkill
was going to be tough and that was the first match
where we were going to be pushed. I felt good about
the way our kids responded and that’s when it hit me
that these guys are pretty spe

The 36-26 win over Schuylkill triggered a furious
finish to the regular season, setting up Berm’s state-
ment performance at the District 3 Team
Championships. There the Eagles were brutally effi-
cient, hammering Columbia, Littlestown, Schuylkill
Valley and Brandywine Heights by an average score
of 47-18.

It was clear that McCollum’s bunch was peaking
at the right time, but did it have enough firepower left

“Going into the season we had high expec-
tations and goals, but never dreamed of win-
ning everything we entered and winding up
in the state finals.”

— Dave McCollum

to bust into the medal round at the state team tourna-
ment?

The E: emphatically answered that question
with wins over Northwestern Lehigh and Bedford,
the respective District 11 and 5 champions, to reach
the gold medal match.

Berm got gutsy efforts up and down the lineup in
the finals against Burrell, much to the delight of the
Sea of Red that crested with every big pin and key
decision. In the end Burrell had just a bit more in the
barrel, however, getting a pin in the final bout of the
match to win, 37-29.

With most of the starting lineup still in the fold
next season, including state medalists Derek
Schmelyun and Devin O’Brien, another magical run
seems possible, even probable

‘That doesn’t come by chance, as the Eagles con-
tinually raise the bar when it comes to off-season
preparation.

“Tt’s almost like wrestling has become a 10-month
season,” McCollum said. “We have kids in various
clubs and tournaments, and you need to let these kids
go out and get involved, and then bring it all back in
the room in November. That’s really helped us the
last couple of years.”

It also helps to have stability within the coaching
ranks, where four of the five assistants on
McCollum’s junior high and varsity staffs are former
Fagle wrestlers.

“These guys giving back speaks a thousand words
for the type of commitment our community has to the
program,” he said. “As a head coach I’m in a unique
situation because we all have ties to the program.
Everybody has been through it, and that’s a special
connection.”

Even with another potential monster season in
McCollum says there is more it than simply

“I's going to take another team that has the same
attitude this group had,” he said. “A team that works
well together, doesn’t give up a lot of bonus points
and can score, not necessarily a team of superstars.
You’ ve got to have a few superstars on the team, but
you've also got to have average to above-average
kids throughout the lineup.

¢ had a super mix of talent, experience and ath-
leticism from top to bottom. That balance is the one
that made us so dangerous.”

headline: Dallastown Junior Wins Medal at Battle at the Beach
summary: Several wrestlers achieved success at regional and district tournaments, with Dallastown's junior winning a medal and James English securing a district crown for Central York.
cleaned_text:
**Dallastown Junior Wins Medal at Battle at the Beach**

Dallastown's junior wrestler secured a third-place finish at the Battle at the Beach tournament in the 160-pound weight class.

**Central York and Kennard-Dale Highlights**

Central York's senior wrestler was 33-2 and finished with a consolation medal after losing a tough 3-2 match.

Kennard-Dale's wrestler had a stellar career, accumulating 125 victories, and reached the 125-pound district final, finishing with a 35-4 record.

**Ober's Success**

Ober's wrestler had a successful season, earning multiple regular-season crowns at the Battle at the Beach tournament. He also won a medal with a 6-5 decision over his opponent.

**Individual Athlete Spotlights**

*   **Nick Blasone (Bermudian Springs):** After missing last season, the Eagle lightweight reached the state tournament for the second time in three years. The junior finished fourth at districts and fifth at regionals, qualifying for the Giant Center action. Blasone ended up with a 38-13 record and contributed several huge performances for the Eagles.
*   **Elias Brown (Delone Catholic):** The junior's second PIAA appearance ended with a fourth-place finish in the Class AA 189-pound weight class. This medal complemented his regional gold, which he won after defeating Brad Stoudt of Brandywine, the state's No. 1-ranked wrestler, and an old nemesis. His 41-5 season featured championships at the Gamet Valley tournament and the Boiling Springs Invitational.
*   **Hunter Doan (Catholic):** Third place at regionals earned this offensive machine his first PIAA tournament spot. Doan reached the Class AA 130-pound district final after claiming a sectional crown during a 26-5 junior season.
*   **Jett Foster (Biglerville):** Despite wrestling the entire season with a knee injury from football, Foster made the regional tournament. However, the injury caught up to him, and he lost two straight matches, finishing fourth in the Class AA.
*   **Walls (Unnamed School):** Walls wrestled a team that had never gone to the PIAA team championships. The squad dropped 29 to District 7 power Burrell in the finals, earning a silver medal. This went with a Division II crown and a District III gold. Individually, the wrestler led the Eagles to the sectional and district title while qualifying four wrestlers for the PIAA championships.
*   **Zach Wolford (Hanover):** The Nighthawk finally found success at regionals with a third-place finish, earning a berth in the PIAA Class AA championships. Wolford dropped a 2-1 decision to the eventual state champion, finishing eighth at 135. He ended his final Hanover season with a 40-6 mark, featuring great bouts with fellow all-stars Schmelyun and Macy.
*   **Tyler Zittle (Littlestown):** The undersized heavyweight rode his quickness and smarts to a PIAA berth and a 35-10 mark. Zittle had a huge postseason, including a fourth-place finish at regionals, a second-place finish at districts, and a sectional championship.
*   **James English (Central York):** English defeated a defending state champion in the semifinals and then the state's No. 1-ranked Class AAA 145-pounder in the finals, earning the Panther program its first district crown.

title: title. Maybe two if Alabama
headline: Wrestling Faces Lawsuits and Old Problem of Skin Infections
summary: As the 2008–09 season begins, wrestling is dealing with both the persistent issue of skin infections and a lawsuit alleging improper medical clearance procedures at York College.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
For The Evening Sun

As the 2008–09 mat season gets underway, the sport of wrestling—which some sources like to bill itself as “the world’s oldest sport”—is grappling with what might be its oldest problem: skin infections.

“We dealt with it when I was in high school. We dealt with it when I was in college at Bloomsburg,” one former wrestler stated. “The coaches we had then, and we dealt with them then just like we have to deal with them now.”

However, not everything remains the same. The difference comes with greater understanding and awareness of the different infections.

The sport is currently facing legal scrutiny. Former York College wrestlers filed a lawsuit earlier this month, claiming that the college and others associated with the program did not follow rules in October 2006. The wrestlers allege they contracted herpes from a teammate who returned to practice before the rules permitted. The college denies the lawsuit’s claims.

To ensure safety, wrestlers who develop a skin infection at either level must obtain clearances from physicians to return to competition. Both the NCAA and Pennsylvania high schools use similar forms, which outline the type of infection and include guidelines for treatment for various types of infections.

At the collegiate level, a physician or certified athletic trainer must check the skin of all wrestlers before each competition. The PIAA guidelines provide specific rules regarding skin lesions, stating that no definitive diagnosis can be made without a physical examination. These guidelines, along with the required clearance form, determine the wrestler's ability to compete.

headline: Wildcats Prepare for Season; New Oxford Challenges Division I
summary: The Bermudian Springs Wildcats are preparing for the wrestling season with key returners and a roster that must find a new identity, while other schools like New Oxford are also gearing up for competition.
cleaned_text:
The Wildcats are gearing up for the season, with key returners like Brandon Deamer (130), Brandon Myers (171), Charlie Rill (145), Drew Sneeringer (171), and Joey Wildasin (145) entering their final year. The team, coached by Greg Sprenkle, will also rely on sophomore Josh Burg and Davis (189). Jonathan Childress, a senior, is back for the Wildcats, and the 125-pound weight class will be filled by a wrestler moving up from the spot vacated by Zach Oberlander. The team will also be dealing with the loss of lightweight Cameron Throckmorton, who opted not to wrestle this year, despite having won a fourth-place PIAA Class AAA medal in the 103-pound weight class as a freshman.

In other wrestling news, New Oxford is expected to be competitive in Division I, returning almost its entire lineup. Meanwhile, in Division II, Kennard-Dale, which swapped spots with West York, is positioned for a strong season. Coach McCollum noted that the team must find a new identity and cannot rely on last year’s success, emphasizing that the challenge will depend on the current roster's ability to answer the call.

title: LEADING OFF
headline: Bermudian Springs Prepares for Season with Smeltzer Leading the Way
summary: Bermudian Springs is preparing for the wrestling season, relying heavily on sophomore Jamie Smeltzer, the defending champion, while coach McCollum guides the team toward state medal potential.
cleaned_text:
**Bermudian Springs Prepares for Season with Smeltzer Leading the Way**

By Sam Latter, Evening Sun Sports Writer

It's two weeks into practice, and the coaching staff is still teaching. They blow their whistles, tip their feet, and wiggle their heels. While some coaches might prefer to have the fundamentals locked away before the teaching begins, the staff at Bermudian Springs is still working hard.

The team is gearing up for the season, with much attention focused on sophomore Jamie Smeltzer. Smeltzer, a 15-year-old heavyweight, is the defending YAIAA Division II champion.

The team faces challenges, particularly with the entrance of key players. The coaches and team need to get answers because the weight for the next McCollum competition could be too much. Smeltzer has shown immense potential, and McCollum sees him as a potential state medal winner.

headline: Sophomore Wrestler Jamie Sneltzer Dies in Car Crash
summary: Sophomore wrestler Jamie Sneltzer died in a car crash near Dartington, according to a report by Alex Latter.
cleaned_text:
Bernudian Sophomore Wrestler Jamie Sneltzer Dies in Car Crash

By Alex Latter
On Friday, December 4, 2008, near Dartington, the death of sophomore wrestler Jamie Sneltzer was reported. The 15-year-old athlete, who was also a college basketball player, died in a car crash.

Dartington High School athletic coach, MCGill, spoke to the press about the tragic loss. The article mentions that Sneltzer was a beautiful boy and that the ordeal was difficult for the community.

headline: Local High Schools Prepare for Wrestling Season
summary: Several local high school wrestling teams, including Bermudian Springs, Delone Catholic, and Fairfield, are preparing for the 2008-2009 season despite key losses and roster changes.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Fri, Dec 5, 2008

**Bermudian Springs**
The Canners enter this season without three of their steadiest wrestlers. For most wrestling programs, losing a three-year starter who was a state medalist and notched 100 career wins would be devastating. It would likely be a down year as the school tries to find someone to fill the void.

**Delone Catholic**
Delone Catholic has posted a 2007-2008 record of 13-5, 8-2 YAIAA II. Key losses included Brayden Daigle and Jeremy Schultz. At the weight classes, they have athletes to watch, including Brown, a senior at 189 pounds; and Hunter Josh Ruppert, a sophomore at 112 pounds.

**Fairfield**
Fairfield has entered the season to keep its team in top form, participating in tournaments and dual meets. The team is led by athletes such as Aaron Blinn, a senior, and Gil Ferguson, a junior, at 171 pounds. They also have Shay Shive, who finished eighth in the state championships, and Foster, a sophomore.

**Quotes and Outlook**
Bob Gano, a coach, stated, “Last season was successful, but it's never a total success unless you win the league and win the district. We lost to Bermudian Springs.”

Another coach, Gano, said, “I think we can be pretty successful this year by working hard, staying injury-free and just letting the chips fall where they may.”

The Knights are pushing their team to win individual matches. Coach Kocsis noted, “It's going to be individual matches, just keying people with opponents and taking the tournament qualifiers in Fairfield, as well as a meet in Flohr, and as well as a meet in Blinn. These kids don't get stagnant or bored with it.”

YAIAA Division II powers have new coaches: Tiree tradition continues.

title: Eagles prove strength in dua
headline: Eagles Clinch York Title at Dallastown Duals
summary: The Bermudian Springs Eagles won the York championship title at the Dallastown Duals, defeating Schuylkill Valley in the final match.
cleaned_text:
The Eagles Prove Strength in Duals

By TOM HUNT
For The Evening Sun

The Bermudian Springs High team had a challenging start but finished the first Saturday of the new year strongly.

Because a couple of wrestlers worked out to make weight, the Eagles arrived late at Dallastown for the Dallastown Duals and missed part of the scheduled weigh-in time. However, after winning its pool with a 3-0 record, the team faced Schuylkill Valley in the championship match and came away with a 36-22 victory, clinching the York title.

The day was capped with style when Schmelyun pinned Schuylkill Valley’s Colin Shober in the final and most anticipated individual bout. Both wrestlers had previously taken home third-place medals from the 2008 PIAA meet. McCollum, the Eagles coach, was understandably happy with his team’s effort against several Class AAA teams and fellow District 3 Class AA power, Schuylkill Valley.

“I’m proud of our kids,” said the Eagles coach. “I felt coming in, if we had the lineup we needed, we had a shot at Bellefonte and Dallastown. And I was concerned about Schuylkill Valley. It’s important for us to wrestle.”

Earlier in the day, the third seed in the eight-team field took care of business first against Dallastown (41-19) and Kennard-Dale (54-10) before facing second-seeded Bellefonte for a berth in the final. Against the Red Raiders from District 6, the defending YAIAA Division II champions won six of the first seven bouts to jump out to a 28-6 lead and cruised from there to a 41-24 victory.

That set up the showdown between the top two-ranked Class AA teams in District 3. The Panthers, 9-0 before Saturday, were ranked second in the district but were seeded No. 1 for the Dallastown Duals.

“We had only one day off over the holidays,” the coach said. “The day after West Mifflin, we were banging hard in the practice room. We put them through the ringer.”

The strategy seemed to pay off as the two teams split the 14 bouts, but only one of the Eagle losses pried any bonus points from a Schuylkill Valley opponent. Meanwhile, the seven Eagle victories earned bonus points.

The big points came via fall: from Jake Stimer (160), Evan Brechbiel (215), Tanner Coyle (112), and Schmelyun (145).

Brechbiel’s and Coyle’s first-period pins sandwiched a huge 11-5 decision by Eagle freshman Brad Farley over Nate Giorgio in a matchup of two of the top Class A 103-pounders in the district.

summary: Head coach Dana McKenna stated that the upper half of their league forms the foundation of the state intercollegiate system, citing the strong records of several teams.
cleaned_text: The upper half of our league is possibly the foundation of the state intercollegiate system, head coach Dana McKenna said. Speaking of Division I, it carries the same importance. McKenna was referring to his Eagles (7-0, 8-1), Bighighill (6-0, 8-2), Denver (6-1, 3-1), Northwestern (16-2, 14-1), and Stanford (3-4, 1-2). You have to be ready to compete every week in this league.

headline: Bermudian Springs Wrestler Excels at D-II Level
summary: Bermudian Springs wrestler Derek Schmelyun is praised by his coach, Kriss Bellanca, for his readiness to compete at the D-II level at Kutztown.
cleaned_text:
Kutztown Attracts Talent as Bermudian Springs Wrestler Excels

Kutztown became a particularly attractive destination for athletes when the coach of Schmelyun’s summer team, STEEL Club, was brought on as an assistant for the Golden Bears.

“Talking to my brother, he wanted me to go to Bloomsburg and go Division I, but at college I want to enjoy myself,” said Derek Schmelyun, a wrestler from Bermudian Springs.

Schmelyun’s coach, Kriss Bellanca, noted that Schmelyun’s dedication to the sport makes him one of the best athletes already. While Schmelyun is preparing himself for the next level—becoming a national champion—Bellanca believes he is ready for the challenge.

“Bellanca said Schmelyun should start for Kutztown immediately, a feat that would not be as likely at the Division I level,” Bellanca said. “If he went to D-I, he would put in the time and struggle and take his lumps. It’s hard to be successful from day one. At the D-II level, as talented as he is, he can come in and make a difference right off the bat.”

Bellanca appreciated the effort Schmelyun has put into wrestling, noting that his hard work has prepared him for the next level. “I think he has pretty much perfected what he’s good at and has also gotten better through experience on the mat. He has gotten better at staying in good position and not making himself vulnerable to other wrestlers. He’s very difficult to point,” Bellanca said.

title: underwater grass beds, key
headline: Pennsylvania's Role in Bay Health and Local Sports Success
summary: The article covers Pennsylvania's significant role in protecting the Chesapeake Bay, reports on successful wrestling matches involving the Bermudian Springs team, and lists nominees for local Scholar-Athlete awards.
cleaned_text:
**Environmental Concerns in the Chesapeake Bay**

While attention often focuses on Maryland and Virginia, Pennsylvania plays a large role in the health of the Chesapeake Bay. More than half of the state lies within the watershed, containing two major river systems: the Potomac (1,600 square miles) and the Susquehanna (2,100 square miles). Together, these watersheds account for 40 percent of the entire bay watershed.

While much focus is placed on Pennsylvania’s farms, the use of fertilizer, and proper land management to prevent erosion, the role of the Keystone State’s forest and wooded regions in keeping the bay clear of pollution is often overlooked. Planting trees and bushes is crucial for minimizing erosion.

headline: Bermudian Springs Wrestling Dominates; Local Sports Roundup
summary: The article reports on the Bermudian Springs wrestling team's dominant victory, alongside multiple local sports victories including Greenwood over Newport and Juniata over Millersburg.
cleaned_text:
The Patriot-News
Tue, Jan 20, 2009

**Bermudian Springs Wrestling Stars; Local Sports Roundup**

**Wrestling**
The Bermudian Springs wrestling team had a dominant night, securing a huge win that gives them a strong chance at the No. 1 seed at the district finals. Coach McCollum stated, “Everyone did their part tonight. This is such a huge win for us... Hopefully when we see these guys again, it will be in the district finals.”

The team’s success was highlighted by several key performances. Sam Matter scored a 16-7 major at 215 over Matthew Wolf. Luke Perry was particularly impressive, turning a reversal into a single-leg cradle to pin Evan Brechbiel in 3:50 for a quick 16-0 lead. Brad Farley added 14 points to the board. Joey Spisak scored an expected pin in 3:03 over Wesly Marshall at 119.

Other standout performances included Nick Blasone, who gave the Eagles hope with a 16-0 tech fall of Trent Bond at 125. Tyler Thumma fought off opposing Anthony, and John Riddle, who beat a guy he had previously defeated at the Carlisle Tournament, was noted for his toughness.

The day’s action continued with several other victories. Derek Schmelyun was awarded a forfeit at 145, and the Eagles’ Devin O’Brien defeated Dakota Kuhn 5-2 at 152. Jacob Stimer followed with a pin of Jake Grove at 160, and Nick Grim scored a 3-0 decision over Mark Hammond, giving the Eagles a four-point lead. Kim Newman led the Eagles in double finals with 16 points, while Lyndsey Michael and Leister each scored 10 points.

**Other Sports Scores**
The local sports coverage also included several other victories.

In a basketball matchup, the Greenwood Wildcats defeated the Newport Buffaloes 47-40. The Wildcats put together a furious fourth-quarter rally, outscoring the Buffaloes 21-13 to pull off the upset victory. Emily Livingston led all scorers with 22 points, scoring her 1,000th career point with 30 seconds remaining. Autumn Pel, Iman, Andi Matter, and Mari Rumberger each contributed significant points.

In another contest, the Juniata Indians defeated the Millersburg team 65-39. Tessa Gilson led the Indians to a comfortable victory with a season-high 26 points.

Finally, Halifax defeated Upper Dauphin 59-25, in a game that was tight until the final minutes.

headline: Bermudian Springs Bubblers Secure Major Wrestling Victory
summary: The article reports on the successful wrestling performance of the Bermudian Springs Bubblers, alongside scores from several other local high school sports teams.
cleaned_text:
**The Patriot-News**
**Tuesday, January 20, 2009**

**Bermudian Springs Bubblers Make Statement at Wrestling Meet**

PAUL CHAPLIN, The Patriot-News

“Everyone fed off each other tonight. This is such a huge win for us, because we have a good chance to get the No. 1 seed at districts. Hopefully, when we see these guys again, it will be in the district finals,” Chaplin stated.

The Bubblers finished the night with an 8-0 record. Sam Matter followed up with a 16-7 victory at 215 over Matthew Wolf. Joey Spisak scored an expected pin in 3:03 over Wesly Marshall at 119.

Coach McCollum noted that the success was unexpected. “I didn’t expect that,” McCollum said. “I didn’t think they would bump him up like that.”

The team’s performance was highlighted by several key victories. Bermudian Springs’ Derek Schmelyun was awarded a forfeit at 145, and the Eagles’ Devin O’Brien defeated Dakota Kuhn 5-2 at 152. Jacob Stimer also contributed to the team’s success.

Other standout matches included Nick Blasone, who secured a pin of Jake Grove at 160, and Nick Grim, who scored a 3-0 defeat of Trent Bond at 125. Brad Farley added 14-1 points to the board, and Tyler Thumma helped give the Eagles a four-point lead.

headline: Schuylkill Valley Blank Nighthawks in District Tournament Opening Round
summary: Schuylkill Valley defeated Hanover in the District 3 Class AA Team Tournament, while several other wrestlers secured victories across various weight classes.
cleaned_text:
Schuylkill Valley ended Hanover's dual-meet season with a 14-13 victory by blanking the Nighthawks in the opening round of the District 3 Class AA Team Tournament. While Schuylkill Valley posted six falls and four technical falls, Hanover suffered two-point decision defeats by Derek Wolford at 145 pounds and Tylor Elder at 160 pounds.

In other matches, Shaffer worked over his opponent, Scott, gaining a tilt with bars for a 64-point win. Shaffer noted that by the third period, he could feel his opponent getting weaker, and he knew his team's conditioning would help him.

In a separate bout at 140 pounds, Logan Wisner led 2-1 after two periods, then escaped Brandon Beltz and held him off for a 3-1 verdict.

Tyler Small and Hunter Doan continued the winning streak with a pin at 125 pounds and a technical fall at 130 pounds, respectively. Small’s pin came on a cradle 32 seconds into his match, while Doan built a 16-2 lead after two periods and ended the match with three near-fall points in the final period. Ryan Statler finished the Squires’ run with a major decision at 135 pounds.

The article also highlighted the continued success of the Bermudian Springs wrestling program, noting that coach McCollum's team was performing strongly throughout the season.

title: Thursday's 26-24 win at South
headline: Bermudian Springs Wrestling Team Advances to PIAA Team Championships
summary: Despite a difficult start to the season, the Bermudian Springs wrestling team advanced to the PIAA Team Championships after defeating Juniata in the district semifinals.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Fri, Jan 30, 2009

**Eagles Battle Back Into Semis**

By Tom Hunt

Last season, the Bermudian Springs wrestling team hit the postseason on cruise control, finishing with a district team title and an appearance in the PIAA Team Class AA finals—a major achievement for both the program and the YAIAA.

This season, the Eagles entered the most important part of the season with little momentum, having lost the last two regular-season dual meets and enduring a barrage of injuries and suspensions.

“It's been a tough season, but we're proud of the kids who we have here,” said the coach. “They really hung in there. They put their hearts into getting back to the Spartan Center.”

The Milton Hershey School's Spartan Center, site of Saturday's District Class AA Wrestling Team Tournament semifinals, is exactly where McCollum’s gritty crew will be heading after Thursday's 32-26 semifinal victory over visiting Juniata.

Awaiting them in Hershey will be none other than Division II champion and bitter rival Biglerville, who won last Saturday with a 30-27 victory over Bermudian Springs. The second-seeded Canners took two matches on Thursday, including a 46-24 thrashing of Wyoming in the quarterfinal round.

The winner of that semifinal will face the winner of the Boiling Springs vs. Schuylkill Valley match in the 6:30 p.m. championship match. Both finalists qualify for the PIAA Team Championships, which start next Tuesday.

headline: Eagles Secure State Tournament Berth at Milton Hershey
summary: The Eagles secured a crucial victory at Milton Hershey School, allowing them to advance to the state tournament, highlighted by key wins from wrestlers like Tanner Coyle and Eric Mezsaros.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Sun, Feb 1, 2009

The Eagles secured a significant victory at Milton Hershey School's Spartan Center. Coach Dave McCollum stated that the win was crucial, as it allowed the team to extend their season into the state tournament.

“The only way to go to the state tournament was to win here in the semifinals. That was our big thing,” McCollum said. “We were still in that mode to come back and get another victory.”

McCollum also recalled a specific match where his team was trailing 90 points heading into the 130-pound weight class. “We got exactly what we wanted after the first three,” he said. “Dalton was trying to reset the headlock, and he just got tolled through and caught on his back. We fought back as hard as we could.”

The day featured several standout performances. Bermudian Springs sophomore Tanner Coyle, who had suffered a loss the previous week, turned the tables on standout freshman Trevor Hernandez. Coyle hit two takedowns and added a three-point nearfall in the final 30 seconds, earning an 8-2 decision.

The Eagles' lightweight division was also strong. At 112 pounds, freshman Brad Farley fought off numerous pinning combinations from Canner's star-in-making Laike Gardner.

In another notable bout, Eric Mezsaros took the air out of Canner Nation, scoring a reversal right before the buzzer to steal a 3-2 victory.

Other wrestlers contributed to the team's success. Nick Grim (171 lbs) and Matt Wolf (215 lbs) were highlighted for turning around earlier losses.

title: Giant Center mats await Eagles
headline: Bermudian Springs Eagles Face Elk Lake at HAA Tournament
summary: The Bermudian Springs Eagles are participating in their first HAA Class AA Tournament round at Giant Center against Elk Lake.
cleaned_text:
**Giant Center Mats Await Bermudian Springs Eagles**

Bermudian Springs will wrestle Elk Lake at 8 a.m. Friday.

By Dan Graybell
The Evening Sun

Bermudian Springs has been to the HAA Class AA Tournament before, but its opponent, Elk Lake, is not, giving the Eagles a first-time experience in the tournament round at Giant Center in Nashville.

The Giant Center is noted as the first in the league to have a technical advantage.

“I think it is a mental advantage. Our opponent’s Springs team doesn’t want to compromise with it. It doesn’t want to do anything like having a slight edge,” said the coach. “I think it is a new year in a new season. It will know that it is a major factor. It will play into us being more prepared.”

The coach continued, noting that Elk Lake has been consistently talented throughout the season. “They continue to win time after time with them. They are so talented. The Warriors even booked a something right,” he said. “McCollum said they were going back to need for a win. Tuesday’s win, he thought, his team tried and slugged, and he needed to be rewarded.”

The matchup is: Washington Springs Eagles (185) vs. Elk Lake (183) HAA Class AA Tournament round at Giant Center in Nashville.

Family tradition continues.

title: The Evening Sun
headline: Bermudian Springs Eagles Advance in PIAA Wrestling Championship
summary: The Bermudian Springs Eagles advanced to the semifinals of the PIAA Team Wrestling Championship after a difficult season, highlighted by freshman Brad Farley's crucial pin victory.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Dave Sat, Feb 07, 2009

[Article Snippet: Johnson's Recruiting Impact]
For many Penn State fans, Larry Johnson Sr. would have made his bones with the university had he done nothing more than to sire Tony and Larry Jr. Tony Johnson was a three-year starter as a wide receiver for the Nittany Lions, and Larry Jr. continued the family legacy in Happy Valley when he rushed for a school-record 2,087 yards in 2002 and scored 23 touchdowns.

[Article Snippet: PIAA Wrestling Championship]
The Eagles advanced the hard way to the semifinals of the PIAA Team Wrestling Championship at Hershey's Giant Center. They had lost in the quarterfinals but managed to win both of their consolation matchups.

Brad Farley, a freshman from Bermudian Springs, stepped into the spotlight with the Eagles trailing 25-23 to Burrell High in the last bout of the meet—a decision away from advancing to today’s action. Farley, who was reportedly nervous entering the match, came up big, pinning Dan Shepherd in 3 minutes and 55 seconds and securing a 29-25 win.

In a lot of ways, Bermudian Springs’ up-and-down performance on the wrestling mat told of a team that had been through a difficult season. The Eagles showed perseverance, demonstrating that even though they lost a good chunk of their team throughout the season, they maintained a constant pace and would not give up.

title: | ticket prices
headline: Farley's Pin Sends Bermudian Springs to PIAA Consolation Semis
summary: After losing in the quarterfinals, Bermudian Springs advanced to the PIAA team consolation semifinals following a strong performance by freshman Brad Farley.
cleaned_text:
Brad Farley’s pin sent Bermudian Springs into the PIAA team consolation semifinals.

By Cory Mull
York Daily Record

Bermudian Springs didn't really have time to think. Coach McCollum made a lot of rash moves, but the rally was furious. In the end, most of them worked. Somehow, the McCollum’s team advanced the hard way to the semifinals of the consolation rounds in the PIAA Championships at Giant Center.

Bermudian Springs lost in the quarterfinals before winning both of its consolation matchups. Up next today at 11 a.m. is a contest against the loser of the Schuylkill Valley Shady Side match. If the Eagles will compete later in the day for third-place, they will face the winner of the Eastern York High School bout of Friday's final match.

Brad Farley, a 103-pound freshman, stepped into the spotlight with the Eagles trailing the Knights. He was nervous before the match, but the diminutive wrestler came up big, pinning Dan Shepard and securing a 2-1 victory.

In a lot of ways, Bermudian Springs’ up-and-down Friday told a story about a team that’s been through so much this season.

title: “Dave h
headline: Schuylkill Valley Falls Short in PIAA Team Tournament
summary: Schuylkill Valley lost in the consolation semifinal of the PIAA Class AA Team Wrestling Championship, marking their second defeat in the District 3 final.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Schuylkill Valley Falls Short in PIAA Team Tournament

Schuylkill Valley’s wrestling team suffered a defeat in the consolation semifinal of the PIAA Class AA Team Wrestling Championship at Hershey’s Giant Center, losing 30-27.

The loss marks the second time the Eagles have fallen short in the District 3 Class AA Team Wrestling Tournament final.

“Well, there was a lot of pressure there, but I tried my best that I could, and it just didn’t happen,” said Kuhn.

The team faced challenges throughout the tournament. Earlier, the Panthers forfeited the last two matches to Derek Schmelyun at 145 pounds and to Devin O'Brien at 152 pounds.

During the tournament, Nick Grim grappled with Schuylkill Valley's Cody Johnson in the 189-pound weight class, pinning Johnson in a match.

The article also noted that the Eagles’ mission on Saturday was to limit bonus points.

title: e round
headline: Hade's Wrestling Comeback Story
summary: After initially committing to football, Hade transitioned to wrestling at Shippensburg University, earning a scholarship and aiming for an All-American title at the upcoming regional tournament despite suffering multiple injuries.
cleaned_text:
Hade struggled to choose a sport, finding himself torn between the gridiron and the mat. He had initially tried to build himself into a college football player at Shippensburg University, making a commitment when the team offered him a deal.

However, his wrestling talent kept calling to him. His breakthrough came during a state wrestling tournament, where he fought his way to the Class AA state finals despite having what he called a "popped rib." People saw a glimpse of his potential.

“I remember back when we were eliminating with one of our better kids, actually, just a freshman,” said McCollum. “He got hit in the head and his head swelled up really big. Usually kids stop when something like that happened, and we told him he could sit out, but ‘No’ he said, he worked through it.” The next day, Hade showed up at wrestling practice with one eye swollen shut, but he was still working and going at it.

After serving two seasons as a team player for Shippensburg University, Hade approached Shippensburg wrestling coach Don Tabar about a possible opening on the mat. Hade had already decided he would quit football, even if Tabar didn't want him. But Tabar offered him an open door.

Hade lost his football scholarship, but Tabar awarded him a wrestling scholarship. The fun began.

“I had to work my butt off,” Hade laughed. “It’s hard enough adapting to the collegiate level, and then to have taken time off, I had to catch up.”

He had previously lost in the NCAA Division II East Regionals, falling two victories short of advancing to the national championships. He listed his recent injuries—stitches for a gash, a broken fourth metacarpal, and a sprained ankle—but Hade couldn't be happier.

“Once I get to 100 percent, I'm going to do my best at regionals,” Hade said. “I think I'm in the hunt to win the regional tournament and take a little step. I want to keep going forward. I'd like to be All-American.”

Spring Green led the way with an excellent performance, securing multiple champions and strong showings across four tournaments. The team achieved a second-place team title with 145.3 points. South Western, which crowned two champions, finished just ahead of the Buckets with 127.5 points.

summary: The article details the progress of several wrestlers in the regional tournament, highlighting Bermudian Springs' Derek Schmelyun's success and listing the remaining competitors in the consolation brackets.
cleaned_text:
Walls in the Consolation Brackets

York Daily Record
Sat, Feb 21, 2009

In the consolation brackets, Walls managed a 7-0 decision over Biglerville’s Nick Leiphart. While Walls could still grab a third-place finish and is likely to earn a regional berth, the loss put the standout Bolt wrestler in check.

While Walls fell, many others did not, including Bermudian Springs’ Derek Schmelyun (140), who won gold at 135 last year. The senior wrestler made quick work of Schuylkill Valley's Noah Horst, pinning him in 1 minute, 32 seconds.

Bermudian Springs head coach Dave McCollum said his senior was to be counted as the favorite in the 140 class. “Tomorrow is hugely important,” McCollum said. “That's where you find what you're made of. With Derek, you'd have to think he’s the favorite at 140.”

Six of Schmelyun’s teammates also moved on to the quarterfinals today. Tanner Coyle (103) wrestled a victory over Brautigam in the quarterfinals to advance. Smyers earned a fall over Newport's Justin McAllister, while the Gardner brothers each recorded close decisions.

Delone Catholic’s Tyler Small (125), Joshua Ruppert, Boyde (119), and Dallas Brown (189) all advanced to today’s action as well. Hanover’s Brent Hoff (1) and Littlestown’s Philip M. (140) also reached the quarterfinals.

Thirteen wrestlers remain alive in the consolation brackets, including Delone Catholic’s Joseph Byers (108), Mitch Kaehler (171), Joseph Kreiner (215), Noah Sneeringer (285), Littlestown’s Walls and Thomas Showvaker (160), Biglerville’s Nick Leiphart (125), and Dustin Bower (119). Fairfield's Kyle Flohr (1), Bermudian Springs’ Kiefer Kuhn (135), Eastern York's Donald Minnier (119), Phillip Landis (171), and York Suburban's Stan Williams (215).

title: “Dave
headline: Bermudian Springs Wrestlers Win Golds, Honoring 30-Year Singlet Tradition
summary: Bermudian Springs wrestlers Derek Schmelyun and Devin O'Brien won District 3 Class AA golds in their respective weight classes, wearing a singlet style that honored a 30-year tradition, despite initial reservations from coach Dave McCollum.
cleaned_text:
Derek Schmelyun and Devin O'Brien have a strong friendship. They have competed together for four years in regular seasons, sectionals, districts, regionals, and states—not to mention outside of them.

"There's not much that one does and the other does not," said one source.

It was only fitting on a night when both senior wrestlers won District 3 Class AA golds in their respective classes—140 and 152—that both also wore the same style of singlet. That singlet was a version produced and worn nearly 30 years ago by Eagle wrestlers.

Bermudian Springs coach Dave McCollum was totally against the idea, but he eventually warmed up to it.

"It was totally against it from the start, but I told them if they were going to do it, that they were going to have to do something special," McCollum said. "It looks like it paid off."

The article also mentions other wrestling achievements, including a Class AAA victory by another wrestler and a Class AA victory by Jon C. Gable (171).

headline: PA State Championship Kicks Off at Ghost Center
summary: The Class A PA State Championship began at Ghost Center, featuring strong performances from athletes representing schools like Bermudian Springs and New Oxford.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Fri. Mar. 6, 2009

The first day of the Class A PA State Championship at Ghost Center in Kennett Square was a major event for local athletes. The girls' preliminary matches featured top classes, with ten wrestlers advancing to today's main matches.

The Western Springs and Bermudian Springs sent a strong contingent of 18 athletes to the championship.

In the wrestling matches, Jordan Conway of New Oxford advanced to the Class AAA match, defeating his opponent with a score of 5 points.

The championship day featured participation from various schools, including Hanover.

title: “Dave h
headline: Mental Toughness and Grit Shine at State Wrestling Championships
summary: The PIAA Class AA championships highlighted the intense physical and mental demands of high school wrestling, with several athletes advancing through the grueling tournament.
cleaned_text:
The mental aspect can be the toughest opponent. That wasn't any truer than the experience of the PIAA Class AA championships at Giant Center. Fifteen area wrestlers made it that far, grappling among the top 16 of the state in each of their weight classes, with ten advancing to today’s matches.

“These are the 16 best kids in the state (in each class),” said Bermudian Springs coach McCollum.

Several wrestlers advanced to today’s action, including Bermudian Springs’ Derek Schmelyun (140 pounds), Biglerville’s Danny Thomas (215), Delone Catholic’s Joshua Ruppert (119), Dal Brown (189), Hanover's Brent Hoff (135), and Fairfield’s Dylan Ferguson (215). Philip Macy of Littlestown (140) also advanced after winning his consolation bout.

Wrestlers battle through pain and injury all the time, but during state championships, everything is under the microscope in front of thousands of people.

In Dylan Ferguson's match, he trailed Athens’ Todd Hall 2-1 shortly after the second period started, when he landed awkwardly on his right wrist. The action stopped for a few minutes, and Ferguson had to diagnose whether he would continue. “I wrestle through things a lot,” Ferguson said. “I have a pretty messed-up knee. It's hard to wrestle through injuries in practice. It's something you learn to do, but once you get out here, you start to go hard, your adrenaline gets up, so it doesn't really matter anymore.”

Meanwhile, on the adjacent mat, Danny Thomas was working time in his bout. Thomas went into his match’s third overtime with Montoursville’s N Dawson. Dawson whistled for a lock-up with eight seconds left. A move gave Thomas an opening, and he held on for a 43-point win.

headline: All-Area Coach and Wrestler of the Year Recognized
summary: The article recognizes Coach Byers for his success at Delone and details the resilience of All-Area Wrestler of the Year Schmelyun, whose coach, McCollum, inspired him after a major defeat.
cleaned_text:
“We took four medals, which no one expected us to do, except for maybe me,” Byers said, earning the title of All-Area Coach of the Year.

One factor in his hiring was the work he did with the Outlaws club team. Byers started this program a couple of years back, which included several York-Adams wrestlers, including Delone junior Tyler Small, a fifth-place PIAA Championship medalist at 125, and Delone’s Joshua Ruppert, who placed eighth at 112.

Naturally, the work he did with Outlaws helped when he began the job at Delone, but he was still met with cynicism from the athletes at the start of the season.

“In the beginning of the year, we started rough because kids were learning new techniques,” Byers said. “Getting kids to buy into that was kind of hard. I've always told kids that, if you want to get better, you have to listen to what I have to say.”

Dallas Brown, the Squires’ 189-pound state runner-up, noted that the team was initially struggling. “It was a little rough,” Brown said. “He didn't budge, and he let us know that. In the end, he’s a good coach, and I really like the guy, in and out of the room.”

The Squires finished the year 13-4 and 82 in YAIAA II, with losses to Biglerville, the York-Adams Division II champion, and Bermudian Springs. Although the 34-28 loss to Biglerville was the first match of the season, Byers believes that if the schedule had been flipped and the Squires had faced the Canners late in the season, things might have been different.

“As the season went on, they started buying into the program,” Byers said. “That's why we were so successful.”

title: The Gettysburg Times
headline: Local News Roundup: Rabies Warning, Community Updates, and Educator Recognition
summary: The article provides a collection of local updates, including a warning about the spread of rabies, the resignation of a YWCA director, and recognition of local educators and community leaders.
cleaned_text:
**The Gettysburg Times**
**Friday, November 20, 2009**

**Out of the Past**
“Frosty the Snowman” appeared at the square in Gettysburg over the weekend and was so popular that he decided to stay for the season. The Gettysburg Jaycees helped put ‘Frosty’ in his rightful place, and Christmas decorations—including a Nativity Scene, Christmas tree lights, and a dusting of snow—gave the center of town a holiday feel days before Thanksgiving.

**Rabies Warning**
Suzanne Yeager, a south central epidemiologist for the Pennsylvania Department of Health, updated members of the SPCA and its auxiliary, the Friends of the Animal Shelter, on the spreading rabies problem. As of last week, the state’s two laboratories had identified 325 confirmed cases of rabid animals, including 54 cases in Adams County. It is believed the epidemic resulted from hunters unknowingly trapping rabid raccoons in Georgia and releasing them for propagation purposes in Virginia, spreading the disease northward into Pennsylvania.

**Community News and Achievements**
Mary Lou Seamens, executive director of the Gettysburg YWCA, recently resigned after serving in that capacity since May 1979. She was praised for her dedication to the organization, having played an important part in the creation of the YWCA Family Recreation Center.

In other local news, Dale Reinecker of York Springs was named the Jaycees outstanding young firm leader.

In education, McCollum, a teacher and coach at Bermudian Springs Middle School, has not missed a day of school since he was hired in 1977. He is running with other state educators to be named PA Best Educator in February.

headline: Bermudian Springs Wrestling Team Falls Short at States
summary: Despite falling short of a state championship due to bonus points, the Bermudian Springs wrestling team is proud of its season and the effort of its senior class.
cleaned_text:
“Dave into tl i school, nobody thought we could get where we are,” Bermudian Springs senior Eric Meszaros said. “No one thought we could get to states, and we came one match and a couple of individuals short of getting there. So we're pretty proud of what we did this year.”

The Eagles had a shot at states but fell short in their semifinal match against Schuylkill Valley (24-0). After building an 186 lead over Schuylkill Valley with pins from Brad Farley at 108 pounds, Tanner Coyle (119), and Wes Marshall (125), the Eagles dropped the next eight matches to lose 44-30. Schuylkill Valley scored bonus points with four pins, a major decision, and a tech-fall to pull away midway through the dual.

“That was the only shot we had at them, was not to give up bonus points,” McCollum said. “Unfortunately, they got bonus points. We almost had to have a perfect match to beat them. But we made them earn it.”

Although the team had numerous entries in individual postseason events, McCollum expressed his gratitude for his current crop of seniors who wrestled their final dual as a team. “This has been an incredible senior class,” McCollum said. “From the start of the season until now, they've given us 150 percent every day, every practice, every match. I can honestly say we did not wrestle a bad match this year.”

title: “Dave
headline: Bermudian Springs Coach McCollum Named All-Area Coach of Year
summary: Bermudian Springs coach McCollum was named All-Area Coach of Year following a successful season for the Eagles, while several other athletes also achieved significant individual and team victories across the area.
cleaned_text:
**Bermudian Springs Coach McCollum Named All-Area Coach of Year**

By TRAVIS JOHNSON
The Evening Sun

Bermudian Springs wrestling coach McCollum was named the All-Area Coach of Year. While the wrestling season was once much longer, even a month removed from the end of the 2009-10 high school season, McCollum is already preparing for next year’s campaign. One might expect the coach, with 33 years of experience, to take a break, especially after his Bermudian Springs Eagles finished atop the YAIAA Division II standings with a 100 mark, ending the team season with an impressive upswing.

The article also highlighted several standout individual performances across the area:

*   **Dalton Anthony, Sr. (Bermudian Springs):** Anthony secured a sectional championship at 140 pounds. Though he could only muster a sectional title in the postseason, the senior still had a terrific regular season, scoring 92 dual-meet points and finishing with an 18-3 dual-meet record. His efforts helped the Eagles achieve an undefeated 18-3 record in YAIAA Division II.
*   **Joey Byers, So. (Delone Catholic):** The talented 103-pounder battled a painful fib injury throughout the season. Despite this, Byers put together his best season as a varsity wrestler, finishing 32-11 and winning 14 dual-meet bouts for the Squires. His season was highlighted by winning the district championship after defeating rival Brad Farley.
*   **Jordan Conaway, Jr. (New Oxford):** Conaway was dominant all season, bouncing between three weight classes and accumulating a 43-2 record. He finished third at the 103-pound weight class.
*   **Laike Gardner, So. (Biglervill):** Gardner was versatile for the Canners, bouncing between 125 and 185 pounds and racking up a 39-8 record. He had a strong postseason, going 11-5, finishing third at the district level, and seventh in the state at 125 pounds.
*   **Trevor Hernandez, So. (Delone Catholic):** Hernandez contributed 128 team points and helped propel the Squires to a 9-1 record in YAIAA Division II. The talented sophomore led his squad with pins and won a sectional title.
*   **Joseph Kreiner, Sr. (Delone Catholic):** Big and powerful, Kreiner finished his senior campaign with a 28-mark. His 76 dual-meet points were crucial to the Squires in an injury-riddled season. Kreiner’s District 3 championship completed the trifecta for Delone Catholic.
*   **Tyler Small, Sr. (Delone Catholic):** Small capped off a dynamic career by winning his third district title and claiming the 125-pound state championship.

headline: Local High Schools Prepare for Wrestling Season
summary: The article provides a detailed roundup of local high school wrestling teams, highlighting key returning athletes, major losses, and predictions for the upcoming season.
cleaned_text:
The article provides a local sports roundup, focusing heavily on the upcoming wrestling season for several high school teams in the region.

At Bermudian Springs, the Eagles are expected to be favorites, boasting a strong roster of key returners including Brad Farley, Tanner Coyle, and Matt Woll. Farley, who was state runner-up last year at 103 pounds, was 38-2. Coach McCollum noted that the team has a good mix of youth and experience.

The Rams are preparing for the season, with Chance Marsteller highlighted as the top incoming freshman. Marsteller, who is expected to wrestle around 145 pounds, previously competed in college tournaments and defeated a reigning PIAA champion in Orlando, Florida. The Rams will also move from Division II to Division I this season.

South Western's team will rely on key returners like Cameron Throckmorton and Jerome Beers, and key tosses such as Drew Rebling, Seth Whedey, and JR. Mummert. Coach Greg Sprenkle stated that the team will rely on wrestlers who haven't seen varsity action.

Eastern York's Golden Knights are counting on key returners Donald Minnier, Alex Cooley, and Buck Kauffman. Key losses include Phil Landis, who was a district champion at 189 pounds, and Robert Aaron Mackley. Coach Dan Gamer noted that Minnier will wrestle at 135 pounds.

York South's team has key returners including Kyle O'LaCruz. The Trojans were 6-4 in the division last year, and three of their wrestlers were state qualifiers.

West York plans to hold a fundraiser for breast cancer awareness, while the South Western Mustangs are recovering from major injuries that hampered them last season.

headline: Bermudian Springs Wrestling Team Begins Season
summary: The article reports on the start of the Bermudian Springs wrestling season and includes an update on coach Meyer's return to Florida athletics.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Thursday, December 9, 2010

**Bermudian Springs Wrestling Team Kicks Off Season**

The Bermudian Springs High School wrestling team will begin its season next Tuesday after getting off the bus at Littlestown High School. The team has a large group of returnees that will lead it on the mats this season.

Coach McCollum stated that the team has been working all offseason, some in premium wrestling clubs and others on their own. He noted that the team will be more physical than the previous year and believes they have the potential to succeed.

Despite losing several seniors—including Dalton Anthony, Eric Meszaros, Wes Marshall, and Jeff Racine—to graduation, McCollum feels the team has another chance.

title: “Dave h
headline: Bermudian Springs Wrestlers Debut Strong at Littlestown
summary: The Bermudian Springs wrestling team debuted strongly at Littlestown High School, with wrestlers like Tristan Sponseller and Austin Jackson securing major decision victories in their first varsity matches.
cleaned_text:
The Bermudian Springs High School wrestling team welcomed four newcomers into the lineup Tuesday night at Littlestown High School, and they did not miss a beat. The Eagles, as a team, defeated the Thunderbolts 55-9.

Ted Marines, Austin Jackson, Jared Spahr, and Tristan Sponseller all made their high school debuts for the Eagles. While Marines won by forfeit, the other three looked solid in their first varsity matches.

Tristan Sponseller of Bermudian Springs faced Tanner Mitz of Littlestown in a 160-pound bout on Tuesday, winning by major decision, 12-0.

In other matches, Jackson competed at 130 pounds, taking down Ryan Rickell in the opening minute and quickly turning him to his back for six near-fall points. Rickell, a sophomore, fended off Jackson’s pinning attempts but could not score against him, who won by major decision, 12-0.

In other bouts, Colby Starner and Tanner Coyle competed in the 119-pound and 125-pound matches, respectively.

McCollum praised his freshmen wrestlers. “I felt like we had them pinned in the cradle,” McCollum said.

title: The Evening Sun
headline: Springs High School Wrestlers Defeat Squires in YAIAA Division I Victory
summary: Springs High School wrestlers defeated the Squires 69-8 in a YAIAA Division I match, with Fitzkee scoring the decisive points after overcoming a deficit.
cleaned_text:
Springs High School wrestlers beat the Squires, 69-8.

Down by one point late in the second period to Delone Catholic’s Aaron Rooney, Fitzkee pulled off an acrobatic forward roll from the bottom position and reversed Rooney to take the lead. In the third period, Fitzkee notched an escape and held on for a 5-3 decision, capping Springs’ 69-8 YAIAA Division I win against the Squires (1-1).

“I knew I was down and needed to capitalize to try to get myself back in the match,” Fitzkee said. Fitzkee called the knee issue a “stinger that went away.”

Earlier in the match, Fitzkee had pulled off a roll to reverse Rooney and take the lead. But in the third period, the Bermudian Springs wrestler was slow to return to the center of the mat again and again.

Bermudian head coach Dave McCollum was worried about how the match would unfold. “I was concerned coming in because Delone was in a tournament last weekend and Rooney already had seven matches coming in here to our one,” McCollum said. “So I figured conditioning might come into play.”

However, Delone’s mat-time advantage wasn’t worth much, as the shorthanded Squires were forced to fight to stay alive.

headline: Bermudian Springs Wins Big at Dale Bock Memorial Tournament
summary: Bermudian Springs dominated the Dale Bock Memorial Tournament, going 5-0 on Thursday and securing a major victory over Chambersburg.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Fri, Dec 31, 2010 - Page 14

**Bermudian Springs Dominates at Dale Bock Memorial Tournament**

The first Dale Bock Memorial Tournament concluded Thursday evening at the Chambersburg Area High Field House, and most of the 10 participating teams had plenty of wrestling to talk about.

Bermudian Springs, coached by McCollum, took home the biggest hardware. The team went 5-0 on Thursday, including a decisive 52-21 win in a showdown against Chambersburg.

During the 125-pound bout, Bermudian Springs’ Tanner Coyle kept the pressure on the Chambersburg opponent, scoring 22 consecutive points. After the Trojans closed within 22-1, Bermudian scored the final 30 points to seal the victory.

Chambersburg High head coach Doug Rine reflected on the dual meet, noting, “It was intended to be a meat grinder. It was. We didn't know what to expect because we've never done this before. We felt going in we had to have everything fall our way to win the match, and we might need an upset, too. But then we lost the coin toss and it didn’t happen. We didn't get the breaks and we fell up four pins bouts.”

title: “Dave
headline: Eagles and Spartans Compete in Local Sports
summary: The article reports on recent athletic performances, detailing the wrestling success of the Eagles and the disappointing basketball loss suffered by the York Spartans.
cleaned_text:
Freshman Austin Jackson gave the Eagles much, much more. Jackson pinned Andrew Mellot in one minute exactly in the final bout, helping the Eagles to a 34-19 victory in York Springs. This win moved Jackson to 13-0 overall on the year.

The Eagles and Boiling Springs are regarded as two of the top Class AA programs in District 3. Bermudian Springs avenged a 38-19 loss to the Bubblers. The Eagles received pins from Brad Farley (112), Tanner Coyle (125), and Jackson. Matt Wolf also contributed a 10-2 major decision at 215 pounds.

title: weight classes, in-
headline: Bermudian Springs Wins Key Wrestling Match
summary: Bermudian Springs secured a victory in a wrestling match against the Nighthawks, highlighted by Farley's decisive win over Brown.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Fri, Jan 14, 2011

**Wrestling Match Highlights**
The Eagles defeated the Nighthawks in a match that featured multiple pins and technical falls. Bermudian Springs finished the match with three pins, two technical falls, and four wins by forfeit, indicating the contest was never in doubt from the start.

The marquee meeting of the night was between Farley and Brown. Farley had to wrestle six minutes and finally found a way to win the match. Coach Neal Dutterer noted that Farley had not had much competition all season, recalling that Farley had lost in the finals of the Carlisle tournament nearly a month prior.

Farley, now 20-1 on the season, stated, “I just wanted to get the first takedown and get ahead off the bat... But then, the second period, I got his wrist and could control him better than I did in the first.” Brown managed an escape at the end of the first period, but Farley was awarded a penalty point late, leading to a 3-1 lead in the third period and the final victory.

Other notable winners included Matt Wolf, Tanner Coyle, and Tyler Fitzkee, who all secured pins for the Eagles. Freshmen Austin (130) and Tristan Sponseller picked up technical falls.

title: g +
headline: Spring Grove Wrestlers Defeat Eagles in District 3 Quarterfinal
summary: Spring Grove wrestlers suffered a defeat to the Eagles in the District 3 Class AAA Team Tournament, while other local teams reported victories in both wrestling and basketball.
cleaned_text:
Spring Grove wrestlers faced a tough day, suffering a 35-19 defeat to the sixth-seeded Eagles in a District 3 Class AAA Team Tournament quarterfinal Thursday. "We told our kids coming in that those close matches were going to be an even day," said coach McCollum. "Every one of the kids came out focused and wrestled hard. We didn't have a bad match; even the kids that lost only gave up 3-pointers and wrestled real well."

In another wrestling event, the Eagles quickly trounced the Buffaloes (16-7), winning 10 of 14 bouts to rack up a 57-12 score and advancing to the semifinal round of the District 3 Class AA Team Tournament.

Meanwhile, at Bermudian Springs, the team kept the dual meet going for longer than an hour by defeating their Buffalo counterparts in eight bouts.

In other sports news, the Warriors dominated their opponents, scoring the first 17 points of the game by playing both a 131 and 221 zone press with equal effectiveness. Led by junior Brianna Thomas' seven first-quarter points, the Warriors didn't give up their first basket until Waynesboro’s Missy Ecker put back a rebound with six seconds remaining in the first quarter. There was no let-up in the second quarter as Gettysburg outscored the Maidens 123 to open up a 29-5 lead. Despite playing sparingly in the second quarter, Thomas still led all scorers with 12 points at the half.

headline: Bubblers Clinch Championship Title After Defeating Canners

The Evening Sun
Sun, Feb 06, 2011 - Page 15

Biglerville’s Jasper Hankey wrestles Boiling Springs’ Joey Byers during Saturday night's championship match.

Bermudian Springs finished third with a 45-20 shellacking of Wyomissing in the third-place match. The Eagles took nine of 14 bouts, but unfortunately for the Eagles, third wasn't their goal.

The YAIAA Division 1 champs’ title hopes were dashed after a loss to Boiling Springs. McCollum said of the early loss, “Probably one of the hardest I’ve had as a coach.”

The Eagles stormed into the tournament's final day with a spotless 24-0 record but would have to battle the Bubblers for a spot in the championship round.

At heavyweight, Unger clinched the championship for the Bubblers with a pin of Trevor Stover in 3:03. Before enacting vengeance on the Canners, the Bubblers served some measure of revenge.

In the 171-pound bout, David Baker found himself on the wrong end of the pier with just a second to go in the opening round, and, right after, Neeley Slonaker lost a match to advance.

The Evening Sun
Wed, Mar 09, 2011

Local athletes celebrated major achievements and farewells during recent high school competitions.

For two standout wrestlers, Coyle and Flohr, their final high school matches will largely be remembered not for the losses, but for the wins. Coyle’s career, in particular, will be remembered for his accomplishments, including a dual-meet record of 72-23 with 31 pins. Flohr, who is recognized as a trailblazer from Fairfield, set a high standard for future athletes, following in the footsteps of greats like Lauren Beckley and Andy Winebrenner.

In other athletic news, David Firestone defeated Dylan Killian in a wrestling match, winning by a score of 5-0.

Meanwhile, Tristan Sponseller showcased his humor during his interviews, joking about wearing his mother’s lipstick for his final bout in the PIAA Class AA Southeast Regional Tournament. His opponent, from Annville-Cleona, was reportedly taken aback by the joke.

The community also celebrated the coaching staff, including wrestling coach McCollum of Bermudian Springs, who continues to guide the next generation of athletes.

cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Fri, Sep 09, 2011

**Local Sports Hall of Fame Nominations**

Fourteen people have been nominated for induction into the Adams County Chapter of the Pennsylvania Sports Hall of Fame. The list of nominees, which includes four deceased finalists, will be sent to chapter members for a vote, which must be returned by September 26. Chapter members seeking ballots may email the chapter at adcohof@gmail.com or call chapter president Scott Meckley at 717-250-6442.

The ten living candidates are Duane Bastress, Maggie Borden, Steve Smith, and William C. Warren. They are joined by deceased nominees Clyde Cleveland, Kevin Gobrecht, Barbara Hibner, and Richard Lawhead.

Duane Bastress, a PIAA Wrestling Championships qualifier, was a two-time NCAA Division III wrestling champion at York College, where he had a 9-7-4 record. Maggie Borden was nominated for her achievements in both volleyball and track at Lock.

“Dave, [the shot] was just coming off making 10 pars in a row, and the situation that was arising required quite a few birdies,” said Schwartzel, one of eight legends who had a share of the lead at some point Sunday at Augusta National. “A lot of times you can push so hard, and a lot of times you end up making bogeys.” He hit a 6-iron for his second shot on the par-5 15th and was stunned to see it release over the back of the green. He nearly holed the chip, and then made the 8-foot birdie putt. “That was the start of his historic finish. If you miss that putt, the chances of making birdies on the other holes are slim,” Schwartzel said. “Because then you're pushing too hard. For me, that was the biggest birdie. I don’t want to say it became easy, but it snowballed from there.” The U.S. Open felt like one big snowball for Schwartzel with a 65 and [the chance to] close the rest.

**Wrestling Article**
Bermudian Springs defeated Littlestown, 61-12, to open its wrestling season.

By Daniel Paulling

The coach for Bermudian Springs noted that the win was a learning opportunity. He was able to see some of his younger wrestlers perform, such as freshmen Briton Shelton and Colton Dull. While he liked what he saw, he believes there is room for improvement. “We're trying to build some team chemistry and get kids to the proper weight classes,” McCollum said. “We were not at full strength tonight.”

This news might be encouraging for the coach across the mat, Littlestown’s Kerry Ferguson. Ferguson pointed out that the experience of five wrestlers making their varsity debut could be valuable against a team that has won two consecutive YAIAA Division III titles.

During the week, Bermudian Springs’ Jake Ford competed in a 126-pound fight against Littlestown’s Jordan Matthews. Ford finished 7-2. Ferguson also noted that Alec Warne and Tyler Fitzkee, who competed in the 170-pound weight class, are tough wrestlers.

headline: Green Knights and Big Spring Shine at Annual Wrestling Tournament
summary: Big Spring won the overall championship at the annual tournament, while Fairfield's Clay Kocsis and Green Knights' Sponseler also claimed individual titles.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
**Green Knights and Big Spring Shine at Annual Wrestling Tournament**

Sun, Dec 18, 2011 – The annual wrestling tournament concluded with Big Spring winning the overall championship with 2,305 points.

Fairfield’s Clay Kocsis secured his championship title in the 152-pound weight class, defeating Wyalusing Valley’s Joe Champuvier. This victory marks the Green Knights’ third championship since Kocsis’ father, Bruce Kocsis, left the team to coach Penn State Mont Alto.

Meanwhile, Sponseler, also of the Green Knights, won his championship bout after working out with former state champion Rick Paile. Sponseler moved up to the 182-pound weight class from 160 pounds. He noted that the weight gain proved beneficial for his four bouts during the two-day tournament. “I really think it does,” Sponseler said. “Last year, when I sucked down, I felt weaker. I was skinnier and didn't have as much strength. I could really feel during the tournament like I had my energy more through the match than before.”

Earlier in the tournament, Fitzkee barely edged Big Spring's Cody Seibert in the championship bout preceding Sponseler’s, winning in an 87-point decision. The score was just 84 midway through the third period. “That being in football shape and not wrestling shape—we were talking about earlier—played a part in that,” Fitzkee said, referring to his conditioning. “He wrestled a good match, but I kind of gassed out at the end, but had enough at the end to hold on.”

McCollum, the coach for Bermudian Springs, advised the team, “We need to work on tying those shoes tighter. That's for sure.”

Other notable results included Nick Mort (160) and Jacob Miller (220) finishing second in their respective weight classes. For the Thunderbolts, Tyler Green finished third in the 152-pound class, and Alec Warner finished fourth in the 171-pound class.

EJ Small, the next head coach for the Green Knights, expressed optimism for the team’s future. “I think a change in spirit,” he said. “I don't know how else to say it. I think they know they can do now what they did last year.” He advised Kocsis that while he wrestled well, the team still needs to work on conditioning to prepare for the postseason.

title: The Patriot-News
headline: Bermudian Springs Starts Dual-Meet Season Undefeated
summary: Bermudian Springs secured a strong dual-meet victory, maintaining an undefeated record despite losing several seniors to graduation.
cleaned_text:
**Bermudian Springs Remains Undefeated After Strong Dual-Meet Start**

By Jeremy Elliott

Boiling Springs, PA – With three seniors lost to graduation and six freshmen in this year’s lineup, there might have been outside concern about the success of the Bermudian Springs team. However, Head Coach Rod Wright was not worried about the group. He knew the freshmen were talented and that he had enough seniors as leaders in his room, and that confidence showed Monday night in a 29-26 measuring stick win against a fellow District 3-AA power.

“I still feel we have a lot of improvement ahead of us,” Wright said. “We're on the right track. The kids wrestled well overall, and a win is a win. Without a doubt, there are three solid teams in the district. We faceBiglerville coming up here.”

The team showed no rust, and each wrestler met their opponent head-on, winning enough points to secure a quality win to kick off the dual-meet season.

“We've had a lot of practice days,” Wright said. “That grinding takes a toll on the kids. We're not really in that dual-meet routine yet, but after tonight, I think we will get in that rhythm.”

The opening match saw Dave McCollum’s team defeat their opponent, with a wrestler responding to an opening match deficit with a reverse in the third period for a 6-5 decision. Brock Linebaugh followed up with a major over Calvin Barrick, giving the Bermudian Springs team a quick 7-0 lead.

That lead was erased in short order. In a sold-out Bubblers gym, 106-pound freshman Korbin Myers followed Tyler Wentzel’s 285-pound decision with a pin of Tyler Kunkle.

McCollum’s team was not the only one shuffling wrestlers. Wright went to his bench, and backup Tyler Kauffman (138) downed Briton Shelton, who was subbing for Jared Spahr, 2-0. Mark Lentz moved up to 145 and scored a takedown and near-fall points in overtime for a 9-4 decision over Colton Dull, giving the Bubblers a 23-17 lead.

After Noah Blasone’s 5-4 decision over John Vaughn at 152 made the score tight, T.J. Nickle took the mat and planted Ryan Starner in 1:22, giving the Bermudian Springs team a nine-point cushion with two matches to go.

“T.J.’s tough,” Wright said. “Last year was his first year, and it took all year to get him to wrestle, but he is doing a really good job. I’m definitely proud of him.”

Nickle added, “We are a pretty good dual-meet team with the freshmen. We have, and I knew it was a close lead to get six.”

**BY SAM BUTLER**
**SENTIRE CORRESPONDENT**

HERSHEY – Boiling Springs’ T.J. Nickel knew exactly what he needed to do with the machine loading 20+ for the third Class AA final against the Bermudian Springs Jaguars.

Coach Rochey Wright’s last instruction was to look back at the opponent, the Bermudian Springs Jaguars.

“I must get a major decision,” Nickel said regarding Wright’s last words of encouragement. “The match would be out of reach. I really wanted to join [Susan], but the make…”

The Wildcats fell to the Rams, but Mechanicsburg could not hold against the Bermudian Springs team, clinching their second consecutive District 3 Class AA Team Championship Saturday at the Spartan Center in the Capitol of Fitzgerald High School with a score of 12-9 in 18-18.

“I’ve never had experiences like this in the past two years. There are two of the most powerful teams in this list. This is just incredible,” said Bubber Junior Amateur Melden.

“Naturally, we pulled 195 to start,” the Bermudian Springs head coach said. “And that was the same weight he started off with on our opponent. In many ways, we were the best team out to get [Tristan] out to get.”

Gun hunters will be interested in the new products on display at Thompson Center Arms, which is introducing its new Dimension bolt action rifle—the first public viewing. The Dimension features an ability to shoot multiple calibers.

Show hours are until 5 p.m. today, and 10 a.m. to 7 p.m. Monday through Saturday; and 10 a.m. to 5 p.m. Sunday.

**Block Shoot**
The McSherrystown Fish and Game Association will hold a non-gauged block shoot on Sunday, February 12. Registration begins at noon, and the shoot starts at 1 p.m.

**Sporting Clays Shoot**
People who are handy with the shotgun might want to test their skill at a shoot planned by Pheasants Forever on Saturday, May 19, at the Central Penn Sporting Clays, 75 Quarry Road, Wellsville. Participants must leave at 8:30 a.m. The entry fee is $50 for 100 targets. A group of five shooters may register for the cost of four. A $10 wobble trap will be available. Children 16 years old and older may participate.

The first few bouts of the Bermudian Springs Eagles' team dual meet against defending Class AA state champion Bethlehem Catholic went fine, but the Eagles suffered a poor finish, losing 47-9 in the first round of the 2011-12 PIAA Class AA Team Wrestling Championship at Giant Center in Hershey.

The Eagles were scheduled to wrestle at 8 a.m. that day against Jefferson Morgan in the first round of the consolation bracket.

"The Eagles have to decide whether to send out sophomore Tristan Sponseller or Brock Linebaugh," said Bermudian Springs wrestling coach McCollum.

Sponseller pinned Paul Aaroe in the first match, giving the Eagles a 6-0 lead. They led 93 after heavyweight Jonathan Jimenez earned a 9-4 decision.

Bermudian Springs' Dalton Becker, middle, was seen sitting beside teammates moments after losing his 106-pound bout to Bethlehem Catholic’s Anthony Lombardo on Thursday at Giant Center in Hershey.

headline: Bermudian Springs Wrestlers Shine at District Tournament
summary: Bermudian Springs wrestlers, including sophomore Austin Jackson and senior Brad Farley, had successful performances at the district tournament, with Jackson pinning a former champion and Farley winning his district title.

Eastern York Wrestler's Success Highlighted

Eastern York came in sixth behind good wrestling performances. Buck Kauffman lost the 220-pound championship bout, 10-6, to Gardner. Bermudian Springs sophomore Austin Jackson wrestled well throughout the tournament, and his best match came when he pinned Boiling Springs’ Mark Lentz, who was a district champion last year.

McCollum had hoped to see Jackson improve his focus and aggressiveness. He noted that Jackson responded to the extra practice and focus this week.

Jackson's teammate, senior Brad Farley, repeated as district champion with a 17-point technical fall of Boiling Springs’ Grant Bond in 4:40 in the 120-pound bout. Farley seemed more aggressive on the mat, which he credited to harder work and gaining weight.

Other wrestlers had their undefeated seasons ended. Brown and Wolford lost the 126- and 195-pound championships, and Koesis placed third in the 152-pound bracket.

Bermudian Springs, Hanover, Fairfield, and Littlestown will have wrestlers competing in this week's PIAA Class AA Southeast Region Championships at Wilson High School in West Lawn.

Four wrestlers qualified for state championships: Logan Feeser and Chance Marsteller claimed district titles.
