headline: Local High Schools Prepare for Wrestling Season
summary: Local high school wrestling teams, including the Eagles, Green Ue, and Spartans, are preparing for the season with key returners and coaching changes.
cleaned_text:
The York Dispatch
Thursday, December 6, 2012

**Division II Wrestling Season Previews**

The local high school wrestling scene is gearing up for the season, with several teams looking to build on previous years' success.

In Division II, the Eagles are expected to be the favorite, despite losing five talented wrestlers. The team boasts nine returning starters, eight of whom were District 3-AA qualifiers last season. The most accomplished of this group are Jackson, Sponseller, and Linebaugh. Last season, Sponseller was the 170-pound District 3-AA champion, while Jackson (132 pounds) took second, and Linebaugh also achieved notable success.

Eastern will also face a challenging season, having compiled only one win in all of the previous six seasons. The Green Ue brings back eight starters, four of whom were district qualifiers a year ago.

Meanwhile, York Tech’s Spartans, coached by second-year coach Mike Diliberti, finished 6-14 overall last year, just one win shy of the program's single-season record. The Spartans went 1-7 in Division II last year, but Diliberti is confident his squad can improve. York Tech brings back eight starters, including four seniors. The most accomplished of this group is Gary Hake (132 pounds), who ended last year one win shy of qualifying for the district tournament.

In other local news, Brad Brosius enters his first year as head coach for Schell, following five years coaching West York's junior high program.

headline: Middletown Blue Raiders Defeat Hershey Trojans in Wrestling Showdown
summary: The Middletown Blue Raiders defeated the Hershey Trojans 35-25 in a wrestling match, securing key victories that boosted their Class AAA power rankings.
cleaned_text:
Middletown Blue Raiders Defeat Hershey Trojans in Wrestling Showdown

The Blue Raiders secured a decisive 35-25 victory over the Hershey Trojans in a highly competitive wrestling match. The victory was fueled by key wins from Bobby Johnson, Seth Babil, and Bryce Killian, who helped put away the Trojans.

During the match, Middletown took an early lead, with the Bubblers maintaining a strong record. The action continued with several key victories: Bobby Johnson defeated Michael Dexheimer at 132 pounds, turning a reversal in the third period for a 4-1 victory. Seth Babil held on for a 4-3 decision over Andrew Schwenk at 138 pounds. Furthermore, Bryce Killian bumped up two weight classes and teched Andrew Boyer at 145 pounds, securing a much-needed Class AAA power-rankings victory for the Blue Raiders.

Blue Raiders head coach Mike Nauman stated that the focus was less on district standings and more on the team's resilience, noting, "If we make districts and get a match or two, that is a bonus for us."

title: The Patriot-News
headline: PIAA Tournament Structure Needs Improvement
summary: The article argues that the PIAA wrestling tournament structure is flawed and needs expansion to ensure that the best teams have a fair chance to compete.
cleaned_text:
The Patriot-News
Sun, Jan 27, 2013

...tion and merit.

The same could be said for District 11 powers: Easton, Nazareth, and Parkland. These are three programs that need no Class AAA introduction.

Five of the six teams are ranked in the top 10 in the state. And yet, two of these squads aren’t good enough to be invited to the PIAA Team Championship party.

We understand that it’s not easy governing 1,417 schools and holding championships for 24 sports in a year. But that doesn’t mean the February trip to Giant Center to crown a dual-meet champion can’t be improved.

We've heard the rhetoric from the PIAA brass: “We want the best, most competitive tournament we can have.” Or something along those lines. But as it stands now, it isn’t the best. Not with the teams.

The 20-team bracket in Bermudian Springs or Biglerville will be sitting at home while a less qualified squad from another region will reach Hershey. That’s unacceptable, if excellence is what you are trying to achieve.

“We are traditionally tough programs that are not getting what they deserve,” stated one source.

“It’s terrible that we don't get the best 20 teams in the state. There are some district champs that we beat by 40 and 50 points.”

We have a solution.

Each bracket has four preliminary-round matches on February 4, with the winners earning a trip to the first round at Giant Center. What does that do to districts with holes?

“Do they get a fourth qualifier? It becomes a judgment call.”

To PIAA’s credit, it has made some adjustments. District 1 is known for being a state power, but according to its Class AAA representation, it is allotted only one state entry. The steering committee recognized this flaw and added a spot for the district runner-up.

It was the right move, but still, state officials need to do more.

We understand that it would be difficult to pick two at-large bids in both classes when you are dealing with that many teams across the state. But just because something is difficult doesn’t mean you ignore the shortcomings of the current practice.

The best product isn’t on the mat. And in this case, the easiest thing to take is the right one. PIAA could use a committee to expand the tournament.

“The problem with rankings is that they are subjective,” Lombard noted. “A consideration could be given to expanding the bracket to more teams. That in itself would add a full round of wrestling. That is something for the committee to review.”

Having a full round on February 4 in a pseudo-preliminary round might be the answer. Under such a scenario, all six of these deserving teams would get an opportunity to show their skills.

headline: Local Wrestling Figure Trimmer Remembered After Fatal Crash
summary: Local wrestling official and community figure Trimmer passed away after a car accident, leaving a void in the District 3 wrestling community.
cleaned_text:
Trimmer, a well-known figure in District 3, passed away at Bucktail Medical Center in Renovo on Saturday night following a two-car crash. The incident occurred when another vehicle swerved in front of his truck.

Trimmer had a deep love for the sport of wrestling, which guided him throughout his life. He began his career in the sport and went on to become a respected official.

“It’s devastating to District 3 when you lose a guy like Rick that you know is always going to give his best,” said McCollum.

McCollum recalled Trimmer’s competitive spirit, noting, “Back then, we competed in the Blue Mountain League, and it was a tough league. Rick was very tough, hard-nosed, a hard worker, and also gave 100 percent. Win or lose, he handled it the same. He was level-headed, and when he came off the mat, he knew he gave it his all.”

That dedication carried over into his time as an official. Trimmer was decisive but also a good communicator to both coaches and wrestlers, and he never made the match about himself.

“He was a lot of fun,” McCollum said. “He was the same way as an official as he was as a wrestler. He was always a joy to be around, an upbeat guy, and it showed in his demeanor. He was easy-going in the heat of the moment and capable of slowing down the action.”

title: 1 of 8 matches
headline: Jackson Wins Hard-Fought Bout, Leading Eagles to 62-11 Victory
summary: Bermudian Springs wrestlers secured a 62-11 victory over Delone Catholic, highlighted by Austin Jackson's hard-fought 9-7 win against Brian Shermeyer.
cleaned_text:
Bermudian Springs' Austin Jackson set the tone for the Eagles in his 188-pound bout on Wednesday night. In the third bout of the night, Jackson prevailed in a hard-fought battle against Delone Catholic's Brian Shermeyer, a match that came down to the final seconds.

Jackson and the Eagles won the first five bouts en route to a 62-11 victory over Delone Catholic.

"I'm proud of our team," said Bermudian Springs head coach Dave McCollum. "We wanted to come in and wrestle well and wrestle hard. It was our second league match, so we wanted to continue to build on our prior success at the Carlisle tournament. We had a great tournament there, and we didn't want to take a step back here."

McCollum continued, "I knew Delone was going to put a few decent wrestlers on the mat, and they definitely did that."

The match against Shermeyer was intense. Jackson scored two points on a reversal and then scored another three on a near fall. It appeared he had Shermeyer for the pin, but could not force both of his shoulders to the mat. Despite trailing 5-0 heading into the third period, Shermeyer came out on a mission. He quickly earned three points on a near fall of his own. After allowing Jackson to get a reversal and extending the lead to 7-3, Shermeyer scored a reversal of his own.

With a two-point lead, Jackson escaped Shermeyer. With just under a minute remaining in the third period, Shermeyer cut the lead to one with a takedown of Jackson. But that was as close as he would get, as Jackson was able to hold on for a 9-7 win.

"Going throughout the first period, I really didn't know who the kid was at first," Jackson said. "Going 0-0 into the second surprised me a little bit."

Jackson attributed his struggle to a lack of conditioning. In order to get to the 138-pound weight class, he had to drop 18 pounds. He regularly goes back up to 150 on the weekends and has to drop back down during the week.

headline: Bermudian Springs Secures YAIAA Division III Title
summary: Bermudian Springs secured the YAIAA Division III title after a decisive victory over Biglerville, despite facing intense competition throughout the season.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Sun, Jan 25, 2015

In advance of Thursday’s showdown with Biglerville, Bermudian Springs had already wrapped up a share of its ninth division title in the last 10 years. Still, the 52-14 win over Biglerville meant more. Bermudian Springs wanted the YAIAA Division III title outright.

“We did have a lock as far as us getting a share, but our kids wanted so much,” said a coach. “They bounced back from that loss last week at Boiling Springs to win the outright league title. They deserved it. I would have been very upset if we were not outright champs.”

The Eagles bounced back in a big way, outscoring the Canners 65-19, individually while building a 46-0 lead.

Sam McCollum, a wrestler, stated, “I'm trying to get to 100 wins, and I’m at 84. We will just have to get to team states, and that’s OK. I want to get some team medals too.”

McCollum discussed strategy, noting, “Any time you score first, you can kind of sit on the lead. As long as you don’t let him score on that, you win. He might start to do something a little out of his head.”

During the match, McCollum waited out a pensive first period before attacking and scoring a takedown with 23 seconds left in the first period. Mentzer escaped early in the second, and McCollum was denied a takedown on the edge of the mat as the two tumbled out of bounds with 21 seconds left in that period.

Other highlights included Brady Linebaugh earning a pin at 285 with a far-side cradle, and Victor Lopez following up at 106 with an arm bar that led to a pin against Will Davis.

Biglerville won three of the last four matches, with Brock Asper pinning Ben Kgenreider and Nate Newberry picking up a 15-0 tech fall at 152 over Sid Stauffer.

Biglerville head coach Nate Becker commented, “They did a nice job. Sometimes it’s tough when we're that far behind to come out and wrestle intense.”

Bermudian Springs Coach McCollum Continues Winning Streak

Bermudian Springs wrestling coach Dave McCollum recently earned his 500th victory. His love of wrestling is evident in his continued success, joining an elite group of high school wrestlers.

During the recent District 3 Sectionals, McCollum's team competed in the individual wrestling postseason. The event schedule included the individual wrestling postseason with team events starting at 9:00 a.m., followed by a second session at 7:00 a.m.

McCollum, who coached the team to a 140-pound state championship match victory in 2006, noted that many students entering high school already have a foundational understanding of the sport. He stated, "When we get kids into high school they are ready through the technique of it and have part of the sport already. We just have to clean up on the high school wrestlers."

cleaned_text:
**PIAA Wrestling: Bermudian Springs Trio Aims for State Medal Sweep**

By Zach Miller

The Bermudian Springs wrestling team is aiming to accomplish something unprecedented at the upcoming state championships. Coach Sam McCollum, who has spent 38 years leading the program, has won 500 dual meets and coached three different state champions, but he has never had a team earn two gold medals at one state championship meet. This year’s squad, featuring seniors Sam McCollum, Colton Dull, and Briton Shelton, is poised to challenge that record.

The three seniors enter the weekend as district and regional champions with a combined record of 11-4. All three had medaled at Giant Center last year, with Shelton taking fourth at 160 pounds, Dull taking seventh at 182 pounds, and McCollum taking eighth at 220 pounds.

Sam McCollum recently secured points against Northern Lebanon's Matt Vines during their 220-pound match at the District 3 championships, entering the state tournament with an undefeated record.

The "big three" seniors enter the tournament with a "gold medal or bust" attitude, full of confidence after going two months or more without a loss. Shelton hasn't lost a match, and the team is highly motivated.

title: © SPORTS
cleaned_text:
**Local Athletes Shine in Football, Basketball, Cheerleading, and Wrestling**

**Penn State Recruiting and Football**
Coach Jerry Gilsky first noticed Connor McGovern as a sixth grader, recognizing his potential as a "giant walking around" the middle school halls. By his junior year at Lake-Lehman High, McGovern’s size and strength had overpowered all opponents. Now, McGovern is transitioning from one of Pennsylvania’s top high school blockers—and the nation’s No. 2-ranked center—to learning the basics of anchoring the Penn State offensive line. He has a significant head start, having graduated from high school in December and being eligible for all team workouts. Despite some late recruiting struggles, Penn State has maintained the commitment of Miles Sanders, arguably the nation’s top high school running back.

**Boys’ Basketball**
Biglerville’s Noah Ayers burst onto the local high school basketball scene two years ago as a freshman, averaging 9.9 points per game. He improved to 18.1 points per game as a sophomore and upped his average again to 18.4 points per game this season. During a game at Bermudian Springs, Ayers became the seventh boy in school history to reach the 1,000-point plateau for his career, ending the game with 12 points and currently holding 1,009 points. Ayers noted that he works hard on his shooting, ball handling, and playing off the ball. This milestone follows Brock Grim, who reached 1,633 career points in 2006.

**Cheerleading**
The New Oxford cheerleading team, the Colonials, won their fourth consecutive PIAA competitive spirit championship at Hershey’s Giant Center. Entering the championships as three-time defending champions, the Colonials won the co-ed division with a score of 81.33, edging Downingtown West by 1.93 points. This marked New Oxford’s third straight co-ed division title, following a medium division state championship in 2013. Head coach Chris Topper noted that the 25-member squad was a mix of talent, and the team was focused on maintaining its championship status.

**Wrestling**
At the recent meet, the action was intense. In one match, the outcome was decided by a single point. In another, the bout was a grueling affair that lasted the full duration. The wrestlers showed incredible grit and determination throughout the day.

headline: Boiling Springs Best Bermudian in Districts
summary: Bermudian Springs, after losing to Boiling Springs in the District 3 Class AA team tournament, needs a victory in the third-place match to advance to the state tournament.
cleaned_text:
Boiling Springs Best Bermudian in Districts

BOILING SPRINGS – It is difficult to fathom now, but Bermudian Springs could be Boiling Springs’ biggest fans. After beating Newport 39-30, but losing to Boiling Springs 45-28 in the District 3 Class AA team tournament, the Eagles will need a win in Saturday’s third-place match and some help if they are to reach state this season.

“Our goal was to get here and get through Newport and have another shot to wrestle next weekend,” said a coach.

The team’s success in the match against Newport was helped by pins from Austin Claubaugh (126 pounds), Darren Beall (145), Noah Fleshman (182), and Landon West (106).

“You've got to give Boiling Springs credit,” McCollum said. “They came out very aggressively and wrestled a great match overall. Having Wetzel back helps them a lot, and losing the flip-flop they could have avoided out there wrestling.”

McCollum also praised Claubaugh, stating, “He just doesn’t get flustered; he doesn’t lose his composure.”

Bermudian Springs will face Hamburg in Saturday’s third-place match at Milton Hershey School. Meanwhile, Boiling Springs will face Northern Lebanon in the district championship.

title: “Dave
headline: Bermudian Springs Secures Dual Meet Victory Despite Injuries
summary: Bermudian Springs won a crucial third-place match at the District 3 Class AA team tournament, securing a vital victory despite missing key wrestlers.
cleaned_text:
The injury bug bit Bermudian Springs again, but it did not prevent the team from securing another dual meet victory.

The Eagles, who have wrestled without their starting 113- and 182-pounders all season, showed up to the District 3 Class AA team tournament third-place match on Saturday. Despite having Isaiah Fannin, a 195-pounder, on the bench, the team displayed remarkable resilience.

“Thinking about the way things are going, these guys have been phenomenal,” said Eagles coach McCollum. “It shows a lot of character. We have great kids; they’re hard workers. They believe in the program, and I’m just really, really proud of them.”

Noah Fleshman (170 pounds) started the day off with a pin, but Bermudian Springs appeared in strong form throughout the match. Luckenbaugh battled from behind to pick up a pin, a feat he had previously earned against Boiling Springs in a second overtime period.

At one point, Bermudian Springs trailed the competition, 27-24, with four matches remaining. However, decisive matches by Jared Miller (138 pounds) and Darren Beall (145 pounds) pushed the Eagles back into the lead. The victory was sealed by a technical edge from Ashton West (152 pounds) with one match left.

The victory significantly improved Bermudian Springs' chances of qualifying for the state tournament.

title: Sun.com THE EVENING SUN
headline: Support System Helps Austin Recover After Family Emergency
summary: After Austin experienced a family emergency involving his mother, Karen Clabaugh, his teammates and friends rallied to support him and help him focus on his wrestling career.
cleaned_text:
Karen Clabaugh logged thousands of miles over the years driving Austin to wrestling practices, camps, and tournaments. She drove him to meets all across Pennsylvania, and even to tournaments as far away as Virginia Beach, Virginia, and Orlando, Florida.

“She would never do anything to hold me back from,” Austin recalled. “As much as I was willing to admit myself, she was always there. You have to stay at your best all the time.”

The support system was tested when Austin walked into Coach McCollum’s office on the morning of Saturday, December 5, and broke into tears. What had started as a normal morning before a tournament had taken a frightening turn when Austin saw Karen collapse in their home. As he and his father tried to help her, a nurse quickly arrived, supporting him and giving him direction.

Bermudian Springs senior Ashton West, a close friend and practice partner of Clabaugh’s since elementary school, joined Austin at the hospital that night. When Austin said he wanted to do something with the team on Sunday, Ashton made sure it happened. He gathered Austin’s teammates, coaches, and other good friends from the school to meet for a meal at Round the Clock Diner in York. Some former teammates also showed up to support him.

“We were very much looking forward to the [tournament],” McCollum said, referring to the recent bout. “We expected Austin to be pretty tough this year. He had a great season last year, qualifying for states was huge, and we were hoping that this year he'd come in a little bit more focused and ready to take the next step. Obviously, we’re hoping to get back to states and get on the podium.”

Clabaugh noted that Austin’s improved technique and aggressiveness from the neutral position are the biggest differences in his wrestling since last season, allowing him to take his opponents down more often to get on top.

title: © SPORTS
cleaned_text:
**© SPORTS**

**Sunday, January 22, 2017**

**AFC CHAMPIONSHIP GAME: PATRIOTS TO HOST STEELERS**
*Teams share mutual respect preparing to face off*
KYLE HIGHTOWER, ASSOCIATED PRESS

FOXBOROUGH, Mass. - There are few things that Bill Belichick respects more than history in the NFL. Few teams elicit as much praise from the Patriots coach as the Pittsburgh Steelers. Part of it is the reverence that Belichick holds for the way Steelers founder Art Rooney and his family has operated a franchise that’s remained among the league's best, winning multiple Super Bowl titles more than 30 years apart. It is a model emulated when Robert Kraft bought the Patriots in 1994 and six years later hired Belichick, who has nurtured a “Patriots Way” that has helped bring four Lombardi trophies to New England.

Those histories will collide when two of the most successful programs of the 2000s meet in Sunday’s AFC championship game.

“We're playing in the AFC championship game. If you can’t get motivated to play in this game, then you're in the wrong sport."
— MATT SLATER, NEW ENGLAND PATRIOTS

“They've been tough to deal with going all the way back to coach (Chuck) Noll in the ’70s,” Belichick said. “They were pretty consistently tough to deal with through that entire period of time, which has been all of my years in the league.”

Pittsburgh or New England has been a part of nine of the 17 Super Bowls since 2000, winning six championships between them.

headline: Eagles Advance to 2A Championship After Key Victories
summary: The Eagles secured their spot in the 2A championship after key victories, including a pinning effort by Bermudian Springs' Ashton West and a win by Trace Grim.

The Eagles (21-1) advanced to Saturday morning’s 2A championship, where they will face Bishop McDevitt, the 43-27 winner, over second-seeded Upper Dauphin Area. Northern Lebanon will meet UDA Saturday, also at 10:30 at the Spartan Center, for third place and a berth in the PLAA team tournament.

In a notable matchup, Northern Lebanon surprised the Eagles by sending Luis Negrerios out to face Nate McCollum, matching Luke Funck (31-3) on Noah Fleshman (28-3) at 195. Negrerios majored McCollum, getting a one-count on a tilt early in the third period.

The championship action saw Northern Lebanon's Kyler Anspach outlast Korey Smith, 9-8, at 106. The Eagles’ Landon West stopped Brandon Breidegan, 6-2, at 113. Colin Leonard flattened Tyler Rawser in 50 seconds at 120.

In the final bouts, the Vikings fell short. Bermudian Springs' Ashton West pinned Kai Whitman at 152, and Trace Grim held Blaise Bressler to a 6-2 victory. These results secured the Eagles' spot in Saturday's championship.

Dear Class I,

We have decided to double down on the option to play for a regular season.

Clayton McCollum, a three-time TAIA qualifier, controlled his opponent for 147 of 179 points. "I took joy to give myself a down," said McCollum. "I was able to ride him down and dominated pretty well through the second period, so I was able to work my way in."

title: “Dave
headline: Wrestling Community Anticipates Positive Changes Following Rule and Uniform Updates
summary: The wrestling community is discussing new rules and the potential return of two-piece uniforms, with coaches generally anticipating positive changes despite concerns over tradition and funding.
cleaned_text:
Spring Grove junior wrestler Dalton Rohrbaugh stated that increased participation is possible, noting that many heavyweights are self-conscious about their appearance. He believes that more kids coming out to wrestle will be positive.

Rohrbaugh said he would be open to trying the new uniform, but as a dedicated wrestler, the rule change doesn't matter much to him. He added that he has heard from former high school wrestlers who are against the change because it breaks tradition.

The Bermudian Springs head coach agreed there was no problem with the change but expressed doubts about whether it would lead to a significant increase in participation. He added that wrestlers used to wear two-piece uniforms during club matches a decade ago, but stopped because they made the wrestlers feel overheated.

While the two-piece uniform is "cool-looking," the material will be the same, and if some people are worried about how they look in a singlet, that won't change.

The article notes that some teams may have to wait a few years to get two-piece uniforms or look for other ways to raise the money. Brenner said he expected Hanover to wait a few years before they could get them, while other schools such as Northern Lebanon will consider using their booster club to make the purchase.

However long it takes for two-piece uniforms to become consistently worn throughout District 3, many in the wrestling community believe only positive things will come from the rule change.

“The singlet is just an excuse (not to wrestle), but this takes the biggest excuse off the table, and anything that gets you a few more numbers is positive,” Northern Lebanon coach Rusty Wallace said. “I think we’re going to see a big impact at the middle school level.”

In additional wrestling news, the NFSHSA has approved a number of other new rules, the most notable of which is that wrestlers can now earn near-falls and pins even if their opponents’ shoulders are out-of-bounds. The intention of this change is to make the sport more offense-oriented.

title: The Daily News
headline: Uniform Debate Sparks Hope for Wrestling Participation in Pennsylvania
summary: Amid struggles with low participation, wrestling coaches and athletes are debating whether adopting two-piece uniforms could boost interest in the sport, particularly among younger and female wrestlers.
cleaned_text:
Considering Pennsylvania has long been a hotbed for wrestling, many programs are still struggling to get wrestlers. Just this past season in the York-Adams league, numerous schools including Hanover, Littlestown, Fairfield, and even Red Lion weren’t able to put out full lineups for dual matches.

There are a number of reasons for this. Many athletes now specialize in one sport, and it’s difficult for smaller schools to fill rosters in plenty of sports. However, coaches and wrestlers believe that the singlet is one of the factors.

“Put it this way, am I considering new uniforms with the compression shirt and shorts? Yes, I am,” said Chambersburg head coach Matt Mentzer. “I think that will allow us to recruit some of these kids if that’s their issue with wrestling. It will help. For communities that aren’t deep in wrestling support and culture and history, it will be a good thing.”

Spring Grove’s Dalton Rohrbaugh, a junior 113-pounder, added, “Absolutely, it could increase participation. There are a lot of heavyweights who are self-conscious about the way they look in it, going to be positive if more [people] come out.”

Rohrbaugh said he’d be open to trying the new uniform, but that as a dedicated wrestler, the rule doesn’t matter much to him. He added that he’s heard from former high school wrestlers who are against it because it breaks tradition.

The Bermudian Springs head coach, McCollum, expressed no problem with the two-piece uniform, but said he has his doubts about whether it will lead to a significant increase in participation. He added that Bermudian wrestlers used to wear two-piece uniforms during club matches a decade ago, but stopped because they made the wrestlers feel overheated.

While most coaches agree that serious high school wrestlers will participate no matter the uniform, the new rule could influence middle school wrestlers to try the sport at a younger age. The two-piece uniforms could also cut back on the spread of infections that have 0% of a wrestler’s body covered.

There has also been talk about whether the new rule could influence more females to try wrestling. But since wrestlers are currently allowed to wear shirts under their singlets, some doubt whether there will be any change in that area. According to the 2015-16 data, five percent of wrestlers across the country are female.

D is for the District 3 playoffs.
There's no more exciting (and ex-
hausting) time of year for sports
writers than the postseason. From
soccer to basketball to softball, the
Hanover-Adams area produced
some great moments in the District
3 playoffs, including four district
champs.

E is for Eckenrode, as in Delone
Catholic girls’ basketball coach
Gerry Eckenrode. The 18-year vet-
eran reached 400 career wins this
season and guided a young and in-
jury-riddled Squirettes team to a
District 3 championship game and
the state playoffs. He also remains
one of the league’s most honest and
entertaining interviews.

F is for Friday nights. As a high
school sports reporter, there is no
greater thrill than the one you get
from covering football on a Friday.
Those nights are busy and stressful,
but almost always memorable and
fun.

G is for the Green Knights of
Fairfield. Seriously, what a year it
was for the smallest school in the
YAIAA. The girls’ soccer team pro-
duced a season for the ages, piling
up an insane amount of goals on its
way to the first team state champi-
onship in school history. But that
was just the beginning. Fairfield
also won district titles in girls’ track
and field and softball. If you didn’t
pay attention to Fairfield before
this year, you probably do now.

H is for holding the ball, some-
thing the Biglerville boys’ basket-
ball team did for the entire third
quarter in a game against Big
Spring. The unorthodox plan
worked, as the Canners ended up
winning, 53-42. I wonder if we'll
ever see that again.

| is for the inflatable bounce
house and obstacle course New Ox-
ford set up before its home football
game against Delone Catholic on
Sept. 15. Sadly, I never got a turn on
either.

Joking aside, this was part of a
tailgating event New Oxford set up
to encourage community involve-
ment in its athletics programs. It
was a great idea, and I'd love to see
more schools do something similar.

Jis for Jon DeFoe. The Bermudi-
an Springs football coach finished
his 18th season leading the Eagles
this fall, making him the league’s
longest-tenured coach in his sport.
In this day and age, you don’t see
many coaches lasting that long at
one place. Bermudian also has the
YAIAA's longest-tenured boys’ bas-
ketball coach in Tom Flaherty and
wrestling coach in Dave McCollum.

K is for Kaelyn Long. The New
Oxford senior field hockey player
was dazzling to watch, consistently
beating double teams to both score
and set up teammates. The Buck-
nell field hockey commit was also
stellar on the basketball court.

L is for Littlestown. This was
quite a year for Littlestown, which
enjoyed a bounce-back season in
football and also sent its field hock-
ey, boys’ basketball, softball and
boys’ tennis teams to the District 3
playoffs. Individual athletes like
wrestler Carl Harris and sprinter
Kristin Langrill had great seasons
for the Bolts as well.

credit where credit is due, andsome
guys in this area can grow impres-
sive facial hair above their lips. I
think the award for Mustache of the
Year is a tie between South Western
wrestler Chase Mowery and Spring
Grove wrestler Jared Barley.

N is for Noah Fleshman. In my
view, no local athlete was more en-
tertaining than the Bermudian
Springs senior wrestler. Fleshman
ate snack cakes before matches,
pounded his chest after wins and
earned a district title at 195 pounds.
He was also good for a few laughs
every interview. And he loves his
fast food.

0 is for offensive lineman. The
big men in the trenches don’t get
much publicity, but you can’t win
games without them. This area had
some great ones this season, includ-
ing Delone’s Bryce Perkins, Spring
Grove’s Owen Jacobs and Littles-
town’s Matthew Becker.

Pis for Phomma Phanhthy. When
he wasn’t busy dancing, Phanhthy
was leading Fairfield’s girls’ soccer
team and girls’ track and field team
to previously unseen heights. The
charismatic coach will have plenty
of talent to work with next year, too.

Q is for quarterbacks. This area
has some good young signal callers,
including Bermudian sophomore
Chase Dull, Hanover junior Cam
Mumma and Fairfield junior Dar-
ian Mort. It’s going to be fun watch-
ing these guys play this fall.

R is for rivalries. It doesn’t mat-
ter the sport, there’s nothing better
than an intense rivalry game where
fans from both teams pack the
building. My two favorites? South
Western-Spring Grove wrestling
and Bermudian Springs-Boiling
Springs wrestling Great atmos-
phere and competition for both.

S is for Seidenstricker, as in re-
cently retired South Western athlet-
ic director Don Seidenstricker. I
wasn't working at GameTimePA
when he was coaching the Mus-
tangs football team, but it wasn’t
long after I started here that I
learned about his program's domi-
nance. I attended a YAIAA football
coaches meeting that Don spoke at
last spring, and it was clear from
the moment he stood up that every
person in the room revered him.
Also, there were many times this
past year when I had to call him for
stories, and he was always willing to
help. I’m sure I’m not alone in wish-
ing him an enjoyable retirement.

T is for track and field. Look,
postseason track meets are some of

the longest and most stressful days
we have as reporters. The meets
last all day, there's always five
things happening at once and the wi-
fi is never good. Still, those week-
ends are some of my favorites be-
cause our local athletes provide so
many good stories. From New Ox-
ford’s Madi Smith to Spring Grove’s
Nathalie Elliott to Bermudian’s Tre-
vor Grim, there was plenty to write
about this spring.

U is for underclassmen. The
Hanover-Adams area has a lot of
good ones, and I'm looking forward
to watching them grow next season.
Keep an eye on Fairfield soccer
players Annabel Anderson and No-
ra Clarke, and Delone Catholic
three-sport athlete Maddie Cla-
baugh.

V is for victories, which are al-
ways more fun to write about than
the losses.

W is for wrestling. Ditto what I
wrote about track and field. Post-
season wrestling provides long
hours and puts lots of miles on your
car but is still worth it. Even if
you're like me and you’ve never
wrestled in your life, it’s impossible
not to admire the commitment
these kids have for their sport.

X is for...well X is a hard one. I'll
give it to the X factors, those kids
who mean so much to their teams.
From Eli Brooks in basketball to
Hannah Logue in soccer, this area
had some good ones this year.

Y is for the YAIAA tournaments.
While these games don’t have any
effect on the District 3 playoffs,
they mean a lot to the teams in-
volved and generate a ton of inter-
est. Shout-out to the Gettysburg
boys’ soccer team, which won its
second-straight league title this
past fall.

Z is for Zurich Storm. The New
Oxford wrestler capped off his im-
pressive career with a District 3 ti-
tle and his fourth PIAA champion-
ships appearance. He also has one
of the coolest names in the YAIAA.
Storm will be continuing his career
at North Carolina State.

That's it, folks. I hope you all en-
joyed this school year as much as I
did. Good luck to all the graduating
seniors, and I look forward to seeing
the rest of the Hanover-Adams ath-
letes back on the field in the fall.

sports for GameTimePA.com.

title: “Dave h
headline: Dull Returns to the Mat as Bermudian Springs Focuses on Team Success
summary: After a strong start to the season, Dull is back on the wrestling mat, and the Bermudian Springs team, led by coach McCollum, is focused on maintaining team balance and achieving state qualification.
cleaned_text:
Dull is back on the wrestling mat and off to a good start.

“It’s awesome,” Dull said of being back with the team. “I'm just trying to wrestle exactly like I've been wrestling during my middle school years.”

So far this season, Dull is 4-2 after opening the season at the Cumberland Valley tournament. His most recent win came on December 13, with a second-period pin against Littlestown’s Cole Minovich in the 220-pound bout, helping the Eagles beat the Bolts, 69-6.

“We're taking it slow with him,” McCollum said. “Really, he probably should be at 195 pounds right now. He’s really sort of wrestling out of his weight class.” McCollum also added that they are trying to get him back into the mindset, but Dull feels like he is already there.

“It all came back to me within the first week,” Dull said. “I kind of remember everything once I started doing it again.”

For Dull, the focus in the first tournament and the first dual was all about the team. “I just want the team to do good,” Dull said. “I want to go to states with the team.”

Last season, the Eagles went 21-3. They are returning a solid lineup, including five district qualifiers, two regional qualifiers, nine returning lettermen, and Landon West, a two-time state qualifier.

“Right now, we're right where we want,” McCollum added. “If we stay on our game plan, we'll see a lot better Dull at the end of the season.”

McCollum expressed happiness with Dull’s performance and high hopes for the team's future advancements. “We have good team balance,” McCollum said. “We wrestled really hard at Cumberland Valley last weekend (Dec. 8-9). You know, we got 10 guys through to the second day, which I thought was great.”

title: IAA have a wrestling tournament?
headline: Scheduling Conflicts Challenge Local Wrestling Tournament Plans
summary: Scheduling conflicts, particularly during the final weekend of January, make it difficult for local wrestling leagues to hold tournaments due to existing playoff and league championship commitments.
cleaned_text:
Scheduling Conflicts Challenge Local Wrestling Tournament Plans

The scheduling of local wrestling tournaments presents a significant challenge. The postseason, which typically occurs in late January, is already a highly congested week for training and competition. This period is followed by league invitations and subsequent competitions, leaving very little time for additional training environments or local tournaments to take place.

"We used to do a league tournament when we were in the Mid-Penn, and we always looked forward to competing against the big schools," said Bermudian Springs coach Dave McCollum. "It was our only opportunity to go in there and compete with a competition, and we did really well because our guys looked well. Other leagues, including Lancaster Lebanon and Berks County, run wrestling tournaments every year."

The timing of these events is difficult to manage. While some tournaments take place after the regular season but before the District 3 playoffs, this still leaves only one available slot during the final weekend of January. Furthermore, the Lancaster Lebanon League, which has existed since 1995, often holds its championship that same weekend, creating scheduling adjustments, especially since many YAIA teams hold final data meets during that time.

title: ne District 3 Section 4 Class 2A
headline: Bermudian Springs Wins Team Competition at Wrestling Tournament
summary: Bermudian Springs won the team competition at the high school wrestling tournament, with seniors Landon West and Darren Beall earning significant victories and advancing to the District 3 tournament.
cleaned_text:
High School Wrestling Highlights at Bermudian Springs

High school wrestling continues to expose the high and low emotions of the sport. The host Eagles at Bermudian Springs had the most to celebrate, sending all but two wrestlers to the District 3 tournament. Landon West and Darren Beall (152) were highlighted for earning 100th wins during the event. Bermudian Springs also won the team competition.

Darren Beall finished the day taking second place in the 120-pound bracket, losing a tight 1-0 decision to Biglerville’s Josh Tuckey in the championship. Beall entered his 152-pound quarterfinal match with 99 career wins. After getting caught in a cradle by Kennard-Dale’s Jacob Rose, he had to put off getting his 100th win until the consolation semifinals. Nevertheless, he prevailed to beat Hanover’s Jerod Flinchbaugh via pin, and wrestled back to take third place.

“I thought we had a heck of a tournament,” said the Bermudian Springs head coach.

Both West and Beall are seniors and captains on the Eagles’ squad and brought in the No. 1 seed into the tournament.

Biglerville advanced five wrestlers into the District 3 tournament: Blake Showers (106), Josh Tuckey (120), Eli Tuckey (126), Nick Wright (182), and Brent Hayes (220).

headline: Local Athletes Shine in Wrestling and Football
summary: The article highlights the strong performances of local athletes, including wrestler Hoffman and quarterback Dull, while also providing commentary from coach Dave McCollum.
cleaned_text:
Hoffman was successful in all four of his state matches, winning by pin or technical fall. Of his 45 wins this season, only two lasted the full three periods.

"His technique is incredible," Dull said. "We were tilted before he was hurt, but we got out of it. I just tried to take the experience in. I knew who I was going up against."

Dull, who is also a three-year starting quarterback for Hermannsburg's football team, noted that the toughest part of returning to the game was adapting to non-stop physical activity without breaks between plays.

Hermannsburg head coach Dave McCollum said it took him the first few months of the season to get a feel for Dull's style. Once the possession rolled around, he was confident the coach could manage it.

"I didn't really see him wrestle at all. In seventh and eighth grade, I wasn't really around him," McCollum said. "I knew he was a great player and he was good at every position. I would say about a third into the season I started thinking I had a great place. We were happy where he got placed in the bracket that he had a legitimate shot at making the final, and obviously Chance believed that."

title: The Catholic reach PIAA semifinals
headline: Drivers Warned to Watch for Deer After Daylight Saving Time Ends
summary: The Pennsylvania Game Commission warns motorists to exercise extreme caution for deer when driving between dusk and dawn following the end of daylight saving time.
cleaned_text:
The end of daylight saving time brings a warning from the Pennsylvania Game Commission: motorists must be vigilant for deer while driving between dusk and dawn. The agency advises that the late afternoon and evening are times when deer are most active. Drivers are urged to slow down and stay alert, as a deer in the headlights may be too slow to react to at certain speeds. According to the Game Commission, deer become more active in the autumn leading up to their full breeding season, commonly known as the "rut." Later in the year, deer disperse from their birth areas and may even migrate.

Watch for deer on the move

The end of daylight saving time brings a warning from the Pennsylvania Game Commission to be on the watch for deer while driving between dusk and dawn.

The annual warning for motorists who are traveling during the late afternoon or evening is a time when deer are moving most.

Slow down and stay alert, says the PCAT. At certain speeds, "a deer in the bedlight" may be too slow.

According to the agency, "Deer be more active in autumn with the lead-up to their full breeding season, commonly referred to as the 'nut.' And later in the year, deer peck disperse from the areas in which they were born and travel, sometimes even migrate."

headline: YAIAA Wrestlers to Watch
summary: Local high school wrestling teams, including Susquehannock, Northeastern, Eastern York, and Bermudian Springs, are preparing for the season, with several key athletes and coaches providing updates on their readiness.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun

**YAIAA Wrestlers to Watch**

The YAIAA has 25 wrestlers who could make noise in District 3 and beyond this season.

**Susquehannock** could be dangerous coming off a 10-6 season. The Warriors bring back six starters, highlighted by junior 152-pounder Colby Romjue. Susquehannock’s biggest loss is the older Romjue brother, Joey, who went 29-3 and won a sectional title at 170 pounds last season. The Warriors should come close to fielding a full lineup.

**Northeastern** drops down a division after struggling in Division I in recent years. The Bobcats have some of the strongest light-weight wrestlers in the league, including state medalist Cole Wilson and Tom Gradwell. Northeastern suffered from forfeiting multiple weights last season, but hopes to come close to a full lineup this year. Head coach Dan Wilson said the team should have at least 12 spots filled.

**Eastern York** went under the radar last season but still finished 1-8 and gained a lot of young wrestlers experience. The team does need to replace former state-qualifier and 182-pounder Jahshim Snyder, who topped 30 wins each of the last.

**Bermudian Springs** brings back 11 returning starters, including five—Chase Dull, Trenton Harder, Nate McCollum, Korey Smith, and Hogan Swenski—who topped 20 wins last season. The Eagles hope to reach the team state tournament after missing out last season. The status of Dull, who reached the 195-pound state finals in Class 2A last season, remains unclear after he injured his leg in the football team's District 3 Championship game. Longtime head coach [Name missing/unclear] called in reference to Dull’s injury.

There has been talk in past years about Bermudian Springs bumping up a division or two, but the Eagles are content where they are as a Class 2A school. The Eagles always compete against a tough non-division schedule that includes a handful of bigger YAIAA teams and rival powerhouse Boiling Springs.

“We get challenged enough in our non-league schedule and at the district level,” said [Name missing/unclear].

title: “Dave
headline: Eagles Edge Out Boiling Springs in Intense Rivalry Dual
summary: The Eagles of Bermudian Springs defeated Boiling Springs in a highly competitive rivalry dual, securing key victories in the early weight classes.
cleaned_text:
The Patriot-News
Sun, Jan 27, 2019

YORK SPRINGS — The early momentum swings were huge in the rivalry match between Bermudian Springs and Boiling Springs. Throughout the intense dual, Bermudian Springs coach Rod Wright remained calm. While he occasionally took exception to calls or his team's actions on the mat, he coached with the demeanor of a man who was not defined by the day's result.

This relaxed approach may have been beneficial, as the Eagles secured the first two bouts, fueled by their home crowd, leading the match to a 31-25 victory.

“Are you kidding me? These kids have worked hard over the last three weeks, and we're right where we want to be as far as our work ethic, our intensity, and our focus,” Wright stated.

The timing of the regular-season dual between two of District 3’s most successful programs fed into Wright’s measured mood. He knew that paths to desirable matchups in the district team tournament existed regardless of whether they won or lost this dual. All that was left was to wrestle the match and let the chips fall.

“It’s all about brackets and where you go,” Wright added. “I’m looking ahead to next week. This match here determined a lot. I want to see what happens now.”

Boiling Springs entered the mat ranked No. 4 in the Class. The Eagles caught momentum and also won most of the bouts in the first two weight classes, securing tight victories at 132 and 138 pounds.

McCollum, the coach for Bermudian Springs, noted that the two Rawsons performed well, stating that everyone wrestled hard, just as they had done in previous matches.

Other standout performances included Ryan Lauver, who limited the competition in the 285-pound weight class. Josh Keller’s performance was also a huge point in the match.

“Lauver wrestled a great match, minutes keeping it to a three-pointer, keeping it to a four-pointer,” one observer noted.

Meanwhile, Boiling Springs state medalist Kollin Myers, who was a King of the Mountain champion on December 15, needed only one flip to secure a path to victory. The Bubblers also earned points for an unsportsmanlike conduct call.

Bermudian Springs wrestling coach McCollum guided the squad to a significant victory at the Blue Streak Duals. In a key match against Manheim Township, the team secured a 40-34 win, marking McCollum’s 600th career victory.

This milestone win capped an undefeated Blue Streak Duals performance for the Eagles (9-0). The team swept five matches to claim their second consecutive Streak Duals title. Although Newport also went 5-0 on the day, the Eagles were declared the champion based on great points.

In his biggest test of the day, the team faced Newport, solidifying their status as a dominant force in the sport.

Drew Crowell traveled to South Korea in 2014 with a fellowship at Athletic Achieves. During his first visit to a seminar, he became deeply invested in the country’s national and world-class coaching methods.

title: ae
headline: Sportsmanship and Season Updates
summary: The article provides updates on local sports teams, highlights the return of wrestling coach McCollum, and addresses the community need for high school officials.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Sun, Dec 11, 2022

**A Note on Sportsmanship**
There is a time to cheer, and a time for silence. When the opposing basketball team is shooting a free throw, have at it. When the official makes a call you don’t agree with, be considerate. When in doubt, remember the letter Z of this list. When the opposing team is being introduced, be considerate. When a race is about to start in swimming, be quiet.

**R - Returning Champ**
Susquehannock’s Jacob Wade picked up plenty of hardware last year. The senior won league titles in the 200 free and 100 fly, but didn’t swim either.

**U - Upstart Central York Panthers**
Central York’s boys’ basketball team is young. Junior Greg Guidinger (6-7) and sophomore Ben Rill (6-8) are receiving Division I attention to play in college. Sophomore guard Ben Natal (6-1) might not be far behind. But the Panthers don’t want to wait to grow into their youngsters. They want to make a splash this season.

**The Mat is Calling**
The man nicknamed “Mouse” is back for another year by the mat. The winning wrestling coach in District 3 history, with a career dual record of 627-229-3, McCollum is back for his 45th season leading the Bermudian Springs team.

**Year-End Break**
Many students enjoy the break from Christmas to New Year’s Day. With both holidays falling on a weekend, their break isn’t as long this year. For winter athletes, however, there is no break. Most basketball teams participate in holiday tournaments, and all sports hold daily practices. Hoop fans will be able to find games from Dec. 27-30 in area gyms.

**The Officials’ Shortage**
The high school officials’ shortage is a real thing. Games were moved in the fall because there weren't enough officials to cover the complete schedule. Fans, players, and coaches can do their part in retaining the officials by being kind to the zebras. And if you think you can do better, become an official.

headline: Bermudian Springs Freshman Yacoviello-Andrus Battles for State Medal
summary: Despite an early loss at the PIAA Wrestling Championships, Bermudian Springs freshman Hayden Yacoviello-Andrus remains in contention for a state medal.
cleaned_text:
Hayden Yacoviello-Andrus, a freshman at Bermudian Springs, aims to become a state champion. He has been wrestling for most of his life and was dominant during his first high school season, compiling a 32-3 record, including 28 wins by pin or technical fall, entering the district tournament last month.

At the PIAA Wrestling Championships, Yacoviello-Andrus faced an extremely tough bout against Burrell's Cooper Hornack in the first round. The freshman lost, 6-1, which immediately dropped him from championship contention. However, Yacoviello-Andrus remains in the running for a Class 2A state medal at 127 pounds.

The team coach, McCollum, spoke about the challenges ahead, noting that "every match (at states) is a war." He expressed confidence in his athletes, stating that the team needs to maintain its focus and determination to reach the top of the podium.

The Morning Call
Two area coaches, Matthew V. Terrell and Chad Shirt, are approaching milestones in their careers. Meanwhile, the Cowell team boasts 697 career victories and has three regular-season dual meets remaining, scheduled for Wednesday at Allentown, Saturday at Wallepuack, and January 29 at Pleasant Valley.

Only four coaches in the state have more wins than Cowell. Other notable coaches mentioned include Ridley's Carl Schenk, Jason, Danielle Bonn, Kramblek, and Barramundi Brister. Schoenfeld, the coach of the Daytime Winston Classic, is also on the team.

Chad Shirt has accumulated 398 wins over 20 seasons with the Phantom. The team has regular-season matches scheduled (Wednesday at Paladie) and a national tournament slam day before the District 11 Class 2A team tournament at Freedom. Earlier this season, Shirt passed the current Valley assistant coach, Ben Rob, in terms of years coaching at Wallepuack.

headline: Local Teams Share Unique Pre-Game Rituals
summary: Local high school teams, including those in basketball, bowling, and swimming, are noted for their unique pre-game routines and traditions.
cleaned_text:
The Evening Sun
Thu Nov 14, 2024

Bermuda Springs boys basketball team and Dover girls basketball team are known for their pre-game routines. While the Dover girls often blast music like Ice Spice before games, the Dover boys team blasts 'I'm a Rose' by Rick Ross in the locker room.

Other local teams also have unique pre-game rituals. The Spring Grove girls bowling team features a rubber duck uniform mascot named 'Turkey.'

In other sports, the Central York girls basketball team sometimes uses a classroom instead of a traditional locker room before a game and at halftime. Meanwhile, the Bermuda Springs coach, Dave McCollum, is noted for his involvement with the team.

The Kennard-Dale School team eats at Plyt Street before each game. The school recently graduated seven seniors, with Scipio and Robby Hicks being two players with vast experience.

West York swim team eats at West York after the district meet.

The Susquehannock girls basketball team is known for having its best practice after a loss.
